EDITOR ’ S PREFACE Neurology’s Action Potential: Reinventing Neurology Education for the AI Era We educators have a peculiar tendency to amplify the risks of new information technologies. The internet, for some reason, has been roundly viliﬁed for its effects on attention span, learning, and attention span. 1 Renaissance scholars worried that the printing press would unleash a ﬂood of misinformation. 2 Even Socrates, whose eponymous teaching style is still used in medical education today, had reservations… about writing 3 (he thought it would detract from the value of learning through dialogue). In this sense, those of us who are anxious about the dangers of AI for clinical training are in good company. AI will deeply alter the practice of medicine and delivery of healthcare. When and how are two noteworthy remaining questions. And in the long run, these tools will almost certainly improve outcomes of clinical care. They will also inevitably change the processes, and the goals, of clinical training. Education pragmatists acknowledge that the era of AI in education is already upon us and here to stay. With immediate access to tools that can instantaneously review and synthesize the literature, trainees see less value in rote memorization of lists, tables, and charts. Does this change mean that the physician will be obsolete, or that current trainees are doomed to become expensively-trained AI prompt engineers? No and no. The role of the human in the clinical loop will change, in some exciting and enriching ways. Skills in empathy and interpersonal connection will be prized. The ability to take an avalanche of clinical data and reconcile it with the patient’s clinical reality will be priceless. As educators, we must anticipate these future competencies. We should have a high risk tolerance for innovation in education, and our profession has an enormous opportunity to redesign our education systems to nurture them in our learners. And this issue of Continuum, expertly guest- edited by Dr Aaron Berkowitz, an esteemed clinician-educator, is a perfect resource for the neurologist, junior or senior, to learn not what to think, but how to think. The issue begins with Dr Berkowitz’s fascinating discussion of avoidable pitfalls in diagnostic reasoning. The reviews that follow are neatly organized by organ system, covering everything the neurologist needs to know about the complications of cardiac mishaps (by Drs Alvin Das and Steven Feske), renal and electrolyte disorders (by Dr Eelco Wijdicks), gastrointestinal disease (by Dr Jessamyn Conell-Price), endocrine pathology (by Dr Raﬁd Mustafa), hematologic disorders (by Drs Lauren Patrick and Mark Terrelonge), rheumatologic disease (by Dr Shamik Bhattacharyya, Continuum’s Associate Editor of Neuroimaging), critical illness (by Dr Shivani Ghoshal), cancer and its exploding list of treatments (by Dr Amy Pruitt), pregnancy and menopause (by Dr Sara LaHue), and the countless CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 11 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by Maria Bonilla on 5 February 2026

EDITOR’S PREFACE (CONTINUED) disasters caused by drugs and alcohol (by Dr Adeline Goss). If you’re looking for deﬁnitive reviews by leading experts on the neurologic manifestations of systemic disease, look no further. An old challenge facing clinician-educators is how to balance clinical demands against the needs of learners. Following the theme of education innovation, this issue’s Selected Topics in Neurology Practice article offers practical, creative advice for neurologists who want to weave education into the fabric of the clinical day, authored by Drs Mariah Lyn Robertson and Rachel Marie Salas. After reading the issue, subscribers can obtain up to 20 AMA PRA Category 1 Credits TM toward self- assessment CME or, for Canadian participants, a maximum of 20 hours toward the Self-Assessment Program [Section 3] of the Maintenance of Certiﬁcation Program of the Royal College of Physicians and Surgeons of Canada with our posttest, written for the issue by Drs Adam Kelly and Allison Weathers. If you enjoy podcasts, Continuum Audio provides easily accessible interviews with our guest editors and expert authors, open to everyone on major podcast platforms. CME for these interviews is available to American Academy of Neurology (AAN) members at continpub.com/AudioCME. Continuum subscribers have access to exclusive interviews not found on the podcast and to CME accompanying those interviews. Verbatim audio recordings of each article are available to subscribers through our Continuum Aloud program, found at the article level at ContinuumJournal.com and in the AAN’s Online Learning Center at continpub.com/Aloud. Subscribers can also download Continuum Aloud recordings for ofﬂine listening. Most reasonable observers acknowledge that physicians will not be replaced by AI, although our roles will deﬁnitely evolve to integrate new tools. But what about publishing? As editor-in-chief of this wonderful journal, I’m often asked (usually in a whisper, in case the AI is listening), “Will AI replace Continuum?” Recognizing that technologic complacency has ﬁlled many graveyards with previously successful institutions, I think the answer is no. Continuum’s vision is to be the essential education resource for neurologic clinicians, and in that spirit, it has adapted to meet our learners where they are (print issues, electronic media, audio products, and so on). Tools that aggregate and analyze the literature are only as good as their inputs, and as the physician’s role evolves to serve the needs of patients, Continuum will evolve to serve the needs of our subscribers. The educational capabilities enabled by AI will easily eclipse its liabilities. After all, the printing press turned out pretty well. —LYELL K. JONES JR, MD, FAAN EDITOR-IN-CHIEF © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. REFERENCES 1 Ophir E, Nass C, Wagner AD. Cognitive control in media multitaskers. Diagnostic Reasoning in Neurologic Consultation By Aaron L. Berkowitz, MD, PhD, FAAN ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article discusses potential pitfalls when consulting on patients who develop neurologic symptoms in the context of systemic disease and describes strategies to avoid diagnostic errors in this context. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Cognitive biases that shape clinical reasoning have been identiﬁed and studied. Improving clinician awareness of cognitive biases can improve clinical reasoning and reduce the risk of diagnostic errors. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Neurologists consulting on patients with systemic disease should be aware of particular cognitive biases that can lead to misdiagnosis in this context, such as overapplication of Occam’s razor, overly narrow framing of a case because of the speciﬁc consult question, oversimpliﬁcation of the case if the consult question is posed as a “curbside” consult, failure to consider less common diagnoses when the signal-to-noise ratio is typically low for the consult question and context, and overlooking potential diagnoses because of the location in which the patient presents within the hospital. INTRODUCTION W hen a neurologist is consulted to evaluate a patient who develops neurologic symptoms in the context of systemic disease, the consult question is generally formulated as follows: “An X-year-old patient with a history of [systemic disease Y] on [treatment Z] presents with [neurologic symptom A]…” Framing such consults with the systemic condition in a prioritized position generally aids clinical reasoning because neurologic symptoms that develop in this context are commonly related to complications of the underlying systemic disease or its treatment. For example, most patients with diabetes who develop a polyneuropathy have diabetic neuropathy, and most patients with systemic cancer on chemotherapy who develop a polyneuropathy have a chemotherapy- associated neuropathy. Therefore, neurologic consultation in patients with systemic disease generally begins with careful review of the medical record for active medical conditions, chronic medical conditions, and current and recently administered medications. This is followed by consideration of the potential CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 13 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):13 – 22. Address correspondence to Dr Aaron L. Berkowitz, Department of Neurology, University of California, San Francisco, 2540 23rd Street, San Francisco, CA 94143, Aaron.Berkowitz@ucsf.edu. neurologic complications of each of these conditions and treatments to assess whether they may be the cause of the patient’s neurologic symptoms, whether such associations are common or rare, and whether any of these associations are life-threatening and should be exonerated even if their relative likelihood may be low. However, framing neurologic consultation in this way can lead to misdiagnosis if the consulting neurologist overlooks the less common possibility that the patient’s neurologic symptoms are due to an independent process unrelated to the patient’s systemic condition. The framing effect is deﬁned as an “error of initiating diagnostic reasoning by overvaluing an item of clinical information that is presented early in the process.” 1 Framing and other cognitive heuristics (eg, anchoring bias, availability bias, representativeness bias 1 ) most often lead to the correct diagnosis. However, the utility of these heuristics and the resultant facility with which they are activated can lead the consultant neurologist astray in the less common scenario in which the patient’s neurologic condition is not explained by the initial frame. To improve clinical reasoning in neurologic consultation in patients with systemic disease, this article presents ﬁve speciﬁc examples of framing effects to be aware of to decrease the risk of a misdiagnosis or a missed diagnosis: Occam’s razor bias, consult question bias, curbside bias, low signal-to-noise bias, and location bias (TABLE 1-1). OCCAM’S RAZOR BIAS When consulting on a patient who develops neurologic symptoms in the context of a systemic condition, the consulting neurologist generally ﬁrst applies Occam’s razor: 2 It is parsimonious to presume that the neurologic condition is in some way related to the systemic condition or its treatment rather than invoke a separate, independent process. Indeed, this logic yields the correct diagnosis in most cases. For example, the most common cause of confusion in patients hospitalized for a systemic condition is delirium or toxic-metabolic encephalopathy, whereas stroke, seizure, meningitis, and encephalitis are rare in this context. Most patients with weakness in the context of systemic illness are deconditioned, fatigued, or both, whereas very few have an independent primary neuromuscular condition. Most patients who develop a tremor after starting a new medication have a drug-induced tremor rather than essential tremor or Parkinson disease. However, one must also consider the possibility that a patient could have a separate, independent neurologic condition unrelated to their systemic disease or its treatment, invoking Hickam’s dictum that “a patient can have as many diseases as they please.” 2 Since this scenario is somewhat less common, neurologists tend to ﬁrst apply Occam’s razor, raising the risk of misdiagnosis if a patient’s clinical diagnosis is better explained by Hickam’s dictum. Neurologists should be aware of the tendency to overapply Occam’s razor when consulting on patients with systemic disease who develop neurologic symptoms. To counteract this bias, in addition to considering the differential diagnosis of a patient’s neurologic symptoms in the context of their systemic condition, the consulting neurologist should consider how the differential diagnosis might change if that context were not present (CASE 1-1). KEY POINTS •The framing effect is deﬁned as an “error of initiating diagnostic reasoning by overvaluing an item of clinical information that is presented early in the process.” •The utility of cognitive heuristics and the resultant facility with which they are activated can lead the consultant neurologist astray in the less common scenario in which the patient’s neurologic condition is not explained by the initial frame. •To counteract Occam’s razor bias, the consulting neurologist should consider how the differential diagnosis would change if that context were not present. •Consult question bias can be mitigated by fully evaluating each patient with a complete neurologic examination, detailed review of neuroimaging and laboratory data, and consideration of the neurologic differential diagnosis independent of the consult question. •Curbside consults have several potential risks: failure to consider important aspects of the case, presumption that the neurologic condition in question is not severe, incidental neuroimaging ﬁndings may not be incidental, and curbside questions about treatment may be based on incorrect diagnoses. 14 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 DIAGNOSTIC REASONING IN N EUROLOGIC CONSULTATION Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

TABLE 1-1 Examples of Framing Effects in Neurologic Consultation in Patients With Systemic Conditions Bias Deﬁnition Application to neurologic consultation Example Considerations to avoid diagnostic error Occam’s razor bias Bias toward the most parsimonious diagnosis Presuming a neurologic symptom in a patient with a systemic condition is related to that condition or its treatment without considering the possibility of an independent, unrelated neurologic diagnosis Presuming a tremor in a patient recently started on a medication is due to that medication as opposed to an independent primary neurologic condition, such as Parkinson disease or essential tremor In addition to considering the differential diagnosis of the patient’s neurologic symptoms in the context of their systemic condition and its treatment, consider the differential diagnosis of the patient’s neurologic symptoms if this context were not present Consult question bias Bias toward framing a case based on the consult question posed Answering a narrow consult question without considering a broader evaluation of the case Answering a question about the treatment of a neurologic condition without evaluating whether the neurologic diagnosis is correct In addition to answering the consult question, perform a complete neurologic examination, review all neuroimaging, and form a diagnostic impression independent of the consult question Curbside bias Bias toward a concrete answer to a discrete question Responding to a simple question from a colleague without evaluating the patient or medical record Answering a question about an incidental neuroimaging ﬁnding without evaluating the patient to determine whether the ﬁnding is truly incidental Ask questions to better determine the context of the curbside consult, review the neuroimaging, and consider converting a curbside consult to a formal consult if the case appears more complex than the curbside question suggests Low signal- to-noise ratio bias Bias toward the most common diagnosis in a clinical scenario in which the likelihood of a serious underlying condition is low Presuming that the most common cause of a patient’s neurologic symptoms is the most likely cause in common consultations in which the likelihood of a primary neurologic diagnosis is extremely low Presuming confusion in a hospitalized patient with a medical illness is due to toxic-metabolic encephalopathy or delirium as opposed to the less likely possibility of stroke, nonconvulsive status epilepticus, meningitis, or encephalitis Consider rarer possible causes of a patient’s neurologic symptoms, particularly if identifying them would change management, be life-threatening, or both Location bias Bias toward a differential diagnosis related to the patient’s location in the hospital Placing too much emphasis on the location of the patient’s presentation (eg, clinic versus emergency department versus ward versus intensive care unit) in formulating a differential diagnosis Neglecting to consider a diagnosis more commonly made in the outpatient setting (eg, Parkinson disease) because the patient is seen in the emergency department with an acute, unrelated symptom Consider reframing the case as if the patient were in a different location (eg, considering whether the differential diagnosis, evaluation, or treatment would be different if the patient were in the clinic rather than the emergency department or vice versa) CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 15 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. CASE 1-1 A 72-year-old woman with a history of breast cancer complicated by brain metastases treated with resection and whole-brain radiation therapy presented to the neurology clinic for evaluation of cognitive and motor decline. She spoke very few words, so the history was obtained from her husband, who recounted that over the years since her brain surgery and radiation treatment, she had become more withdrawn and unable to walk unassisted, requiring a wheelchair. The patient’s chart and neuroimaging were reviewed prior to her clinic appointment, and it was presumed that her decline was related to extensive cerebral metastases, resection of a large frontal metastasis, and extensive white matter changes due to whole-brain radiation therapy. On examination, the patient was awake and alert but had minimal speech output, which was delayed and soft. She had subtle cogwheeling in her upper limbs. With the unexpected discovery of this sign of parkinsonism, her blunted affect and slow, delayed verbal and motor output—initially attributed to abulia due to severe frontal and subcortical white matter injury—were reinterpreted as hypomimia, hypophonia, and bradykinesia due to parkinsonism. The neurologist explained that the patient’s symptoms were likely multifactorial and that her parkinsonism may be radiation induced, but that a trial of carbidopa/levodopa would be reasonable given the possibility of concurrent Parkinson disease unrelated to her cancer history. After 1 week of carbidopa/levodopa 25/100 three times a day, the patient was able to walk unassisted and speak ﬂuently with only mild cognitive deﬁcits. COMMENT This case illustrates the risk of overapplying Occam’s razor in patients who present with neurologic symptoms in the context of systemic disease. Based on the chart review and initial clinical impression, the case was reﬂexively framed parsimoniously by attributing the patient’s symptoms to her cancer and its treatment, neglecting the possibility of an independent primary neurologic condition. Parkinson disease was only considered when the neurologist inadvertently found cogwheeling when testing the patient’s strength. Occam’s razor serves clinicians well most of the time. In many patients who develop neurologic symptoms in the context of cancer or its treatment, as in this case, the cause is indeed the cancer or its treatment. To avoid overapplying Occam’s razor, consulting neurologists should consider thinking through such cases with the context removed to see if any additional differential diagnoses emerge that had not initially been considered when framing the case based on the context of the patient’s systemic condition. 3 For example, in this case, removing the frame of the patient’s cancer and formulating a differential diagnosis for cognitive and motor slowing in this age group would have likely led to consideration of Parkinson disease. 16 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 DIAGNOSTIC REASONING IN N EUROLOGIC CONSULTATION Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 1-2 The neurology team was consulted by the oncology team to provide recommendations for medical management of a patient with subdural hematomas deemed nonoperable by the neurosurgery team. The subdural hematomas had developed in a 50-year-old man with acute lymphocytic leukemia who had undergone bone marrow transplantation, was severely systemically ill, had thrombocytopenia, and had no history of head trauma. On evaluation, the patient was somnolent and arousable only brieﬂy to sternal rub. There were no focal ﬁndings. A head CT had been read as showing bilateral small subdural hematomas with no intraparenchymal abnormalities. The subdural hematomas were stable on follow-up imaging and were attributed to the patient’s thrombocytopenia. The initial neurology consultant told the primary team that they agreed there was no surgical intervention indicated for the small stable subdural hematomas and that the patient’s mental state was unlikely to be attributable to them, but rather to his severe systemic illness. The neurology team was subsequently reconsulted because of the patient’s persistent altered mental status after his acute systemic medical condition had improved. Reconsidering the neuroimaging independent of the patient’s leukemia, the possibility was raised that the bilateral atraumatic subdural hematomas could be due to intracranial hypotension. Further review of the sagittal imaging demonstrated brain sag. Chart review revealed that the patient had undergone intrathecal chemotherapy, and the diagnosis of frontotemporal brain sag syndrome due to CSF leak was made. 4 After placement of an epidural blood patch, the patient’s mental state improved back to his baseline. 5 COMMENT In this case, the consult question seemed to have a straightforward answer: the small, stable, nonoperable subdural hematomas required no additional medical management, were unlikely to explain the patient’s depressed level of consciousness, and were presumed to be attributable to the patient’s hematologic disease. However, evaluating the case independent of the consult question led to a reframing of the consult as a diagnostic question rather than a therapeutic one: why did this patient develop bilateral subdural hematomas? This led to consideration of a neurologic diagnosis unrelated to the patient’s primary medical condition and related in an atypical way to the treatment of that condition (ie, due to repeated lumbar puncture for intrathecal chemotherapy rather than due to direct toxicity of treatment). The diagnosis resulted in an intervention that dramatically improved the patient’s mental state. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 17 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CONSULT QUESTION BIAS The way a consult question is posed can help guide the consultant to focus their evaluation of a complex case. However, the question may also bias the consultant to frame the case in an overly narrow fashion, increasing the risk of misdiagnosis or incorrect treatment. These risks can be mitigated by fully evaluating each patient with a complete neurologic examination, detailed review of neuroimaging and laboratory data, and consideration of the neurologic differential diagnosis independent of the consult question. Particular care should be taken when being consulted regarding treatment of a previously diagnosed neurologic condition, always conﬁrming that the neurologic diagnosis is indeed correct before providing advice on treatment, prognosis, or both (CASE 1-2). CURBSIDE BIAS A seemingly straightforward consult question for a neurologist from a patient’s primary provider may be posed as a “curbside” consult rather than a CASE 1-3 An internist asked a neurologist the following curbside consult question about a patient admitted for management of alcohol intoxication: “Is the evaluation for incidental cerebellar stroke the same as the evaluation for incidental cerebral stroke?” The initial response from the neurologist was “Yes, it is the same.” The neurologist then realized that they should have reviewed the imaging themselves before providing advice. The cerebellar stroke turned out to be quite large, causing subtle mass effect on the fourth ventricle. The neurologist evaluated the patient and found him to be obtunded. The medical team had attributed the patient’s depressed level of consciousness to alcohol intoxication (which was indeed contributing to his mental state), and the stroke had been considered incidental on a head CT obtained because the patient was found down. The patient was subsequently transferred to the neurologic intensive care unit for emergent management of elevated intracranial pressure and recovered with only mild ataxia. COMMENT In this case, the curbside question seemed straightforward: recommendations for evaluation of a patient with an incidental ischemic stroke. However, the primary provider had not recognized the severity of the stroke due to the confounder of alcohol intoxication and reliance on the radiology report rather than reviewing the CT images. Although some curbside questions are straightforward and may not require full consultation, the accuracy and severity of the neurologic diagnosis should always be conﬁrmed through chart review and evaluation of the primary data (eg, neuroimaging), with a low threshold for converting a curbside consult to a formal consult if the question seems more complex than can be effectively answered without examining the patient and thoroughly reviewing their chart. 18 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 DIAGNOSTIC REASONING IN N EUROLOGIC CONSULTATION Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 1-4 The neurology team was consulted by a nurse in the postoperative anesthesia care unit to evaluate an 83-year-old woman who developed confusion after ankle surgery performed under general anesthesia. The nurse stated that the orthopedic surgeon who operated on the patient was not concerned, recommended the patient be observed and discharged in a few hours, and did not think a neurology consultation was necessary. However, the nurse remained concerned about the patient and consulted the neurology team. As the neurology consult team went to the postoperative anesthesia care unit, they discussed that acute altered mental status in the hospital rarely turns out to be caused by a primary neurologic condition such as stroke, that an ankle surgery would be an extremely low-risk procedure from a cerebrovascular perspective, and that this was likely a postanesthesia effect given the patient’s age and that the surgeon was not concerned. On examination, the patient was awake and alert but did not know her location or the date. The neurologic examination did not reveal any obvious focal ﬁndings. When the neurology team attempted to reassure the nurse that this was likely a postanesthesia effect, the nurse showed the neurology team the patient’s dinner tray, pointing out that the patient had only eaten half of the food on her plate. This led the neurology team to evaluate the patient for neglect, and the patient was found to have extinction to double simultaneous visual and tactile stimulation on the left. Head CT revealed a right superior parietal intraparenchymal hematoma. The cause was presumed to be intraoperative hypertension. COMMENT Neurologists are commonly consulted for confusion in older hospitalized patients, a scenario in which the signal-to-noise ratio for a primary neurologic diagnosis is relatively low. In this case, the only clue to a serious underlying etiology of the patient’s mild confusion was the ﬁnding of extinction to double simultaneous stimulation on examination. Patients are not always assessed for extinction to double simultaneous stimulation unless there is clinical concern for neglect or as part of the National Institutes of Health (NIH) Stroke Scale, and this was therefore not tested for in the initial evaluation of this patient. Although acute confusion in hospitalized patients is rarely due to a primary neurologic condition, life-threatening causes should always be considered. Additionally, in this case, more weight should have been given to the postoperative anesthesia care unit nurse’s level of concern, given that the nurse had extensive experience with how patients typically appear after anesthesia. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 19 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

request for a full consultation. Curbside consults have several potential risks: the way the question is framed may lead to failure to consider important aspects of the case (see the section Consult Question Bias), the casual nature of a curbside consult may bias neurologists to presume the neurologic condition in question is not severe, presumptive incidental neuroimaging ﬁndings may not be incidental (which may be difﬁcult to determine without examining the patient), and curbside questions about treatment may be based on incorrect neurologic diagnoses. A few strategies can help mitigate these risks. When a curbside consult is requested for a neuroimaging ﬁnding, the neuroimaging study should always be reviewed. When a curbside consult is requested for medication titration CASE 1-5 The neurology team was consulted to evaluate a 64-year-old man who presented to the emergency department with dizziness and unsteadiness after a fall. The patient had been brought to the emergency department by bystanders who saw him fall and hit his head. The patient reported mild dizziness, unsteadiness, and headache since the fall. On examination, the patient had weakness of dorsiﬂexion and eversion of his left foot that he said was long- standing, caused by “a slipped disk” and “neuropathy,” and sometimes led to falls. He felt he was at his baseline and asked to be discharged. The neurology team explained to the patient that his fall was likely due to his chronic foot drop (and recommended an ankle-foot orthosis) and that his dizziness and headache were likely due to the head strike and would improve over the coming days. However, during this explanation, a few fasciculations were noted on the patient’s chest by the neurology resident. The patient was referred for outpatient nerve conduction studies and EMG and was found to have amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. COMMENT In this case, the patient’s location in the emergency department suggested acuity, and the patient attributed the neurologic deﬁcit noted on examination (foot drop) to chronic conditions. This led the consulting neurologist to initially set the patient’s foot drop aside and focus instead on the acute problems (fall and dizziness), as is typically done in the emergency department. It was the inadvertent discovery of fasciculations that led to a reframing of the patient’s case not as “acute unsteadiness and fall” but as “chronic foot drop with fasciculations concerning for motor neuron disease.” As discussed in the Consult Question Bias section, this case would have beneﬁted from a reframing that considered the patient’s examination independent of the consult question: asking why the patient had a chronic foot drop rather than why the patient was dizzy after a fall. Given that the fall was the presenting symptom and the patient was in the emergency department, the foot drop was not the primary focus until fasciculations were noted, leading to a reframing of the case. 20 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 DIAGNOSTIC REASONING IN N EUROLOGIC CONSULTATION Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

(eg, for an antiseizure medication), the chart should be reviewed to determine if the diagnosis has been conﬁrmed by a neurologist and how the patient has been managed previously. In all but the simplest of curbside consults (eg, medication titration in a patient with a conﬁrmed neurologic diagnosis), it is often prudent to offer a formal consult, evaluating the patient at the bedside and reviewing the chart in detail to assure nothing is missed (CASE 1-3). LOW SIGNAL-TO-NOISE RATIO BIAS Neurologists are frequently consulted to evaluate patients who develop altered mental status while hospitalized with systemic disease. Most commonly, the cause is toxic-metabolic encephalopathy or delirium, whereas a primary neurologic etiology is relatively rare. 6 The low signal-to-noise ratio for primary neurologic diagnoses in such consults can bias the consultant toward a common-things-being-common frame, leading to misdiagnosis in less common instances in which the patient has a primary neurologic condition. To counteract low signal-to-noise ratio bias, the neurology consultant should always consider potential life-threatening diagnoses that could cause the neurologic presentation, however unlikely they may be. If these diagnoses cannot be deﬁnitively exonerated by bedside evaluation, diagnostic studies such as neuroimaging, electroencephalography, and CSF analysis should be considered, depending on the clinical scenario (CASE 1-4). LOCATION BIAS The patient’s location at the time of presentation often frames the initial differential diagnosis. When consulted in the emergency department, one’s ﬁrst impression is generally that the problem is acute––either a new acute problem or an acute exacerbation of a chronic problem. Therefore, it can be challenging to consider the possibility of an undiagnosed chronic condition in this context. Similarly, patients generally present to the outpatient clinic for evaluation of chronic conditions, so consultants may not initially consider the less likely possibility of a neurologic condition that was acute in onset but has persisted until the time of presentation. To counteract this location bias, the consultant neurologist should consider how the differential diagnosis, evaluation, and treatment would change if the patient had presented in a different location: for example, if a patient in the emergency department had instead presented to the clinic or a patient in the clinic had instead presented to the emergency department (CASE 1-5). CONCLUSION When neurologists are consulted to evaluate neurologic symptoms in a patient with systemic disease, the most prominent differential diagnoses are generally those related to the patient’s systemic condition or its treatment. However, neurologists should be wary of the framing effect in this context, which can lead to failure to consider the possibility of an independent neurologic condition unrelated to the patient’s systemic disease or its treatment. To counteract the framing effect when consulting on patients with systemic disease who develop neurologic symptoms, neurologists should be mindful of ﬁve speciﬁc examples of framing effects that can occur in this KEY POINTS •In all but the simplest of curbside consults, it is often prudent to offer a formal consult, evaluating the patient at the bedside and reviewing the chart in detail to assure nothing is missed. •The low signal-to-noise ratio for primary neurologic diagnoses in certain consult scenarios can bias the consultant toward a common-things- being-common frame, leading to misdiagnosis in less common instances in which the patient has a primary neurologic condition. •To counteract low signal-to-noise ratio bias, the neurology consultant should always consider potential life-threatening diagnoses that could cause the neurologic presentation, however unlikely they may be. •To counteract location bias, the consultant neurologist should consider how the differential diagnosis, evaluation, and treatment would change if the patient had presented in a different location. context: Occam’s razor bias, consult question bias, curbside bias, low signal-to-noise bias, and location bias. Reframing a neurologic consult with these cognitive biases in mind can reduce the risk of misdiagnosis or a missed diagnosis. REFERENCES 1 Vickrey BG, Samuels MA, Ropper AH. Neurologic Complications of Drug and Alcohol Use By Adeline L. Goss, MD ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article reviews the neurologic syndromes associated with substance use and suggests an approach for identifying and managing substance use disorders. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Substance use and overdose mortality, largely associated with fentanyl, rose sharply during the COVID-19 pandemic. Although recent data indicate modest decreases, current rates of overdose remain higher than before the pandemic. A wide variety of opioid-related toxic encephalopathies have been identiﬁed recently. Many novel psychoactive substances are unregulated and easily obtained online or in stores; several have been associated with seizures and other neurologic complications. The use of cannabis and hallucinogens is rising as more states legalize or decriminalize their use, and some studies suggest an independent association between cannabis use and ischemic stroke. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Neurologists often encounter severe complications of substance use and have an opportunity to guide patients with substance use disorders toward treatment. INTRODUCTION T he COVID-19 pandemic fueled a surge in substance use and overdose deaths that has only recently begun to decrease. The drug supply has shifted, with synthetic drugs such as fentanyl and methamphetamine increasingly replacing plant-derived substances, although alcohol, cannabis, and cocaine remain widely used. 1 Illicit drugs, alcohol, inhalants, and misused prescription drugs can cause a wide variety of neurologic syndromes and should be considered in virtually any neurologic presentation (TABLE 11-1). Substance use can progress to substance use disorders, deﬁned as a pattern of use that affects physical or mental health or leads to clinically signiﬁcant functional impairment or distress. 2 These disorders became more common in the years following the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. 3 Environmental exposures are associated with an increased risk of substance use disorders, including younger age of substance use initiation, childhood adverse experiences, psychiatric comorbidities, low income, unemployment, housing instability, and the effects of racism and discrimination. 2 Genetic studies also CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 277 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):27 7– 309. Address correspondence to Dr Adeline L. Goss, Highland Hospital, OA2 #11, 1411 E 31st St, Oakland, California, adgoss@ alamedahealthsystem.org. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Goss has received personal compensation in the range of $5000 to $9999 for serving as an editor, associate editor, or editorial advisory board member for the American Neurological Association. UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Dr Goss reports no disclosure. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

reveal variants associated with substance use disorders, with alcohol use disorder showing the strongest genetic association. 3 Neurologic complications of substance use may be the ﬁrst symptoms that lead patients with substance use disorders to seek care. Therefore, neurologists have a key role in identifying patients with substance use disorders and connecting them to treatment. TABLE 11-1 Neurologic Syndromes Associated With Speciﬁc Substances and Withdrawal Syndromes Syndrome Substance Agitation Delirium tremens (alcohol withdrawal), methamphetamine, cocaine, phencyclidine, dextromethorphan, γ-hydroxybutyrate (GHB), synthetic cathinones, N- (2-methoxybenzyl)phenethylamines (NBOMes) Psychosis, hallucinations Alcohol intoxication, alcohol withdrawal, benzodiazepine withdrawal, methamphetamine, cocaine, kratom, ketamine, phencyclidine, dextromethorphan, psychedelics, synthetic cathinones, synthetic cannabinoids, cannabis Seizures Alcohol withdrawal, benzodiazepine withdrawal, methamphetamine, cocaine, cocaine (levamisole)–induced multifocal leukoencephalopathy, kratom, ﬂunitrazepam, GHB, synthetic cathinones, NBOMes, synthetic cannabinoids, hufﬁng Ischemic stroke Methamphetamine, cocaine, khat, synthetic cannabinoids, chronic cannabis use, chronic tobacco use Intracerebral hemorrhage Chronic alcohol use complicated by cirrhosis, methamphetamine, cocaine, synthetic cannabinoids Subarachnoid hemorrhage Methamphetamine, cocaine, synthetic cannabinoids Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome Methamphetamine, cocaine, cannabis Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) Methamphetamine, cocaine Serotonin syndrome Methamphetamine, cocaine, 3,4-methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA), amphetamines, dextromethorphan, lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD), NBOMes, combinations of these drugs with other serotonergic agents Movement disorders Alcohol (tremor), methamphetamine (punding, choreoathetoid movements, bruxism, tremor, dystonia, orolingual dyskinesias); cocaine (dyskinesias, dystonia, tics), dextroamphetamine-amphetamine salts and methylphenidate (tics), MDMA (bruxism, trismus, tremor, myoclonus), phencyclidine (myoclonus, dystonia), dextromethorphan (dystonia), synthetic cathinones (bruxism) Cerebellar signs Alcohol intoxication, Wernicke encephalopathy, alcoholic cerebellar degeneration, ketamine, phencyclidine, dextromethorphan, GHB, synthetic cannabinoids, chronic toluene hufﬁng Myeloneuropathy Alcohol-related vitamin B 12 , folate, or copper deﬁciency; nitrous oxide Peripheral neuropathy Chronic alcohol use, alcohol-related thiamine or folate deﬁciency, hufﬁng 278 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

SUBSTANCE USE HISTORY, EXAMINATION, AND TESTING An empathetic, nonjudgmental approach is essential to gathering a substance use history. Helpful strategies include asking ﬁrst about more socially accepted substances (eg, tobacco, alcohol, cannabis) and providing a medical rationale for asking about use (eg, “Strokes are uncommon in young people, but some drugs can cause strokes. Have you ever tried cocaine or methamphetamine?”). Because many drugs cause cumulative nervous system injury, patients should be asked about both present substance use and past use, recent frequency of use, and date of last use. It is also important to ask about the route of drug use, particularly whether patients are injecting drugs. When patients have altered mental status, are intubated, or are otherwise unable to provide a reliable history, collateral history about potential drug exposures should be gathered. Electronic prescription drug monitoring programs and laboratory data can provide further clues. Patients with conﬁrmed substance use should undergo a thorough social and psychiatric history. Vital signs and the neurologic examination are the most useful clues to intoxication, but certain physical examination ﬁndings may also be present in chronic substance use. For example, jaundice, palmar erythema, and asterixis may accompany alcohol use disorder complicated by liver disease. Methamphetamine use can cause repetitive skin picking, causing ulcers and excoriations, 4 as well as increased dental wear and decay. Injection drug use can produce scars (ie, “track marks”) at injection sites, bruising, and patches of hyperpigmentation, most commonly but not exclusively in the antecubital fossa. “Skin popping” (ie, injecting opioids, stimulants, and other drugs under the skin) can produce round scars, abscesses, and cellulitis. 5 Suggested laboratory testing for the evaluation of substance use is shown in TABLE 11-2. 6 It is important to note the limitations of urine drug screens, notably that these immunoassays typically do not detect fentanyl or other synthetic drugs; as of late 2025, only 31% of United States emergency department encounters for overdose included fentanyl testing. 7 However, in 2022, California became the ﬁrst state to enact a law requiring acute care hospitals to include fentanyl testing when conducting urine drug screens, 8 and several other states have followed suit. ALCOHOL In the United States, approximately 1 in 10 people older than 12 years has alcohol use disorder, and alcohol is the most common cause of substance-related emergency department visits. 9 Alcohol causes relaxation and disinhibition through enhanced activity of γ-aminobutyric acid A (GABA A ) receptors and inhibition of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors. 10 Chronic alcohol use is toxic to neurons, glia, and myelin sheaths by causing oxidative stress and enhanced apoptosis. 11 Alcohol Intoxication Alcohol intoxication causes gait ataxia, ataxic dysarthria, and nystagmus, caused by effects on the cerebellum, as well as impaired executive function, inattention, anxiety, and depressed mood caused by effects on the prefrontal and limbic regions. 12,13 These symptoms can be seen at blood alcohol concentrations of 0.08 g/dL to 0.10 g/dL, 10 whereas concentrations greater than 0.40 g/dL can cause stupor, respiratory depression, and death. 11 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 279 KEY POINTS •Illicit drugs, alcohol, inhalants, and misused prescription drugs can cause a wide variety of neurologic syndromes and should be considered in virtually any neurologic presentation. •Substance use disorders became more common in the years following the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. •An empathetic, nonjudgmental approach is essential to gathering a substance use history. •It is important to note the limitations of urine drug screens, notably that these immunoassays typically do not detect fentanyl or other synthetic drugs; as of late 2025, only 31% of emergency department encounters for overdose included fentanyl testing. •Alcohol intoxication causes gait ataxia, ataxic dysarthria, and nystagmus, as well as impaired executive function, inattention, anxiety, and depressed mood due to effects on the cerebellum and prefrontal and limbic regions. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

TABLE 11-2 Laboratory Evaluation in Suspected Substance Use Laboratory ﬁndings suggestive, but not conﬁrmatory, of chronic alcohol use 6 u Blood alcohol concentration (1-12 hours after consumption; indicative of recent but not chronic use) u Serum γ-glutamyltransferase (GGT) >0.58 μkat/L u Aspartate transaminase (AST) to alanine transaminase (ALT) ratio >2 u Elevated carbohydrate-deﬁcient transferrin u High-normal mean corpuscular volumes (≥96 fL) u Low serum magnesium, phosphate, or both u Low vitamin B 12 , folate, or both General laboratory evaluation in suspected intoxication u Fingerstick blood glucose u Arterial blood gas u Basic metabolic panel (anion gap, creatinine, blood urea nitrogen) u Liver enzyme panel u Creatine kinase u Blood alcohol concentration u Rapid urine toxicology u Rapid urine fentanyl if available u ECG Urine drug screens u These are inexpensive, rapid tests for common, traditional recreational drugs and their metabolites; most panels include amphetamines, cannabis, cocaine, and opiates u Immunoassay uses antibodies to detect the presence of certain drugs and their metabolites in the urine u Most standard urine drug screens do not detect synthetic opioids, including fentanyl, methadone, and tramadol, or semisynthetic opioids such as oxycodone; given the surge in clinical presentations related to fentanyl, it is advisable for hospital laboratories to perform in-house urine drug screens for fentanyl u Screens generally detect drug use within the past 24-72 hours, but amphetamines and cocaine accumulate in the urine with repeated doses; methamphetamine has been detected up to 7 days and cocaine up to 14 days after last use on urine drug screens CONTINUED ON PAGE 281 280 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CONTINUED FROM PAGE 280 u False-positive results for methamphetamines may occur with use of decongestants (pseudoephedrine, brompheniramine, phenylpropanolamine, nasal inhaler, ranitidine), antidepressants (bupropion, trazodone), antipsychotics (chlorpromazine, promethazine), and beta-blockers (labetalol, esmolol); because beta-blockers are routinely administered after intracerebral hemorrhage or subarachnoid hemorrhage, it is important not to diagnose a patient with stimulant-induced hemorrhage based on urine drug screens alone u Phencyclidine urine testing may produce false positives in patients using dextromethorphan or carvedilol Serum drug tests and screens u In many hospitals, these are send-out tests that take days or weeks to receive results; specimens should be collected as soon as possible after presentation; some laboratories offer panels (eg, for novel psychoactive substances) Liquid chromatography–high-resolution mass spectroscopy u Comprehensive toxicology screening that allows for untargeted testing of a sample u Chemical substances within the sample are separated, individually identiﬁed via mass spectrometer and identiﬁed based on mass u Primarily available at large academic hospitals and specialized toxicology laboratories CSF ﬁndings u CSF proﬁle is generally normal, aside from levamisole-associated leukoencephalopathy, mild CSF pleocytosis in some cases of substance-related seizures, and infectious complications of drug use Imaging ﬁndings u Patterns by toxic exposure u Bilateral signal change is typical Other tools u Nerve conduction studies and EMG can occasionally be useful (eg, distinguishing the acute peripheral neuropathy of thiamine deﬁciency from acute inﬂammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy) u National Poison Control Center: (800) 222-1222 in the United States CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 281 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Alcohol intoxication can also cause periods of temporary anterograde amnesia (blackouts), 10 or, less commonly, auditory hallucinations. 14,15 Intoxication also disrupts sleep by altering sleep architecture and reducing total sleep time, efﬁciency, and non–rapid eye movement (REM) sleep. 16 Alcohol Withdrawal Alcohol withdrawal occurs when patients with chronic, excess alcohol use suddenly stop drinking. Symptoms start as early as 6 hours after the last drink, peak around 72 hours, and generally subside within a week, although some patients experience prolonged anxiety or sleep disturbances. 10 Common early features of alcohol withdrawal include headache, anxiety, insomnia, tachycardia, diaphoresis, and tremulousness (typically a high-frequency, low-amplitude, irregular action tremor) that usually resolve within 48 hours. 16 Complicated alcohol withdrawal refers to progression to seizures (typically generalized tonic-clonic and occurring 6 to 48 hours after consuming alcohol, even in the absence of other withdrawal symptoms), 17 hallucinations (eg, auditory hallucinations of derogatory voices, or visual and tactile hallucinations of animals or bugs, with intact orientation, occurring 12 to 48 hours after consuming alcohol), 18,19 or delirium tremens (a life-threatening syndrome of fever, tachycardia, hypertension, respiratory alkalosis, agitation, disorientation, and hallucinations that arises from a surge in NMDA, dopamine, and norepinephrine and reduced GABA A activity occurring 48 to 96 hours after consuming and lasting 1 to 5 days). 20 Seizures occur in about 5% and delirium tremens in about 2% of patients with alcohol use disorder. 20-22 Alcohol withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens can be prevented if symptom-triggered management with benzodiazepines (eg, with the Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol–Revised [CIWA-Ar] scale) is initiated early when patients are at risk for withdrawal or demonstrate signs of early withdrawal. 23 Phenobarbital loading can be an effective supplement to benzodiazepines for patients at risk of complicated withdrawal. 24-26 Other medications such as gabapentin, clonidine, valproic acid, dexmedetomidine, and ketamine have also been incorporated into benzodiazepine-sparing alcohol withdrawal protocols, but more research is needed into outcomes of these strategies. 27-29 If alcohol withdrawal seizures occur, they should be treated with benzodiazepines; barbiturates or propofol should be added if benzodiazepines are inadequate because these drugs are considered more effective than other antiseizure medications for the treatment of alcohol withdrawal seizures. 30 Direct Toxic Neurologic Effects of Alcohol Moderate to heavy alcohol use is associated with gray and white matter atrophy. 31 The term alcohol-related dementia encompasses a spectrum of pathologies rather than one disease and may involve direct neurotoxic effects of alcohol, nutritional deﬁciencies, and sequelae of alcohol-related liver disease, head injury from recurrent falls, and alcohol-related intracerebral hemorrhage or ischemic stroke. 32 Chronic alcohol use may also cause alcoholic cerebellar degeneration, 33 leading to an irreversible balance and gait disorder, often accompanied by nystagmus. Neuroimaging in these patients shows atrophy of the cerebellar vermis. Alcohol is also toxic to peripheral nerves and muscle tissue. A length- dependent, sensory-predominant, painful axonal peripheral neuropathy can 282 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

occur in patients with alcohol use disorder and is caused by alcohol’s direct toxic effects, independent of vitamin deﬁciencies. 34 Chronic alcohol-related myopathy presents with proximal muscle weakness that progresses over weeks to months, usually after decades of alcohol use disorder, with creatine kinase elevated up to 6 times normal. 35 Acute alcohol myopathy, which is less common, is characterized by proximal muscle weakness, pain, and swelling after a period of heavy alcohol drinking, with serum creatine kinase levels up to 10 times normal. Thiamine Deﬁciency in Alcohol Use Disorder Alcohol use disorder leads to reduced caloric intake and impaired digestion and absorption, which can result in deﬁciencies of water-soluble B vitamins (eg, thiamine, riboﬂavin, pyridoxine, folate, cobalamin) and trace elements (eg, copper, magnesium). Of these, thiamine deﬁciency is often the ﬁrst to manifest because body thiamine stores are depleted after only about 4 to 6 weeks. 36 Thiamine is a cofactor for several enzymes involved in glucose catabolism and is necessary to synthesize myelin and several neurotransmitters. 37,38 Manifestations of thiamine deﬁciency include Wernicke encephalopathy, Korsakoff syndrome, and dry beriberi (peripheral neuropathy). Wernicke encephalopathy is an acute thiamine deﬁciency syndrome associated with a classic triad of ocular ﬁndings (nystagmus, bilateral lateral rectus palsies, and conjugate gaze palsies), ataxia, and altered mental status. Because the full triad is present in only about 16% to 38% of patients, 36 any patient with known or suspected alcohol use disorder and any of these clinical features should receive empiric thiamine replacement. Serum thiamine concentrations are not accurate markers for tissue levels, so testing is often unnecessary and should never delay treatment. 38 Although traditional teaching suggests giving thiamine before glucose to avoid taxing thiamine-dependent glucose catabolism pathways, there is no evidence that a single bolus of glucose independently causes symptomatic thiamine deﬁciency. 39 Therefore, both hypoglycemia and thiamine deﬁciency should be treated promptly and in no particular order. Brain MRI in patients with Wernicke encephalopathy may show symmetric T2 hyperintensities and enhancement in the bilateral medial thalami, mamillary bodies, and periventricular regions of the third ventricle, aqueduct, and fourth ventricle. At this stage, the symptoms often improve or reverse with aggressive thiamine supplementation. However, severe acute thiamine deﬁciency can progress to coma, and MRI can show diffusion restriction in thiamine- dependent structures (including cortical ribboning) and petechial hemorrhage into the mammillary bodies on susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) (FIGURE 11-1). 36,40 Some patients progress to frank hemorrhage into the mamillary bodies or thalami. 40 If acute thiamine deﬁciency is not treated or treatment is delayed, patients may progress to Korsakoff syndrome, a chronic state characterized by amnesia (especially loss of recent episodic and semantic memory) with confabulation, lack of insight, executive dysfunction, and apathy. 37 Dry beriberi is a length-dependent sensorimotor polyneuropathy due to thiamine deﬁciency that can present either gradually or acutely. Peripheral neuropathy can co-occur with Wernicke encephalopathy or Korsakoff CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 283 KEY POINTS •Common early features of alcohol withdrawal include headache, anxiety, insomnia, tachycardia, diaphoresis, and tremulousness (typically a high-frequency, low- amplitude, irregular action tremor) that usually resolve within 48 hours. •Of the deﬁciencies of water-soluble B vitamins, thiamine deﬁciency is often the ﬁrst to manifest because body thiamine stores are depleted after only about 4 to 6 weeks. •Wernicke encephalopathy is an acute thiamine deﬁciency syndrome associated with a classic triad of ophthalmoparesis, ataxia, and altered mental status. Because the full triad is present in only about 16% to 38% of patients, any patient with known or suspected alcohol use disorder and any of these clinical features should receive empiric thiamine replacement. •If acute thiamine deﬁciency is not treated or treatment is delayed, patients may progress to Korsakoff syndrome, a chronic state characterized by amnesia with confabulation, lack of insight, executive dysfunction, and apathy. •Dry beriberi is a length- dependent sensorimotor polyneuropathy caused by thiamine deﬁciency that can present either gradually or acutely. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

syndrome but may occur in isolation. 41,42 The clinical syndrome of beriberi can mimic Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome, with acute pure sensory, pure motor, or sensorimotor neuropathy or polyradiculoneuropathy. CSF protein is typically normal, but albuminocytologic dissociation has been described. 41 Electrodiagnostic testing shows an axonal polyneuropathy. Because this presentation can mimic Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome, nerve conduction studies and EMG are not universally available, and both the clinical and electrophysiologic presentation of pure motor beriberi may mimic acute motor axonal neuropathy (AMAN), empiric treatment with thiamine should be strongly considered in patients with a history of heavy alcohol use, risk of malnutrition, or both who present with acute polyneuropathy. Marchiafava-Bignami Disease Marchiafava-Bignami disease is a rare syndrome associated with alcohol use disorder and malnutrition that can cause neuropsychiatric symptoms (including apathy and psychosis), pyramidal signs, gaze disorders, and seizures. 38,43 MRI shows T2 hyperintensities and diffusion restriction in the corpus callosum, usually spanning the entire corpus callosum (splenium, body, and genu) 44,45 and, at times, involving the corticospinal tracts, cerebellar peduncles, and centrum semiovale. 43,45 These lesions may peripherally enhance in the acute phase. 46 Although some patients recover, others develop callosal necrosis and atrophy with irreversible neurologic deﬁcits. 45 Secondary Effects of Alcohol on the Central Nervous System Excessive alcohol consumption can produce hyponatremia through multiple mechanisms, and patients may develop osmotic demyelination syndrome if they undergo overly rapid sodium correction. Osmotic demyelination syndrome causes encephalopathy, extrapyramidal symptoms, seizures, and coma. 47,48 MRI classically shows diffusion restriction in the lower pons (with a trident-shaped appearance), basal ganglia, midbrain, and subcortical white matter. 49 For further discussion of osmotic demyelination syndrome, see the article “Neurologic Manifestations of Renal and Electrolyte Disorders” by Eelco F. M. Wijdicks, MD, PhD, FAAN, FACP, FNCS, 50 in this issue of Continuum. Finally, undiagnosed cirrhosis and hepatic encephalopathy should be considered in any patient with current or past alcohol use disorder and unexplained encephalopathy. 51 OPIOIDS Opioids include both natural compounds derived from the opium poppy plant (opiates, eg, heroin, morphine) and synthetic compounds that mimic opiates (eg, fentanyl, oxycodone). The current wave of the US opioid crisis has been FIGURE 11-1 Axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) showing petechial hemorrhage (hypointensity, arrows) of the mammillary bodies in a patient with alcohol use disorder and undertreated thiamine deﬁciency. 284 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

driven by illicitly produced fentanyl, which the Drug Enforcement Agency (DEA) calls the deadliest drug threat the United States has ever faced. 1,52 Fentanyl is more potent, shorter-acting, and cheaper than heroin, costing as little as $0.50 to $2 per pill. 53,54 These properties contributed to a 3.6-fold increase in drug overdose deaths in the United States between 2003 and 2022. Although the number of overdose deaths has declined since, they have not returned to pre-COVID-19 pandemic rates. 55 Fentanyl contamination of stimulants and counterfeit prescription pills is also increasing. 1 This trend is extremely dangerous for people without fentanyl tolerance, leading to accidental overdoses. Additionally, intentional combined opioid and stimulant use is rising. Combined fentanyl-stimulant overdoses rose from 0.6% of overdose deaths in 2010 to 32.3% in 2021, with cocaine being the most commonly coinvolved stimulant in the northeastern and southeastern United States and methamphetamine more common in the western United States. 56 In addition to fentanyl, heroin and diverted and illicitly manufactured prescription opioids (eg, oxycodone, codeine, morphine) remain in use. 50 Opioid Intoxication and Overdose Opioids are used for their euphoric and analgesic effects, producing easily recognizable signs of intoxication and withdrawal, as outlined in TABLE 11-3. The analgesic effects and respiratory depression are produced via opioid receptors in the brainstem, thalamus, and spinal cord and euphoria and reinforcement through dopaminergic pathways. 57,58 IV and inhaled opioid use causes more euphoria than oral or transdermal routes because of the more rapid rate of change of dopaminergic activity. 59 Opioid overdose causes severe respiratory depression, which can result in cardiac arrest and hypoxic-ischemic brain injury. However, several MRI patterns can be seen in the setting of opioid overdose that must be distinguished from the classic pattern of diffusion restriction caused by hypoxic-ischemic brain injury (ie, involving the basal ganglia, cerebellar hemispheres, cerebral cortex, thalami, and hippocampi). 60 Toxic Encephalopathies From Opioid Overdose A variety of clinical-radiologic syndromes have been described in patients with opioid overdose, including heroin-associated spongiform leukoencephalopathy; opioid-associated amnestic syndrome; cerebellar, hippocampal, and basal nuclei transient edema with restricted diffusion (CHANTER) syndrome; and pediatric opioid use-associated neurotoxicity with cerebellar edema (POUNCE). Chasing the dragon refers to inhaling vapors of heroin heated over aluminum foil. In some patients, this exposure can cause heroin-associated spongiform leukoencephalopathy, a syndrome characterized by progressive apathy and cerebellar signs that may be accompanied by spastic quadriparesis, parkinsonism, pseudobulbar palsy, tremor, chorea, and myoclonus and can progress to coma or death. 61,62 MRI shows conﬂuent, symmetric white matter diffusion restriction and T2 hyperintensities involving the cerebellar white matter and posterior limb of the internal capsule, extending into the corticospinal tracts, but sparing the subcortical U-ﬁbers. 63,64 There is no evidence-based treatment, although antioxidant therapy has been suggested. 65 Recovery is variable and occurs over months. A similar clinical-radiographic syndrome has been reported with fentanyl and other opioids (CASE 11-1). 66 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 285 KEY POINTS •Opioid overdose causes severe respiratory depression, which can result in cardiac arrest and hypoxic-ischemic brain injury. •Heroin-associated myelopathy is an acute myelopathy that most commonly occurs in patients with a history of chronic heroin use who resume use after a period of abstinence.
