Opioid-associated amnestic syndrome is a more focal toxic encephalopathy caused by opioid overdose, resulting in profound anterograde amnesia, with MRI showing diffusion restriction selectively in the hippocampi and, less frequently, the globus pallidi. Recovery varies, with some patients improving over months and others having persistent anterograde memory deﬁcits. 67 CHANTER syndrome is characterized by overdose-related stupor or coma, with MRI showing bilateral, symmetric diffusion restriction in the cerebellar cortex, hippocampi, and basal ganglia, but sparing the cerebral cortex. Some patients may progress to severe cerebellar edema and obstructive hydrocephalus and may require emergent neurosurgical decompression. The prognosis from CHANTER syndrome ranges from functional independence to death. 68 POUNCE syndrome presents as encephalopathy, mutism, ataxia, visual impairment, quadriparesis, and seizures in children who overdose on opioids. On MRI, this manifests as symmetric diffusion restriction and T2 hyperintensity of the cerebellar hemispheres. POUNCE syndrome may progress to malignant cerebellar edema, hydrocephalus, and tonsillar herniation, at times requiring posterior decompression. 69-71 Heroin-Associated Myelopathy Heroin-associated myelopathy is an acute myelopathy that most commonly occurs in patients with a history of chronic heroin use who resume use after a period of abstinence. 72,73 The clinical syndrome is one of complete transverse myelopathy, affecting motor, sensory, and sphincter function. MRI shows a longitudinally extensive, transverse T2 hyperintensity with cord expansion spanning the cervical and thoracic regions. 74 Enhancement and diffusion restriction are typically absent. CSF values are usually normal, although some case reports describe mild pleocytosis. 72 Patients typically make a partial or complete recovery over weeks to months. The condition may not be unique to heroin; a similar presentation was reported in a patient after a period of abstinence from fentanyl. 75 Opioid Withdrawal Opioid tolerance develops within days to weeks of daily use. 59 Once a patient develops tolerance, withdrawal symptoms (TABLE 11-3) begin within hours of the last dose and can last days (short-acting opioids) or weeks (long-acting opioids). Opioid withdrawal is extremely uncomfortable but rarely fatal in adults. In contrast, the abstinence syndrome that occurs in neonates with fetal exposure to opiates can cause signiﬁcant morbidity, including feeding difﬁculties, tremor, myoclonus, and seizures. 76 STIMULANTS Widely used stimulants include methamphetamine and cocaine, both of which can cause stroke, seizures, hyperkinetic movement disorders, and psychosis, as discussed in this section. Several less commonly used stimulants can also produce neurologic complications, including khat, kratom, and misused prescription stimulants. 288 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Methamphetamine Amphetamines (including methamphetamine) are synthetically produced; this property makes these drugs easier to produce than plant-derived cocaine, with global amphetamine use now surpassing cocaine and heroin use combined. 52 Methamphetamine increases the release of dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine and blocks their reuptake and degradation, with a longer half-life than cocaine (approximately 10 to 12 hours versus 60 to 90 minutes) and clinical effects lasting up to 24 hours. 77 Repeated use disrupts dopamine transmission 78 and damages neurons via oxidative stress, neuroinﬂammation, and excitatory neurotoxicity. 77 Methamphetamine appears as a highly pure crystalline form (known as “ice,” “crystal,” and “glass”), as a less pure powder or base, 78 or as tablets that may be falsely labeled as prescription drugs or party drugs, which can lead to unintentional use. 1 People who use methamphetamine often seek euphoria, improved self-esteem, and increased energy or alertness. Signs of intoxication are listed in TABLE 11-3 and include agitation, psychosis, and hyperadrenergic signs. The acute subjective effect diminishes over about 4 hours, but cardiovascular effects last longer and can be ampliﬁed by repeated use. In patients with chronic use and abrupt cessation, methamphetamine withdrawal begins within 12 to 24 hours and is characterized by exhaustion, increased sleep duration, depressed mood, and anxiety. A 2- to 4-week withdrawal phase follows, with vivid dreams, sleep disturbances, increased appetite, and psychomotor slowing. 78 Cocaine Cocaine (benzoylmethylecgonine) is a weak base derived from the leaves of the Erythroxylum coca plant grown in South America. Despite a contracting US market, 52 cocaine-related deaths have risen every year since 2015, driven by polysubstance use, including fentanyl. 1 Cocaine is a white powder (“blow,” “powder”) or whitish rock crystal (“crack”) used by snorting, smoking, or injecting. It acts as a potent stimulant by increasing synaptic concentrations of dopamine, norepinephrine, and serotonin through reuptake inhibition, and its subjective effects mirror those of methamphetamine but are shorter lasting (generally 30 to 60 minutes for snorted cocaine, 5 to 10 minutes for crack cocaine). 79 Clinical features of intoxication and withdrawal are similar to those of methamphetamine (TABLE 11-3). 80 Cerebrovascular Complications of Methamphetamine and Cocaine Both methamphetamine and cocaine cause a dose-dependent hypertensive surge that can damage and rupture small penetrating arteries, leading to intracerebral hemorrhage, cause aneurysm formation and subarachnoid hemorrhage, or result in small or large vessel ischemic disease, causing ischemic stroke. Methamphetamine-associated and cocaine-associated intracerebral hemorrhage follow a hypertensive hemorrhage pattern, with deep foci more common than lobar hemorrhages. 81,82 Intracerebral hemorrhage is the most common stroke type in individuals actively using these drugs. 83 Methamphetamine-associated and cocaine-associated subarachnoid hemorrhage is usually aneurysmal, 83,84 with aneurysm morphology and risk of CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 289 KEY POINTS •Amphetamines (including methamphetamine) are synthetically produced; this property makes these drugs easier to produce than plant-derived cocaine, with global amphetamine use now surpassing cocaine and heroin use combined. •Cocaine’s subjective effects mirror those of methamphetamine but are shorter lasting (generally 30 to 60 minutes for snorted cocaine, 5 to 10 minutes for crack cocaine). Clinical features of intoxication and withdrawal are similar to those of methamphetamine. •Both methamphetamine and cocaine cause a dose- dependent hypertensive surge that can damage and rupture small penetrating arteries, leading to intracerebral hemorrhage, cause aneurysm formation and subarachnoid hemorrhage, or result in small vessel ischemic disease, causing ischemic stroke. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

vasospasm comparable with patients who do not use stimulants. 85,86 Management of methamphetamine-associated and cocaine-associated intracerebral hemorrhage and subarachnoid hemorrhage is the same as non– stimulant-associated intracerebral hemorrhage and subarachnoid hemorrhage, with an additional emphasis on stimulant use counseling. Although beta- blockers have generally been recommended against in patients with acute stroke related to stimulant use because unopposed alpha-stimulation could increase systemic vasoconstriction, several studies have demonstrated the safety and efﬁcacy of beta-blockers in patients with stimulant toxicity. 87,88 Methamphetamine and cocaine use also increases ischemic stroke risk through many pathways, including accelerated small and large vessel atherosclerosis. 81,83 Stroke may also be mediated by cardiac toxicity, including stimulant-associated cardiomyopathy, atrial ﬁbrillation and ﬂutter, 89 myocardial infarction causing cardioembolic stroke, and acute arterial CASE 11-1 A 28-year-old man with a history of schizophrenia was found unresponsive and tachypneic but not hypoxic. He received naloxone in the ﬁeld without improvement and was intubated for airway protection. On examination in the emergency department, he was comatose, with preserved brainstem reﬂexes, triple ﬂexion in his lower extremities, and absent muscle stretch reﬂexes throughout. Urine toxicology screening was positive for fentanyl and amphetamines, later conﬁrmed by serum toxicology to be methamphetamine. CT of his head showed large hypodensities in the bilateral cerebellum and globus pallidi. CT angiography of his head and neck was normal. MRI of his brain 24 hours after admission showed symmetric diffusion restriction in the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres, globus pallidi, and subcortical and perirolandic white matter, sparing the U-ﬁbers (FIGURE 11-2). The patient was started empirically on coenzyme Q10 300 mg 4 times a day, vitamin E 2000 mg a day, vitamin C 2000 mg a day, thiamine 500 mg 3 times a day, and a multivitamin. Two weeks after hospital admission, he remained unresponsive, and his family elected to transition to withdraw life- sustaining therapy. COMMENT Toxic leukoencephalopathy from opioids can be difﬁcult to distinguish from hypoxic-ischemic brain injury, particularly because hypoxia often complicates opioid overdose, but here, the lack of involvement of the cerebral cortex and hippocampi was atypical for hypoxic injury. Globus pallidi and cerebellar cortex involvement was consistent with cerebellar, hippocampal, and basal nuclei transient edema with restricted diffusion (CHANTER) syndrome, but the cerebral white matter involvement was more akin to heroin-associated spongiform leukoencephalopathy. This illustrates that toxic injury from opioid overdose may produce a spectrum of clinical-radiologic presentations. Antioxidant therapy was trialed in this case, borrowing from practices with heroin-associated spongiform leukoencephalopathy. 290 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

dissection. 90 Despite evidence of vasoconstriction and vasospasm from stimulant use, there are few cases of reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome in the literature attributed to methamphetamine or cocaine alone. 91 Limited data exist on thrombolysis and thrombectomy for stimulant-related acute ischemic stroke 81 ; in the absence of such data, patients should be offered the same treatment for acute ischemic stroke as in other contexts. Noncerebrovascular Neurologic Complications of Methamphetamine and Cocaine Use Acute methamphetamine and cocaine use can also cause psychosis with hallucinations (auditory more commonly than visual or tactile) and delusions (eg, persecution, reference, mind-reading, and parasitosis). 78,92,93 Psychosis resolves within 1 month of drug cessation for about half of patients, but prolonged psychosis is also possible. 92,94 Patients may also develop punding (ie, FIGURE 11-2 Diffusion-restricting lesions that are hyperintense on axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI; A, C, and E) and hypointense on apparent diffusion coefﬁcient MRI (B, D, and F) from the patient in CASE 11-1 who experienced a combined fentanyl methamphetamine overdose. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 291 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

compulsive performance of repetitive, non–goal-directed tasks, such as assembling or disassembling a mechanical object, stringing beads, or grooming), likely caused by dopaminergic overactivity, as can be seen in patients with Parkinson disease on dopamine replacement therapy. 95,96 Acute stimulant use can trigger generalized-onset seizures; focal seizures warrant neuroimaging to evaluate for a seizure focus. Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) is associated with stimulants, likely related to acute hypertension. 97 Methamphetamine and cocaine intoxication can cause various movement disorders, including choreoathetoid movements, 77,98 bruxism, tremor, dystonia, and orolingual dyskinesias. 99 Levamisole is a white- powder antihelminthic agent often added to cocaine as a cutting agent that can trigger cerebral demyelination (levamisole-induced multifocal leukoencephalopathy) within days to weeks after exposure; CSF in these cases is normal or may show a mild pleocytosis or unique oligoclonal bands, whereas MRI shows bilateral white matter ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) hyperintensities, at times with patchy or partial ring enhancement or diffusion restriction (FIGURE 11-3). Treatment usually begins with IV methylprednisolone, and IV immunoglobulin (IVIg), plasma exchange, or cyclophosphamide may be added in refractory cases. Khat An estimated 10 million to 40 million people worldwide use the stimulant khat, the fresh or dried shoots and leaves of the plant Catha edulis Forsk, which is chewed or brewed as tea. The plant is endemic to the regions around the Red Sea and Eastern Africa, such as in Somalia, Ethiopia, and Yemen, and may be used by people who have immigrated from these regions. 100 The main neuroactive ingredient is cathinone, which is structurally comparable with amphetamines. The drug causes mild euphoria, increased energy, and excitement but may also cause depression, paranoia, headaches, insomnia, tremor, or loss of short-term memory. Limited research has suggested that long-term khat use is associated with short-term memory loss. 101 Additionally, small studies suggest an association between khat use and stroke. 102-104 Kratom Kratom is an herbal substance derived from the leaves of the Mitragyna speciosa tree, grown in Southeast Asia, that produces stimulant effects at low doses and analgesic and sedative effects at higher doses. 105 People may chew the tree’s leaves, ingest dry kratom, or add the extract to a liquid to achieve pain relief, increased energy, and improved mood; some patients with substance use disorders self-medicate with kratom in an attempt to cut back on substance use. The compound is not currently scheduled by the US federal government and is sold widely in stores, although some states and municipalities have banned it; additionally, there are no safety standards for kratom production or content. Acute intoxication has been associated with agitation, sedation, hallucinations, tachycardia, hypertension, and vomiting and can progress to respiratory depression and coma. 105 Multiple case reports have described kratom-associated seizures, but causation is uncertain because many cases involve polysubstance use. 106,107 Cessation of kratom use after chronic use may produce withdrawal symptoms akin to opioid withdrawal. 105 292 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Prescription Stimulants Dextroamphetamine-amphetamine salts (known as “addys” or “bennies”) and methylphenidate are treatments for attention deﬁcit hyperactivity disorder that are frequently diverted for nonmedical use. 108 Federal data showed that 0.9% of children 12 to 17 years old misused prescription stimulants in 2022, with higher rates among college-age individuals. 109,110 Rates of misuse vary across schools, with higher rates at schools where students receive high numbers of stimulant prescriptions. 110 Prescription stimulants have been associated with worsening of preexisting tic disorders, with weaker evidence suggesting they may cause new-onset tics. 111 Psychosis has also been reported in children exposed to these medications, especially at high doses or in patients with underlying psychiatric disorders, although causation has not been proven. 112 HALLUCINOGENS Hallucinogens are categorized by their pharmacologic mechanisms and include psychedelics, hallucinogens with stimulant properties, and dissociative drugs, which distort perception and produce feelings of detachment. Psychedelics Psychedelics include lysergic acid diethylamide (LSD) (“acid”), psilocybin (“mushrooms”), ayahuasca, and mescaline (peyote). These drugs produce changes in mood, thought, and perception. They do not produce classic withdrawal syndromes, but some people using these drugs report psychological dependence or compulsive use. 113 Many of these drugs are being investigated as treatments for depression, addiction, end-of-life anxiety, cluster headache, and migraine. 113-115 Although several US states have decriminalized their use, they remain controlled substances under federal law. 116 Emergency department visits related to psychedelics are increasing but uncommon, and most visits are the result of psychiatric distress rather than toxicity. 117 Psychedelic intoxication should be considered in patients presenting with a constellation of hallucinations, synesthesia, mood lability, paresthesia, tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, nausea or vomiting (especially with ayahuasca and mescaline), tremor, and fever. LSD and psilocybin can contribute to serotonin syndrome (eg, fever, clonus, agitation, diaphoresis, hypertonia) in combination with other serotonergic medications. 118 Rarely, psychedelics can produce hallucinogenic persisting perception disorder, a syndrome of prolonged or recurring perceptual symptoms (most commonly visual symptoms), such as altered motion perception, halos, palinopsia, macropsia, and micropsia, 119 lasting weeks or months. 120 3,4-Methylenedioxymethamphetamine 3,4-Methylenedioxymethamphetamine (MDMA) is a stimulant with hallucinogenic properties, primarily available as pills (“ecstasy”) or powder (“molly”). It is usually taken orally but can be snorted. MDMA may cause relaxation, euphoria, and enhanced empathy, openness, and communication, with effects lasting several hours. Signs of MDMA intoxication include tachycardia, hypertension, bruxism, trismus, tremor, myoclonus, and fever (TABLE 11-3). At raves, crowded environments and dancing may exacerbate fever. Severe intoxication can lead to serotonin syndrome and systemic complications, including rhabdomyolysis, hepatotoxicity, disseminated intravascular coagulation, cardiac arrhythmia, CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 293 KEY POINTS •Acute methamphetamine and cocaine use can also cause psychosis with hallucinations. Patients may also develop punding. •Prescription stimulants have been associated with worsening of preexisting tic disorders. •Emergency department visits related to psychedelics are increasing but uncommon, and most visits are the result of psychiatric distress rather than toxicity. •Rarely, psychedelics can produce hallucinogenic-persisting perception disorder, a syndrome of prolonged or recurring perceptual symptoms, especially visual symptoms, lasting weeks or months. •Frequent misuse of ketamine has been associated with impairment in executive function and memory, although the severity and reversibility vary across studies. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

cardiac arrest, and hyponatremia (caused by polydipsia and increased antidiuretic hormone secretion), which can lead to seizures, cerebral edema, and, in some patients, death. 121,122 The mainstay of treating MDMA intoxication is supportive care with a focus on emergent cooling in febrile patients. Cyproheptadine can be added for serotonin syndrome, and serotonergic medications such as fentanyl should be avoided. 121 Some people with a history of long-term MDMA use report emotional blunting and mood dysregulation; however, others report long-term beneﬁts such as enhanced emotional and social functioning. 123,124 The drug is being investigated as a treatment for psychiatric conditions, including depression and posttraumatic stress disorder. Ketamine Ketamine is a US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)–approved dissociative anesthetic that binds to and acts as an antagonist to NMDA receptors. The drug has long been tested and prescribed off-label for mood disorders and administered in outpatient infusion clinics, but COVID-19 pandemic–related laws that expanded access to telemedicine fueled a proliferation of telehealth clinics that prescribe frequent, even daily doses of ketamine in tablets, lozenges, and nasal sprays. Several reports suggest this has led to markedly increased rates of misuse. 125,126 Ketamine (“K,” “Special K,” “vitamin K”) can produce hallucinations, positive mood, and a sense of detachment lasting 30 to 60 minutes with intranasal or IV administration or longer with ingested forms. 52,126 Intoxication may also produce agitation, confusion, diplopia, nystagmus, tachycardia, and hypertension, and respiratory depression can occur with high doses 127 ; however, in one series, symptoms usually resolved by the time a clinician evaluated the patient. 128 Chronic ketamine use can cause ketamine bladder syndrome, with urinary frequency, urgency, and pain that may initially be misattributed to a neurologic cause; permanent bladder dysfunction is possible. Frequent misuse of ketamine has been associated with impairment in executive function and memory, although the severity and reversibility vary across studies. 129,130 FIGURE 11-3 Demyelinating lesions in cocaine (levamisole)-induced multifocal leukoencephalopathy, as seen on axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI; A), T1-weighted precontrast MRI (B), T1-weighted postcontrast MRI (C), and ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI (D). 294 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Phencyclidine or Phenylcyclohexyl Piperidine Phencyclidine or phenylcyclohexyl piperidine (PCP) (“angel dust”) is a dissociative anesthetic and NMDA receptor antagonist previously used in veterinary medicine. PCP is a crystalline powder, liquid, or tablet that may be ingested orally, injected, snorted, or smoked to achieve a dreamlike, detached, numb, and hallucinatory state. Its use has been declining since the 1980s. Acute PCP intoxication is characterized by anxiety, paranoia, delusions, hallucinations, and agitation. Intoxication can also cause hypertension, tachycardia, fever, agitation, cerebellar signs (ataxia, dysarthria, and horizontal or vertical nystagmus), muscle rigidity (which can lead to rhabdomyolysis), movement disorders (myoclonus, dystonia, choreoathetoid movements), convulsions, and coma. Chronic use of PCP can lead to insomnia, severe social and behavioral changes, and persistent psychotic symptoms. 131 Dextromethorphan Approximately 1.6 million US youth and young adults misuse the over-the- counter cough suppressant dextromethorphan annually. 132 Dextromethorphan binds sigma opioid receptors in the medulla (blocking cough) and is an agonist at 5-hydroxytryptamine 2 (5-HT 2 ) receptors. At higher-than-recommended doses, it is an NMDA receptor antagonist, producing a dissociative state, hallucinations, psychosis, agitation, ataxic gait, nystagmus, dystonia, tachycardia, hypertension, mydriasis, and diaphoresis. 133 Agitation can be severe enough to produce rhabdomyolysis. High doses can produce coma. Combining dextromethorphan with other serotonergic agents can lead to serotonin syndrome. 134 Coingestion with other cough medicine ingredients can produce additional toxidromes (eg, anticholinergic poisoning with antihistamines; hypertensive emergency with pseudoephedrine). Chronic use of dextromethorphan can produce psychosis and cognitive decline. Rarely, heavy use can cause bromism, a condition of fatigue, headache, dysarthria, memory impairment, altered mental status, and even coma caused by toxicity from bromide salts present in some dextromethorphan formulations; this condition may be accompanied by a hyperchloremic nonanion gap or negative anion gap acidosis. 135 SEDATIVES Sedatives include benzodiazepines and other substances that act as central nervous system depressants. They may be intentionally used or administered without consent by perpetrators of assault (eg, ﬂunitrazepam, gamma hydroxybutyrate). In addition to the benzodiazepines listed here, designer benzodiazepines (eg, bromazolam 136 ) and benzodiazepine analogues (eg, thienodiazepine derivatives like etizolam 137 ) cause similar effects and may be sold outright or manufactured to mimic name-brand prescription benzodiazepines. Benzodiazepines Benzodiazepines, particularly alprazolam and clonazepam, may be diverted from medical sources or illicitly manufactured and then ingested, snorted, or, less commonly, injected. 1 Benzodiazepines bind GABA A receptors with broad central nervous system inhibitory effects. Overdose produces confusion and CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 295 KEY POINTS •Benzodiazepines bind γ-aminobutyric acid A (GABA A ) receptors with broad central nervous system inhibitory effects. •Benzodiazepine withdrawal is similar to alcohol withdrawal, although psychosis is less common. •At high doses of γ-hydroxybutyrate, patients enter a coma that suddenly reverses after several hours; as the drug wears off, patients may develop agitation, confusion, myoclonus, and seizures. •An ever-increasing array of novel synthetic drugs, including synthetic cathinones, synthetic cannabinoids, and phenylethylamine derivatives, is widely available online and may be sold in gas stations and vape shops. Often marketed to young adults, many of these substances can cause psychosis and seizures. •Bath salts have cognitive and behavioral effects similar to other stimulants, causing euphoria and heightened alertness that can be accompanied by confusion, psychosis, agitation, aggression, bruxism, sweating, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, mydriasis, rhabdomyolysis, seizures, and, rarely, death. •Misuse of nitrous oxide among young people is rising, in part because large canisters of it are sold online and in head shops. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

sedation, which can lead to respiratory depression, coma, or death at high doses. Withdrawal causes tachycardia, tremor, agitation, and seizures. Benzodiazepine withdrawal is similar to alcohol withdrawal, although psychosis is less common in patients with benzodiazepine withdrawal. Treatment of these symptoms generally involves IV benzodiazepines followed by a slow taper. Flumazenil is a benzodiazepine reversal agent used to treat benzodiazepine overdose, but it can precipitate withdrawal seizures in patients who chronically use benzodiazepines. Therefore, ﬂumazenil should only be considered for patients who clearly lack a history of chronic benzodiazepine use (eg, accidental pediatric ingestion) and should not be used in patients with epilepsy. Flunitrazepam Flunitrazepam (“rooﬁes”) is a benzodiazepine derivative; outside the United States, it has been used for preoperative sedation and to treat insomnia, but it has never been approved by the FDA. The tablet can be swallowed whole, dissolved in liquid, crushed, or snorted, with resultant hypnotic, anxiolytic, and amnestic effects. Overdose can produce sedation, respiratory depression, and coma. Flumazenil can reverse the effects of the drug but can precipitate benzodiazepine withdrawal in individuals regularly using the drug. 138,139 γ-Hydroxybutyrate γ-Hydroxybutyrate (GHB) is a precursor to GABA. Its sodium salt form (sodium oxybate) is FDA-approved for the treatment of narcolepsy. When illicitly produced, GHB is a colorless liquid or white powder that has no distinctive color or odor and produces amnesia. At low doses, GHB produces euphoria and disinhibition. At high doses, patients enter a coma that suddenly reverses after several hours; as the drug wears off, patients may develop agitation, confusion, myoclonus, and seizures. 140 Alcohol and GHB act synergistically, increasing the risk of severe respiratory depression and overdose. The mainstay of treatment is cardiorespiratory support. NOVEL PSYCHOACTIVE SUBSTANCES An ever-increasing array of novel synthetic drugs, including synthetic cathinones, synthetic cannabinoids, and phenylethylamine derivatives, is widely available online and may be sold in gas stations and vape shops. Often marketed as “nootropics” (cognitive enhancers), many of these substances can cause psychosis and seizures. Standard urine and serum toxicology studies do not detect these drugs, which can be part of their appeal. In addition to the substances listed here, online vendors and smoke and vape shops may sell non– FDA-approved pharmaceuticals (eg, tianeptine 141 or “gas station heroin,” an antidepressant and mu opioid receptor agonist that can cause altered mental status and seizures) or neuroactive substance mixes labeled as hemp-derived or “mushroom extracts” with unpredictable and undisclosed ingredients. For example, in 2024, one brand of chocolate bars, cones, and gummies, sold in stores across the United States, was linked to at least 180 cases of serious illness (seizures, agitation, depressed level of consciousness, hypotension) and three deaths. FDA testing found that the products contained synthetic psilocybin analogues, pregabalin, kava derivatives, and an Amanita muscaria mushroom derivative called muscimol. 142 296 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Synthetic Cathinones Use of synthetic cathinones (“bath salts”) rose in the United States in the early 2010s. 143 These drugs are chemically related to cathinone, the substance found naturally in khat. Bath salts are often sold online or in convenience stores, gas stations, or adult bookstores and may be labeled misleadingly (ie, “plant food,” “glass cleaner”) to distract from their intended use. They may also be disguised as MDMA or used as adulterants in other illicit drugs. 143 Bath salts have cognitive and behavioral effects similar to other stimulants, causing euphoria and heightened alertness that can be accompanied by confusion, psychosis, agitation, aggression, bruxism, sweating, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, mydriasis, rhabdomyolysis, seizures, and, rarely, death. 144 Toxic leukoencephalopathy has also been reported. 145 Phenethylamines N-(2-methoxybenzyl)phenethylamines (“NBOMes”) are synthetic derivatives of the 2C phenethylamine hallucinogens that act as agonists at serotonin 5-HT 2A receptors and produce potent psychedelic effects. They are distributed as liquids, powders, or on paper blotters and may be snorted, injected, smoked, or placed sublingually or buccally. Intoxication can be complicated by serotonin syndrome. 146 Other potential complications include aggression, paranoia, panic attacks, and seizures. 147 There have also been some case reports of toxic leukoencephalopathy. 146,148 Other novel synthetic phenethylamines have similar effects; not all of these have yet been banned by the DEA. 149 INHALANTS Inhalants comprise a wide range of compounds that are misused via inhalation. Nitrous Oxide Nitrous oxide gas is an NMDA antagonist that produces a rapid euphoric effect and disinhibition when inhaled from a balloon or cannister (eg, whipped cream cannister). Although some US states restrict sales and possession of nitrous oxide, federal law does not prohibit possession provided the intended use is not for intoxication. Misuse among young people is rising, in part due to changes in marketing; large canisters of nitrous oxide are now sold online and in head shops with ﬂavors such as blue raspberry and fruit punch. 150 Videos of young people using nitrous oxide receive millions of views on social media. 151 Acute nitrous oxide intoxication can cause dizziness, euphoria, ataxia, and dissociation; these symptoms usually resolve rapidly, but toxicity can rarely lead to death. 152 In contrast, neurologic sequelae from chronic use can be severe. Nitrous oxide gas converts vitamin B 12 to an inactive form that inhibits methionine synthase, leading to impaired myelin maintenance and DNA synthesis. Chronic use (or, rarely, acute use in patients with preexisting vitamin B 12 deﬁciency) can produce myelopathy, peripheral neuropathy, or myeloneuropathy akin to that caused by vitamin B 12 deﬁciency, with prominent vibratory and position sense loss, sensory ataxia, and sometimes with urinary retention or cognitive impairment (CASE 11-2). Serum vitamin B 12 level may be normal, although methylmalonic acid is often elevated. MRI of the spine may show T2 hyperintensity in the posterior columns, particularly in the cervical cord. The condition can be fully or partially reversed if treated CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 297 KEY POINTS •Chronic use of nitrous oxide (or, rarely, acute use in patients with preexisting B 12 deﬁciency) can produce myelopathy, peripheral neuropathy, or myeloneuropathy akin to that from B 12 deﬁciency, with prominent vibratory and position sense loss, sensory ataxia, and sometimes with urinary retention or cognitive impairment. •Most US states now allow cannabis production and sale for medical and nonmedical use. These policy changes have accelerated cannabis use, particularly among young adults, and have diversiﬁed the range of delta-9- tetrahydrocannabinol products such as edibles and e-cigarettes. •Cigarette smoking has well-established cardiovascular complications, including increased risk of stroke. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

promptly with intramuscular vitamin B 12 supplementation and nitrous oxide abstinence. Chronic use has also been associated with cognitive impairment, mood disturbances, and psychosis; these symptoms may occur with low vitamin B 12 levels or with normal levels. 153 Hufﬁng Hufﬁng is the inhalation of volatile compounds such as organic solvents (eg, glues, nail polish remover, paint thinner, petrol) and aerosols (spray paints, vegetable oil sprays, deodorant) to achieve a high. These substances are readily available around the home, posing a risk to children. Intoxication from hufﬁng causes euphoria, disinhibition, dizziness, slurred speech, blurred vision, and confusion that can progress to coma, respiratory depression, and seizures at high doses. 154 Chronic hufﬁng of n-hexane (eg, petrol, many glues) is associated with painful axonal peripheral neuropathy. Chronic toluene use can cause cerebellar and cortical dysfunction and can rarely lead to toxic CASE 11-2 A 24-year-old woman with tobacco use disorder presented to the emergency department with 3 weeks of progressive lower and upper limb paresthesia, numbness, and weakness. The paresthesia and numbness began in her toes, then ascended to her thighs, ﬁngertips, and eventually forearms. One week before presentation, she developed bilateral foot drop that progressed to hand weakness, and at presentation, she was only able to hold a fork with her ﬁsts. She reported no bladder or bowel symptoms, saddle anesthesia, alcohol use, recent illness, vaccination, travel, rash, or weight loss. She reported inhaling 1 box (25 hits) of whippet nitrous oxide cartridges 4 days a week for the past year. Neurologic examination was notable for distal more than proximal weakness in her upper and lower extremities, length-dependent vibration loss involving her legs and hands with a positive shield sign (sensory loss in a shield distribution on the chest), absent joint position sense in her great toes and ankles, brisk reﬂexes at her patellae, and absent ankle jerks. Diagnostic evaluation was notable for low serum vitamin B 12 of 127 pg/mL and elevated methylmalonic acid of 5308 µmol/L, with normal hemoglobin A 1C , creatine kinase, folate, and homocysteine; normal CSF proﬁle; and abnormal MRI of her cervical spine (FIGURE 11-4). The patient was treated with intramuscular vitamin B 12 injections of 1000 mcg for 6 weeks, with signiﬁcant improvement in strength and sensation, and was then started on an oral vitamin B 12 supplement. She returned to the emergency department 6 months after her initial presentation with recurrent myeloneuropathy due to ongoing nitrous oxide use. COMMENT This case demonstrates a classic presentation of nitrous oxide–associated myeloneuropathy. The clinical history of relapse illustrates that nitrous oxide abstinence is necessary to treat this condition, which does not respond to vitamin B 12 supplementation alone. 298 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

leukoencephalopathy with cognitive impairment and ataxia, with MRI showing diffuse white matter T2 hyperintensity. 154-156 CANNABIS Most US states now allow cannabis production and sale for medical and nonmedical use. 157 These policy changes have accelerated cannabis use, particularly among young adults, and have diversiﬁed the range of delta-9- tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) products such as edibles and e-cigarettes. Cannabis sativa contains dozens of pharmacologically active compounds called cannabinoids, the most abundant of which are THC and cannabidiol (CBD). Cannabinoids stimulate endogenous cannabinoid receptors (CB1 and CB2). THC intoxication produces relaxation and mild euphoria, usually accompanied by some impairment in cognition, concentration, and perceptual and psychomotor function; higher doses can cause anxiety, paranoia, and hallucinations. CBD is less psychoactive and does not appear to be addictive. 158 Figure 11-4 Imaging ﬁndings from the patient in CASE 11-2. Sagittal (A) and axial (B) T2-weighted MRI of the cervical spine shows typical MRI ﬁndings of nitrous oxide myelopathy with symmetric hyperintensities in the dorsal columns of the cervical spinal cord. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 299 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

THC-infused edibles resembling name-brand candy, snacks, or cereal caused a reported 1375% rise in accidental cannabis exposures in children younger than 5 years between 2017 and 2021, with about 600 children requiring critical care admission. The great majority of exposures occurred in the child’s home. 159 In adults, cannabis-associated emergency department presentations are less common and typically for transient, cannabis-induced psychosis. 157 Research in 2023 showed a dose-response relationship between cannabis and stroke, even in patients who had never used tobacco or vaping products. 160 Cannabis is also associated with reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome. 97 Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome is a condition characterized by recurrent episodes of severe nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain in people using cannabis chronically, often relieved temporarily by hot baths or showers. Symptoms typically resolve with cessation of cannabis use, which is the only deﬁnitive treatment. Chronic use of cannabis can cause decreased motivation and impaired learning and memory, and early cannabis exposure in adolescence is also associated with a risk of schizophrenia in adulthood, although causation is not proven. 161,162 Cannabis is used medicinally to alleviate nausea, chronic pain, and other conditions. 157 A CBD formulation is FDA-approved for the treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, Dravet syndrome, and tuberous sclerosis complex, and CBD is being investigated as a treatment for a range of neurologic conditions. Synthetic Cannabinoids Synthetic cannabinoids were ﬁrst developed by scientists to study the cannabinoid system and explore therapeutic uses. 163 They are chemically similar to psychoactive components of cannabis but have greater toxicity because they bind more strongly to cannabinoid receptors. 163 Illicit synthetic cannabinoids (eg, “Spice”) are generally combined with dried herbal mixes or packaged as vape liquids and sold online or in head shops, with bright colored packaging resembling snack food, or intentionally mislabeled as “incense,” “potpourri,” or other herbal substances (eg, “valerian root” 164 ). Hundreds of these mixtures exist, and the market is constantly evolving. 163 Smoking or ingesting these products may produce a state of relaxation, happiness, and sedation but may also cause paranoia, psychosis, agitation, sedation, ataxia, vertigo, muscle weakness, abnormal movements, tachycardia, myocardial infarction, and, rarely, death. 165 Seizures are among the most frequently reported neurologic complications. 163 Ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, and subarachnoid hemorrhage have also been reported in young people within hours after synthetic cannabinoid use, although causation remains unclear. 166-168 TOBACCO Cigarette smoking is becoming less common worldwide, but tobacco remains one of the most commonly used addictive substances. Tobacco contains the stimulant nicotine, as well as many other chemicals, some of which have depressant or vasodilatory effects. Cigarette smoking has well-established cardiovascular complications, including increased risk of stroke; mechanisms 300 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF DRUG AND ALCOHOL USE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

include a proinﬂammatory and hypercoagulable state, platelet activation, and increasing ﬁbrinogen levels. 169 US youth now use e-cigarettes (vape) more than any other tobacco product. An estimated 5.9% of US middle school and high school students currently vape. Most e-cigarettes contain nicotine, but vaping products also contain ﬂavorings and other chemicals, and some include CBD and THC. 170 More data are needed on the long-term cognitive and cardiovascular risks of vaping. NEUROLOGIC RISKS OF INJECTION DRUG USE Many of the drugs previously discussed pose additional neurologic risks when injected. Injection drug use can cause infective endocarditis, commonly caused by Staphylococcus and Streptococcus species, 140 which may cause embolic ischemic strokes (with risk of hemorrhagic transformation), intracerebral abscesses, and mycotic aneurysms that may rupture and cause subarachnoid hemorrhage. For further discussion of infective endocarditis, refer to the article “Neurologic Complications of Cardiac Disease and Cardiac Procedures” by Alvin S. Das, MD, and Steven K. Feske, MD, 171 in this issue of Continuum. Injection drug use can lead to spinal epidural abscess and spinal cord compression through bacteremia or direct extension from local infections. Needle sharing can transmit human immunodeﬁciency virus, hepatitis C, and human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1), with their associated neurologic complications. Skin popping opioids, methamphetamine, or cocaine creates anaerobic conditions, which can rarely lead to botulism spore germination and wound botulism; most cases have been reported in individuals who used black tar heroin. 172,173 Patients who develop botulism should be thoroughly examined for deep tissue wounds and superﬁcial dermatologic ﬁndings that may suggest skin popping. SUBSTANCE USE TREATMENT For some patients, a major neurologic complication of substance use may catalyze substance use disorder recovery. Hospitalization and rehabilitation can create a period of temporary abstinence, presenting a chance to connect patients with treatment and long-term support. Neurologists should take these opportunities to screen for substance use disorders, engage in motivational interviewing, and link patients to appropriate resources. Motivational interviewing aims to elicit and reinforce “change talk” (the patient’s reasons for quitting) while exploring but not reinforcing “sustain talk” (the patient’s arguments for continued use). 174 Neurologists should know about local community-based or hospital-based resources, provide information about these resources to patients, and place referrals. These efforts are more likely to result in patient engagement than simply advising abstinence or deferring treatment initiation to other providers. Finally, neurologists should be champions of destigmatized, nondiscriminatory care for patients with substance use disorders. Messaging to patients, family members, and the public that addiction is a complex CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 301 KEY POINTS •US youth now use e-cigarettes (vape) more than any other tobacco product. •Injection drug use can cause infective endocarditis, spinal epidural abscess, and spinal cord compression. •Needle sharing can transmit human immunodeﬁciency virus (HIV), hepatitis C, and human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1), with their associated neurologic complications. •“Skin popping” can rarely lead to botulism spore germination and wound botulism. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

biopsychosocial condition, rather than a moral failing, can help reduce stigma and improve care. CONCLUSION A wide range of neurologic complications of substance use has been described, including recently recognized neurologic syndromes and neurologic complications of novel psychoactive substances. Identifying these substance use–associated neurologic conditions can guide toxicology screening, avoid unnecessary evaluation and intervention, and provide an opportunity for neurologists to identify substance use disorders to guide patients to treatment resources. Neurologic Complications of Cancer and Its Treatment By Amy A. Pruitt, MD, FAAN ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article discusses the diagnosis and treatment of cancer- related neurologic complications including (1) diagnosis and management of brain, spinal, and leptomeningeal metastases; (2) toxicities of conventional cytotoxic, biologic, and targeted therapies with a focus on immunotherapies (immune checkpoint inhibitors and chimeric antigen receptor [CAR] T-cell therapy); (3) paraneoplastic syndromes; and (4) acute and long-term sequelae, including seizures, stroke, neuropathy, and long-term effects of young adult and childhood cancer treatment. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Brain metastases can differ genetically from the primary neoplasm, with some responding to systemic rather than local therapies. Increasing use of immune checkpoint inhibitors has increased the incidence of immune-mediated neurologic complications, including paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes. CAR T-cell therapy, initially developed for leukemia and lymphoma, is being applied to an increasing range of cancers and can cause complications such as cytokine release syndrome and immune effector cell–associated neurologic syndrome, for which speciﬁc therapeutic protocols are available. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Prompt recognition of direct and indirect neurologic complications of systemic cancers and their evolving treatments is essential to maximize favorable outcomes. Neurologists should be familiar with common and rare neurologic toxicities of conventional chemotherapy, immune checkpoint inhibitors, and CAR T-cell therapy. INTRODUCTION T he therapeutic landscape for systemic cancers has rapidly expanded in recent years with the emergence of immune checkpoint inhibitors and chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy and increasing indications for these treatments. 1 Although these treatments have led to signiﬁcantly improved outcomes and prognosis in patients with systemic cancer, they have also been observed to cause a wide variety of neurologic complications, and many beneﬁts of recent diagnostic and therapeutic advances are not available to patients outside academic medical centers. 2 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 211 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):211 – 242. Address correspondence to Dr Amy A. Pruitt, 3400 Spruce St, Philadelphia, PA 19104, pruitt@pennmedicine. upenn.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Pruitt reports no disclosure. UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Dr Pruitt discusses the unlabeled use of lorlatinib and osimertinib for the treatment of brain and leptomeningeal metastases. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Immune checkpoint inhibitors can cause a range of central and peripheral nervous system complications, including encephalitis, paraneoplastic syndromes, Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome, and myasthenia gravis. CAR T-cell therapy can result in severe immune effector cell–associated neurologic syndrome and recently recognized parkinsonian syndromes. This article reviews the diagnosis and treatment of nervous system metastases (brain, leptomeningeal, and spinal), paraneoplastic syndromes, and neurologic toxicities of radiation and chemotherapy (conventional, immune checkpoint inhibitor, and CAR T-cell therapy). FIGURE 9-1 Neuroimaging ﬁndings in patients with systemic cancer. A, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI of a 38-year-old woman with a new diagnosis of ERBB2- positive breast cancer and a recent increase in frequency and severity of longstanding migraines shows a large homogeneously enhancing mass with mass effect in the posterior fossa. The tumor is a meningioma: a known bidirectional increased risk exists between these two conditions. B, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI in a 61- year-old woman with fallopian tube cancer that was removed 12 years before presenting with headache and imbalance. Pathology showed a necrotic tumor consistent with her original primary tumor, a very unusual ﬁrst site of relapse after a long interval. C, Axial gradient recalled echo (GRE) MRI image in a 42-year-old man with widely metastatic systemic melanoma with intractable seizures and multiple, hemorrhagic cortically based metastases. D, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI in a 59-year-old man with innumerable micrometastases throughout the supratentorial and infratentorial regions from small cell lung cancer with no targetable mutations. E, F, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI shows two enhancing masses in a 69-year-old man with adenocarcinoma of the lung without targetable mutations. G, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI in a 72-year-old man with isolated right frontal metastasis from adenocarcinoma of the lung with EGFR variation. H, Coronal T1-weighted gadolinium- enhanced MRI in a 49-year-old man with extensive dural metastases from a testicular primary. I, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI in a 61-year-old man with innumerable ring-enhancing lesions and a new diagnosis of mediastinal mass, which was found to be small cell lung cancer. J, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI in a 66-year-old man with multiple myeloma at ﬁrst appeared to demonstrate two isolated metastases, but coronal views (not shown) and posterior fossa examination showed diffuse leptomeningeal involvement by tumor. 212 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

NERVOUS SYSTEM METASTASES Up to 10% of patients with systemic cancer develop brain metastases, totaling more than 200,000 new patients annually in the United States. 3 Less common sites of nervous system metastases include leptomeningeal metastases (carcinomatous meningitis) and epidural spinal metastases. The presence of central nervous system metastases inﬂuences overall treatment and prognosis, with neurologists often being consulted to correlate clinical symptoms with metastases to determine the appropriate management of the patient’s nervous system disease. Brain Metastases Although most cancers metastasize to the brain late in the course of the illness at the time of widespread systemic metastatic disease, brain metastases occur at the time of initial cancer diagnosis in up to 25% of patients with lung cancer (both small cell and non–small cell) and melanoma, 10% of patients with renal cell carcinoma, and 7% of patients with breast cancer. 3 Some cancers, such as prostate cancer and hepatocellular carcinoma, rarely metastasize to the brain. DIAGNOSIS. Brain metastases can have a variety of radiologic appearances (FIGURE 9-1). The most common radiologic appearance is one or more ring- enhancing lesions, often at the gray-white junction due to hematogenous dissemination, the most common mode of spread to the central nervous system. Other appearances include more solidly enhancing lesions, miliary micrometastases, and hemorrhagic metastases. Melanoma, renal cell carcinoma, thyroid cancer, and choriocarcinoma metastases tend to be hemorrhagic, but lung cancer metastases may also be hemorrhagic. Dural or leptomeningeal metastases may be present in isolation or with concurrent brain metastases. Radiologic mimics of solitary or multifocal brain metastases include infection, subacute stroke, demyelination, and autoimmune conditions, all of which occur with higher frequency in patients with systemic cancer compared with other patient populations. 4 Surgery may be necessary to conﬁrm the diagnosis, reduce mass effect, or both. TREATMENT. Treatment of brain metastases depends on tumor type, location, number of metastases, whether the metastases are symptomatic or asymptomatic, and the patients’ overall burden of systemic cancer. Treatment options can be divided into local therapies (surgery, stereotactic radiosurgery), whole-brain radiation, and systemic modalities (immune checkpoint inhibitors, targeted oral or IV agents). 5-8 Increasingly, patients with multifocal small and asymptomatic brain metastases are treated with systemic therapies targeted to particular cancer mutations, including osimertinib for EGFR-positive non–small cell lung cancer, tucatinib and trastuzumab deruxtecan for ERBB2-positive breast cancer, sacituzumab govitecan and datopotamab deruxtecan for triple-negative breast cancer, and immune checkpoint inhibitors such as nivolumab and ipilimumab for BRAF-positive and wildtype melanoma. 3 Surgery is indicated when the diagnosis is uncertain or if a solitary metastasis is symptomatic or causing mass effect, and the patient is a good surgical candidate without uncontrolled systemic disease. Patients who are frail, have severe cardiopulmonary problems, or have signiﬁcantly impaired cognition are generally not considered candidates for surgery. KEY POINTS •Up to 25% of patients with lung cancer and melanoma may present with brain metastases at the time of initial cancer diagnosis, as do 10% of patients with renal cell carcinoma and 7% of those with breast cancer. •There is marked variability in the prognosis and radiographic appearance of brain metastases. Surgery may be necessary to conﬁrm the diagnosis, reduce mass effect, or both. •For brain metastases from non–small cell lung carcinoma, breast cancer, and melanoma, the treatment paradigm has shifted to targeted therapies focused on speciﬁc molecular drivers. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 213 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Stereotactic radiosurgery is indicated for patients with one to four asymptomatic brain metastases and no systemic therapy options, excluding small cell lung carcinoma. Patients with small cell lung carcinoma were excluded from landmark stereotactic radiosurgery trials because of concerns for a high likelihood of short-interval, multifocal central nervous system progression with this histology and the frequent use of prophylactic cranial radiation. Stereotactic radiosurgery to the surgical resection cavity should also be offered to patients with one to two resected brain metastases. Whole-brain radiation is indicated for patients with no systemic options, progressive systemic disease, and poor performance status, or as salvage CASE 9-1 A 62-year-old woman presented after a focal seizure involving her right face and arm. MRI revealed an enhancing left frontal lesion with surrounding edema (FIGURE 9-2A and FIGURE 9-2B). Further evaluation discovered EGFR-positive adenocarcinoma of the lung. The patient was placed on levetiracetam for seizure control, the brain lesion was treated with stereotactic radiosurgery, and the lung cancer was treated with osimertinib and the immune checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab. Dexamethasone was instituted at a dose of 2 mg 2 times a day with plans for a rapid taper. Over the next 2 weeks, the patient developed increasing headache and right-sided face and arm weakness and was found to have increasing vasogenic edema, leading to escalation of the dexamethasone dose and discontinuation of the immune checkpoint inhibitor. Bevacizumab therapy was started for vasogenic edema to try to minimize the corticosteroid requirement, but after the second dose, the patient complained of severe headache and visual problems and was found to have blood pressure of 210/130 mm Hg. MRI demonstrated symmetric ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) signal abnormalities both in the occipital regions and centrally, consistent with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) (FIGURE 9-2C). The bevacizumab was discontinued, and the patient improved enough to continue her cancer treatment as an outpatient. COMMENT This case illustrates the interdisciplinary teamwork necessary to design the optimal program for a patient with brain metastasis and to manage the adverse effects of these therapies. The patient had a symptomatic lesion in an eloquent brain area such that surgery was not feasible. The lesion was small enough to allow for stereotactic surgery, and the EGFR variant could be targeted with systemic therapy (osimertinib) as well. Steroids were kept at the lowest possible dose to maximize function and reduce the likelihood of seizure while not interfering with the efﬁcacy of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, but the patient developed early radiation-related edema, possibly exacerbated by the use of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, requiring higher doses of steroids. Bevacizumab, a vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) inhibitor, can be useful in vasogenic edema management, but has been associated with hypertension and PRES. 214 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

therapy at the time of recurrence after systemic treatment. Patients who undergo whole-brain radiation should undergo a hippocampal sparing protocol (unless there are hippocampal metastases) and receive memantine to reduce the risk of short-term memory impairment. 5 Patients with asymptomatic brain metastases with either a Karnofsky scale score of less than 50 or a Karnofsky scale score of less than 70 with no systemic therapy options do not derive beneﬁt from radiation therapy. 9-13 CASE 9-1 illustrates the complicated decision-making involved in choosing the optimal treatment strategy for brain metastases. FIGURE 9-2 Imaging of the patient in CASE 9-1. A, Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR), and B, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI show a posterior frontal ring-enhancing mass with surrounding edema. The patient had a symptomatic lesion due to seizures and therefore required immediate local as well as systemic therapy for her EGFR-positive adenocarcinoma with lung primary. She was started on steroids as well as osimertinib, but she then developed severe hypertension and blurred vision. C, Axial FLAIR image at the time of her hypertensive urgency shows posterior hemispheric lesions consistent with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) but also less typical, centrally placed FLAIR abnormalities in a pattern called central PRES. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Leptomeningeal Metastases Leptomeningeal metastases (also called carcinomatous meningitis) can present with headache, cranial nerve palsies, radiculopathies, and signs of elevated intracranial pressure. MRI can demonstrate leptomeningeal, cranial nerve, and nerve root enhancement (FIGURE 9-3) but is normal in nearly one- half of cases, requiring CSF cytology and ﬂow cytometry to conﬁrm the diagnosis, which may require 2 or 3 lumbar punctures. CSF cell-free DNA is an emerging technique for diagnosing and monitoring leptomeningeal metastases. 14 The prognosis is poor, with median life expectancy ranging from 2 to 6 months, depending on the primary cancer. Proton beam irradiation can facilitate local control of bulky disease, and ventriculoperitoneal shunting and acetazolamide can palliate symptoms of elevated intracranial pressure. Case reports suggest leptomeningeal metastases may respond to third-generation anaplastic lymphoma kinase inhibitors such as lorlatinib, EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as osimertinib (for EGFR-positive non–small cell lung carcinoma), and immune checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab (for breast and lung cancer). 15,16 Spinal Metastases Spinal metastases are most commonly epidural (spreading from vertebral body metastases) but can also be intradural and intramedullary. The differential diagnosis of cancer-associated myelopathy also includes immune-mediated processes that can be immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy–associated or paraneoplastic, infectious myelopathy (varicella-zoster virus [VZV], cytomegalovirus, West Nile virus), radiation myelopathy, chemotherapy toxicity (methotrexate, nelarabine), superﬁcial siderosis, nutritional FIGURE 9-3 Leptomeningeal metastases. A, Sagittal T1-weighted, gadolinium-enhanced spine MRI shows extensive leptomeningeal and intramedullary enhancement in the thoracic spine of a 58-year-old woman with a 10-year disease-free period after mastectomy for triple-negative breast cancer. B, Sagittal T1-weighted, gadolinium-enhanced lumbar MRI shows enhancing cauda equina nerve roots and further leptomeningeal disease. C, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced brain MRI at the level of the cerebellar folia, which are avidly enhancing, consistent with sulcal involvement from carcinomatous meningitis. 216 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

deﬁciencies (vitamin B 12 , copper, vitamin B 6 ), and surgery-related complications such as dural arteriovenous ﬁstulas. Of particular importance to the neurologic consultant is the recognition of spinal cord syndromes that suggest the presence of occult cancer in a patient not known to have malignancy: the pattern of tractopathies involving the lateral or dorsal columns longitudinally, often with enhancement, should precipitate a search for a paraneoplastic antibody and its associated malignancy. 17 The management of spinal metastases differs based on the location (extradural, intradural extramedullary, intradural intramedullary). 18,19 Other Sites of Metastasis Numb chin syndrome with paresthesia or sensory loss in the distribution of the mental nerve has been described in several patients with cancer, notably breast and lung metastases to the mandible or leptomeninges. COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER TREATMENT Although cancer therapies such as radiation, conventional chemotherapy, and immunotherapy improve outcomes and prognosis in patients with systemic cancer, they can also cause nervous system toxicity. Complications of Radiation Therapy Radiation complications are divided into acute (during or just after treatment), subacute (months after treatment), and chronic (years after treatment). Acute complications of brain radiation include headache, nausea and vomiting, and seizures. Acute complications of spinal radiation include rapid (several days) evolution of weakness and numbness referable to spinal segments within the radiation ﬁeld that may be associated with a Lhermitte sign. Corticosteroids may reverse some of the acute symptoms of brain and spine radiation. Subacute radiation complications occur 2 weeks to 6 months after radiation therapy and manifest with symptoms similar to acute radiation complications; these symptoms may also respond to a short course of corticosteroids. Late consequences of cranial irradiation include diffuse leukoencephalopathy (which can lead to long-term memory and executive dysfunction), vasculopathy (which can lead to stroke), cavernomas (which can hemorrhage, cause seizures, and are correlated with cognitive decline), and localized radiation necrosis with a contrast-enhancing mass that must be distinguished from tumor recurrence (by magnetic resonance spectroscopy, MRI with perfusion sequences, positron emission tomography [PET], or biopsy or resection) (FIGURE 9-4). For the treatment of localized radiation necrosis, dexamethasone or bevacizumab, a vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) inhibitor, may be used; bevacizumab is typically administered every 3 weeks at doses of 5 mg/kg to 7.5 mg/kg (CASE 9-1). Complications of bevacizumab include hypertension, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), impaired wound healing, renal dysfunction, ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke, deep vein thrombosis (DVT), and colonic perforation. Whole-brain radiation therapy can cause progressive short-term memory impairment, behavioral change, and impaired executive function. The risk of these sequelae of whole-brain radiation is diminished with hippocampal KEY POINTS •Local therapy, such as surgery or stereotactic radiosurgery, should be offered to patients with symptomatic brain metastases regardless of the systemic therapy used, whereas the decision to defer local therapy for patients with asymptomatic brain metastases should be based on a multidisciplinary discussion of potential beneﬁts and harms. •CSF cell-free DNA is an emerging technique for diagnosing and monitoring leptomeningeal metastases. •Spinal metastases most often result from epidural spread from vertebral body metastases. •The differential diagnosis of cancer- associated myelopathy includes autoimmune processes, infections, radiation myelopathy, chemotherapy toxicity, surgery-related complications, and nutritional deﬁciencies. •The numerous sequelae of cranial radiation therapy include delayed leukoencephalopathy, stroke, radiation-induced secondary neoplasms, endocrine dysfunction, stroke-like migraine after radiation therapy (SMART) syndrome, and radiation necrosis. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 217 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

avoidance radiation strategies and memantine, an N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonist. 20 Stroke-like migraine after radiation therapy (SMART) syndrome is an underrecognized complication of brain radiation that can occur from 4 weeks after treatment to decades later. SMART syndrome presents as episodes of focal neurologic deﬁcits, such as hemianopsia, hemisensory symptoms, and, less often, aphasia that last hours to days, usually accompanied or followed FIGURE 9-4 Radiation therapy sequelae. A, Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) brain MRI shows brain volume loss as well as extensive T2 hyperintensity reﬂecting signiﬁcant white matter damage in a 58-year-old man who received whole-brain radiation therapy for three small brain metastases from adenocarcinoma of the lung 18 months before he had this MRI; the clinical correlate was progressive and ultimately severe cognitive impairment. B, Axial FLAIR image of the patient described in CASE 9-4. This long-term leukemia survivor, who received cranial irradiation at age 11 years, had episodic spells of headache and left body paresthesia. There is diffuse sulcal swelling consistent with stroke-like migraine after radiation therapy (SMART) syndrome. C, Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) of the posterior circulation in a 61-year-old woman who had whole-brain radiation therapy for a posterior fossa small cell lung cancer 5 years prior. There is diffuse narrowing of the basilar artery; the same ﬁnding was seen in her anterior and middle cerebral artery territories. D, Axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) of the patient in CASE 9-4 shows multiple cavernous malformations likely related to radiation. E, Axial T1-weighted gadolinium- enhanced MRI of a 49-year-old woman shows an enhancing lesion in her temporal lobe 3 years after treatment for a grade 2 IDH-variant astrocytoma. MR spectroscopy showed a spectrum consistent with radiation necrosis, which was surgically resected. F, Axial T-1 weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI of a 39-year-old woman who had radiation 32 years prior for a thalamic astrocytoma and who developed acute speech difﬁculty and seizures. The image shows a heterogeneously enhancing mass that appeared to be largely extraaxial, although there was clearly adjacent parenchymal edema. Resection revealed a grade 2 meningioma, likely radiation induced. 218 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

by a contralateral migraine-type headache. MRI demonstrates gyral swelling (usually with some gyral enhancement) in a nonvascular distribution (FIGURE 9-4B). During episodes, EEG shows attenuated activity over the affected area, and PET scan demonstrates decreased glucose metabolism in these areas. Management includes administration of antiseizure medications if seizures occur, but there are no other established therapies; corticosteroids, anticoagulants, and migraine therapies are not considered effective. However, prompt recognition of SMART syndrome can avoid unnecessary diagnostic tests such as angiography or biopsy and should alert the clinician to the likely presence of additional long-term radiation sequelae. 21 ,22 Radiation myelopathy can appear from several months to many years after treatment directed at spinal metastatic disease or malignancy involving tissues adjacent to the spine that are in the radiation ﬁeld. It is characterized by progressive, usually painless myelopathy; spinal cord hyperintensity or atrophy may be seen on MRI. Proton therapy and spinal cord protection strategies with stereotactic body radiation reduce the risk of radiation myelopathy. 23 Radiation myelopathy can be slowed in some cases with the VEGF inhibitor bevacizumab, although recovery is usually incomplete. Radiation can also cause cranial neuropathy or plexopathy, often presenting years after the initial treatment. Brachial and lumbosacral plexopathies generally present with progressive, painless, sensorimotor dysfunction. Radiation-induced plexopathy is distinguished from tumor inﬁltration of the plexus by the presence of myokymic discharges on EMG in the former. 24 Complications of Chemotherapy Conventional chemotherapeutic agents can cause a wide variety of adverse effects on the nervous system, summarized in TABLE 9-1. 25 Two commonly encountered neurologic toxicities of chemotherapy are chemotherapy-induced cognitive impairment (also known colloquially as “brain fog”) and peripheral neuropathy. Chemotherapy-induced cognitive impairment is characterized by short- term memory impairment and diminished executive function, often accompanied by mood dysfunction and signiﬁcant anxiety. Data suggest that alterations in cerebral connectivity may play a role in the pathophysiology and that recovery may occur 2 to 3 years after treatment is concluded, but data are limited with respect to prognosis. 26-30 Chemotherapy-associated peripheral neuropathy is typically an axonal sensory neuropathy that is often painful and develops during treatment and usually resolves following treatment, although it may transiently worsen (a phenomenon called “coasting”) or persist after completion of chemotherapy. Common causes include taxanes, platins, vinca alkaloids, bortezomib, and lenalidomide. Neuropathic pain can be treated with duloxetine, gabapentin, pregabalin, or tricyclic antidepressants, with the most evidence for duloxetine. 24 There are no effective agents for preventing this complication. Complications of Immunotherapy With the proliferation of cancer immunotherapy over the past decade, an ever-increasing spectrum of central and peripheral nervous system complications related to this therapy has been recognized. Neurologic KEY POINTS •Chemotherapy-induced cognitive impairment or “brain fog” reduces quality of life. In one study, patients with breast cancer had largely recovered by 2 to 3 years; factors other than chemotherapy may contribute to symptoms. TABLE 9-1 Adverse Effects of Chemotherapy, Targeted Biologic Agents, and Immunotherapies a Syndrome Conventional chemotherapy Biological and immunologic therapies Central nervous system syndromes
