Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

consultants must recognize the sometimes life-threatening complications and advise on the feasibility of continuing anti-neoplastic therapy under these circumstances. IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITORS. Immune checkpoint inhibitors engage the host immune system to elicit an antitumor response. This increased activity of the immune system can lead to immune-mediated neurologic adverse events, which occur in 1% to 14% of patients and are more common when both cytotoxic T-lymphocyte–associated antigen 4 (CTLA4) and programmed death ligand-1 (PD-L1) or programmed cell death protein-1 (PD-1) inhibitors are used simultaneously. 31-34 The presentation of neurologic adverse events from immune checkpoint inhibitors may be acute or subacute and can occur at any time, from after the ﬁrst dose to more than 1 year after initiation of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, with a median time of onset of approximately 6 weeks (two to three cycles) after therapy initiation. Most patients with neurologic complications of immune checkpoint inhibitors also have one or more systemic complications such as pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, or autoimmune diabetes. Peripheral nervous system toxicities are more common than central nervous system toxicities. 32 ,35 These complications include Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome (acute inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy [AIDP] or acute axonal neuropathy), chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP), brachial neuritis, cranial neuritis (cranial nerve VII is the most commonly affected, followed by cranial nerve VIII, cranial nerve II, and cranial nerve VI), myositis (which may be accompanied by myocarditis), myasthenia gravis, or a combination of these syndromes. FIGURE 9-5 Frequency and mortality rates of neurologic adverse events related to immune checkpoint inhibitor use. The histogram illustrates the frequency and mortality of central and peripheral complications of immune checkpoint inhibitors. Multiple organ systems (not shown), as well as the nervous system, can be affected by immune checkpoint inhibitors. GBS = Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome. a Frequency among all central nervous system and peripheral nervous system complications due to immune checkpoint inhibitor use. Modiﬁed with permission from Marini A, et al, Neurology. 32 © 2021 American Academy of Neurology. 222 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

FIGURE 9-5 illustrates the frequency of nervous system complications and their mortality rates. Central nervous system complications include encephalitis, cerebellitis, PRES, pseudoprogression (worsening edema and enhancement during the course of treatment with radiation therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, which resolves after a few weeks), hypophysitis, and myelitis (CASE 9-2). Immune checkpoint inhibitors can also cause the emergence of paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes (discussed later in this article). Treatment of immune checkpoint inhibitor–associated neurologic adverse events is generally with corticosteroids and withdrawal of the immune checkpoint inhibitor treatment. There is good recovery in roughly three- fourths of patients, but the safety of resuming immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy is unclear. Rechallenge with an immune checkpoint inhibitor is a multidisciplinary decision, dependent on the oncologist’s assessment of immune checkpoint inhibitor beneﬁt and the extent of recovery from the initial neurologic adverse event as assessed by the consulting neurologist (CASE 9-2). 33,34,36 Concern has been raised about whether immune checkpoint inhibitors could lead to ﬂares of preexisting immune-mediated conditions. In a population of older patients with inactive multiple sclerosis, the use of immune checkpoint inhibitors did not result in unusual toxicity or multiple sclerosis ﬂares, but the generalizability of this ﬁnding to younger patients with more active disease is not yet clear. 37 CHIMERIC ANTIGEN RECEPTOR T-CELL THERAPY. CAR T-cell therapy consists of genetically modiﬁed T cells engineered to express a receptor that recognizes various tumor cell-surface proteins, allowing T cells to kill tumor cells. The ﬁrst CAR T-cell therapy was approved in 2017, and six CAR T-cell products are approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of six hematologic malignancies, including B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, large B-cell lymphoma, follicular lymphoma, mantle cell lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, and multiple myeloma; many other indications are under investigation. 38 The most common complications of CAR T-cell therapy are cytokine release syndrome and immune effector cell–associated neurotoxicity syndrome. Cytokine release syndrome emerges 1 to 5 days after treatment and can cause fever, hypoxia, coagulopathy, and capillary leak syndrome (a life-threatening syndrome triggered by infection or cancer and characterized by hypotension and hypoalbuminemia). Immune effector cell–associated neurotoxicity syndrome occurs 5 to 7 days after treatment and is characterized by variable grades of neurologic dysfunction that can include seizures, raised intracranial pressure, and dysgraphia, aphasia, or both. Cytokine release syndrome is treated with tocilizumab, and immune effector cell–associated neurotoxicity syndrome is treated with corticosteroids. 36,39,40 Most patients recover from CAR T-cell therapy toxicity completely, even when cytokine release syndrome and subsequent immune effector cell–associated neurotoxicity syndrome are severe. Five of the six approved CAR T-cell therapy products have been associated with rare T-cell cancers. 41,42 KEY POINTS •Immune checkpoint inhibitors have improved the prognosis for several systemic malignancies but are associated with a series of novel peripheral and central nervous system adverse events, including acute and subacute presentations, sometimes after a single dose but with a median time of onset of approximately 6 weeks after therapy initiation. •Chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy can trigger cytokine release syndrome and immune effector cell– associated neurotoxicity syndrome, and aphasia and dysgraphia are hallmarks of immune effector cell– associated neurotoxicity syndrome. Although these patients can appear very sick, complete recovery is possible. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 223 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

B-cell maturation antigen CAR T-cell therapy is given as a third- or fourth- line strategy in patients with heavily pretreated multiple myeloma whose prior therapies have also produced neurotoxicity. Although most CAR T-cell therapy toxicities occur within the ﬁrst week after treatment, a recently described cognitive and motor syndrome of parkinsonism, short-term memory loss, and somnolence in patients receiving B-cell maturation antigen CAR T-cell therapy can occur 1 to 2 months after treatment. This complication does not appear to be responsive to dopaminergic or corticosteroid treatment. CASE 9-3 and TABLE 9-2 43 illustrate additional nervous system complications in patients with multiple myeloma. 43-51 PARANEOPLASTIC NEUROLOGIC SYNDROMES Paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes are immune-mediated conditions triggered by the presence of cancer and often associated with speciﬁc CASE 9-2 A 48-year-old woman with metastatic melanoma presented with lower back pain, bilateral leg stiffness, and urinary retention over several days, which began 1 day after her second course of ipilimumab and nivolumab. Examination demonstrated bilateral leg weakness, diminished vibration sense at the ankles, hyperreﬂexia in all four limbs, and bilateral Babinski signs. A spinal MRI with contrast showed holocord diffuse signal abnormality from C2 to the conus medullaris with slight cord expansion (FIGURE 9-6). CSF studies revealed 72 white blood cells/mm 3 (90% lymphocytes), 2 red blood cells/mm 3 , protein 149 mg/dL, and glucose 48 mg/dL with concurrent blood glucose of 110 mg/dL. CSF bacterial cultures were negative, as were polymerase chain reaction (PCR) tests for varicella-zoster virus (VZV), human herpesvirus 6, Epstein-Barr virus, and cytomegalovirus. CSF cytology was negative for malignant cells, and cultures were negative for bacterial pathogens. Serum aquaporin-4 antibodies were negative, as were her serum and CSF paraneoplastic panels. She was diagnosed with immunotherapy-related myelitis and started on steroids. A repeat spine MRI 2 months later demonstrated signiﬁcant interval improvement in the signal abnormality, and the patient’s clinical symptoms also improved completely. COMMENT This case illustrates one of the central nervous system complications of immune checkpoint inhibitors. It is important to exclude metastases, infection, and other immune-mediated conditions. This patient also had a negative CSF paraneoplastic antibody panel and did not meet criteria for an immune checkpoint inhibitor–induced paraneoplastic syndrome. The decision about whether to reinstitute immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy for patients who have sustained a complication depends on the oncologist’s estimates of potential beneﬁt. One might consider using only nivolumab because the incidence of neurologic complications is less than 2% with single immune checkpoint inhibitor use but increases to nearly 14% when both nivolumab and ipilimumab are given in combination, as was done in this case. 30 ,31 224 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

antineuronal antibodies. Paraneoplastic syndromes may also be triggered by immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. Paraneoplastic syndromes occur in 1% to 2% of patients with cancer and usually predate the discovery of the cancer unless they are associated with immune checkpoint inhibitor treatment. Antibodies are classiﬁed as intracellular (eg, antineuronal nuclear antibody type 1 [ANNA-1; also known as anti-Hu]) or cell surface (eg, anti-NMDA receptor), with the former more commonly associated with an underlying cancer and generally less responsive to treatment. Classic paraneoplastic syndromes are summarized in TABLE 9-3. The presence of any of these syndromes should lead to a search for an underlying cancer with a chest, abdomen, and pelvis CT or a body PET and sending both serum and CSF paraneoplastic panels. Guidelines recommend rescreening with CT scans or PET every 6 months for at least 2 years if a classic FIGURE 9-6 Imaging of the patient in CASE 9-2. Sagittal T2- weighted spine MRI shows a slightly expanded spinal cord with holocord hyperintensity from the cervical region to the conus, consistent with severe longitudinally extensive myelitis from immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. The patient responded well to corticosteroids. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 225 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 9-3 A 65-year-old man presented with several months of trouble walking and a month of progressive left jaw pain and headache. He had sustained a 40-pound unintentional weight loss over the prior year and had poorly controlled hypertension, hypothyroidism, and mild chronic renal insufﬁciency. Examination revealed diminished sensation in the distribution of his mental nerve, profound loss of vibration and proprioception in all limbs, a right foot drop, and absent muscle stretch reﬂexes. Splenomegaly was noted. MRI of his brain demonstrated marked dural enhancement (FIGURE 9-7). A positron emission tomography (PET) scan showed multiple ﬂudeoxyglucose-avid areas, including his left jaw. He was diagnosed with IgG lambda multiple myeloma with an elevated serum vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), conﬁrming the diagnosis of POEMS (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, and skin changes) syndrome. He received multiple treatments, including lenalidomide, bortezomib, dexamethasone, daratumumab, and an autologous stem cell transplant. His neuropathy improved somewhat, but he then relapsed and received B-cell maturation antigen chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T-cell therapy (ciltacabtagene autoleucel), sustaining grade 2 immune effector cell– associated neurotoxicity syndrome symptoms, which resolved. Two months after CAR T-cell therapy, he presented with progressive weakness. Neurologic examination was notable for ﬂat affect, bradykinesia, rigidity, and a parkinsonian gait disorder. MRI showed resolution of the dural enhancement seen initially, and CSF showed 40 lymphocytes/mm 3 with cultures as well as metagenomic next-generation sequencing negative for infection. He was treated with corticosteroids and later carbidopa/levodopa with minimal improvement and succumbed to a large pulmonary embolism after 6 weeks in the hospital. COMMENT This case shows many of the neurologic complications of multiple myeloma and its treatment (TABLE 9-2). The patient presented with mental neuropathy and a peripheral neuropathy that resembled chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP). The MRI was a clue to underlying POEMS syndrome, conﬁrmed by elevated serum VEGF level. He failed numerous therapies, including a stem cell transplant, and then had a newly recognized complication of CAR T-cell therapy, which, unlike immune effector cell–associated neurotoxicity syndrome and cytokine release syndrome, can be delayed by many weeks. Although some patients have responded to dopaminergic therapy, this patient did not. He sustained yet another complication of multiple myeloma, thrombophilia, which led to fatal pulmonary embolism. 226 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

FIGURE 9-7 Imaging of the patient in CASE 9-3. Axial (A) and coronal (B) T1-weighted gadolinium-enhanced MRI shows diffuse, thick dural enhancement. The patient also had evidence of demyelinating neuropathy and was found to have a lambda light chain M spike consistent with multiple myeloma. He had a polyneuropathy characteristic of POEMS (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal gammopathy, and skin changes) syndrome, but none of the other features. paraneoplastic syndrome and associated antibody are found without initial evidence of systemic cancer. 52,53 Decades of immunoﬂuorescence testing in the Mayo Clinic Neuroimmunology Laboratory found that the rate of clinically pertinent autoantibody detection was highest when both serum and CSF were tested. 52-55 Small cell lung carcinoma is the malignancy most frequently associated with paraneoplastic syndromes; it is the underlying neoplasm in most cases of ANNA-1–associated paraneoplastic syndromes and approximately one- half of cases of anti-γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) B receptor–associated paraneoplastic syndromes. Some antibodies are associated with a variety of syndromes, whereas others are associated with a speciﬁc syndrome. For example, ANNA-1 antibodies can be associated with sensory neuronopathy, limbic encephalitis, and encephalomyelitis. Anti-NMDA and anti-GABA B receptor antibodies are associated predominantly with limbic encephalitis, although patients with NMDA encephalitis also can develop chorea, dysautonomia, and dystonia. TABLE 9-2 Neurologic Presentations and Complications of Multiple Myeloma a Direct inﬁltration of bone, nerve, or leptomeninges u Skull, jaw: headache, seizures, numb chin syndrome u Spine: bone pain, radicular pain, cord compression with vertebral collapse u Leptomeninges: cauda equina, radiculopathy, cranial neuropathy, encephalopathy u Peripheral nerve: numb chin syndrome, asymmetric neuropathy and plexopathy, amyloid-associated neuropathy Remote effects u Paraneoplastic: POEMS (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, and skin changes), anti–myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG) neuropathy, γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) A encephalitis u Metabolic: hypercalcemia, uremia u Thrombophilia: deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism, stroke, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis u Amyloid: peripheral neuropathy with or without autonomic neuropathy Treatment-related effects u Infections due to immunosuppression u Neuropathy: bortezomib, vincristine, pomalidomide u Myopathy: corticosteroids u Seizures: melphalan u Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES): bortezomib u Parkinsonian syndrome: B-cell maturation antigen–directed chimeric antigen receptor CAR T-cell therapy a Modiﬁed with permission from Smith N, et al, Pract Neurol. 43 © 2019 Author(s) (or their employer[s]). 228 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Recent work has shown that particular genetic mutations in small cell lung carcinoma may be associated with particular paraneoplastic syndromes. One example of these associations is that tumors of patients with anti-GABA B receptor paraneoplastic syndromes often have a gain of three genes on chromosome 5q, compared with the tumors of ANNA-1 and control patients. Tumors of patients with anti-GABA B receptor encephalitis were enriched in B-cell signatures, whereas patients with anti–Hu-associated syndromes had overexpression of T-cell– and interferon-γ–related signatures. 56 Immune checkpoint inhibitors can trigger paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes identical to paraneoplastic syndromes that occur without immune checkpoint inhibitor exposure (TABLE 9-4 57-59 ). 57,58,60 Immune checkpoint inhibitor–triggered paraneoplastic syndromes have poorer outcomes than other neurologic adverse events associated with immune checkpoint inhibitors. Approximately 54% of patients with an immune checkpoint inhibitor–associated clinical syndrome consistent with a central nervous system paraneoplastic syndrome have a speciﬁc autoantibody. 56 Although these antibodies are sometimes detected before treatment, it is not currently a standard recommendation to evaluate for paraneoplastic antibodies before immune checkpoint inhibitor initiation. Patients on immune checkpoint inhibitors may develop rare, novel autoantibodies or multiple autoantibodies, but the clinical signiﬁcance of these is unclear. New associations are published regularly, as demonstrated by the description of Kelch-like protein 11 rhombencephalitis associated with esophageal adenocarcinoma after exposure to an immune checkpoint inhibitor. 57 Management of paraneoplastic syndromes involves treatment of the underlying malignancy and immunomodulatory therapy. Classic syndromes associated with intracellular antibodies are generally refractory to treatment because neuronal damage has often occurred before recognition of the syndrome. When paraneoplastic syndromes develop in the context of immune checkpoint inhibitor use, steroids or plasmapheresis may be considered, but these may reduce the antineoplastic effects of the immune checkpoint inhibitor. However, these treatments are generally given if the syndrome is severe. Cyclophosphamide has been suggested because it crosses the blood- brain barrier, and natalizumab has been reported as a successful treatment in one patient with ANNA-1 encephalitis. 59,61 OTHER NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER Patients with systemic cancer are at increased risk of cerebral edema, seizure, stroke, and infection. These complications have been comprehensively reviewed. 50,51 Cerebral Edema Cerebral edema due to brain metastases is vasogenic and can therefore be managed with steroids if the patient is symptomatic or the tumor causes signiﬁcant mass effect. Dexamethasone or its equivalent methylprednisolone dose (0.75 mg dexamethasone = 4 mg methylprednisolone) is most commonly administered in divided doses in the morning and early afternoon to minimize sleep disruption. The tapering schedule should decrease the dose by no more than 2 mg every 4 to 5 days KEY POINTS •Multiple myeloma affects the nervous system either by direct bone or nerve inﬁltration, through tumor-associated neuropathies such as POEMS (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, and skin changes), and by other paraneoplastic syndromes such as γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) A encephalitis and a novel CAR T-cell therapy–associated extrapyramidal syndrome. •Paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes are immune-mediated disorders that are triggered by the presence of cancer and often associated with speciﬁc neuronal antibodies and have also emerged in the context of immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy, the use of which sometimes triggers multiple antibodies. •Small cell lung carcinoma is the most frequent malignancy associated with paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes. symptoms eventually progress to involve trunk and limbs, becoming a severe pancerebellar syndrome MRI is often initially normal; subsequently, cerebellar atrophy might be found PCA-1 (ovary and breast tumors); anti- CV2/CRMP-5 (small cell lung cancer, thymoma); PCA2 (small cell lung cancer, non–small cell lung cancer, breast tumors); anti- amphiphysin (small cell lung cancer, breast tumors); LUZP4 (seminoma) Opsoclonus- myoclonus syndrome Involuntary, arrhythmic, multidirectional, saccadic ocular movements without a saccadic interval, and action myoclonus often involving trunk, limbs, and head; patients might also have cerebellar ataxia and encephalopathy Variable according to the areas involved ANNA-2 (breast and lung tumors [adults],ovarianteratoma [young women], neuroblastoma [children]) Sensory neuronopathy Paraesthesia and painful dysesthesia of usually asymmetriconset,predominantly in the upper limbs; progressive extension to the lower limbs and sometimes trunk and face, loss of deep sensation manifesting as proprioceptive ataxia, pseudoathetosis, and hypopallesthesia Nerve conduction studies: abolition or substantial reduction (ie, greater than 30%) of sensory action potentials (non–length-dependent patterns) not explained by entrapment neuropathies; spine MRI, possibly with posterior columns T2 hyperintensity ANNA-1 (small cell lung cancer, non–small cell lung cancer, neuroendocrine tumors); anti- CV2/CRMP-5 (small cell lung cancer); antiamphiphysin (small cell lunger cancer, breast tumors) CONTINUED ON PAGE 231 230 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. with close monitoring of the patient for signs of reemerging elevation in intracranial pressure, such as headache, seizure, or focal deﬁcits. 62-64 It is commonly said that if a patient has a new brain mass concerning for neoplasm that steroids should not be given as they may alter the biopsy result in patients with lymphoma. However, some studies found no difference in biopsy yield whether patients had received steroids before biopsy or not. 65 This area remains controversial and, although steroids ideally should be avoided before biopsy, they should not be withheld if there is critical mass effect due to cerebral edema. Therefore, if a patient with a new brain mass has signiﬁcant, symptomatic peri-lesional cerebral edema, steroids may be given before brain biopsy. Seizure Seizures are a common complication of brain metastases, occurring in approximately 25% of patients overall, although incidence depends on both tumor type and location. 66 Brain metastasis–associated seizures should be treated with an antiseizure medication, preferably levetiracetam to avoid the potential drug-to-drug interactions with chemotherapy agents that can occur with older enzyme-inducing agents. 67 ,68 The safety of discontinuing antiseizure medication in patients with brain metastases is uncertain. 69 In general, well-tolerated antiseizure medications should be continued to minimize seizure risk and associated injury and rehospitalization. In patients with a new diagnosis of brain metastases who have not had a seizure, guidelines do not recommend prophylactic antiseizure medication; CONTINUED FROM PAGE 230 Syndrome Clinical presentation Paraclinical ﬁndings Antibody association and underlying malignancy Gastrointestinal pseudo-obstruction Persistent constipation, abdominal pain or distention, nausea, vomiting, or weight loss X-ray or CT scan shows dilatation of stomach, small intestine, or colon; esophageal manometry shows impaired motility in the absence of mechanical obstruction ANNA-1 (small cell lung cancer, non–small cell lung cancer, neuroendocrine tumors); anti- CV2/CRMP-5 (small cell lung cancer) Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome Proximal muscle weakness, areﬂexia (reﬂexes might appear after physical exercise), and dysautonomia (mainly dry mouth or sexual dysfunction) Nerve conduction studies show low amplitude of the compound motor action potential with a 100% increase or greater after high-frequency repetitive supramaximal nerve stimulation (or physical exercise) Anti–P/Q type VGCC (small cell lung cancer); neurologic syndromes with combination CTLA4 and PD-1 treatment with associated antibodies to N-methyl- D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor, SRY-box transcription factor 1 (SOX-1), Hu, Ma2. 232 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

there is insufﬁcient evidence for guideline recommendations on how to use tumor location, histology, grade, molecular characteristics, or neuroimaging to guide this decision. Evidence is insufﬁcient for guideline recommendations on prescribing antiseizure medications to reduce the risk of seizures in the perioperative or postoperative period in patients who undergo surgery for brain metastases. 67,68 Stroke The mechanisms of stroke in patients with systemic cancer include hypercoagulability, infection, immobility, radiation vasculopathy, and chemotherapy (particularly L-asparaginase, methotrexate, and lenalidomide). 70 The optimal secondary prevention agent is uncertain in patients who develop ischemic stroke due to hypercoagulability of malignancy. Among ARCADIA (AtRial Cardiopathy and Antithrombotic Drugs in Prevention After Cryptogenic Stroke) trial participants with a history of cancer, apixaban was slightly, but not signiﬁcantly, favored over aspirin for risk reduction for major ischemic stroke, although analyses were underpowered. 71 In patients with cancer and thrombocytopenia who require anticoagulation, guidelines recommend full anticoagulation if the platelet count is above 50,000/µL, half-dose anticoagulation if the platelet count is between 25,000 and 50,000/µL, and holding anticoagulation if the platelet count is below 25,000/ µL. 72-75 Venous Thromboembolism Patients with systemic cancer are at increased risk for venous thromboembolism. Patients with brain metastases who are hospitalized with an acute medical condition should receive venous thromboembolism prophylaxis with apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, or low-molecular-weight heparin. 72 Exceptions to this recommendation include patients with hemorrhagic brain metastases such as melanoma, although this is an individualized risk-beneﬁt decision in conjunction with the patient’s primary oncology team. After neurosurgery for brain tumor resection, the timing of venous thromboembolism prophylaxis should be determined with the neurosurgery team. Routine outpatient venous thromboembolism prophylaxis for patients with cancer is not recommended. 72 In a comparative effectiveness study with 5100 adult patients with cancer-associated venous thromboembolism, direct-acting oral anticoagulants outperformed aspirin and were associated with a higher adherence rate, lower risk of venous thromboembolism recurrence, lower risk of major bleeding, and improved mortality. 75 Central Nervous System Infection Patients with cancer can become immunocompromised in the setting of the cancer itself (eg, hematologic malignancy), bone marrow transplant, or immunosuppressive chemotherapy (eg, B-cell depletion with rituximab). Therefore, clinicians should have a high index of suspicion for nervous system infection in these patients, noting that immunocompromised patients may not be able to mount a fever (particularly when being treated with corticosteroids) or develop other signs of infection seen in patients who are immunocompetent. KEY POINTS •Patients with newly diagnosed brain tumors who have not had a seizure should not be prescribed antiseizure medications; however, when antiseizure medicines are deemed necessary, physicians may consider the use of levetiracetam, given its more favorable adverse effect proﬁle. •Thrombophilia due to neoplasia, infection, immobility, radiation, and chemotherapy agents are the main causes of stroke in patients with cancer. •Immunocompromised patients may manifest few signs of infection because they may lack the robust inﬂammatory response, including response to vaccination, of a patient whose immune system is not compromised. •Much work still needs to be done to ﬁnd more effective and less toxic cancer therapies that will extend survival, improve quality of life, and reduce long-term treatment sequelae for patients treated in adolescence and young adulthood. Infections to consider in particular oncologic contexts frequently encountered by neurologic consultants are listed as follows: u Patients receiving transplants or those receiving other steroid-based immunosuppressive therapy can develop VZV infection, which can appear at any time after transplantation and can present with a myriad of syndromes, including retinitis, dermatomal or disseminated rash, stroke, or myelitis. Vaccination may not fully protect patients who cannot mount a robust response to the VZV vaccine or other vaccines. u Patients receiving rituximab or other treatments causing prolonged B-cell depletion with hypogammaglobulinemia are at risk for persistent enterovirus meningitis, West Nile virus encephalomyelitis, and Powassan virus meningoencephalitis. 76 u Patients receiving transplants and other patients receiving intensive, prolonged chemotherapy regimens are at risk for progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy due to JC virus (FIGURE 9-8). Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy is usually characterized by gradually enlarging, nonenhancing white matter lesions. Case reports suggest response to pembrolizumab. 77 u Patients with lymphoma receiving Bruton kinase inhibitors have been speciﬁcally identiﬁed to be at risk for Aspergillus fumigatus infections that are angioinvasive and can present with aneurysmal hemorrhage. 78 Long-Term Neurologic Sequelae of Cancer Therapies Nearly 80% of the 70,000 patients between the ages of 15 and 39 years who are diagnosed with cancer annually in the United States survive more than FIGURE 9-8 Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy in a patient with chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) (A) and diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) (B) MRI sequences demonstrating an advancing rim of DWI hyperintensity, characteristic of JC virus–related progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, conﬁrmed by CSF examination. The images are from a patient with a 10-year history of chronic lymphocytic leukemia who was treated with multiple agents, including brentuximab, and presented with a 2-month history of progressive left arm dysfunction and was found to have a T2 hyperintense lesion in the right juxtacortical white matter and a leading edge of DWI hyperintensity. 234 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CANCER AND ITS TREATMENT Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

5 years after their cancer diagnosis and may experience a variety of long-term neurologic sequelae of radiation and chemotherapy that affect quality of life (TABLE 9-5 79 ). 80 ,81 CASE 9-4 illustrates many of the chronic problems involving the nervous system that affect survivors of cancer. Many cancer centers now have multidisciplinary survivorship clinics in which neurologists play an important role in evaluating for and treating these long-term neurologic sequelae. 82 ,83 HEALTHCARE DISPARITIES IN THE TREATMENT OF CANCER PATIENTS WITH NEUROLOGIC PROBLEMS Signiﬁcant disparities exist in access to both advances in cancer therapy and expertise in diagnosis and treatment of neurologic complications. 2 Steps to overcome these disparities require efforts to improve access through technologies such as telemedicine, improved primary care–level cancer screening, access to genetic testing, widespread development of cancer survivorship clinics, ﬁnancial support for advanced cancer treatments, involvement of palliative care specialists, and inclusion of historically underrepresented population groups in clinical trials. CASE 9-4 A 33-year-old woman presented to the neurology clinic to establish care for multiple neurologic concerns. She was initially treated for acute myelogenous leukemia at age 11 years. She received several doses of intrathecal methotrexate and cytarabine as well as neuraxis radiation, the doses of which were unavailable to the neurology team. She developed high-frequency hearing loss, requiring ampliﬁcation by age 21 years. Seizures developed at age 14 years, and a small parasagittal meningioma was diagnosed, as was a different, nonenhancing growth in her posterior fossa. The cerebellar mass was resected and was found to be a grade 2 IDH-mutant astrocytoma. The patient completed high school with an individualized education plan and sought part-time employment after graduation. MRI showed multiple cavernomas (FIGURE 9-4D). At age 28 years, she began having episodes of prolonged visual auras alternating between her left and right visual ﬁelds that were sometimes accompanied by speech difﬁculty or left-sided numbness. Throbbing headaches followed these episodes and lasted for hours. During one such episode, an MRI was obtained (FIGURE 9-4B). COMMENT This case illustrates many of the long-term sequelae of cancer therapy in early life, including cognitive issues, secondary neoplasms (meningioma and astrocytoma), cavernous angiomas, strokelike migraine after radiation therapy (SMART) syndrome, and hearing loss. It further highlights the social and employment issues that face young adults who have survived cancer therapy earlier in life. It underscores the observation that even low-dose radiation may damage neural stem cells with delayed clinical consequences, requiring ongoing close surveillance. CONCLUSION Neurologic complications of systemic cancer include central nervous system metastases, toxicities of treatment, paraneoplastic syndromes, seizures, and stroke. As cancer treatments continue to evolve, new neurologic sequelae are identiﬁed, such as the toxicities associated with immune checkpoint inhibitors and CAR T-cell therapy. Neurologists play an integral role in evaluating patients to determine the neurologic syndrome, the likely causes, and the optimal management. Although much work remains to be done to achieve a high quality of life in survivors of neurologic complications of cancer, ongoing interdisciplinary collaboration among neurologists, oncologists, neurosurgeons, and scientists promises more effective and less toxic therapies in the future. Neurologic Complications of Cardiac Disease and Cardiac Procedures By Alvin S. Das, MD; Steven K. Feske, MD ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article reviews the neurologic complications of common cardiac conditions and cardiac procedures. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : During the past two decades, there has been an expansion of pharmacologic and procedural strategies to reduce stroke risk in patients with atrial ﬁbrillation (eg, direct oral anticoagulants, left atrial appendage occlusion, and catheter ablation), patent foramen ovale (ie, percutaneous closure in selected patients), and those undergoing cardiac surgery (eg, embolic protection devices). ESSENTIAL POINTS : Because stroke is the most common neurologic complication associated with cardiac disease, most newer pharmacologic agents and interventions have been designed to reduce the risk of cardioembolism, particularly from atrial ﬁbrillation, the most prevalent cardiac arrhythmia. Common cardiac procedures such as coronary artery bypass grafting and valve replacement surgery can result in stroke and cognitive decline. Neurologists must be aware of the evolving evidence and tools to mitigate neurologic risks in these patients. INTRODUCTION B ecause of the anatomic and functional relationship between the heart and the brain, dysfunction in the cardiac system can inﬂuence neurologic health and vice versa. Given that the brain receives up to 15% of cardiac output, structural abnormalities of the heart can have direct effects on the brain. Several vascular factors that increase the risk of cardiac disease are also risk factors for neurologic diseases such as stroke and dementia. Fortunately, an increasing array of pharmacologic therapies and medical devices has been developed to address cardiac conditions associated with increased risk of stroke. Stroke is the most common neurologic complication arising from cardiac disorders. Both congenital and acquired structural defects of the heart can lead to increased blood stasis and endothelial injury, predisposing to thrombus formation, which can embolize and lead to symptomatic strokes or silent infarctions. In addition to structural heart disease, cardiac arrhythmias can CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 23 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):23 – 56. Address correspondence to Dr Alvin S. Das, 110 Francis St, Lowry Medical Ofﬁce Building, Suite 9A-05, Boston, MA 02215, adas4@bidmc.harvard.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Das reports no disclosure. Dr Feske has received publishing royalties from a publication relating to health care. UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Drs Das and Feske report no disclosure. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

result in contractile dysfunction and stasis, promoting thrombus formation and embolization. Atrial ﬁbrillation (AF), the most prevalent clinically signiﬁcant arrhythmia, is associated with an increased risk of embolic stroke, requiring screening for this arrhythmia in the appropriate context and providing antithrombotic or procedural interventions to reduce stroke risk if detected. Cardiac interventions, although essential for treating heart disease, carry the risk of neurologic complications such as stroke, which can occur intraoperatively or postoperatively. Because ischemic stroke after cardiac surgery has been associated with increased morbidity, mortality, and cost, interventions that reduce stroke risk in patients undergoing cardiac surgery are critical. A variety of strategies, including embolic protection devices and intraoperative monitoring techniques, have been used to reduce these risks, although the risk of neurologic complications remains. This article reviews structural and rhythm disorders of the heart that commonly lead to neurologic complications, advances in the treatment of common cardiac conditions to reduce stroke risk, and the neurologic risks associated with cardiac procedures and how to mitigate them. NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF STRUCTURAL CARDIAC DISEASE Structural heart disease can involve the valves, walls, or chambers of the heart, and may be congenital or acquired. Although structural heart disease is common, not all cardiac abnormalities confer an increased risk for ischemic stroke. Some structural heart defects are only pathogenic if the clinical context and nature of the defect carry high-risk features (eg, patent foramen ovale [PFO]). Here, we review neurologic complications of PFO, atrial septal defect, cardiomyopathy, left ventricular thrombus, mechanical circulatory devices, valvular heart disease, cardiac tumors, and endocarditis. Patent Foramen Ovale A PFO is a small opening between the left and right atria of the heart that, in the fetal circulation, allows oxygenated blood from the placenta to reach the left side of the heart, bypassing the unaerated lungs. With expansion of the lungs at birth, increased left atrial pressures, and reduced right atrial pressures from pulmonary blood ﬂow result in functional closure of the PFO, with anatomic closure occurring by adherence of the valvular ﬂaps later in infancy. The presence of a PFO may pose a risk for stroke because a venous embolus (eg, deep vein thrombosis [DVT]) may bypass the lungs and enter the arterial circulation, leading to a paradoxical embolic stroke. For this reason, when a PFO is identiﬁed on echocardiography during an evaluation for stroke etiology, it is advisable to conduct lower extremity ultrasonography to screen for DVT. In population-based studies of healthy adults without stroke, persistent PFO occurs in 15% to 24% of the population, depending on the method used to assess for the PFO. 1 The high prevalence of PFO in patients with cryptogenic stroke (approximately 40%) has led to an interest in PFO closure. 2 However, because the stroke recurrence rate among patients with PFO is low (2.3% over a 4-year period in one study), 3 and the prevalence of PFO in the general population is high, the 10-point Risk of Paradoxical Embolism (RoPE) score has been devised to determine the likelihood that the presence of a PFO is responsible for the index stroke (TABLE 2-1 4 and TABLE 2-2). This scoring system is based on the established evidence that a PFO is more likely to be associated with a 24 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. TABLE 2-2 Association Between Risk of Paradoxical Embolism (RoPE) Scores and Patent Foramen Ovale Attributable Fraction and Risk of Stroke or Transient Ischemic Attack Recurrence a,b Total RoPE score Patent foramen ovale attributable fraction (95% conﬁdence interval) 2-year recurrence (95% conﬁdence interval) 0 0% (0-4) 20% (12-28) 1 0% (0-4) 20% (12-28) 2 0% (0-4) 20% (12-28) 3 0% (0-4) 20% (12-28) 4 38% (25-48) 12% (6-18) 5 34% (21-45) 7% (3-11) 6 62% (54-68) 8% (4-12) 7 72% (66-76) 6% (2-10) 8 84% (79-87) 6% (2-10) 9 88% (83-91) 2% (0-4) 10 88% (83-91) 2% (0-4) a Modiﬁed with permission from Kent DM, et al, Neurology. 4 © 2013 American Academy of Neurology. b A higher likelihood of stroke attributable to a patent foramen ovale is associated with a lower risk of stroke or transient ischemic attack recurrence. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 25 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

paradoxical embolism in younger patients with minimal vascular risk factors and a cortically-based infarct on neuroimaging. 4 The PFO-Associated Stroke Causal Likelihood (PASCAL) classiﬁcation system incorporates the RoPE score as well as the presence of additional high-risk anatomic features of the PFO that have been identiﬁed through several PFO trials (large shunt [more than 20 to 30 bubbles] and atrial septal aneurysm). 5 This system categorizes stroke causality as “unlikely” when neither a high RoPE score (≥7) nor high- risk PFO anatomical features are present, “possible” when only one of these features is present, and “probable” when both a high RoPE score (≥7) and high-risk anatomical features are present. A “probable” classiﬁcation indicates a high likelihood that the PFO contributed to the stroke. 5 Among patients with cryptogenic stroke, several trials have examined the efﬁcacy of endovascular PFO closure to reduce the rate of recurrent stroke. Three early trials of transcatheter PFO closure did not demonstrate a beneﬁt of closure. 6-8 In contrast, three later trials that used newer devices and included patients with strokes at higher risk of being attributable to the PFO yielded positive results. 9-11 The strength of these trials compared with the three earlier negative trials was their rigorous exclusion of other stroke etiologies and inclusion of high-risk PFOs (presence of atrial septal aneurysm or large shunt). A meta-analysis of these trials conﬁrmed the reduction in stroke risk with device closure and further demonstrated that the greatest beneﬁt of closure occurred in those with a “probable” likelihood in the PASCAL classiﬁcation system. 12 Therefore, transcatheter closure (with concurrent antiplatelet therapy) should be strongly considered in stroke patients in whom (1) a paradoxical embolism through PFO is the most likely cause for stroke (high RoPE score) after exclusion of other causes (eg, large-artery atherosclerotic disease, small vessel occlusion, hypercoagulable disorder, arterial dissection) and (2) the PFO carries high-risk anatomic features, such as atrial septal aneurysm or large shunt size. 13 It should be noted that the risk of AF following PFO closure in these trials ranged from 4.6% to 6.6%, although most postprocedural AF was transient. 12 Although the clinical signiﬁcance of post-PFO closure AF is unclear and practice patterns vary, anticoagulation should generally be initiated in patients who develop AF after PFO closure. Because new-onset AF often resolves within 45 days after percutaneous PFO closure, 14 it is generally considered a low-risk entity. In one proposed algorithm for post-PFO closure AF, it is recommended that short-term oral anticoagulation be considered for early onset AF (45 days or fewer after closure). 15 However, for patients with early AF in whom conversion to sinus rhythm does not occur within 48 hours, or for those with recurrent AF, long-term anticoagulation is recommended. Late-onset AF (occurring more than 45 days after closure) is likely to represent primary AF, rather than being secondary to PFO closure, and therefore also warrants long- term anticoagulation. 15 However, the increased hemorrhagic risk associated with anticoagulation should be carefully considered, given that dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin and clopidogrel) is often used for 3 to 6 months following PFO closure. 16 Atrial Septal Defect Atrial septal defect is the third most common congenital heart defect, occurring in 2.5 of 1000 live births, a quarter of which are detected in adulthood. 17 The most common type of atrial septal defect is ostium secundum defect, followed 26 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

by ostium primum and sinus venosus defects. 18 In contrast to a PFO, which is caused by failure of fusion of the foramen ovale, an atrial septal defect is a true congenital defect in which there is absent tissue in the interatrial septum that causes biatrial ﬂow. Ostium secundum defects, which are located in the fossa ovalis, predominantly occur in females and carry the highest risk of paradoxical embolism. 18 Although data are limited, the prevalence of paradoxical embolism in one study of patients with atrial septal defect referred for closure was 14%. 19 Although patients with atrial septal defects predominantly experience left-to- right shunting, transient right-to-left shunting and paradoxical embolism may occur in patients with elevated right-atrial pressures or during transient elevations in right-atrial pressure (eg, Valsalva maneuver). 19 In contrast to PFOs, atrial septal defects are larger and are more likely to be accompanied by hemodynamic compromise. A small atrial septal defect may be difﬁcult to differentiate from a PFO by surface echocardiography and warrants additional imaging with transesophageal echocardiography. Atrial enlargement is common in patients with atrial septal defects as a result of prominent left-to- right atrial shunting, which can lead to reduced left ventricular preload and right ventricular overload. 20 Because of this atrial remodeling, patients with atrial septal defects are at increased risk of AF, which can subsequently lead to stroke. Closure of an atrial septal defect without any concurrent arrhythmia mitigation strategy (eg, Cox maze procedure) does not reduce the risk of AF. 21 In the event of paradoxical embolism attributable to an atrial septal defect, transcatheter or surgical closure of the atrial septal defect is the mainstay of therapy. Patients with atrial septal defects should be referred to a cardiologist for evaluation. Cardiomyopathy Both ischemic and nonischemic cardiomyopathy can result in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, posing an increased risk of embolism from increased ventricular stasis. Among patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and normal sinus rhythm, it remains unclear whether anticoagulation confers any beneﬁt over antiplatelet therapy in the prevention of stroke. In the WARCEF (Warfarin Versus Aspirin in Reduced Cardiac Ejection Fraction Trial) trial, 2305 patients were randomized to warfarin versus aspirin for a treatment duration of 6 years (mean duration 3.5±1.8 years). 22 Although there was no difference in the composite endpoint of ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, or death from any cause between groups, in a time-varying analysis, the beneﬁt of warfarin therapy increased over time and was statistically signiﬁcant by the fourth year of follow-up. Despite the fact it was a secondary endpoint, warfarin reduced the rate of ischemic stroke compared with aspirin without increasing intracranial hemorrhage risk. Although the rate of strokes was lower in the warfarin group compared with the aspirin group (2.5% versus 4.7%, P<.005), the overall rate of strokes in this study was low. In the COMMANDER HF (A Randomized, Double-blind, Event-driven, Multicenter Study Comparing the Efﬁcacy and Safety of Rivaroxaban With Placebo for Reducing the Risk of Death, Myocardial Infarction or Stroke in Subjects With Heart Failure and Signiﬁcant Coronary Artery Disease Following an Episode of Decompensated Heart Failure) trial, patients with ischemic cardiomyopathy and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (ejection fraction 40% or less) were randomized to low-dose rivaroxaban (2.5 mg twice a CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 27 KEY POINTS •Stroke is the most common neurologic complication of cardiac disorders. •PFO-Associated Stroke Causal Likelihood (PASCAL) and Risk of Paradoxical Embolism (RoPE) scores should be used when considering patent foramen ovale closure. •Three randomized controlled trials demonstrated a beneﬁt of patent foramen ovale closure in selected patients. •Atrial septal defects can cause stroke through transient right-left shunting and paradoxical embolism. •Anticoagulation may be considered in patients who have had strokes and have heart failure and reduced ejection fraction. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

day) plus antiplatelet therapy versus placebo plus antiplatelet therapy. 23 Rivaroxaban did not reduce the composite endpoint of stroke, myocardial infarction, or death after a median follow-up period of 21.1 months. The stroke rate was low overall, with no signiﬁcant differences between groups (2% in the rivaroxaban group versus 3% in the placebo group). Furthermore, the rate of hemorrhages into a critical site (including intracerebral hemorrhage) was not different between groups. Because this study and WARCEF were predominantly primary prevention studies with low stroke rates and composite endpoints, it remains unclear whether patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and normal sinus rhythm who develop a stroke should be anticoagulated for secondary prevention. In a 2021 post-hoc analysis of a study that aimed to determine whether anticoagulation with rivaroxaban was superior to aspirin in reducing the risk of recurrent stroke and systemic embolism in patients with embolic source of undetermined source, patients with left ventricular dysfunction (deﬁned as moderate to severely impaired global left ventricular contractility, any regional wall motion abnormality, or left ventricular ejection fraction less than 40%) who were randomized to rivaroxaban had a lower risk of recurrent stroke compared with those who received aspirin. 24 In light of these studies, the decision to initiate anticoagulation versus antiplatelet therapy in patients with reduced ejection fraction should be individualized after weighing the hemorrhagic risk and recurrent stroke risk. 13 CARDIAC ENCEPHALOPATHY. In addition to stroke, patients with heart failure are at risk of cardiac encephalopathy, which has been characterized by a decreased level of alertness, asterixis, and diffuse slowing on EEG, thought to be caused by increased venous pressure, decreased perfusion, and possibly concurrent renal and hepatic dysfunction associated with heart failure. 25 In addition to cardiac encephalopathy, patients with congestive heart failure are also at risk of cognitive impairment and dementia, likely caused by chronic microembolism and hypoperfusion. 26 The risk of cognitive impairment in patients with congestive heart failure is inﬂuenced by the patient’s age and the extent of left ventricular dysfunction. Left Ventricular Thrombus In patients with a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction who have experienced a stroke or transient ischemic attack (TIA), there should be a high suspicion for an intracardiac thrombus, particularly in patients with apical wall motion abnormalities detected on echocardiography (as illustrated in CASE 2-1). These patients are at increased risk of thrombus formation because of the Virchow triad: stasis from reduced ventricular function, endocardial injury, and inﬂammation and hypercoagulability. Intracardiac thrombi are diagnosed by transthoracic echocardiography with ultrasound-enhancing agents (echogenic gas-ﬁlled microbubbles with a lipid or albumin shell that provides increased contrast resolution), which may reveal a ﬁlling defect suggestive of a thrombus. 27 In patients in whom there is a high suspicion for left ventricular thrombus despite an equivocal or negative transthoracic echocardiogram, cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement may be considered. Although transesophageal echocardiography is commonly performed if transthoracic echocardiography ﬁndings are equivocal, it does not offer additional value 28 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

in detecting left ventricular thrombus because of its limited ability to visualize the left ventricular apex. In one study, cardiac MRI had a sensitivity of 88% and a speciﬁcity of 99% for detecting left ventricular thrombus, compared with 40% sensitivity and 96% speciﬁcity of transesophageal echocardiography, and 23% sensitivity and 96% speciﬁcity of transthoracic echocardiography. 28 If a left ventricular thrombus is detected, anticoagulation with warfarin is recommended for a minimum of 3 months. 13,29 After 3 months, it is reasonable to repeat cardiac imaging to assess for the presence of residual thrombus. Anticoagulation may be discontinued if no thrombus is identiﬁed or continued if the thrombus persists. Although large, well-powered randomized controlled trials are lacking, meta-analyses of smaller studies suggest that direct oral anticoagulants may be a reasonable alternative to warfarin for treating left ventricular thrombus. 29,30 Mechanical Circulatory Assist Devices Mechanical circulatory assist devices, such as left ventricular assist devices (LVADs), are commonly used in patients with severe heart failure as a bridge to transplantation, recovery from heart failure, decision-making regarding the long-term treatment strategy for advanced heart failure, or as destination therapy (permanent LVAD placement in nontransplant candidates). Most LVADs are continuous-ﬂow LVADs, which are rotary pumps that use electromagnetic and mechanical forces to pump blood into the aorta. Because contact between blood and mechanical devices promotes clotting through platelet adhesion, activation, aggregation, and coagulation pathway activation, patients with LVADs should remain on anticoagulation with warfarin (with or without antiplatelet therapy depending on the type of LVAD), aiming for an international normalized ratio (INR) of 2 to 3. 31 There is limited evidence supporting direct oral anticoagulants as an alternative to warfarin; however, a small single-center feasibility study suggested that warfarin and apixaban may offer similar efﬁcacy. 32 Valvular Heart Disease Valvular heart disease refers to any structural or functional abnormalities of the cardiac valves and affects an estimated 2.5% of adults in the United States. 33 Although many valvular heart diseases exist, those conferring the greatest risk of ischemic stroke are aortic stenosis and mitral stenosis. In addition to the embolic risk from the valvular lesions themselves, aortic and mitral valve disorders may cause atrial remodeling leading to AF, which is a known risk factor for stroke. For severe valvular heart disease, valve replacement with either a bioprosthetic or mechanical valve is the mainstay of therapy. Although modern mechanical valves confer a reduced risk of thromboembolism compared with their predecessors, mechanical valves still carry a signiﬁcant risk of stroke. This risk is attributable to a combination of blood ﬂow stagnation, platelet activation from shearing forces, and clotting activation promoted by the surface of the valve. Therefore, patients with mechanical heart valves warrant indeﬁnite anticoagulation with warfarin. In this section, we highlight the current recommendations for mitigating stroke risk in patients with various valvular abnormalities (summarized in TABLE 2-3). CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 29 KEY POINTS •Left ventricular thrombus should be suspected in patients who have had strokes and who have low ejection fraction. •Patients with left ventricular thrombus should receive anticoagulation as early as safely possible. •Patients on mechanical assist devices often require anticoagulation with warfarin. •Patients with bioprosthetic valves without atrial ﬁbrillation (AF) should be on long- term antiplatelet therapy. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

NATIVE VALVULAR HEART DISEASE OR BIOPROSTHETIC VALVES WITHOUT ATRIAL FIBRILLATION. In the absence of AF, the risk of stroke is not deﬁnitively higher in patients with nonrheumatic mitral valve disease or native aortic valve disease. 29 Therefore, antiplatelet therapy (rather than anticoagulation) is recommended for patients who have experienced a stroke or a TIA. 13 In patients with or without prior stroke undergoing surgical bioprosthetic valve replacement (aortic valve or mitral valve) who are at low risk of bleeding, anticoagulation with warfarin to achieve an INR of 2.5 is recommended for 3 to 6 months, after which time single antiplatelet therapy can be used. 34 In patients with or without CASE 2-1 A 68-year-old woman with hypertension and diabetes presented to the emergency department with palpitations. She was found to have new- onset atrial ﬁbrillation with rapid ventricular response (FIGURE 2-1A) and was admitted to the cardiology service, where she was started on apixaban (CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc score 4). A transthoracic echocardiogram revealed moderate left atrial enlargement, severe biventricular systolic dysfunction with an ejection fraction of 20% (FIGURE 2-1B), and moderate to severe mitral regurgitation. Despite escalating doses of beta-blocker therapy and digoxin, she remained in atrial ﬁbrillation with rapid ventricular response. The decision was made to pursue direct current cardioversion, for which she underwent a transesophageal echocardiography to rule out an intracardiac thrombus. The transesophageal echocardiogram revealed a large left atrial appendage thrombus with minimal mobility (FIGURE 2-1C). She was started on a heparin infusion, but shortly afterward, she was found to have nonﬂuent aphasia, right hemiplegia, and left gaze deviation (National Institutes of Health [NIH] Stroke Scale score 17). A noncontrast CT scan did not show any evidence of acute hemorrhage, and thrombolysis was deferred because of concurrent anticoagulation. CT angiography demonstrated a terminal internal carotid artery occlusion extending into the left middle cerebral artery (FIGURE 2-1D). She underwent urgent endovascular therapy, which conﬁrmed the left terminal internal carotid artery occlusion (FIGURE 2-1E). Following one pass with a stent retriever, there was successful recanalization of the left middle cerebral artery (FIGURE 2-1F). A brain MRI was performed after the procedure and revealed a large left middle cerebral artery infarct (FIGURE 2-1G). COMMENT This patient experienced a stroke despite direct oral anticoagulation therapy, which may not have been sufﬁcient to treat the intracardiac thrombus. An intracardiac thrombus should be suspected in patients who develop ischemic stroke in the context of heart failure with a signiﬁcantly reduced ejection fraction. 30 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

stroke undergoing transcatheter aortic valve replacement, dual antiplatelet therapy (with aspirin and clopidogrel) or warfarin with a goal of INR 2.5 can be used for at least 3 months. 34 NATIVE VALVULAR HEART DISEASE OR BIOPROSTHETIC VALVES WITH ATRIAL FIBRILLATION. Atrial tachyarrhythmias are common in patients with valvular heart disease and increase the risk of ischemic stroke. In patients with native aortic valve disease or nonrheumatic mitral stenosis and AF who have a stroke, direct oral anticoagulants are recommended as the ﬁrst-line therapy. 35 If these FIGURE 2-1 Findings of the patient in CASE 2-1. A, 12-lead ECG showing atrial ﬁbrillation with rapid ventricular response. B, Transthoracic echocardiogram of severe biventricular systolic dysfunction with moderate left atrial enlargement. C, Transesophageal echocardiogram demonstrating a large thrombus (1.2 cm [.47 in] × 0.7 cm [.28 in]) within the left atrial appendage (circle). D, Axial head CT angiogram demonstrating terminal internal carotid artery occlusion extending into the left middle cerebral artery (arrow). E, Digital subtraction angiogram indicating terminal internal carotid artery occlusion (arrow). F, Digital subtraction angiogram indicating recanalization of the internal carotid artery after one stent retriever pass. G, Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) brain MRI showing left middle cerebral artery distribution stroke. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 31 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

patients undergo bioprosthetic valve replacement, warfarin therapy (with an INR of 2.5) should be administered for 3 to 6 months, followed by long-term direct oral anticoagulant therapy. If patients with bioprosthetic valves experience thromboembolic events such as stroke, consideration should be given to switching to warfarin. In patients with AF and concomitant moderate- to-severe mitral valve stenosis (valvular AF), warfarin is the mainstay of therapy and should be continued before and after bioprosthetic valve replacement. Because patients with valvular AF were excluded from direct oral anticoagulant trials, the safety and efﬁcacy of direct oral anticoagulants in this population remain unknown. VALVULAR HEART DISEASE WITH MECHANICAL VALVE REPLACEMENT. Given the increased stroke risk in patients with mechanical valves (with or without AF), warfarin is the primary antithrombotic strategy for these patients. 34 Dabigatran was tested as an alternative to warfarin in patients with mechanical valves, which demonstrated an increase in both thromboembolic events and bleeding events in patients receiving dabigatran compared with patients receiving warfarin. 36 The target INR goal in patients with mechanical valves is often dependent on the type of valve replacement and the presence of additional risk factors. Among patients with a mechanical aortic valve replacement, warfarin with a goal INR of 2.5 (range 2 to 3) is recommended. 34 In the presence of additional thromboembolic risk factors such as stroke, anticoagulation can be intensiﬁed (increasing the INR goal to 3), or aspirin can be added to warfarin. 13 Among patients with mechanical mitral valves, an INR target of 3 should be targeted (range 2.5 to 3.5). In patients with mechanical mitral valves or mechanical aortic valves with additional thromboembolic risk factors, bridging with heparin is recommended if the INR is subtherapeutic. 34 If patients with mechanical valves experience subsequent thromboembolic TABLE 2-3 General Recommendations For Long-Term Secondary Stroke Prevention Antithrombotic Therapy In Patients With Structural Cardiac Disorders Structural heart disease No concomitant atrial ﬁbrillation Concomitant atrial ﬁbrillation Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction Antiplatelet therapy, direct oral anticoagulant, or warfarin Direct oral anticoagulant or warfarin Left ventricular thrombus Warfarin Warfarin Left ventricular assist devices Warfarin with or without antiplatelet Warfarin with or without antiplatelet therapy Native aortic or nonrheumatic mitral valve stenosis Antiplatelet therapy Direct oral anticoagulant Moderate to severe mitral stenosis Antiplatelet therapy Warfarin Bioprosthetic aortic or mitral valve replacement Antiplatelet therapy Direct oral anticoagulant or warfarin Mechanical aortic valve or mitral valve replacement Warfarin with or without antiplatelet therapy Warfarin with or without antiplatelet therapy 32 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

events, including stroke, further intensiﬁcation of warfarin therapy or the addition of aspirin should be considered. Cardiac Tumors Primary cardiac tumors originate from the pericardium or myocardium. These tumors are rare, with an estimated prevalence of 0.03% in autopsy studies. 37 Although most primary cardiac tumors are benign, they may lead to thromboembolism, resulting in ischemic stroke. Cardiac tumors include myxomas (most common), lipomas, papillary ﬁbroelastomas, hemangiomas, and paragangliomas. Because they are commonly found in the interatrial septum of the left atrium, myxomas may cause cerebral embolism. A thrombus can form on the surface of the myxoma and embolize, or fragments of the myxoma can embolize. The former observation has led to the suggestion of anticoagulation as a potential treatment strategy. In addition, small studies have shown a reduction in stroke risk with surgical resection of left-sided myxomas. 38 Right-sided myxomas without concomitant right-to-left shunting (eg, a PFO) do not pose a risk of stroke, and so if they do not cause cardiac dysfunction, they may be monitored with serial imaging. Other neurologic complications that have been attributed to myxomas include intraparenchymal hemorrhage (caused by rupture of small vessels weakened from embolized tumor deposition), subarachnoid hemorrhage (due to myxomatous embolization to an arterial wall leading to aneurysm formation and rupture), syncope (caused by left ventricular outﬂow obstruction or decreased right ventricular ﬁlling), seizures (secondary to cerebral metastasis of the myxoma), altered mental status, and spinal cord embolism. Papillary ﬁbroelastoma is a rare, benign, primary cardiac tumor typically affecting the aortic or mitral valve. Similar to myxomas, papillary ﬁbroelastomas can result in emboli to the brain, leading to stroke or TIA. In these situations, the mainstay of treatment is surgical resection. Although transthoracic echocardiography carries a high sensitivity (89%) for detecting large papillary ﬁbroelastomas (greater than 2 mm), its sensitivity signiﬁcantly decreases for smaller tumors (less than 2 mm), dropping to 62%. 39 If the clinical suspicion for a papillary ﬁbroelastoma is high (eg, in patients with recurrent embolic stroke of undetermined source), transesophageal echocardiography may be considered. 39 Endocarditis Infective endocarditis is an infection of the heart valves that affects 3 to 10 per 100,000 people annually in the United States. 40 Neurologic complications of infective endocarditis are due to septic emboli, which can cause ischemic stroke, mycotic aneurysm (with resultant rupture and intracranial hemorrhage), meningitis, and abscess (FIGURE 2-2 41 ). 42 The most common neurologic complication of infective endocarditis is septic embolism, 43 which can lead to ischemic strokes that may be symptomatic or asymptomatic. Risk factors for embolization include left-sided valvular disease (ie, aortic or mitral valve), Staphylococcus aureus infection, and vegetation size greater than 10 mm. 44 Endocarditis should be considered in patients with ischemic stroke and positive blood cultures, as well as in patients with intracerebral hemorrhage CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 33 KEY POINTS •Valvular AF includes AF that occurs with mechanical valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. •Patients with mechanical heart valves, regardless of AF, should receive warfarin therapy. •Cardiac myxoma is a rare cause of cardioembolic stroke. •The most common neurologic consequence of infective endocarditis is septic embolism. •Risk factors for septic embolism in endocarditis include left-sided endocarditis, Staphylococcus aureus infection, and large vegetation size. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

associated with distal aneurysms. In addition, patients who use injection drugs or who have prosthetic valves or cardiac implantable electronic devices are at high risk of developing infective endocarditis. To diagnose endocarditis, transthoracic echocardiography should ﬁrst be performed in all suspected patients. Because transthoracic echocardiography carries a 75% sensitivity for detecting a vegetation, there should be a low threshold to perform transesophageal echocardiography if clinical suspicion is high and transthoracic echocardiogram is normal because transesophageal echocardiography carries a sensitivity of 87% to 100% and speciﬁcity of 87% to 100% for native valve disease, although it may be lower in patients with prosthetic valves or cardiac implantable electronic devices. 45 In such cases, adjunctive imaging with multidetector CT or cardiac positron emission tomography (PET) may provide added speciﬁcity. The cornerstone of endocarditis treatment and prevention of neurologic complications is early initiation of antibiotic therapy, which reduces the risk of embolism. 46 In patients in whom endocarditis results in severe valvular dysfunction (eg, aortic root abscess, severe regurgitation), severe heart failure, recurrent systemic embolization, or persistent bacteremia despite FIGURE 2-2 Neuroimaging spectrum of infective endocarditis in different patients. A, Axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) sequence indicating right middle cerebral artery territory stroke. B, Axial DWI showing cortical and subcortical septic emboli (arrows). C, Axial susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) showing sulcal hypointensities indicating subarachnoid hemorrhage. D, Axial head CT demonstrating a large right-sided intraparenchymal hemorrhage. E, Coronal head CT revealing a basilar tip mycotic aneurysm (inset, arrow). F, Digital subtraction angiogram demonstrating a basilar tip mycotic aneurysm (inset, arrow). Reprinted with permission from Das AS, et al, J Neuroimaging Off J Am Soc Neuroimaging. 41 © 2022 American Society of Neuroimaging. 34 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

antimicrobial therapy, cardiac surgery is often warranted. In patients with large strokes or intracerebral hemorrhage, cardiac surgery should generally be delayed by at least 4 weeks when the cardiac functional status allows. 46 There is no strong evidence that supports delaying cardiac surgery in patients with small or asymptomatic strokes. However, the timing of cardiac surgery in patients with endocarditis and neurologic complications should weigh the risk of hemorrhagic conversion from anticoagulation administered during surgery against the risks of ongoing valvular sequelae (eg, heart failure, continued embolization) that led to the need for surgery. This decision should be made through shared decision-making with a multidisciplinary team that includes neurologists and cardiac surgeons. Antiplatelet therapy and anticoagulation are not recommended in patients with endocarditis (whether for primary or secondary stroke prevention), given the high rate of hemorrhagic events (eg, hemorrhagic transformation of ischemic stroke, mycotic aneurysm rupture). 47 In patients with a strong indication for antiplatelet therapy (eg, recent cardiac stent), it may be reasonable to continue antiplatelet therapy. 46 Even in patients with mechanical valves, guideline recommendations suggest discontinuing all forms of anticoagulation for atleast 2 weeksin patientswho have had a stroke secondary to endocarditis. 46 Thrombolysis for acute ischemic stroke is not recommended in patients with infective endocarditis because of the possibility of hemorrhagic conversion of septic emboli or rupture of an occult mycotic aneurysm. Mycotic aneurysms are a rare yet potentially fatal complication of infective endocarditis. Current recommendations strongly suggest neuroimaging to detect intracranial mycotic aneurysms or hemorrhage in patients with endocarditis and severe headaches, neurologic deﬁcits, or meningeal signs. 46 Based on small studies showing that routine CT angiography to detect intracranial mycotic aneurysms changed management in a signiﬁcant number of patients (eg, prompted referral for endovascular or surgical treatment of an identiﬁed aneurysm, changed antithrombotic therapy after a mycotic aneurysm was detected, or altered the type of cardiac surgery based on a detected aneurysm), 48,49 guidelines recommend considering cerebrovascular imaging in patients with left-sided endocarditis even if there are no neurologic symptoms or signs. 46 Because mycotic aneurysms are typically small and distal, they often escape detection by CT angiography or MR angiography (as illustrated in CASE 2-2); however, it is unclear whether digital subtraction angiography should be performed in all cases of endocarditis. One meta-analysis using digital subtraction angiography as the reference standard showed that CT angiography and MR angiography had pooled sensitivities of 0.71 (95% conﬁdence interval, 0.54 to 0.85) and speciﬁcities of 0.90 (95% conﬁdence interval, 0.83 to 0.95) for detecting cerebral mycotic aneurysms. 50 Observational studies suggest that digital subtraction angiography should not be routinely performed in all patients, unless lateralizing neurologic deﬁcits are present or an intracranial hemorrhage is identiﬁed on noninvasive imaging. 51,52 In these small studies, it appeared that the incidence of mycotic aneurysms was very low in patients without intracerebral hemorrhage or subarachnoid hemorrhage (negative predictive value of approximately 97%). Because a randomized controlled trial comparing digital subtraction angiography with CT angiography in patients with infective endocarditis and CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 35 KEY POINTS •Antimicrobial therapy is the mainstay of treatment in patients with infective endocarditis. •Prophylactic antithrombotic therapy is not recommended to treat or prevent stroke in patients with infective endocarditis. •Digital subtraction angiography or CT angiography should be performed in patients with endocarditis and severe headaches, neurologic deﬁcits, or meningeal signs to detect mycotic aneurysms. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

neurologic symptoms is unlikely to be performed, it remains uncertain whether the risks of the procedure outweigh its potential beneﬁts in this context. However, because the presence of mycotic aneurysm adds a high risk of major neurologic deterioration from rupture and hemorrhage, there should be a low threshold to perform digital subtraction angiography in patients with infective endocarditis and neurologic symptoms, even if prior CT angiography does not reveal mycotic aneurysms. Acknowledging that practice patterns vary, the authors’ practice is to perform digital subtraction angiography in any patient undergoing cardiac surgery with evidence of septic embolism by clinical signs CASE 2-2 A 39-year-old man with a history of IV drug use and infective endocarditis requiring a mechanical aortic valve replacement and who was on warfarin therapy presented to the emergency department with fever, nausea, and vomiting. Initial laboratory evaluation was notable for an elevated white blood cell count of 25.68 K/μL and an international normalized ratio (INR) of 1.7.
