He was initiated on a heparin drip and broad-spectrum antibiotics. Given the concern for recurrent endocarditis from active drug use, transthoracic echocardiography was performed, but it was negative for any vegetations. However, 2 days after his admission to the hospital, he developed right-sided hemiparesis. A head CT demonstrated a left frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage (FIGURE 2-3A), and a CT angiogram was negative for any underlying vascular lesion. Because of the strong clinical suspicion for endocarditis, a conventional angiogram was pursued, which revealed a mycotic aneurysm at a distal branch of the middle cerebral artery (FIGURE 2-3B). The aneurysm was treated with a liquid embolic agent, n-butyl cyanoacrylate. Because of the size of the hemorrhage and prominent midline shift, the patient subsequently underwent a craniectomy with evacuation of the hematoma. Postoperatively, his blood cultures identiﬁed methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus, for which his antibiotics were narrowed to nafcillin. Additional blood cultures remained negative, and repeat CT angiography performed 5 days later did not show any new mycotic aneurysms. Transesophageal echocardiography was performed and revealed a posterior periannular abscess (FIGURE 2-3C). Ten days into his hospitalization, he experienced a respiratory arrest requiring intubation. A repeat CT scan was notable for intraventricular hemorrhage (FIGURE 2-3D), and a CT angiogram demonstrated a new mycotic aneurysm in the left posterior cerebral artery (FIGURE 2-3E), which was conﬁrmed on three-dimensional rotational angiography (FIGURE 2-3F). Although he underwent coiling of this aneurysm and external ventricular drain placement, his condition continued to get worse, and he was transitioned to comfort measures. COMMENT CT angiography may not be adequately sensitive to detect mycotic aneurysms. In patients with intracerebral hemorrhage and a high suspicion for endocarditis, digital subtraction angiography should be performed even if the initial CT angiography study is unremarkable. 36 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

and symptoms or by imaging. Imaging should be performed in this context even if blood cultures are no longer positive because mycotic aneurysms may develop even after the resolution of bacteremia (as illustrated in CASE 2-2). Although surgical clipping is still widely used for treating intracranial mycotic aneurysms, endovascular neurosurgical treatment has emerged as the ﬁrst-line strategy because it is associated with lower mortality, increased ability to access distal aneurysms, and lower hemorrhagic complications. Securing the aneurysm endovascularly may reduce the risk of hemorrhage related to systemic anticoagulation required during cardiac surgery. However, if the aneurysm is FIGURE 2-3 Imaging ﬁndings of the patient in CASE 2-2. A, Axial head CT revealing a left frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage. B, Digital subtraction angiogram demonstrating a left middle cerebral artery branch mycotic aneurysm (arrow). C, Transesophageal echocardiogram indicating a posterior perivalvular abscess (circle). D, Axial head CT revealing intraventricular hemorrhage (arrow). E, Coronal head CT angiogram demonstrating a new left posterior cerebral artery aneurysm (arrow). F, Three-dimensional rotational angiogram conﬁrming a left posterior cerebral artery aneurysm (circle). CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 37 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

treated surgically (via clipping), cardiac surgery may need to be delayed, given the risk of anticoagulation in the immediate postcraniotomy period. Although the management of unruptured mycotic aneurysms is not entirely deﬁned, in patients who do not need cardiac surgery, mycotic aneurysms may be managed conservatively with antibiotics alone and serial imaging (the authors repeat imaging weekly), especially if the mycotic aneurysm is small. In these patients, any mycotic aneurysms that increase in size or persist on repeat imaging should be strongly considered for neurosurgical treatment. 53 In patients with unruptured mycotic aneurysms who need cardiac surgery, neurosurgical treatment should be performed before the cardiac surgery. In patients with small aneurysms that are managed conservatively and resolve based on CT angiography, it is the authors’ practice to perform digital subtraction angiography before cardiac surgery. If any aneurysms are identiﬁed, they should be treated before cardiac surgery. In patients with ruptured mycotic aneurysms who are too unstable to undergo neurosurgical treatment, conservative management with antibiotics alone may be appropriate. Otherwise, ruptured mycotic aneurysms should generally be treated endovascularly or surgically. NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC RHYTHM DISORDERS Disorders of cardiac rate and rhythm are common, and in one population- based study, they occurred at an incidence rate of 0.5% per year. 54 Atrial arrhythmias are associated with an increased risk of ischemic stroke, whereas ventricular arrhythmias can lead to cardiac arrest. Several advances in the TABLE 2-4 CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc Score for Atrial Fibrillation a Risk factor Points Congestive heart failure 1 Hypertension 1 Age Younger than 65 years 0 65-74 years 1 75 years or older 2 Diabetes 1 History of stroke, transient ischemic attack, or thromboembolism 2 Vascular disease b 1 Sex Female 1 Male 0 a Modiﬁed with permission from Lip GYH, et al, Chest. 58 © 2010 American College of Chest Physicians. b Vascular disease includes peripheral artery disease, aortic plaque, or prior myocardial infarction. 38 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

treatment of cardiac arrhythmias have been developed over the past several decades to address this signiﬁcant health care burden. Atrial Fibrillation AF is the most common clinically signiﬁcant arrhythmia and is a risk factor for ischemic stroke. In the United States, at least 3 to 6 million people are affected by AF, with projections suggesting that the number could increase to approximately 6 to 16 million by 2050. 55 Age is one of the strongest risk factors for AF, with up to 30% of individuals older than 84 years affected by this condition. 56 AF is an irregular supraventricular tachyarrhythmia characterized by ineffective contraction of the atria. Because of the loss of the atrial kick that occurs with coordinated atrial contraction, increased stasis can occur in the left atrial appendage, which may lead to thrombus formation and subsequent stroke. Although the risk conferred by AF depends on accompanying risk factors, in general, AF increases the risk of stroke up to ﬁvefold, and valvular AF (ie, AF with moderate-severe mitral stenosis) increases the risk 20-fold. 57 The CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc (congestive heart failure, hypertension, age ≥ 75 years, diabetes, stroke, vascular disease, age 65–74 years, sex category) score (TABLE 2-4 58 and TABLE 2-5 59 ) is a risk prediction model used to estimate the risk of stroke in patients with AF based on age, sex, presence of hypertension, diabetes, congestive heart failure, vascular disease history (including prior myocardial infarction, peripheral artery disease, or aortic plaque), and history of stroke, TIA, or thromboembolism. 60 TABLE 2-5 CHA2 DS 2 -Vasc Score, Corresponding Stroke Risk, and General Antithrombotic Recommendation a Score Stroke risk b Antithrombotic therapy 0 0.2% None 1 0.6% Antiplatelet or oral anticoagulation 2 2.2% Oral anticoagulation (men), antiplatelet or oral anticoagulation (women) 3 3.2% Oral anticoagulation 4 4.8% Oral anticoagulation 5 7.2% Oral anticoagulation 6 9.7% Oral anticoagulation 7 11.2% Oral anticoagulation 8 10.8% Oral anticoagulation 9 12.2% Oral anticoagulation a Modiﬁed with permission from Friberg L, et al, Eur Heart J. 59 © 2012 The Author. b The stroke risk is per 100 patient-years, unadjusted for aspirin use, and reﬂects the risk of stroke without warfarin. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 39 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

To reduce the risk of AF-related stroke, long-term oral anticoagulation is the mainstay of therapy in patients with a high risk of stroke, deﬁned as a CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc score greater than 1 in men and greater than 2 in women. All patients with nonvalvular AF and a history of stroke or TIA are at high risk for recurrent cardioembolic stroke and should be on anticoagulation therapy unless there is a compelling hemorrhagic contraindication. Direct oral anticoagulants have replaced warfarin as the preferred initial therapy for thromboembolism prevention in patients with nonvalvular AF because of the improved safety proﬁle of direct oral anticoagulants compared with warfarin (decreased risk of intracranial and systemic hemorrhage), fewer drug interactions, and increased adherence to therapy (likely because INR monitoring is not needed). In a meta-analysis of randomized controlled direct oral anticoagulant trials, dabigatran, apixaban, rivaroxaban, and edoxaban all demonstrated a reduced risk of intracerebral hemorrhage compared with warfarin. 61 Warfarin is used in patients with valvular AF (AF in patients with moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valve), and warfarin may also be considered in nonvalvular AF if a direct oral anticoagulant is not tolerated or not affordable. Ongoing trials are currently investigating whether factor XI inhibitors in patients with AF offer a safer alternative to direct oral anticoagulants with lower hemorrhagic risk. 62 After a cryptogenic ischemic stroke, long-term rhythm monitoring for AF should be performed with either outpatient mobile cardiac telemetry or an implantable loop recorder. 13 Although the duration for which monitoring should be performed is unclear, studies that have used extended monitoring strategies have detected more AF. In the CRYSTAL-AF (CRYptogenic STroke And underLying AF Trial) study, which randomized patients who had cryptogenic stroke to long-term monitoring with an insertable cardiac monitor compared with conventional follow-up, AF was detected in 8.9% of patients in the insertable cardiac monitor group compared with 1.4% of patients in the control group after 6 months. 63 Nearly all of the patients found to have AF were initiated on anticoagulation. Although fewer patients had a recurrent stroke or TIA in the insertable cardiac monitor group, this study was not adequately powered or designed to address the efﬁcacy of anticoagulation in patients with device-detected AF. Although no randomized controlled trial has demonstrated any improvement in clinical outcomes with extended monitoring after stroke or TIA, the American College of Cardiology recommends a minimum of 14 days of cardiac monitoring in patients with ischemic stroke without a clear etiology. 64 Although the minimal duration of AF that warrants anticoagulation is controversial, the ARTESIA (Apixaban for the Reduction of Thrombo- Embolism in Patients With Device-Detected Sub-Clinical Atrial Fibrillation) study demonstrated a reduction in stroke or systemic embolism with apixaban usage in patients with device-detected AF lasting from 6 minutes to 24 hours. 65 Among patients in this study with a history of stroke or TIA, apixaban therapy led to a 7% absolute risk reduction in stroke or systemic embolism after 3.5 years of follow-up. 66 Although the efﬁcacy of anticoagulation in patients with low AF burden (fewer than 6 minutes) remains unclear, this study underscores the beneﬁt of anticoagulation for device-detected AF after an ischemic stroke or TIA. AF detected after an ischemic stroke may carry a lower risk of recurrent stroke compared with AF detected at the time of stroke. 67 However, the implications of this ﬁnding are unclear, and current practice is generally to 40 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

initiate anticoagulation in patients with ischemic stroke or TIA who have any duration of AF detected on heart rhythm monitoring, regardless of when the AF is detected, unless there are contraindications to anticoagulation. In patients with AF, most thrombus formation occurs in the left atrial appendage, the primordial remnant of the embryonic left atrium. The left atrial appendage has a complex internal structure with several prominent trabeculations that promote thrombus formation. Considering its embolic potential, endovascular closure of the left atrial appendage has emerged as a potential stroke reduction strategy in patients with AF who cannot tolerate long-term anticoagulation (eg, gastrointestinal bleeding, intracerebral hemorrhage). Two catheter-based closure devices are approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for stroke prevention in AF. 68,69 Although the scope of use of these devices may expand as new trial data become available, currently, they should be considered in patients with AF who have a stroke or TIA but have a contraindication to long-term anticoagulation. 13 Although further study is needed, left atrial appendage closure may be considered in patients with AF and cerebral amyloid angiopathy, a cerebral small vessel disease that carries an increased risk of intracerebral hemorrhage. In the event of recurrent stroke despite oral anticoagulation for AF, the optimal management strategy is undeﬁned. However, patients with AF who develop recurrent stroke despite anticoagulation should be thoroughly evaluated for alternative etiologies, such as hypercoagulability or large artery atherosclerosis, which may warrant different treatment strategies. In patients with recurrent cardioembolic stroke on oral anticoagulation, observational studies have not demonstrated a clear advantage of switching between direct oral anticoagulants or from a direct oral anticoagulant to warfarin, but have shown some beneﬁt to performing adjunctive left atrial appendage occlusion. 70-73 The ongoing LAAOS-4 study (The Fourth Left Atrial Appendage Occlusion Study) 74 will provide further insight into this question, testing the safety and efﬁcacy of left atrial appendage closure in patients with AF who remain at high risk of stroke despite receiving anticoagulant therapy (CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc score of 4 or greater and a history of ischemic stroke or systemic embolism). Although randomized controlled trial data are lacking, it appears that restoration of normal sinus rhythm in patients with AF through either catheter ablation or antiarrhythmic medications does not fully eliminate the risk of cardioembolic stroke. 75 However, the EAST-AFNET 4 trial (Early Therapy of Atrial Fibrillation for Stroke Prevention Trial) demonstrated that early rhythm control (within 1 year of diagnosis) with catheter ablation or antiarrhythmic medications in addition to anticoagulation reduced the composite outcome of death from cardiovascular causes, stroke, or hospitalization for heart failure or acute coronary syndrome compared with usual care (anticoagulation and rate- control therapy with rhythm-control therapy reserved for only the treatment of AF-related symptoms). 76 This study also demonstrated a lower stroke rate in the early rhythm control group compared with usual care, suggesting added beneﬁt for rhythm control in addition to anticoagulation in patients with AF. Because rhythm control strategies to mitigate AF burden are complex and involve a variety of factors, including age, presence of heart failure, concomitant cardiac symptoms (eg, palpitations, shortness of breath, and fatigue), quality of life, and patient preferences, patients with AF-associated CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 41 KEY POINTS •CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc is a risk prediction model used to estimate the risk of stroke in patients with AF. •Direct oral anticoagulants have replaced warfarin as the preferred initial therapy for stroke prevention in patients with AF. •Most thrombus formation in patients with AF occurs in the left atrial appendage. •Percutaneous left atrial appendage closure may be considered in patients with AF who have a contraindication to long- term oral anticoagulation. •Patients with newly diagnosed AF should be referred to a cardiologist for consideration of early rhythm control. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

stroke should be cared for by a multidisciplinary team that includes neurologists, general cardiologists, and cardiac electrophysiologists. ATRIAL FIBRILLATION AND DEMENTIA. Over the past decade, AF has emerged as an independent risk factor for dementia, even among patients without any clinical history of stroke or TIA. 77 Although this remains an active area of investigation, it is likely that covert embolism and cerebral hypoperfusion are contributing factors. The BRAIN-AF study (Blinded Randomized Trial of Anticoagulation to Prevent Ischemic Stroke and Neurocognitive Impairment in AF) evaluated whether the composite primary outcome of stroke, TIA, or cognitive decline was reduced in low-risk patients with AF who received rivaroxaban or placebo. 78 The results of this study will provide insight into whether microembolization mediates cognitive impairment in patients with AF. To determine whether hypoperfusion contributes to cognitive impairment, studies have explored reducing AF burden, which is theorized to improve perfusion. One observational study compared patients with AF who underwent catheter ablation with age- and sex-matched controls who did not undergo ablation and found a lower risk of Alzheimer disease in the ablation group (0.2% versus 0.9%, P<.0001). 79 Although these results are encouraging, it remains uncertain whether cardiac ablation or anticoagulation reduces the risk of cognitive impairment in patients with AF. Atrial Flutter Atrial ﬂutter also represents a signiﬁcant risk factor for stroke and is the second most common clinically signiﬁcant cardiac arrhythmia after AF. Atrial ﬂutter is typically caused by a macroreentrant circuit around the tricuspid annulus. The hallmark feature on ECG is “sawtooth” ﬂutter waves in the inferior leads, which correspond to atrial rates of 250 to 350 beats per minute. One population-based study estimated the incidence of atrial ﬂutter to be 200,000 patients annually in the United States and also demonstrated an increased incidence in men and in those with heart failure or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. 80 Atrial ﬂutter and AF often coexist, with 80% of patients with atrial ﬂutter also experiencing AF. 81 Because of the loss of the atrial kick, atrial ﬂutter poses an increased risk for stroke. Therefore, patients with atrial ﬂutter should be offered oral anticoagulation as in patients with AF. Although catheter ablation is very effective in controlling atrial ﬂutter, the occurrence of postablation AF is high, with one study reporting a 22% incidence within 3 years. 82 For this reason, anticoagulation should be continued for at least 1 month after ablation, 83 although consideration should be given to indeﬁnite anticoagulation in patients with atrial ﬂutter and a history of cardioembolic stroke. Cardiac Arrest Cardiac arrest results from the cessation of mechanical cardiac activity, leading to hemodynamic failure and resultant loss of systemic circulation. Out-of- hospital cardiac arrest is common and accounts for a signiﬁcant burden of neurologic disability and mortality in the United States. Despite advancements in resuscitation care, more than two-thirds of patients with cardiac arrest die from hypoxic-ischemic brain injury, even if early resuscitative efforts are performed. 84 During cardiac arrest, four major cardiac rhythms have been 42 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

identiﬁed: pulseless electrical activity, asystole, ventricular ﬁbrillation, and pulseless ventricular tachycardia. Regardless of the cardiac rhythm, the extent of neurologic injury following cardiac arrest is dependent on the time to achieve return of spontaneous circulation and the quality of resuscitative efforts. During the initial cardiac arrest, cardiac output to the brain ceases (no ﬂow). Neuronal injury occurs within minutes because of mitochondrial dysfunction, which leads to ion pump failure and loss of plasma membrane potential. The subsequent opening of voltage-gated ion channels ultimately leads to cellular edema, intracellular calcium accumulation, and protease activation. 85 During cardiopulmonary resuscitation, the partial restoration of cardiac output (low ﬂow) can cause secondary injury through free radical production and neuronal apoptosis. Following return of spontaneous circulation, additional secondary injury may arise due to reperfusion and inﬂammation, which can be exacerbated by seizures or hyperthermia. The ultimate manifestation of these pathologies is hypoxic-ischemic brain injury, seen on brain MRI as diffusion restriction affecting the cerebral cortex (with propensity for the occipital cortex and medial prefrontal cortex), basal ganglia, thalami, hippocampus, and cerebellum. To assess the degree and extent of hypoxic-ischemic brain injury in patients with cardiac arrest, brain MRI should be performed no earlier than 48 hours after the arrest and ideally later because ischemic changes on apparent diffusion coefﬁcient sequences peak at around 3 to 5 days. 86 Neurologists are consulted in cases of cardiac arrest to assist in decisions about whether targeted temperature management should be performed and to assist in prognostication. Several randomized controlled trials have been performed to assess temperature regulation to mitigate neurologic injury after cardiac arrest, using different patient populations and yielding conﬂicting results. 87-89 Based on these studies, current guidelines suggest maintaining a temperature between 32°C and 37.5°C (89.6°F and 99.5°F) for 24 hours after cardiac arrest to reduce neurologic injury. 90 However, therapeutic hypothermia (32°C to 35°C [89.6°F to 95°F]) should be avoided in patients with intracranial hemorrhage, hemorrhagic shock, a Glasgow Coma Scale score greater than 8, and hemodynamic instability despite pressors. In patients who are being evaluated for therapeutic hypothermia, a head CT should be performed in any individual suspected of having an intracranial hemorrhage. The ICECAP trial (Inﬂuence of Cooling Duration on Efﬁcacy in Cardiac Arrest Patients—A Multicenter, Randomized, Adaptive Clinical Trial to Identify the Optimal Duration of Induced Hypothermia for Neuroprotection in Comatose Survivors of Cardiac Arrest), 91 which is evaluating varying durations of therapeutic hypothermia in patients with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest, may provide additional insight into the beneﬁt of targeted temperature management. Despite interventions aimed at reducing neurologic injury following cardiac arrest, hypoxic-ischemic injury remains a common occurrence and is the primary contributor to the withdrawal of life-sustaining therapy. Neuroprognostication after cardiac arrest is challenging, given the heterogeneity of hypoxic-ischemic brain injury and the self-fulﬁlling prophecy that often exists in neurocritical care. To standardize the approach to neuroprognostication, the Neurocritical Care Society has outlined recommendations on the reliability of clinical tools and established best practices in clinical decision-making. 92 The following discussion of the prognostic value of clinical signs and ancillary testing is drawn from these CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 43 KEY POINTS •AF is an independent risk factor for cognitive impairment even in patients without stroke. •Atrial ﬂutter and AF frequently coexist. •Anticoagulation should be considered for atrial ﬂutter using the same considerations given to AF. •Four cardiac rhythms can result in cardiac arrest: pulseless electrical activity, asystole, ventricular ﬁbrillation, and pulseless ventricular tachycardia. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

guidelines. Because current prognostication models have signiﬁcant limitations, the assessment of neurologic prognosis should be multimodal, incorporating the clinical examination, EEG, somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs), and neuroimaging. Following cardiac arrest, patients should be placed on EEG and initiated on targeted temperature management (if there are no contraindications to the latter). After 48 hours, the patient should be weaned off sedation (if feasible), and SSEPs and a brain MRI should be performed. After at least 72 hours following return of spontaneous circulation or after rewarming (in patients treated using therapeutic hypothermia), EEG ﬁndings should be documented, and a motor examination, along with quantitative pupillometry, should be conducted (in the absence of sedation or other confounders). The absence of the pupillary light reﬂex is a reliable predictor of poor functional outcome at 3 months or later. Motor response characterized by withdrawal, localization, or command following suggests that a favorable functional outcome is more likely at 3 months than a poor outcome, although an absent or extensor motor response is not a reliable biomarker for a poor outcome. Absence of diffusion-weighted imaging abnormalities suggests a favorable functional outcome is more likely at 3 months than a poor outcome. The presence of a diffuse pattern (across vascular distributions that involves the cerebral cortex and deep gray matter) of diffusion restriction on MRI is a moderately reliable predictor of poor functional outcome at 3 months. The presence of suppression or burst suppression on EEG suggests a poor functional outcome at 3 months, and the bilateral absence of the N20 cortical response on SSEPs is a reliable predictor of poor functional outcome at 3 months. Although no single test should be interpreted in isolation, the most reliable indicators of a poor prognosis are the absence of the pupillary light reﬂex on quantitative pupillometry and bilateral absence of the N20 cortical response on SSEPs. Of note, the concentration of serum neuron-speciﬁc enolase, a neuronal isoenzyme often considered a surrogate for neuronal damage that is often tested in patients with cardiac arrest, is not a reliable predictor of functional outcome at any speciﬁc threshold; therefore, it should not be relied on in neuroprognostication. 92 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC SURGERY AND CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION Perioperative stroke is one of the most common complications of cardiac surgery, with an incidence ranging from 1% to 8.7% depending on the type of surgery. 93 Stroke in this context can be further classiﬁed into intraoperative stroke (occurring during the surgery and diagnosed after extubation) and postoperative stroke (occurring after emergence from anesthesia). In addition to stroke, other neurologic complications after cardiac procedures include encephalopathy and long-term cognitive impairment. Patients who experience postoperative neurologic complications are at an increased risk of poor functional outcomes, requiring preoperative evaluation to assess and mitigate risk, as well as collaboration between neurologists and cardiac surgeons in the postoperative setting to maximize recovery. 44 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Preoperative Screening Because of shared risk factors between carotid artery disease and coronary artery disease, nearly 10% of patients being evaluated for coronary-artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery are found to have carotid stenosis greater than 50%. 94 Although extracranial carotid disease is a risk factor for the development of intraoperative stroke after CABG, 94 the beneﬁt of evaluating and treating carotid stenosis before CABG is debated. Because the decision to pursue carotid revascularization and the appropriate intervention (ie, endarterectomy versus stent) is a nuanced decision that should be individualized to each patient, the European Society of Cardiology guidelines recommend the involvement of a multidisciplinary team that includes neurologists. 95 Duplex ultrasonography is recommended as the ﬁrst-line strategy for preoperative carotid artery screening, but should only be performed in patients with a recent (fewer than 6 months) stroke or TIA or patients at high risk of perioperative stroke (age 70 years or older, multivessel coronary artery disease, peripheral arterial disease, or carotid bruit). 95 If carotid stenosis is identiﬁed, revascularization in the setting of CABG should only be pursued in patients with the highest risk of stroke (eg, those with stroke attributable to moderate- to-severe carotid stenosis [50% to 99%] or asymptomatic bilateral severe stenosis [70% to 99%]). 92 Among patients with extracranial carotid artery stenosis being evaluated for CABG, the decision to pursue carotid artery revascularization follows the same principles as those applied to patients not undergoing CABG. To review, revascularization should be performed in patients with symptomatic severe carotid artery stenosis (ie, those who have experienced a recent stroke or TIA attributable to carotid artery stenosis of 70% to 99%), provided that the perioperative morbidity and mortality risk is 6% or less. 13 In addition, revascularization for symptomatic ipsilateral moderate stenosis (50% to 69%) can also be considered based on patient-speciﬁc factors (eg, age, sex, comorbidities, and life expectancy). 13 In patients with asymptomatic extracranial carotid stenosis, although earlier trials demonstrated a small beneﬁt to intervention in patients with severe stenosis, the extent of beneﬁt is unclear in the modern era of cholesterol-reducing therapies and antiplatelet therapy. However, on a case-by-case basis, carotid revascularization may be considered for select patients with severe asymptomatic stenosis, a favorable life expectancy, and a low perioperative risk. Among patients undergoing cardiac surgery, revascularization should be performed in patients with severe symptomatic stenosis and may be considered in patients with asymptomatic carotid stenosis who have bilateral severe stenosis (70% to 99%) or unilateral severe stenosis with a contralateral occlusion. 96 In patients undergoing CABG surgery deemed appropriate candidates for carotid revascularization, it remains unclear whether a staged approach (carotid revascularization performed before CABG) or a simultaneous approach (carotid revascularization performed at the same time as CABG) to carotid and coronary revascularization is more favorable and, if staged, which procedure should be performed ﬁrst. In general, performing cardiac surgery ﬁrst has an increased risk of stroke, whereas performing carotid revascularization ﬁrst has an increased risk of cardiac complications. Based on current practice patterns, a staged approach with carotid artery intervention CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 45 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

before CABG is typically preferred over a simultaneous approach, unless the indication for CABG is urgent. 93 However, surgical planning should be individualized, taking into account each patient’s comorbidities and risks of surgical complications. For patients undergoing CABG who require carotid intervention, the European Society of Cardiology recommends carotid endarterectomy as the ﬁrst-line method for carotid revascularization in patients with symptomatic carotid artery stenosis, whereas no speciﬁc recommendation (ie, carotid endarterectomy or carotid artery stenting) is given for asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis. 95 This recommendation is based on a meta-analysis of patients undergoing carotid artery stenting before CABG, which found that patients with symptomatic carotid artery stenosis undergoing stenting had a much higher rate of stroke within 30 days compared with patients with asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis undergoing stenting (15% compared with 3% to 4%). 97 Because this study demonstrated a high rate of strokes in patients with symptomatic carotid artery disease who underwent carotid artery stenting, carotid endarterectomy is generally preferred over carotid artery stenting as the revascularization strategy in such patients. However, carotid endarterectomy may not be feasible in all patients with symptomatic carotid artery stenosis because patient-speciﬁc factors, perioperative risks, and anatomic features may favor carotid artery stenting in certain cases. For patients with asymptomatic carotid artery stenosis, the same meta- analysis mentioned previously demonstrated that 30-day outcomes were similar whether carotid artery stenting or carotid endarterectomy was performed before CABG. Therefore, in patients with asymptomatic carotid stenosis undergoing revascularization before CABG, the decision about which type of intervention to perform should be made on a case-by-case basis. Notably, among all patients with either asymptomatic or symptomatic carotid artery stenosis undergoing stenting before CABG, the rate of death, stroke, or myocardial infarction was highest (16%) in patients who were on dual antiplatelet therapy (to maintain carotid artery stent patency) that was not discontinued before cardiac surgery. Given these complexities, the decision to pursue carotid artery stenting or carotid endarterectomy should be made in conjunction with the cardiac surgery team to determine the optimal strategy that reduces perioperative stroke risk without signiﬁcantly increasing hemorrhagic risk. Intraoperative Complications Because of various nonmodiﬁable factors that contribute to intraoperative cardiac surgery–associated stroke, preventing these strokes has proved challenging. The incidence of perioperative stroke varies depending on the type of surgery, with valvular procedures and surgeries involving cardiopulmonary bypass posing the highest risk of stroke. The primary driver of intraoperative stroke is aortic manipulation during cannulation for cardiopulmonary bypass or aortic cross-clamping, which causes embolization of atherosclerotic plaques. For this reason, an aortic “no-touch” (anaortic off- pump) technique has been developed, which eliminates the need to manipulate the ascending aorta and avoids the use of cardiopulmonary bypass. These strategies can be implemented if preoperative or intraoperative aortic imaging shows a signiﬁcant atherosclerotic burden. 46 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

In addition to aortic embolism, intraoperative hypoperfusion is a potential cause of stroke, which can result from hypotension during off-pump surgery or cardiopulmonary bypass. Hypotension can cause watershed (border zone) strokes, which are associated with poor clinical outcomes. 98 Therefore, managing intraoperative blood pressure during cardiopulmonary bypass is critical, with expert recommendations suggesting a mean arterial pressure goal of 60 to 65 mm Hg, with potentially higher targets for patients who have had a stroke. 93 Additional risk factors, including age, prior stroke, preoperative AF, elevated serum creatinine, and prolonged cardiopulmonary bypass time, have also been identiﬁed as predictors of intraoperative stroke, although these are not generally modiﬁable. 99,100 Because preoperative AF is a signiﬁcant risk factor for intraoperative stroke, and most thrombi in AF originate in the left atrial appendage, concomitant occlusion of the left atrial appendage during cardiac surgery has been explored as an adjunctive procedure to reduce perioperative stroke risk. In the LAAOS III study (Left Atrial Appendage Occlusion Study III), 2,379 patients with AF and a CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc score of 2 or greater were randomized to undergo or not undergo left atrial appendage occlusion during surgery. 101 All patients continued optimal medical therapy, including oral anticoagulation, over a mean follow-up period of 3.8 years. Among patients in the left atrial appendage occlusion group, the rate of ischemic stroke or systemic embolism was lower compared with those who did not undergo closure (4.8% versus 7.0%, P=.001). These ﬁndings suggest that surgical occlusion of the left atrial appendage during cardiac surgery confers additional stroke reduction in patients with AF receiving ongoing anticoagulation. Noninvasive near-infrared spectroscopy, transcranial Doppler ultrasonography, and EEG have been used to identify acute intraoperative changes in cerebral function. Near-infrared spectroscopy measures brain tissue oxygen saturation as a surrogate for cerebral blood ﬂow. Desaturation episodes detected by near-infrared spectroscopy have been linked to worsened neurologic outcomes and an increased risk of cognitive decline. 102 Transcranial Doppler ultrasonography monitors cerebral blood ﬂow and can detect microemboli through high-intensity transient signals. However, multiple studies have failed to demonstrate a strong correlation between the presence of high-intensity transient signals and clinical outcomes. 103,104 EEG can detect neurophysiologic signatures of reductions in blood ﬂow and early ischemic changes. However, because EEG requires skilled technicians and readers and is inﬂuenced by anesthetic agents, it is less widely used. For this reason, processed EEG (eg, the Bispectral Index or the Patient State Index), which is easier to interpret and obviates the need for interpretation by specialists, is more commonly used. However, it is also affected by anesthetic agents and may be unreliable for detecting intraoperative cerebral ischemia. 105 Postoperative Complications Postoperative strokes are more common than intraoperative strokes and are also associated with increased mortality. Shared risk factors between intraoperative and postoperative strokes include older age, prior stroke, and preoperative AF. 100 Although intraoperative stroke risk is increased with cardiopulmonary bypass, a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 47 KEY POINTS •The primary driver of intraoperative stroke is aortic arch manipulation during cannulation or cross-clamping. •Adjunctive surgical left atrial appendage occlusion during cardiac surgery in patients with preoperative AF may reduce the risk of postoperative strokes. •A variety of intraoperative monitoring strategies, such as near- infrared spectroscopy, transcranial Doppler ultrasonography, and EEG, have been used to identify acute intraoperative changes in cerebral function. •Shared risk factors between intraoperative and postoperative stroke include older age, prior stroke, and preoperative AF. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

comparing on-pump and off-pump approaches did not show any signiﬁcant reduction in perioperative stroke with off-pump surgery. 106 In addition to preoperative AF and vascular risk factors, new-onset postoperative AF also increases the risk of postoperative stroke. 107 AF is the most common clinically signiﬁcant arrhythmia observed after cardiac surgery, occurring in 20% to 80% of patients. 108 The incidence rates vary by age and type of surgery, with the lowest rates observed in isolated CABG surgery and the highest in combined CABG surgery and valvular surgery. Although most postoperative AF develops on postoperative day 2, a small subset of patients may develop AF after discharge from the hospital. 108 Most postoperative AF is self-limited and resolves spontaneously within 24 hours of onset. However, given the potential for AF recurrence beyond 30 days postoperatively, close monitoring of patients with postoperative AF is essential. For stroke prevention, preoperative or immediately postoperative aspirin lowers the risk of postoperative stroke even in patients who do not develop AF. 96 However, the efﬁcacy of anticoagulation for postoperative AF is unclear, given the transient nature of the condition and the increased hemorrhagic risk associated with anticoagulation use in the early postoperative period. One study demonstrated that anticoagulation for postoperative AF did not reduce stroke risk within 30 days but increased the risk of major bleeding. 109 In the absence of randomized controlled trials, current guidelines adopt the anticoagulation strategy from a rate control versus rhythm control trial for postoperative AF, which initiated anticoagulation if AF persisted or recurred 48 hours after surgery. 110 In patients with new-onset AF who have also had a perioperative stroke, anticoagulation is generally initiated. Further guidance on this clinical question may result from the PACES trial (Anticoagulation for New-Onset Post- Operative Atrial Fibrillation After CABG), 111 which aims to evaluate the effectiveness of oral anticoagulation combined with antiplatelet therapy in reducing thromboembolic events in postoperative AF. Based on the LAAOS III study, it remains unclear whether surgical occlusion of the left atrial appendage reduces postoperative stroke in cardiac surgery patients without preoperative AF. The LeAAPS (Left Atrial Appendage Exclusion for Prophylactic Stroke Reduction Trial) 112 randomized, multicenter trial is currently underway to assess whether left atrial appendage excision with an epicardial clipping device speciﬁcally designed to close off the left atrial appendage is effective at reducing ischemic stroke or systemic embolism in high-risk patients without preexisting AF undergoing cardiac surgery. Cognitive Impairment After Cardiac Surgery Postoperative cognitive dysfunction is a common neurologic complication following cardiac surgery, characterized by impairments in executive function, attention, language, visuospatial function, and motor skills. Early studies may have overestimated the prevalence of cognitive impairment because of the nonstandardized deﬁnitions of cognitive decline, lack of control groups, and inability to account for presurgical cognitive impairment. 113 Other studies, however, have demonstrated a high rate of preexisting cognitive impairment and cerebral small vessel disease in patients undergoing cardiac surgery. 114 48 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CARDIAC DISEASE AND CARDIAC PROCEDURES Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Cognitive impairment after cardiac surgery can be categorized into postoperative delirium (typically occurring within the ﬁrst week), delayed neurocognitive recovery (cognitive decline diagnosed up to 30 days postsurgery), and postoperative neurocognitive disorder (cognitive decline occurring from 30 days to 12 months). 115 Delirium occurs in up to half of all patients undergoing cardiac surgery and is associated with age, female sex, diabetes, heart failure, prior stroke, depression, cognitive impairment, and education level. 116 Patients who experience postoperative delirium after cardiac surgery are at an increased risk of mortality and longer hospitalization times. Although several pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic interventions have been proposed, the optimal approach to prevent and treat delirium remains unclear. Delayed neurocognitive recovery is characterized by impairments in memory, attention, and executive function occurring from 7 days to 30 days after surgery. It is common in patients with advanced age or preoperative cognitive impairment and often leads to prolonged hospitalization. Although it generally has a favorable prognosis, it can increase the risk of long-term cognitive decline and mortality. Baseline risks for postoperative neurocognitive disorder are older age, lower education level, lower baseline cognitive function, and diabetes as preoperative predictors. 117 Intraoperative factors identiﬁed in observational studies as contributors to postcardiac surgery neurocognitive disorder include the use of cardiopulmonary bypass, increased surgery duration, hypotension, and speciﬁc anesthesia agents. 118 However, a randomized study comparing on-pump with off-pump approaches did not show any signiﬁcant reduction in cognitive impairment in the off-pump cohort. 119 Several pathophysiologic mechanisms, such as inﬂammation, microemboli, and cerebral hypoperfusion, have been proposed to explain postoperative cognitive impairment, although no leading hypothesis has emerged. Given that postcardiac surgery cognitive decline is strongly inﬂuenced by preoperative cognitive impairment and vascular risk factors, addressing these factors through risk factor modiﬁcation may reduce the risk of further cognitive decline after cardiac surgery. Cardiac Catheterization Cardiac catheterization is a commonly performed procedure that can be both diagnostic and therapeutic (eg, percutaneous coronary intervention and transcatheter aortic valve implantation). Because cardiac catheterization involves traversing the aortic arch, atherosclerotic plaques encountered by the catheter may become dislodged and embolize, leading to stroke. Microemboli of air entering during the injection of saline or contrast may be another potential source of stroke. For these reasons, diagnostic cardiac catheterization carries a 0.05% to 1% risk of stroke, with a slightly higher risk among patients who undergo percutaneous coronary intervention. 120 Risk factors for stroke in the context of cardiac catheterization include older age, prior stroke, and retrograde catheterization into the left ventricle. 121 Although symptomatic strokes are rare, asymptomatic infarcts detected on routine brain MRI after catheterization are common, occurring in up to 35% of patients. 122 In the event of a symptomatic stroke, eligible patients should be considered for thrombolysis or endovascular thrombectomy. Although further studies are needed to assess the efﬁcacy and safety of these interventions in this patient population, a small multicenter study has shown favorable results. 123 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 49 KEY POINTS •New-onset AF after cardiac surgery is a signiﬁcant risk factor for postoperative strokes. •Patients who undergo cardiac surgery have a high incidence of cognitive impairment. •Half of all patients who undergo cardiac surgery will experience delirium. •Cardiac catheterization poses a small risk for ischemic stroke through dislodged atherosclerotic plaques. •Contrast-induced encephalopathy is a rare complication of cardiac surgery because of iodinated contrast agents. •Neuroimaging ﬁndings in patients with contrast- induced encephalopathy can range from normal to cerebral edema and cortical enhancement. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Transcatheter aortic valve implantation has emerged as an attractive strategy for aortic valve replacement because it is minimally invasive and suitable for patients across all levels of surgical risk. Compared with surgical aortic valve replacement, transcatheter aortic valve implantation carries a similar risk of stroke (approximately 3%), although the incidence of new-onset AF may be lower. 124 To reduce the rate of periprocedural stroke, cerebral embolic protection devices have been used to capture and remove embolic material during the procedure. However, their effectiveness in reducing the incidence of any stroke, disabling stroke, or all-cause mortality was not supported by a 2023 meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. 125 Contrast-induced encephalopathy is a rare condition related to cardiac catheterization that can mimic a stroke. The condition occurs within minutes to hours after contrast administration during cardiac catheterization and can cause encephalopathy, seizures, cortical blindness, and focal neurologic impairment. It is suspected that neurotoxicity from iodinated, nonionic, water-soluble contrast agents may disrupt the blood-brain barrier, leading to direct neurotoxicity and cerebral edema. Risk factors for developing contrast- induced encephalopathy include male sex, advanced age, hypertension, kidney disease, and the administration of a large volume of contrast. 126 Neuroimaging may reveal cerebral edema and cortical enhancement, but it may be normal in some cases. The condition typically resolves spontaneously without treatment beyond supportive care as the contrast agent is cleared by the kidneys, usually within days. CONCLUSION Neurologic complications arising from cardiac disorders and cardiac procedures are prevalent and represent a signiﬁcant source of morbidity and mortality. Neurologists play a key role in collaborating with cardiologists and cardiac surgeons in the diagnosis and treatment of conditions such as perioperative stroke, prognosis after cardiac arrest, secondary prevention of stroke related to cardiac disease, and postoperative cognitive impairment. Given the limited and at times conﬂicting evidence to guide appropriate management in many neurologic conditions arising from cardiac diseases and procedures, it is critical for neurologists to be familiar with available management strategies, ensuring that individualized patient care can be effectively provided by considering both the neurologic complication and the associated cardiac condition or procedure. Neurologic Complications of Endocrine Disorders By Raﬁd Mustafa, MD ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article reviews the neurologic manifestations of endocrine disorders with respect to pathophysiology, clinical presentation, diagnosis, and treatment. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Studies have further elucidated the mechanisms by which endocrine disorders affect neurologic function, from peripheral nervous system manifestations of diabetes to central nervous system complications of pituitary apoplexy. Glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists used to treat obesity and diabetes are associated with an increased risk of developing diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy and common peroneal neuropathy. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Neurologic complications of endocrine disorders are diverse and may arise before systemic manifestations. Therefore, neurologists should consider endocrine diseases in the differential diagnosis of syndromes such as altered mental status, coma, peripheral neuropathy, and myopathy, and be familiar with the appropriate evaluation for these disorders. INTRODUCTION T he endocrine system is an intricate network of hormone-producing glands that plays a pivotal role in regulating various physiologic processes. Endocrine diseases result in excess or deﬁciency of one or more hormones, which can cause both systemic symptoms and a wide range of neurologic manifestations. This article discusses neurologic disorders associated with diseases of the hypothalamus, pituitary gland, thyroid gland, adrenal gland, and endocrine pancreas. Early recognition of neurologic manifestations associated with endocrine disease is essential because neurologic symptoms and signs may represent the presentation of an undiagnosed underlying endocrine disorder, and because many neurologic complications of endocrine disorders are reversible with timely treatment. A summary of the key aspects of these entities can be found in TABLE 5-1. THE ENDOCRINE SYSTEM Endocrine glands secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream to regulate various physiologic functions. The primary glands of the endocrine system are CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 105 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, SYSTEMIC DISEASE): 105 – 130. TABLE 5-1 Common Neurologic Manifestations of Endocrine Disorders Endocrine disorder Systemic features Central nervous system manifestations Peripheral nervous system manifestations Notes Pituitary crisis can cause confusion or coma Cushing syndrome Weight gain, central obesity, hypertension, hyperglycemia, striae Cognitive dysfunction, mood swings, anxiety, depression, irritability Myopathy, dysfunction, depression, coma (in severe cases), stroke, movement disorders (diabetic striatopathy), seizures Diabetic peripheral neuropathy, thoracic radiculopathy, lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy Rapid correction of hyperglycemia can lead to treatment-induced neuropathy 106 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. the pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal gland, endocrine pancreas, and gonads (FIGURE 5-1). This interconnected system is regulated through the three-tiered hypothalamic- pituitary-target organ axis (FIGURE 5-2 1 ). The hypothalamus links the nervous system with the endocrine system by producing, releasing, and inhibiting hormones that regulate anterior pituitary gland secretion. The pituitary gland, situated just inferior to the hypothalamus, consists of two lobes: the anterior and posterior pituitary. The anterior pituitary is regulated by the hypothalamus and secretes thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), luteinizing hormone, follicle-stimulating hormone, growth hormone (GH), and prolactin. These hormones, in turn, regulate hormone secretion by target organs such as the thyroid gland, adrenal glands, and gonads. The posterior pituitary stores and releases hormones produced by the hypothalamus, antidiuretic hormone and oxytocin, which play crucial roles in water balance and reproductive processes, respectively. The hypothalamic-pituitary-target organ axis works through feedback loop mechanisms by which hormone release at one level regulates hormone release by another level. For example, low thyroid hormone levels trigger the hypothalamus to release thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), stimulating the anterior pituitary to secrete TSH, which then acts on the thyroid gland to produce thyroid hormones (triiodothyronine [T 3 ] and thyroxine [T 4 ]). Elevated thyroid hormone levels then signal the hypothalamus and pituitary to reduce TRH and TSH production, maintaining hormonal balance. Disruptions in hormonal balance can lead to neurologic manifestations because of the critical role these hormones play in maintaining homeostasis within the body’s intricate systems and their interrelation with the nervous system. This article is organized by endocrine organ, describing diseases that affect each organ and the potential neurologic complications of either increased or decreased hormone levels caused by endocrine dysfunction at each level of the hypothalamus-pituitary-target organ axis. FIGURE 5-1 The endocrine system. The endocrine system (pituitary, thyroid, adrenal glands, pancreas, ovaries) is shown in a female body. The inset shows the testes, which are part of the male endocrine system. Used with permission of Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. © 2015 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 107 KEY POINTS •The primary glands of the endocrine system are the pituitary, thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal gland, endocrine pancreas, and gonads. This interconnected system is regulated through the three-tiered hypothalamic-pituitary- target organ axis. •The hypothalamic- pituitary-target organ axis works through feedback loop mechanisms through which hormone release by one level regulates hormone release by another level.
