DISORDERS OF THE HYPOTHALAMUS AND PITUITARY GLAND Disorders of the hypothalamus can arise from tumors (eg, craniopharyngiomas, gliomas, hamartomas), inﬂammatory diseases (eg, hypophysitis, sarcoidosis), infections (eg, tuberculosis, bacterial meningitis), or genetic mutations (eg, Kallmann syndrome) leading to disruptions in the production of releasing and inhibiting hormones, which can result in secondary dysfunction of the pituitary gland. For example, a hypothalamic tumor may impair the secretion of TRH, leading to decreased TSH release from the anterior pituitary, resulting in hypothyroidism. Pituitary gland disorders include tumors (eg, adenomas), vascular disorders (eg, apoplexy, Sheehan syndrome), inﬂammatory and infectious diseases (similar to those of the hypothalamus), or manifestations of underlying genetic diseases (eg, multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1). The most common pituitary pathology is pituitary adenoma, a benign tumor that can secrete excessive amounts of hormones, cause mass effect that disrupts normal pituitary function, or both. For example, hypersecretion of GH from a pituitary adenoma can lead to acromegaly, and hypersecretion of ACTH can lead to Cushing disease. Pituitary Tumors Pituitary adenomas are the most common pituitary tumors in adults, present in 10% of the general population. 2 The majority of these are pituitary microadenomas (smaller than 10 mm in diameter) and are often incidentally discovered on imaging or autopsy, whereas clinically signiﬁcant pituitary adenomas are present in less than 0.1% of the general population. 2 Macroadenomas (10 mm in diameter or larger) account for nearly 50% of clinically signiﬁcant pituitary adenomas. 2 In children, craniopharyngiomas are much more common, accounting for 6% to 13% of all intracranial tumors in this population. 3 Pituitary macroadenomas frequently cause neurologic symptoms secondary to mass effect, namely visual ﬁeld abnormalities in 18% to 78% of FIGURE 5-2 Control of hypothalamic-pituitary-target organ axes. ACTH = adrenocorticotropic hormone; CRH = corticotropin-releasing hormone; FSH = follicle-stimulating hormone; GH = growth hormone; GHRH = GH-releasing hormone; GnRH = gonadotropin-releasing hormone; IGF-1 = insulin-like growth factor 1; LH = luteinizing hormone;T 4 = thyroxine;T 3 = triiodothyronine; TRH = thyrotropin-releasing hormone; TSH = thyroid-stimulating hormone. Modiﬁed with permission from Melmed S, J Clin Invest. 1 © 2003 American Society for Clinical Investigation. Figure redrawn by Chihiro Saka. 108 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

patients and headache in 17% to 75% of patients. 2 Bitemporal hemianopia represents the most common presenting visual impairment (32% to 81% of patients with visual symptoms), caused by compression of the optic chiasm. 4 Junctional scotoma (ipsilateral central scotoma accompanied by a contralateral superior temporal visual ﬁeld defect) due to mass effect at the junction between the optic nerve and optic chiasm and relative afferent pupillary defect due to asymmetric compression of optic nerve ﬁbers originating from one eye compared with the other are also commonly encountered (4% to 39% of patients for junctional scotoma; 15% to 77% of patients for relative afferent pupillary defect). 4 The phenotype of headache associated with pituitary adenoma is most frequently migraine (30% to 46% of patients), followed by primary stabbing headache (27% of patients) and trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias, particularly short-lasting unilateral neuralgiform headache attacks with conjunctival injection and tearing (SUNCT; 5% of patients), and cluster headache (4% of patients). 5 Parasellar extension of pituitary macroadenomas into the cavernous sinus occurs in 10% to 40% of patients, although symptomatic cranial nerve deﬁcits and associated binocular diplopia are seen in only 2% to 10% of patients. 4,6 In addition to neurologic symptoms from mass effect, functioning adenomas (roughly 70% of all pituitary adenomas) may cause secondary neurologic effects from excess hormone production, most frequently prolactin (53% of all pituitary adenomas), GH (12% of all pituitary adenomas), and ACTH (4% of all pituitary adenomas). 2 Prolactinomas rarely cause associated neurologic symptoms because most prolactin-secreting adenomas are microadenomas without associated mass effect. The most common symptoms of hyperprolactinemia are non-neurologic and include galactorrhea, menstrual irregularities, sexual dysfunction, and osteoporosis. Excessive GH secretion can lead to acromegaly, particularly affecting the bones of the hands, feet, and face. 7 Acromegaly of the wrist causes median neuropathy at the wrist (carpal tunnel syndrome) in 19% to 64% of patients. 8,9 Sleep apnea, resulting from the enlargement of the tongue and pharyngeal tissues, is seen in 27% to 88% of patients with acromegaly and can lead to signiﬁcant fatigue and cognitive dysfunction. 10 Joint pain (70% of patients), reduced exercise intolerance (75% of patients), and limb girdle muscle weakness (50% of patients) are also frequently encountered. 10,11 The neurologic manifestations of ACTH-secreting pituitary adenomas are discussed in the Cushing Syndrome section. Management of pituitary adenomas includes medical, surgical, and radiation therapy approaches depending on tumor size, secretory function, and associated visual impairments, with the ultimate treatment goals of controlling tumor growth, restoring endocrine function, and preserving and recovering visual function. 4 Medical management of hormonal imbalance (eg, dopamine agonists for prolactinomas and somatostatin analogs for GH-secreting tumors) is typically guided by an endocrinologist. Surgical resection is typically performed via a transsphenoidal approach and is generally considered for tumors causing mass effect with associated visual impairment or those unresponsive to medical treatment. Most patients (79% to 95%) have some degree of visual recovery following surgery, ranging from mild improvement to complete resolution. 12 Although rare, the most common postsurgical complications include endocrine dysfunction (eg, diabetes insipidus, hyponatremia, hypopituitarism; CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 109 KEY POINTS •Disorders of the hypothalamus can arise from tumors, inﬂammatory diseases, infections, or genetic variations leading to disruptions in the production of releasing and inhibiting hormones, which can result in secondary dysfunction of the pituitary gland. •Pituitary macroadenomas frequently cause neurologic symptoms secondary to mass effect, namely visual ﬁeld abnormalities in 18% to 78% of patients and headache in 17% to 75% of patients. •In addition to neurologic symptoms from mass effect, functioning adenomas (roughly 70% of all pituitary adenomas) may cause secondary neurologic effects from excess hormone production, most frequently prolactin, growth hormone, and adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH). Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

3.6% to 4.3%), CSF leak (2.6%), and epistaxis (1.1%). 13 Radiation therapy is reserved for residual or recurrent adenomas. Close follow-up with regular hormonal assessment and cranial imaging is needed to monitor for recurrence and manage long-term outcomes. 2 Neuro-ophthalmologic surveillance with visual acuity, color vision, and static perimetry should be performed within 2 to 3 months of surgical resection or radiation therapy. Thereafter, routine neuro-ophthalmologic surveillance is recommended at 3- to 12-month intervals to monitor for the development of subclinical visual loss, which can be the ﬁrst sign of tumor recurrence (occurring in 5% to 15% of patients). 2,4 Disorders of the Posterior Pituitary Gland Common disorders of the posterior pituitary gland include diabetes insipidus and syndrome of inappropriate secretion of antidiuretic hormone (SIADH). Diabetes insipidus is a disorder of vasopressin deﬁciency, characterized by polyuria, polydipsia, and an inability to concentrate urine. SIADH is a condition characterized by excessive release of vasopressin, leading to water retention and hyponatremia. Hyponatremia can lead to neurologic complications such as altered mental status and seizures, and overly rapid correction of hyponatremia can result in osmotic demyelination. These disorders are discussed in detail in the article “Neurologic Manifestations of Renal and Electrolyte Disorders” by Eelco F. M. Wijdicks, MD, PhD, FAAN, FACP, FNCS, 14 in this issue of Continuum. Pituitary Apoplexy Pituitary apoplexy refers to acute hemorrhage into or infarction of the pituitary gland. Although pituitary apoplexy is rare in the general population, with a prevalence of 6.2 cases per 100,000 people, patients with preexisting pituitary adenomas are at higher risk (2% to 12% of patients with pituitary adenoma experience apoplexy). 15,16 Various precipitating factors have been associated with pituitary apoplexy, including recent surgical interventions, anticoagulation, hypertension, head trauma, and dynamic pituitary testing with agents such as insulin, protirelin, gonadotropin-releasing hormone, or GH-releasing hormone that may alter pituitary metabolism and the blood ﬂow dynamics around the pituitary gland. 16 No predisposing factor is identiﬁed in up to 40% of patients. 15 A sudden-onset thunderclap headache is the hallmark clinical symptom of pituitary apoplexy (seen in 80% to 90% of patients and illustrated in CASE 5-1), arising from the rapid expansion of the pituitary lesion and stretching of the dura mater. 15 These headaches are frequently accompanied by nausea, vomiting, or both (40% of patients). 17 Neuro-ophthalmologic manifestations are common, including bitemporal hemianopia (30% to 71% of patients) from compression of the optic chiasm, decreased visual acuity (20% of patients) from compression of the optic nerve, and diplopia, ptosis, or both (39% to 52% of patients) from compression of ocular motor cranial nerves within the cavernous sinus. 15 Mental status changes can range from encephalopathy to coma (42% of patients). 15 Neuroimaging ﬁndings of pituitary apoplexy include hyperdense blood products within the pituitary on CT and varied T1 and T2 signal characteristics representing blood products in the pituitary on MRI, depending on the timing of acquisition relative to the acuity of the hemorrhage; blood-sensitive sequences such as gradient echo and susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) 110 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

may demonstrate blooming artifact in the pituitary. 18 Over the past few decades, there has been a shift toward using MRI rather than CT alone for evaluation of pituitary apoplexy, given superior detection of hemorrhage with MRI compared with CT: 17 Head CT has low sensitivity (21% to 46%) in detecting apoplexy compared with MRI (88% to 90%), given that MRI contains multiple sequences to allow for characterization of blood products. 18 Immediate management of pituitary apoplexy is focused on stabilizing the patient and addressing intracranial pressure, electrolyte imbalances, and hormonal deﬁciencies. High-dose corticosteroids are often administered to reduce inﬂammation and edema and to prevent adrenal insufﬁciency. Multidisciplinary collaboration with an endocrinologist and neurosurgeon is vital for the management of hormonal deﬁciencies and consideration of surgical intervention (typically transsphenoidal resection), respectively. 15 Prognosis varies depending on the severity of the initial presentation and the timeliness of intervention. Early diagnosis and prompt treatment are critical for reducing the risk of permanent neurologic deﬁcits and improving overall outcomes. Long-term follow-up is essential because patients may develop hormonal deﬁciencies requiring lifelong hormone replacement therapy, although the neurologic outcome of most patients with pituitary apoplexy is excellent. 17 Other Neurologic Conditions Related to the Pituitary Gland The pituitary gland is also implicated in other neurologic conditions, such as transient prolactin elevation in epileptic seizures and the radiologic ﬁnding of an empty sella. The following sections review the clinical relevance, diagnostic utility, and implications of each phenomenon in neurologic practice. SERUM PROLACTIN IN DISTINGUISHING EPILEPTIC SEIZURES FROM OTHER SPELLS. Transient elevations in prolactin levels have been used to distinguish epileptic seizures from other spells (eg, syncope and nonepileptic events) based on one Class I and several Class II studies (Class B evidence). 19 The mechanism behind this phenomenon is hypothesized to be secondary to alterations in hypothalamic regulation of prolactin release due to propagation of epileptic activity from mesial temporal lobe structures to the hypothalamus. Most studies suggest using a serum prolactin level at least twice that of baseline value to differentiate epileptic seizures (either generalized tonic-clonic or focal seizures) from nonepileptic events when measured in the appropriate clinical setting 10 to 20 minutes after a suspected event. 19 However, serum prolactin level alone should not be used as the sole differentiator, given its low sensitivity (46% to 60%) and low negative predictive value. 19 Additionally, other conditions, including syncope, can also cause transient hyperprolactinemia, limiting the utility of prolactin as a screening tool. 20 EMPTY SELLA. An empty sella is a phenomenon characterized by herniation of suprasellar arachnoid and subarachnoid space CSF into the sella turcica (the depression in the body of the sphenoid bone where the pituitary gland sits). This phenomenon is one of the most commonly described imaging signs associated with idiopathic intracranial hypertension, seen in up to 94% of patients and presumed to be an imaging correlate of chronically elevated intracranial pressure. 21 Although the incidence of idiopathic intracranial hypertension is CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 111 KEY POINTS •A sudden-onset thunderclap headache is the hallmark clinical symptom of pituitary apoplexy (seen in 80% to 90% of patients), arising from the rapid expansion of the pituitary lesion and stretching of the dura mater. •Immediate management of pituitary apoplexy is focused on stabilizing the patient and addressing intracranial pressure, electrolyte imbalances, and hormonal deﬁciencies. •Serum prolactin level alone should not be used as the sole differentiator between epileptic seizures and other spells (eg, syncope and nonepileptic events), given its low sensitivity (46% to 60%) and low negative predictive value. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

relatively rare in the general population (estimated at approximately 1 case per 100,000 individuals), 22 an incidental empty sella turcica is seen in approximately 12% of asymptomatic patients undergoing MRI of the brain. 23 In one study, incidental radiologic signs of idiopathic intracranial hypertension, including empty sella, were detected in 49% of patients undergoing MRI of the brain for various clinical indications, although only 2% had clinical evidence of papilledema. 24 Thus, it remains critical to interpret the signiﬁcance of the MRI ﬁnding of an empty sella in the context of other clinical and diagnostic ﬁndings. CASE 5-1 A 52-year-old woman with a medical history notable only for hypertension presented to the emergency department with a sudden onset of severe headache and nausea. She reported a feeling of “fuzziness” in her vision but no frank visual ﬁeld abnormalities nor diplopia. Neurologic examination, including fundoscopic examination, was normal. Head CT without contrast was performed, revealing a heterogeneously attenuating 2.6 cm suprasellar mass (FIGURE 5-3A). Subsequent brain MRI revealed a heterogeneously T1 isointense, T2 hyperintense lesion in the suprasellar region with associated blooming artifact on gradient echo sequences in areas that were hyperdense on the previous head CT, consistent with hemorrhage (FIGURE 5-3B, 5-3C, and 5-3D). Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) of the head did not demonstrate any connection between the suprasellar mass and the arterial system. The patient was observed closely in the neurosciences intensive care unit and empirically initiated on hydrocortisone, both to treat the edema and to avoid acute glucocorticoid deﬁciency. The following day, hormonal assays revealed panhypopituitarism with low levels of cortisol, thyroid- stimulating hormone (TSH), and free thyroxine. Serum prolactin, however, was markedly elevated, suggesting a preexisting pituitary adenoma. Because her vision was fully preserved, surgical intervention was not pursued emergently, but transsphenoidal surgical decompression was performed after several days. Intraoperatively, a hemorrhagic pituitary macroadenoma was conﬁrmed and evacuated. Postoperatively, she was continued on hydrocortisone and additionally initiated on levothyroxine and desmopressin for hormone replacement. Her headache and nausea improved signiﬁcantly, and by the time of hospital discharge, she was nearly asymptomatic, with only mild residual headache. COMMENT Pituitary apoplexy is a medical emergency characterized by sudden hemorrhage into or infarction of the pituitary gland, often within a preexisting adenoma. This case underscores the importance of prompt recognition and treatment, which frequently involves acute neurosurgical intervention and hormone replacement therapy. Early diagnosis and management are critical to prevent permanent visual impairment (in patients with optic chiasm compression or cavernous sinus involvement) and hypopituitarism. 112 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

DISORDERS OF THE THYROID GLAND The thyroid hormones T 3 and T 4 play crucial roles in regulating metabolism, energy production, and overall growth and development. Both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism can cause neurologic symptoms and signs, which may be the initial manifestation of an undiagnosed thyroid disorder. In most cases, however, the neurologic manifestations of thyroid disease occur in conjunction with other systemic features of thyroid dysfunction. FIGURE 5-3 Imaging ﬁndings in the patient in CASE 5-1. A, Axial head CT demonstrating a heterogeneously attenuating 2.6 cm suprasellar mass (arrows), with the largest area of hyperattenuation most prominent over the right anterior portion of the mass. B, Axial T1-weighted brain MRI revealing a predominantly T1 isointense mass in the suprasellar region with an associated rim of T1 hyperintensity (arrows). C, On axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI, the mass is heterogeneously hyperintense (arrows). D, Axial gradient echo MRI, demonstrating prominent blooming artifact (arrow) in the region corresponding to the highest hyperattenuation on the CT in panel A. These ﬁndings are consistent with hemorrhage into a pituitary macroadenoma. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 113 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Hyperthyroidism Hyperthyroidism is characterized by excess thyroid hormone secretion either due to thyroid overactivity (primary hyperthyroidism) or a TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (secondary hyperthyroidism). The most common cause of hyperthyroidism is Graves disease, an autoimmune disorder affecting the TSH receptor. Other causes of hyperthyroidism include toxic multinodular goiter, solitary toxic adenoma, thyroid malignancy, and iatrogenic excess thyroid hormone supplementation. Hyperthyroidism causes a hypermetabolic state with widespread systemic effects, including weight loss (despite an increased appetite), heat intolerance, increased sweating, anxiety, restlessness, irritability, emotional lability, impaired concentration, and insomnia. The most common neurologic manifestations of hyperthyroidism are enhanced physiologic tremor (seen in more than 75% of patients with hyperthyroidism) and hyperreﬂexia (seen in 38% of patients with hyperthyroidism); rarely, patients may develop other movement disorders such as choreoathetosis (less than 2% of patients). 25,26 Treatment with beta-blockers may be helpful for these hyperkinetic movement disorders. 26 Approximately 50% of patients with hyperthyroidism secondary to Graves disease will develop Graves ophthalmopathy, which classically manifests with proptosis, strabismus, and diplopia caused by antibodies targeting the eye muscles and surrounding connective tissue. 26 Central nervous system manifestations of hyperthyroidism include mental status changes ranging from mild cognitive impairment (with impaired concentration) to lethargy (referred to as apathetic thyrotoxicosis) to coma and a decreased seizure threshold, particularly in patients with epilepsy. 27,28 Encephalopathy occurs in about one-third of patients with hyperthyroidism, most commonly in conjunction with systemic manifestations of hyperthyroidism. 29 Among a large cohort of patients presenting with undifferentiated dementia, only 1% were found to have clinical hyperthyroidism. 30 Hyperthyroidism is also associated with an increased risk of cardioembolic stroke mediated by the increased risk of atrial ﬁbrillation in patients with hyperthyroidism. 31,32 Peripheral nervous system manifestations of hyperthyroidism include myopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and thyrotoxic periodic paralysis. Myopathy is seen in 60% to 80% of patients with untreated hyperthyroidism and is usually proximal with a normal serum creatine kinase level; EMG may be normal or show myopathic ﬁndings. 33-36 Sensory-predominant axonal neuropathies are seen in up to 24% of patients with hyperthyroidism. 33 Thyrotoxic periodic paralysis is a rare complication of hyperthyroidism (seen in 2% of patients), in which patients develop sudden episodes of limb weakness often triggered by high carbohydrate intake or exercise (similar to familial hypokalemic periodic paralysis). 37 Potassium supplementation and nonselective beta-blockers are used to treat acute attacks, although ultimately correction of the underlying thyroid disorder is necessary. 38 Up to 14% of patients with myasthenia gravis have autoimmune thyroid disease. 39 Treatment of one disorder does not necessarily improve outcomes in the other, and independent disease-directed treatment for each disorder is essential. 114 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

In all of these scenarios, treatment of the underlying thyroid disorder and restoration of a euthyroid state often lead to improvement or resolution of symptoms. Hypothyroidism Hypothyroidism is characterized by insufﬁcient production of thyroid hormones or decreased stimulation of the thyroid gland, leading to decreased release of thyroid hormones (eg, secondary or tertiary hypothyroidism). Common causes include autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto thyroiditis), iodine deﬁciency (particularly in regions where iodine intake is insufﬁcient), and treatment of hyperthyroidism with thyroidectomy or radioactive iodine therapy. Central hypothyroidism develops as a consequence of insufﬁcient stimulation of the thyroid gland by TSH, caused by either pituitary dysfunction (secondary hypothyroidism) or hypothalamic dysfunction (tertiary hypothyroidism). Hypothyroidism leads to a hypometabolic state with various systemic effects, including weight gain, cold intolerance, dry skin, hair thinning, fatigue, depression, and constipation (FIGURE 5-4 40 ). The most common neurologic symptom of hypothyroidism is cognitive dysfunction (seen in 60% to 90% of patients), ranging from brain fog and fatigue to impaired concentration, short-term memory difﬁculties, social withdrawal, psychomotor slowing, depression, and apathy to psychosis to depressed level of consciousness. 41 The observation of a delayed relaxation phase of muscle stretch reﬂexes (Woltman sign) can be a helpful clue to the FIGURE 5-4 Clinical features of hypothyroidism. Note the facial pufﬁness, thinning at the hairline, and tiny eyelashes (A), as well as dry ﬂaking skin (B). Reprinted with permission from Wijdicks, Oxford University Press 40 . © 2014 Oxford University Press. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 115 KEY POINTS •The most common neurologic manifestations of hyperthyroidism are enhanced physiologic tremor (seen in more than 75% of patients with hyperthyroidism) and hyperreﬂexia (seen in 38% of patients with hyperthyroidism). •Hyperthyroidism is associated with an increased risk of cardioembolic stroke mediated by the increased risk of atrial ﬁbrillation in patients with hyperthyroidism. •The most common neurologic symptom of hypothyroidism is cognitive dysfunction (seen in 60% to 90% of patients), ranging from brain fog and fatigue to impaired concentration, short-term memory difﬁculties, social withdrawal, psychomotor slowing, depression, and apathy to psychosis to depressed level of consciousness. diagnosis of hypothyroidism as the etiology of altered mental status. 42 Among patients 65 years or older, those with hypothyroidism have an 81% increased risk of having dementia. 30 However, the likelihood of discovering clinical hypothyroidism among patients with undifferentiated dementia is low (0.9%). 30 Rarely, patients with hypothyroidism may develop myxedema coma, characterized by encephalopathy and hypothermia, with some patients developing seizures, cardiac disturbances (eg, hypotension, bradycardia), respiratory dysfunction (eg, hypoventilation), and metabolic derangements (eg, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia). Myxedema coma may be precipitated in patients with hypothyroidism by factors such as infection, cold exposure, trauma, or the use of certain medications, particularly sedative drugs such as opioids. 43 Myxedema coma carries a high mortality rate if left untreated, but early treatment with thyroid hormone replacement and supportive care can lead to recovery. 43 Patients often require intensive care monitoring and a multidisciplinary approach to address the complex and multifaceted nature of this condition. Peripheral nervous system manifestations of hypothyroidism include myopathy and neuropathy. The myopathy (seen in 30% to 80% of patients with hypothyroidism) is characterized by proximal weakness accompanied by myalgia, muscle stiffness, and associated cramping pain; affected muscles may appear enlarged and exhibit myoedema, a mounding phenomenon of muscle in response to percussion. 33,44,45 In most patients with hypothyroid myopathy (60% to 90%), serum creatine kinase is elevated up to ten times the upper limit of normal. 44 The degree of elevation does not correlate with the severity of the myopathy. EMG is normal in most patients, although myopathic motor unit potentials may be seen. 44 Sensory-predominant polyneuropathies are also frequently encountered among patients with hypothyroidism (42%). 33 On the contrary, although many patients with polyneuropathy undergo thyroid function screening in their evaluation (more than 80% of patients), the results are rarely clinically meaningful. 46,47 In one study of 138 patients with polyneuropathy, 81% of patients underwent a serum TSH study, yet none had an abnormal result. 46 In another study of 225 patients with undifferentiated neuropathy, 195 (86.7%) underwent thyroid function studies, of which 14 (7.2%) were abnormal. 47 Among those with abnormal study results, the majority of patients were already known to have an underlying thyroid disorder, and the result had no meaningful impact on management in all cases. Thus, although neuropathy is frequently seen in the context of clinical thyroid disease, the value of routine screening for thyroid disease in undifferentiated neuropathy is limited compared with more high-yield tests such as blood glucose, serum B 12 with metabolites, and serum protein immunoﬁxation electrophoresis as recommended by the American Academy of Neurology (AAN) and the Association of Neuromuscular and Electrodiagnostic Medicine. 48 Less common neurologic manifestations of hypothyroidism include seizures, hearing loss, and truncal ataxia from cerebellar degeneration. 26 In general, the neurologic complications of hypothyroidism resolve with thyroid replacement therapy. However, the degree of recovery depends on the duration and severity of the hypothyroidism before treatment. 116 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Hashimoto encephalopathy (also called steroid-responsive encephalopathy associated with autoimmune thyroiditis) has been reported to be associated with autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto thyroiditis), but it can occur simply in the presence of antithyroid antibodies despite normal thyroid function. 49 This condition is characterized by the development of subacute-onset encephalopathy, positive serum antithyroid antibodies (antithyroglobulin and antithyroid peroxidase), and immunotherapy responsiveness in the absence of speciﬁc neuronal autoantibodies. Most patients with suspected Hashimoto encephalopathy have normal thyroid function tests at the time of evaluation. Of note, incidental antithyroid peroxidase antibodies occur frequently in the general population (13%) and even more frequently in those older than 60 years (20%). 50 At one institution, 73% of patients referred with suspected Hashimoto encephalopathy were ultimately found to have an alternative non–immune-mediated diagnosis, and more than one-half had no evidence of a primary neurologic disorder. 51 Therefore, the presence of antithyroid antibodies should not necessarily be interpreted to be causative of a patient’s clinical presentation, and other potential causes of subacute cognitive decline should be investigated. DISORDERS OF THE ADRENAL GLANDS The adrenal glands produce three hormones from the adrenal cortex (glucocorticoids [eg, cortisol], mineralocorticoids [eg, aldosterone], and gonadal hormones [eg, androgens]), as well as catecholamines from the adrenal medulla. These hormones serve to regulate metabolism, immune response, blood pressure, and response to stress. Neurologic complications of adrenal dysfunction are most commonly due to alternations in cortisol levels, which are inﬂuenced by ACTH production by the pituitary gland and through exogenous administration of corticosteroids. Adrenal Insufﬁciency Adrenal insufﬁciency is a condition characterized by inadequate production of hormones by the adrenal glands due to disruption in the hypothalamic- pituitary-adrenal axis. In primary adrenal insufﬁciency (Addison disease), there is decreased production of both glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids. 52 This results in elevated levels of ACTH from the pituitary gland and corticotropin-releasing hormone from the hypothalamus due to a lack of inhibitory feedback from the adrenal gland. Causes include autoimmune disease (eg, Addison disease or autoimmune adrenalitis), infections (eg, tuberculosis), metastatic cancer, or adrenal hemorrhage. In secondary adrenal insufﬁciency, pituitary pathology (eg, tumor, surgery, or radiation) results in insufﬁcient production of ACTH. This leads to a deﬁciency in cortisol production, with aldosterone production typically preserved because it is regulated primarily by the renin-angiotensin system. Secondary adrenal insufﬁciency can also develop in patients on chronic corticosteroid therapy (1% of the United States population 53 ) from suppression of the hypothalamic- pituitary-adrenal axis. The risk of adrenal insufﬁciency develops when doses of glucocorticoids are above physiologic daily cortisol production equivalents (eg, greater than 4 mg to 6 mg of prednisone) for more than 3 to 4 weeks and thereafter discontinued abruptly. 53 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 117 KEY POINTS •In most patients with hypothyroid myopathy (60% to 90%), serum creatine kinase will be elevated up to ten times the upper limit of normal. •Although many patients with polyneuropathy undergo thyroid function screening in their evaluation (more than 80% of patients), the results are rarely clinically meaningful. •Neurologic manifestations of adrenal insufﬁciency include fatigue, muscle weakness, and cognitive disturbances such as confusion, apathy, and other neuropsychiatric symptoms. •Severe adrenal insufﬁciency can result in adrenal crisis, characterized by profound hypotension, hypoglycemia, and electrolyte imbalances (notably hyponatremia and hyperkalemia), which may precipitate seizures or coma. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Neurologic manifestations of adrenal insufﬁciency include fatigue, muscle weakness, and cognitive disturbances such as confusion, apathy, and other neuropsychiatric symptoms. Severe adrenal insufﬁciency can result in adrenal crisis, characterized by profound hypotension, hypoglycemia, and electrolyte imbalances (notably hyponatremia and hyperkalemia), which may precipitate seizures or coma. Chronic adrenal insufﬁciency can also cause myopathy or neuropathy. The myopathy is characterized by proximal muscle weakness that may be associated with myalgias and muscle stiffness. EMG and serum creatine kinase are usually normal. 54 Muscle biopsy in this setting is generally unremarkable. Patients may also experience weakness due to hyperkalemia, which at times may mimic familial hyperkalemic periodic paralysis. 54 Neuropathy in patients with adrenal insufﬁciency is less common; when it occurs, it typically causes sensory disturbances or autonomic dysfunction. 52 Timely diagnosis and treatment of adrenal insufﬁciency with glucocorticoids, ﬂuid resuscitation, and correction of electrolyte imbalances generally improves neurologic manifestations. 52 Cushing Syndrome Cushing syndrome is a disorder that results from prolonged exposure to high levels of glucocorticoids, namely cortisol. This condition can be caused by various factors, including exogenous administration of glucocorticoids for medical conditions or endogenous overproduction of cortisol due to adrenal adenomas, adrenal carcinomas, or ectopic ACTH production by nonpituitary tumors. Cushing disease refers speciﬁcally to Cushing syndrome caused by overproduction of cortisol due to an ACTH- secreting pituitary adenoma. 55 This condition accounts for the majority of cases of Cushing syndrome due to endogenous cortisol excess (ie, as opposed to iatrogenic due to corticosteroid administration) and requires distinct diagnostic and therapeutic approaches as discussed previously in the section Pituitary Tumors. Systemic features of Cushing syndrome include central obesity, facial rounding (“moon facies”), dorsocervical fat pad accumulation (“buffalo hump”), acne, hirsutism, and skin changes FIGURE 5-5 Clinical features of Cushing syndrome. Classic clinical features of Cushing syndrome (red cheeks, dorsocervical fat pads, abdominal striae, easy bruising, pendulous abdomen, and thinning of arms and legs) are shown. Used with permission of Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. © 2005 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. 118 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

such as thinning, easy bruising, and abdominal stretch marks (“purple striae”) (FIGURE 5-5). Patients may also experience hypertension, glucose intolerance, and osteoporosis. 52 Neurologic manifestations of Cushing syndrome are varied, resulting from the direct effects of hypercortisolism on nervous system function or through secondary effects of metabolic and vascular changes. Patients with Cushing syndrome frequently report cognitive concerns (more than 80% of patients), including memory impairment, decreased concentration, and difﬁculties with executive functioning. 56 Prolonged elevation of cortisol levels has been associated with cortical and hippocampal atrophy. 57 Psychiatric manifestations are present in more than 50% of patients with Cushing syndrome, including depression, anxiety, insomnia, mania, and psychosis. 52 A proximal myopathy (steroid myopathy) develops in more than 60% of patients with Cushing syndrome from chronic glucocorticoid exposure. 52,58 This myopathy causes gradually progressive, painless weakness of the lower limbs more than the upper limbs. Serum creatine kinase and EMG are generally normal, although myopathic features without ﬁbrillation potentials may be seen. Muscle histopathology in these patients classically demonstrates selective type II ﬁber atrophy, particularly type IIb subtype ﬁbers. 52 The isolated involvement of type II ﬁbers likely accounts for the unremarkable EMG ﬁndings in these cases because the analysis of motor unit potentials is mainly limited to type I ﬁbers because these are the ﬁrst to be recruited with muscle contraction, concealing type II ﬁber abnormalities. 59 The severity of weakness is associated with both the level and duration of cortisol exposure (whether endogenous or exogenous corticosteroids). Myopathy in Cushing syndrome is generally reversible with treatment of the underlying cause of corticosteroid excess. In patients with Cushing disease, accompanying elevated mineralocorticoids can cause hypokalemia that further worsens muscle weakness. Polyneuropathy can also develop in patients with Cushing syndrome due to the metabolic disturbances associated with hypercortisolism, such as impaired glucose metabolism. 52 DISORDERS OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS The endocrine pancreas plays a crucial role in regulating blood glucose levels through the secretion of hormones such as insulin and glucagon. Insulin decreases blood glucose levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose into cells, whereas glucagon increases blood glucose levels by promoting the release of glucose from the liver. The balance between these hormones ensures proper glucose metabolism, which is essential for the normal functioning of various organ systems, including the nervous system. The most common cause of impaired glucose metabolism is diabetes, a highly prevalent, chronic metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia resulting from defects in insulin secretion, insulin action, or both. Diabetes can cause a wide variety of peripheral nervous system manifestations (eg, diabetic neuropathy, thoracic radiculopathy, diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy [DLRPN], treatment-induced neuropathy) and central nervous system manifestations (eg, elevated ischemic stroke risk and diabetic striatopathy). CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 119 KEY POINTS •Patients with Cushing syndrome frequently report cognitive concerns (more than 80% of patients), including memory impairment, decreased concentration, and difﬁculties with executive functioning. •A proximal myopathy develops in more than 60% of patients with Cushing syndrome, resulting from chronic glucocorticoid exposure (steroid myopathy). •An axonal peripheral neuropathy is the most common form of diabetic neuropathy. Classically, this presents as a gradually progressive, sensory (or sensory-predominant), length-dependent neuropathy. •Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is highly prevalent, encountered in nearly 30% of patients with diabetes. Both the duration and severity of diabetes are associated with the likelihood of developing neuropathy, as is the presence of other metabolic risk factors such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, and obesity. •American Academy of Neurology (AAN) guidelines recommend the use of four different medication classes for the treatment of painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy: gabapentinoids, serotonin norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs), tricyclic antidepressants, and sodium channel blockers such as lamotrigine or oxcarbazepine. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy An axonal peripheral neuropathy is the most common form of diabetic neuropathy. Classically, this disorder presents as a gradually progressive, sensory (or sensory-predominant), length-dependent neuropathy. Patients may initially be asymptomatic (ie, sensory dysfunction noted only on examination), before developing altered sensation in the feet that gradually spreads symmetrically to involve the calves and ankles before affecting the ﬁngers and hands in a stocking-glove distribution. Symptoms vary from patient to patient, with some reporting more negative symptoms such as anesthetic-like numbness and imbalance, and others reporting more positive symptoms such as tingling, burning pain with hyperalgesia. Neurologic examination may demonstrate small ﬁber sensory abnormalities, large ﬁber sensory abnormalities, or a combination of the two in a length-dependent pattern in addition to reduced or absent reﬂexes, most commonly the Achilles reﬂex. In severe cases, motor and autonomic involvement can develop. The former leads to weakness in a length-dependent pattern, whereas the latter leads to orthostatic intolerance, gastroparesis, urinary urgency, and sexual dysfunction. Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is highly prevalent, encountered in nearly 30% of patients with diabetes. 60,61 Both the duration and severity of diabetes (correlated with hemoglobin A 1C ) are associated with the likelihood of developing neuropathy, as is the presence of other metabolic risk factors such as hypertension, dyslipidemia, and obesity. 62,63 However, even mild glucose intolerance (eg, prediabetes with hemoglobin A 1C between 5.7% and 6.4%) has been associated with the development of diabetic neuropathy. 64 The pathophysiology of diabetic peripheral neuropathy is complex and involves metabolic, vascular, and neurotrophic factors. In addition to hyperglycemia-induced microangiopathy, chronic hyperglycemia contributes to the formation of advanced glycation end products, oxidative stress, and inﬂammation, all of which lead to neuronal injury. 65 The diagnosis of diabetic peripheral neuropathy is clinical. Nerve conduction studies and EMG are not recommended for characteristic presentations but should be performed in atypical cases (eg, motor- predominant, asymmetric, and rapid-onset presentations). 66 When performed, electrodiagnostic testing may demonstrate features of both axonal degeneration and demyelination; 67 however, although slowing of conduction velocity may be seen in patients with diabetic neuropathy, conduction block and temporal dispersion are not commonly seen, distinguishing diabetic neuropathy from demyelinating neuropathies such as chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP). 68 There are currently no disease-modifying therapies for diabetic peripheral neuropathy, so management is focused on risk factor modiﬁcation (glycemic control) to reduce disease progression, as well as management of neuropathic pain if present. 69 AAN guidelines recommend the use of four different medication classes for the treatment of painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy: gabapentinoids such as gabapentin and pregabalin, serotonin norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) such as duloxetine and venlafaxine, tricyclic antidepressants such as amitriptyline or nortriptyline, and sodium channel blockers such as lamotrigine or oxcarbazepine. 70 Topical agents (eg, capsaicin) may also be considered. 120 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Thoracic Radiculopathy Diabetic radiculopathy most commonly affects a single thoracic nerve root, 71 causing severe unilateral neuropathic pain in the thorax or abdomen that does not cross the midline. The pain is classically worse at night. Neurologic examination may demonstrate hypoesthesia or hyperalgesia in the affected dermatome. Patients often have abdominal tenderness that does not increase or decrease with contraction of the abdominal wall muscles. 72 The differential diagnosis includes intrathoracic or intra-abdominal pathologies, other causes of radiculopathy, and herpes zoster. Electrodiagnostic testing can be helpful to conﬁrm the diagnosis of diabetic thoracic radiculopathy, particularly demonstrating neurogenic abnormalities on needle EMG of the thoracic paraspinal or abdominal wall muscles. 67,73 The condition is generally self-limited and resolves within 6 to 12 weeks, although it can recur in the same or another dermatomal distribution. 72 Treatment is symptomatic with the same agents previously listed for painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy. In severe cases, a targeted local anesthetic block can be pursued. Diabetic Lumbosacral Radiculoplexus Neuropathy DLRPN is an immune-mediated condition affecting the lumbosacral nerve roots, lumbosacral plexus, and peripheral nerves. 74 This condition presents with unilateral acute-onset pain (described most often as sharp, lancinating pain; deep aching pain; or burning pain) and allodynia in the proximal lower extremity, followed by subacute development of weakness in proximal more than distal lower limb muscles. Over time, the initially unaffected leg will become involved in a similar fashion, and more rarely, the upper limbs (1.6% of patients) or trunk (12.9% of patients) can become affected. 75 The weakness can be severe, with nearly one-half of patients requiring a wheelchair at the nadir of illness. Although DLRPN is an uncommon disorder, its annual incidence in Olmsted County, Minnesota, has been reported to be 2.79 cases per 100,000 people, which is higher than the incidence of other inﬂammatory neuropathies such as Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome, CIDP, and inﬂammatory brachial plexus neuropathy (neuralgic amyotrophy) in the same population. 76 Of note, lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy can occur in patients who do not have diabetes, although patients with diabetes are 8 times more likely to develop the disorder. 76 Although the exact etiology of DLRPN is unknown, the disease process frequently occurs in the setting of recent and often rapid diabetes control and weight loss (generally more than 4.4 kg [10 lb]). 74 Although diabetes is the strongest risk factor for the development of lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy, other risk factors include comorbid autoimmune disorders, history of stroke, and elevated body mass index, suggesting that altered metabolism and immune dysfunction play signiﬁcant roles in the disease process. 77 Additionally, glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists used to treat obesity and diabetes have recently been demonstrated to be associated with an increased risk of developing DLRPN. 78 The underlying pathophysiology of DLRPN is thought to be focal microvasculitis and ischemic injury of individual nerves. 74,79 The diagnosis is based on clinical history, examination, and electrodiagnostic testing. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate low amplitude or absent compound muscle action potentials in the tibial and peroneal nerves, as well CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 121 KEY POINTS •Diabetic radiculopathy most commonly affects a single thoracic nerve root, causing severe unilateral neuropathic pain in the thorax or abdomen that does not cross the midline. •The incidence of diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy (DLRPN) in Olmsted County, Minnesota, has been reported to be 2.79 cases per 100,000 people, which is higher than the incidence of other inﬂammatory neuropathies such as Guillain-Barr´e syndrome, chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP), and inﬂammatory brachial plexus neuropathy (neuralgic amyotrophy) in the same population. •Additionally, glucagon- like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists used to treat obesity and diabetes have recently been demonstrated to be associated with an increased risk of developing DLRPN. •CSF studies should be obtained to evaluate for mimics of DLRPN, such as infectious radiculitis, malignant leptomeningeal involvement of nerve roots, and neurolymphomatosis. •The signiﬁcant improvement in pain among patients with DLRPN receiving IV corticosteroids suggests that early treatment with immunotherapy may be warranted. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

as a low amplitude or absent sural sensory nerve action potential. 75 EMG shows ﬁbrillation potentials and reduced recruitment of large motor unit potentials in affected muscles. These electrophysiologic ﬁndings are often patchy and more prominent in the more affected limb. MRI of the lumbosacral spine is generally normal but should be obtained to evaluate for structural radiculopathies as an alternative explanation for the presenting symptomatology. CASE 5-2 A 60-year-old woman with a 5-year history of type 2 diabetes presented to the neurology clinic with 6 months of progressive bilateral leg weakness and pain. Her symptoms began after aggressive treatment of her diabetes, resulting in improvement in her hemoglobin A 1C concentration from 11.3% to 7.8% over 3 months. Her symptoms started with a sudden onset of excruciating, shock-like pain in her left leg, which was followed by weakness and paresthesia that developed over days. Two weeks after onset, similar symptoms developed in her right leg. Over the following 2 months, her weakness progressively worsened, eventually requiring the use of a walker for ambulation. By the 4th month, she required a wheelchair. Neurologic examination was notable for severe (Medical Research Council grade 3/5) proximal and distal lower limb weakness with areﬂexia and distal sensory loss. MRI of the lumbosacral spine showed no structural abnormalities to account for her symptoms. MRI of the lumbosacral plexus showed T2 hyperintensity, enlargement, and enhancement of multiple lumbosacral nerve roots and the bilateral lumbosacral plexus (FIGURE 5-6A and FIGURE 5-6B). CSF analysis revealed elevated protein at 110 mg/dL (normal is less than 35 mg/dL), with normal total nucleated cell count, normal glucose, negative infectious studies (including herpes simplex virus and varicella-zoster virus polymerase chain reactions [PCRs]), and negative cytology. Because of the progressive nature of her symptoms, a left sural nerve biopsy was performed, revealing a diagnosis of microvasculitis (FIGURE 5-6C). The patient was started on a 12-week course of IV methylprednisolone. By the 6th week of treatment, she began to show signs of clinical improvement, particularly with respect to her pain. At the end of the 12-week course, she regained the ability to walk with a walker. By week 24, she was able to walk independently, demonstrating signiﬁcant functional recovery. COMMENT The presentation of severe, initially asymmetric lower limb pain followed by lower limb weakness in a patient with recent rapid improvement in diabetes control suggests diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexus neuropathy (DLRPN). As highlighted in this case, the diagnostic evaluation should involve studies to exclude mimics, including structural radicular etiologies and infectious or neoplastic causes of radiculitis. Immunotherapy with IV corticosteroids can signiﬁcantly improve pain in this condition. 122 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

MRI of the lumbosacral plexus will generally demonstrate T2 hyperintensity and thickening of the nerves of the lumbosacral plexus (as illustrated in CASE 5-2). 75 On postgadolinium fat-saturated sequences, enhancement throughout the plexus may be observed, although this is seen less frequently (13% of patients in one study). 75 CSF studies should be obtained to evaluate for mimics of DLRPN, such as infectious radiculitis, malignant leptomeningeal involvement of nerve roots, and neurolymphomatosis. CSF analysis in DLRPN generally demonstrates elevated protein without FIGURE 5-6 MRI and pathologic ﬁndings in the patient in CASE 5-2. A, T2-weighted oblique MRI of the lumbosacral plexus demonstrating nerve enlargement with associated T2 hyperintensity (arrows). B, T1-weighted postgadolinium oblique MRI of the lumbosacral plexus revealing associated gadolinium enhancement (arrows). C, Left sural nerve biopsy with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining highlighting perivascular inﬂammatory cell collections in a pattern consistent with microvasculitis. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 123 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

accompanying pleocytosis, supporting nerve root involvement in the underlying disease process. 79 No treatment has been clearly shown to effectively treat DLRPN. Because of the underlying immune-mediated pathophysiology of the disorder, attempts have been made to treat DLRPN with immunotherapy, namely corticosteroids and IV immunoglobulin. 80,81 A multicenter prospective, double-blinded, placebo-controlled trial of 75 patients with DLRPN compared the effectiveness of a 12-week regimen of IV methylprednisolone with placebo. 82 The primary outcome of the study (time to improvement in functional status based on the neuropathy impairment score) was not signiﬁcantly different between the two groups. However, many of the secondary outcome measures were signiﬁcantly improved in the treatment group, particularly those related to pain and positive sensory symptoms, and many patients were enrolled late in their disease course, often after having reached their disease nadir, at which point irreparable axonal loss may have already developed, suggesting exploration of earlier treatment trials. Ultimately, the signiﬁcant improvement in pain among patients with DLRPN receiving IV corticosteroids suggests that early treatment with immunotherapy may be warranted. Even without treatment, patients generally gradually improve over several months, although complete recovery is rare, and most patients have some residual neurologic deﬁcits such as pain, weakness, and sensory loss, resulting in ongoing functional impairment. 75 Treatment-Induced Neuropathy of Diabetes Treatment-induced neuropathy of diabetes (previously known as insulin neuritis) is a rare but signiﬁcant complication that occurs in the context of rapidly improved glycemic control. This condition is characterized by the onset of neuropathic pain and autonomic dysfunction following a substantial and rapid reduction in blood glucose levels. 83 Patients with treatment-induced neuropathy of diabetes typically present with acute-onset, severe, burning pain that symmetrically affects distal regions such as the feet and lower legs. The pain can be debilitating and is often accompanied by signs of autonomic dysfunction, including orthostatic intolerance, gastrointestinal disturbances, erectile dysfunction, and changes in heart rate or blood pressure. These symptoms typically appear within 8 weeks of initiation of intensive glycemic control. 84 The underlying pathophysiology of this disease process is not entirely understood, but it is postulated to involve microvascular injury to the nerves, similar to DLRPN. Rapid correction of hyperglycemia may lead to an abrupt change in the metabolic and vascular environments, causing nerve ischemia and subsequent neuropathy. This phenomenon can be particularly pronounced in patients who have experienced prolonged periods of poor glycemic control before rapid correction. The risk of developing the syndrome is directly proportional to the magnitude and rate of change in hemoglobin A 1C . One 2015 study found that an absolute decrease in hemoglobin A 1C of 2% to 3% over 3 months confers a 20% risk of developing treatment-induced neuropathy, and a drop of more than 4% confers a greater than 80% risk. 83 The diagnosis of treatment-induced neuropathy is primarily clinical, based on the characteristic symptoms in the context of recent rapid glycemic control. Electrodiagnostic studies are frequently normal because the disease process 124 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

predominantly affects small nerve ﬁbers. Currently, there is no speciﬁc treatment for reversing treatment-induced neuropathy of diabetes. Management focuses on symptomatic relief of neuropathic pain and supportive care for autonomic dysfunction. Medications used for pain management in diabetic peripheral neuropathy, such as gabapentinoids, SNRIs, tricyclic antidepressants, and sodium channel blockers, may be used. Stable glucose control and prevention of labile glycemic levels is important to prevent recurrence. Liberalizing blood glucose control is not recommended. The prognosis for treatment-induced neuropathy of diabetes varies. Although some patients experience a gradual improvement in symptoms over time, others may have persistent pain and autonomic dysfunction. Close monitoring and supportive care remain critical components of managing this challenging condition. 83,84 Central Nervous System Manifestations of Diabetes Individuals with diabetes have a signiﬁcantly increased risk of stroke compared with the general population. The mechanisms behind this increased risk include accelerated atherosclerosis as well as comorbid hypertension and hyperlipidemia, which are common in patients with diabetes. The pathophysiology of stroke in patients with diabetes is multifactorial. Chronic hyperglycemia leads to the formation of advanced glycation end products, which contribute to vascular inﬂammation and endothelial dysfunction. This, in turn, accelerates the development of atherosclerotic plaques in the cerebral arteries, increasing the risk of both ischemic and hemorrhagic strokes. Additionally, diabetes is associated with increased platelet aggregation and coagulation abnormalities, further predisposing individuals to thrombotic events. 85 Diabetic ketoacidosis (more common in patients with type 1 diabetes) and nonketotic hyperosmolar coma (more common in patients with type 2 diabetes) are acute, life- threatening complications of diabetes characterized by severe hyperglycemia and ketoacidosis (in diabetic ketoacidosis). The primary neurologic manifestation is encephalopathy, ranging from mild alteration in consciousness to coma. Cerebral edema can rarely occur. Treatment of diabetic ketoacidosis and nonketotic hyperosmolar coma involves aggressive ﬂuid resuscitation, close monitoring and correction of electrolytes FIGURE 5-7 Neuroimaging features of diabetic striatopathy. Axial T1-weighted MRI demonstrating left putaminal T1 hyperintensity in a patient with poorly controlled diabetes (hemoglobin A 1C 14.4%) presenting with 1 week of chorea involving the right arm. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 125 KEY POINTS •Treatment-induced neuropathy of diabetes (previously known as insulin neuritis) is a rare but signiﬁcant complication that occurs in the context of rapidly improved glycemic control. •Patients with treatment- induced neuropathy of diabetes typically present with acute-onset, severe, burning pain that symmetrically affects distal regions such as the feet and lower legs. •Individuals with diabetes have a signiﬁcantly increased risk of stroke compared with the general population. •Diabetic striatopathy is a rare (prevalence of 1 in 100,000 people) neurologic complication of diabetes characterized by the development of unilateral hemichorea or hemiballism. This condition is associated with poorly controlled blood glucose, with an average hemoglobin A 1C of 13% at presentation. •Rarely, individuals with poorly controlled diabetes may present with occipital lobe seizures, particularly in the context of nonketotic hyperosmolar coma. Patients frequently describe a homonymous hemianopsia, with some endorsing superimposed visual hallucinations (eg, ﬂashing lights, patterns, or shapes). Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

(particularly potassium), and gradual correction of hyperglycemia with insulin. With appropriate treatment, the prognosis is favorable. 86 Diabetic striatopathy is a rare (prevalence of 1 in 100,000 people) neurologic complication of diabetes characterized by the development of unilateral hemichorea or hemiballism. 87 This condition is associated with poorly controlled blood glucose, with an average hemoglobin A 1C of 13% at presentation. 87 MRI demonstrates unilateral T1 hyperintensity in the striatum contralateral to the unilateral movement disorder (FIGURE 5-7). 88,89 With gradual improvement in glycemic control, most patients (76%) will achieve symptom resolution in days to weeks, although symptoms may recur in approximately 18% of patients. 87 Addition of symptomatic medications (eg, dopamine antagonists) to control chorea may be indicated if the movement disorder is severe and disabling. Rarely, individuals with poorly controlled diabetes may present with occipital lobe seizures, particularly in the context of nonketotic hyperosmolar coma. 90 Patients frequently describe a homonymous hemianopsia, with some endorsing superimposed visual hallucinations (eg, ﬂashing lights, patterns, or shapes). EEG demonstrates seizures arising from the occipital region contralateral to the patient’s visual symptoms. 91 MRI may show T2 hypointensity in the subcortical white matter with T2 hyperintensity in the overlying cortex in regions of the occipital lobe corresponding to the contralateral visual symptoms (FIGURE 5-8). 92,93 With treatment of hyperglycemia, symptoms generally resolve, and follow-up imaging weeks after presentation is frequently normal. CONCLUSION Neurologic complications of endocrine disorders can affect any component of the nervous system and may be the initial manifestation of an undiagnosed underlying endocrine condition. Therefore, neurologists should be familiar with the potential neurologic manifestations of endocrine disorders as well as which endocrine disorders to consider in various neurologic presentations. As FIGURE 5-8 Neuroimaging features of hyperglycemia- associated occipital lobe seizures. Axial ﬂuid- attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI demonstrating right occipital lobe T2 hypointensity in the subcortical white matter with T2 hyperintensity in the overlying occipital cortex in a patient presenting with hyperglycemia (blood glucose 577 mg/dL), left homonymous hemianopia, and superimposed visual hallucinations of ﬂashing lights in the left visual ﬁeld. With correction of the hyperglycemia, the patient’s symptoms and neuroimaging abnormalities resolved. 126 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF ENDOCRINE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

our understanding of these intricate relationships between endocrine and neurologic health deepens, so too does our ability to provide targeted and compassionate care to patients affected by these disorders. Neurologic Complications of Hematologic Disorders By Lauren Patrick, MD; Mark Terrelonge, MD, MPH ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article reviews the epidemiology, clinical presentation, diagnosis, and management of neurologic complications associated with hematologic disorders. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Hematologic disorders may manifest with cerebrovascular and neuromuscular complications due to thrombotic, hemorrhagic, or immune-mediated mechanisms. Advances in screening, targeted therapies, and interdisciplinary care have improved outcomes for conditions such as sickle cell disease, myeloproliferative neoplasms, thrombotic microangiopathies, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, and plasma cell dyscrasias. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Neurologic complications of hematologic disorders range from cerebrovascular disease to peripheral neuropathy. Early recognition and appropriate management in collaboration with a hematologist are essential to reduce morbidity and mortality. INTRODUCTION N eurologic complications of hematologic disorders are frequently encountered in clinical practice and can involve both the central and peripheral nervous systems. These complications may arise from altered blood viscosity, thrombosis, hemorrhage, or autoimmune phenomena. Prompt recognition of these manifestations allows for targeted diagnostic evaluation and prompt initiation of appropriate therapy in collaboration with hematologists. This article reviews the neurologic manifestations of red blood cell disorders (sickle cell disease, paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, and polycythemia vera), platelet disorders (immune thrombocytopenia [ITP], heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, and essential thrombocythemia), thrombotic microangiopathies, disorders of coagulation (inherited thrombophilia and antiphospholipid antibody syndrome), and plasma cell disorders (multiple myeloma; Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia; polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, and skin changes [POEMS] syndrome; and AL amyloidosis) (TABLE 6-1). TABLE 6-1 Neurologic Complications of Hematologic Disorders Disorder Neurologic manifestations When to suspect Diagnostic evaluation Treatment Sickle cell disease Silent cerebral infarcts, ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, cerebral venous thrombosis, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) Pediatric or young adult patients with sickle cell disease and acute neurologic symptoms Brain MRI, transcranial Doppler ultrasonography, hemoglobin S quantiﬁcation, vascular imaging for moyamoya Exchange transfusion (acute), long-term transfusions, hydroxyurea, surgical revascularization Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria Ischemic stroke, cerebral venous thrombosis Stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis with unexplained cytopenia, Coombs- negative hemolysis, or aplastic anemia Flow cytometry for glycosylphosphatidylinositol- anchored proteins Complement inhibition (ravulizumab), anticoagulation, platelet transfusion if thrombocytopenic Polycythemia vera Ischemic stroke, cerebral venous thrombosis, intracerebral hemorrhage, chorea, cognitive changes Stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis with unexplained erythrocytosis, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, or low erythropoietin Complete blood cell count, erythropoietin level, JAK2 variant testing Hematocrit control (<45%), phlebotomy, aspirin, hydroxyurea or interferon (if high risk), anticoagulation if prior thrombosis Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) Hemorrhagic stroke, ischemic stroke, cerebral venous thrombosis(in vaccine- induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia) Stroke or hemorrhage with isolated thrombocytopenia Complete blood cell count, rule out secondary causes (eg, autoimmune disease, infection, malignancy) Corticosteroids, IV immunoglobulin (IVIg), platelet transfusion if life-threatening hemorrhage Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia Ischemic stroke, cerebral venous thrombosis Thrombosis or stroke within 5-10 days of heparin exposure and platelet decrease >50% 4Ts score, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia antibody testing Stop heparin, start nonheparin anticoagulant (argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux), IVIg for autoimmune heparin-induced thrombocytopenia Essential thrombocythemia Ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, subarachnoid hemorrhage Stroke or hemorrhage with persistent, unexplained thrombocytosis Complete blood cell count, JAK2, CALR, and MPL variant testing Aspirin, cytoreduction (hydroxyurea or interferon), manage cardiovascular risk factors Thrombotic microangiopathies (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, hemolytic uremic syndrome, disseminated intravascular coagulation) Encephalopathy, seizures, ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke Stroke or altered mental status with thrombocytopenia, anemia, and schistocytes Peripheral blood smear, ADAMTS13 activity, lactate dehydrogenase, haptoglobin, coagulation studies Plasma exchange, steroids (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura), treat underlying cause (disseminated intravascular coagulation), supportive care CONTINUED ON PAGE 133 132 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. DISORDERS OF RED BLOOD CELLS The most common neurologic manifestations of red blood cell disorders are cerebrovascular: ischemic stroke, intracranial hemorrhage, and cerebral venous thrombosis. This section reviews the epidemiology, diagnosis, and treatment of cerebrovascular and other neurologic complications of sickle cell disease, polycythemia vera, and paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria. Red blood cell disorders contribute to cerebrovascular disease through impaired blood ﬂow, increased viscosity, and abnormal clot formation, leading to thrombotic or hemorrhagic events. CONTINUED FROM PAGE 132 Disorder Neurologic manifestations When to suspect Diagnostic evaluation Treatment Inherited thrombophilia Ischemic stroke via paradoxical embolism, cerebral venous thrombosis Patients younger than 50 years with cryptogenic stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis and personal or family history of venous thromboembolism Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, protein C and protein S, antithrombin, factor VIII Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin), anticoagulation if venous thromboembolism Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome Ischemic stroke, cerebral venous thrombosis, transient ischemic attack Cryptogenic stroke or transient ischemic attack in patients younger than 50 years or cerebral venous thrombosis with history of autoimmune disease or pregnancy loss Lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-β2 glycoprotein-I (IgG and IgM), repeated after 12 weeks Warfarin preferred, avoid direct oral anticoagulants in arterial events Plasma cell disorders Peripheral neuropathy (axonal or demyelinating), hyperviscosity-related stroke or confusion Progressive neuropathy with monoclonal gammopathy, evidence of systemic myeloma or monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined signiﬁcance (MGUS) Serum and urine protein electrophoresis with immunoﬁxation, serum free light chains, bone marrow biopsy, nerve conduction studies and EMG Treat underlying plasma cell disorder; immunotherapy for paraproteinemic neuropathies, plasmapheresis for hyperviscosity Lymphoma Neurolymphomatosis, paraneoplastic neuropathy, myelopathy, stroke from hypercoagulability Multifocal or painful neuropathy, cranial neuropathies, or meningitis with malignant or autoimmune features Brain and spine MRI with contrast, CSF cytology and ﬂow cytometry, whole-body positron emission tomography-computed tomography (PET-CT), nerve biopsy if neurolymphomatosis is suspected High-dose methotrexate for central nervous system disease, systemic or targeted chemotherapy for systemic lymphoma, steroids for acute symptoms, radiation for focal lesions CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 133 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology.
