Sickle Cell Disease Sickle cell disease is an autosomal recessive hemoglobinopathy affecting nearly 8 million individuals globally, with the highest burden in sub-Saharan Africa. 1 In the United States, approximately 120,000 individuals have sickle cell disease, which is more common in people of African ancestry, with a prevalence of 1 in 365 African American newborns. 2 Despite advances in newborn screening and treatment, many patients experience preventable complications (including neurologic complications such as stroke) due to health care disparities in access to these advances. 1 Sickle cell disease results from a point variant in the HBB gene, producing hemoglobin S. 3 Under hypoxic conditions, hemoglobin S polymerizes, leading to erythrocyte sickling, microvascular occlusion, endothelial activation, inﬂammation, and hypercoagulability. Chronic hemolysis exacerbates anemia and can cause acute pain crises due to tissue ischemia and end organ damage, including acute chest syndrome and renal, hepatic, and cardiac failure, TABLE 6-2 Evidence-Based Recommendations From the American Society of Hematology 2020 Guidelines for the Management of Cerebrovascular Disease in Patients With Sickle Cell Disease a u Annual transcranial Doppler ultrasonography is recommended for children (2-16 years) with sickle cell disease for the primary prevention of stroke u For children (2-16 years) who have abnormal transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities, regular blood transfusions are recommended (if feasible) to maintain hemoglobin S levels below 30% and hemoglobin levels >9 g/dL for stroke prevention u For children (2-16 years) with abnormal transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities who have been receiving transfusion therapy for at least 1 year and are interested in stopping transfusions, hydroxyurea therapy is suggested as a reasonable substitute for ongoing primary stroke prevention u Immediate blood transfusion is recommended for patients with sickle cell disease presenting with acute neurologic deﬁcits, including possible cerebral ischemia; exchange transfusion is suggested over simple transfusion, although simple transfusion can be performed to avoid delays in treatment of >2 hours for presenting hemoglobin levels ≤8.5 g/dL u Evaluation for revascularization surgery is suggested for patients with sickle cell disease who develop moyamoya syndrome with a history of stroke or transient ischemic attack u For adult patients with sickle cell disease presenting with acute ischemic stroke otherwise meeting eligibility criteria, IV thrombolysis is suggested but should not delay blood transfusion therapy; IV thrombolysis is not recommended for children with sickle cell disease younger than 18 years u One-time MRI screening is recommended for early school-age children and suggested for adults with sickle cell disease to detect silent cerebral infarcts u Referral for neuropsychological testing is recommended for patients with sickle cell disease and cognitive impairment a Data from DeBaun MR, et al, Blood Adv. 4 134 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

increasing morbidity and mortality. 1 Severe genotypes of sickle cell disease include hemoglobin SS and hemoglobin Sβ° thalassemia, although other clinically signiﬁcant forms, such as hemoglobin SC disease, hemoglobin Sβ + thalassemia, and other compound heterozygous variants, can also present with systemic complications. 4 The most common neurologic complication of sickle cell disease is ischemic stroke, which may be subclinical (referred to as silent cerebral infarct) or clinically apparent. Sickle cell disease can also cause moyamoya vasculopathy, intracranial hemorrhage, cerebral venous thrombosis, and posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Guidelines from the American Society of Hematology for the management of cerebrovascular disease in patients with sickle cell disease are included in TABLE 6-2. SILENT CEREBRAL INFARCTS. Silent cerebral infarcts are MRI-detected lesions that lack overt clinical symptoms but contribute to cognitive impairment and are associated with an increased risk of clinical stroke. Silent cerebral infarcts occur in nearly 40% of children with hemoglobin SS or hemoglobin Sβ° thalassemia by age 18 years and more than 50% of adults by the age of 30 years. 4 The SIT trial (Silent Cerebral Infarct Transfusion Multi-Center Trial) demonstrated that long-term transfusion therapy reduces the risk of overt stroke and silent cerebral infarct progression. 5 ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE. Children and adults with sickle cell disease face a high risk of ischemic stroke, particularly in low-resource settings in which preventive measures such as exchange transfusion are limited. 4 In the absence of primary stroke prevention measures, the previous incidence of ﬁrst stroke by 20 years of age, 30 years of age, and 45 years of age in patients with sickle cell disease were estimated at 11%, 15%, and 24%, respectively, in patients with hemoglobin SS; the incidence of stroke in patients with hemoglobin SC ranged from 2% to 10%. 6 In patients with sickle cell disease, most ischemic strokes arise from large artery occlusion and watershed infarcts due to moyamoya vasculopathy. 7 Chronic hemolysis-induced endothelial injury, abnormal vasoconstriction, and intimal hyperplasia lead to progressive stenosis of the distal internal carotid and proximal anterior and middle cerebral arteries, triggering the formation of fragile collateral vessels. 8 Patients with hemoglobin SS and hemoglobin Sβ° thalassemia have the highest risk of developing moyamoya, whereas those with hemoglobin SC disease have a lower but still elevated risk. 6 Other potential stroke mechanisms in patients with sickle cell disease include chronic inﬂammation and hypercoagulability. PRIMARY STROKE PREVENTION. Transcranial Doppler ultrasonography monitoring is essential for stroke prevention in patients with sickle cell disease. The STOP trial (Stroke Prevention Trial in Sickle Cell Anemia) found that elevated middle cerebral artery blood ﬂow velocity predicts stroke risk and that long-term transfusion therapy reduces stroke risk by 93%. 9 The STOP 2 trial conﬁrmed that stopping transfusions leads to stroke recurrence. 10 The TWiTCH (TCD With Transfusions Changing to Hydroxyurea) trial showed that hydroxyurea at maximum tolerated dosage is a viable alternative to CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 135 KEY POINTS •Neurologic complications of sickle cell disease include ischemic stroke, moyamoya vasculopathy, intracranial hemorrhage, cerebral venous thrombosis, and posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). •Silent cerebral ischemia in patients with sickle cell disease is common, leading to cognitive impairment and elevated stroke risk. Pediatric patients often experience ischemic stroke, whereas adults are prone to intracranial hemorrhage due to moyamoya syndrome and hypercoagulability. •Monitoring intracranial arterial velocities with transcranial Doppler ultrasonography is crucial in predicting stroke risk in patients with sickle cell disease, and both hydroxyurea and red blood cell transfusions have been shown to reduce stroke risk in sickle cell disease. •Stroke should be suspected in any patient with sickle cell disease who has an acute onset of neurologic symptoms. Acute stroke treatment requires urgent red blood cell standard or exchange transfusion. •Long-term red blood cell transfusions are necessary for secondary stroke prevention in patients with sickle disease. Surgical revascularization is considered for patients with sickle cell disease as well as stroke and vasculopathy. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

long-term transfusion therapy in children with abnormal transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities but without severe vasculopathy. 11 Annual transcranial Doppler ultrasonography is recommended for children 2 years to 16 years with hemoglobin SS or hemoglobin Sβ° thalassemia. Current guidelines from the American Society of Hematology do not recommend routine transcranial Doppler ultrasound screening for adults with hemoglobin SS or patients of any age with hemoglobin SC because its utility in these groups has not been established. Surveillance may be considered on a case-by-case basis for adults with moyamoya or a history of stroke. 4 ACUTE STROKE TREATMENT. Acute stroke management in patients with sickle cell disease focuses on reducing hemoglobin S levels to less than 30% by emergent red blood cell transfusion. 3 Exchange transfusion is preferred over simple transfusion because exchange transfusion more effectively reduces hemoglobin S burden and lowers recurrent stroke risk ﬁvefold. 3 IV thrombolysis may be considered in adults with sickle cell disease–associated acute ischemic stroke, particularly patients with additional vascular risk factors because the risk of thrombolysis-associated intracranial hemorrhage in patients with sickle cell disease (4.9%) is comparable with that in the general population (3.2%). 12 SECONDARY STROKE PREVENTION. For secondary stroke prevention, long-term transfusion therapy remains the standard of care because hydroxyurea alone was insufﬁcient in the SWiTCH (Stroke With Transfusions Changing to Hydroxyurea) trial. 13 Antiplatelet therapy remains controversial but may be considered in patients with conventional vascular risk factors in addition to sickle cell disease. 3 SURGICAL INTERVENTIONS. Surgical revascularization is an option for severe vasculopathy, including moyamoya syndrome and critical arterial stenosis, because in patients with moyamoya syndrome, the risk of stroke can still be high despite long-term transfusion therapy. 3 Retrospective studies suggest indirect bypass procedures such as encephaloduroarteriosynangiosis may reduce stroke risk (CASE 6-1). 14 INTRACRANIAL HEMORRHAGE. Adults with sickle cell disease have a higher risk of hemorrhagic stroke than children with sickle cell disease, including subarachnoid and intracerebral hemorrhage. 3 Risk factors include prior ischemic stroke, moyamoya vasculopathy, intracranial aneurysm, and hypertension. CEREBRAL VENOUS THROMBOSIS. Cerebral venous thrombosis is an underrecognized complication of sickle cell disease linked to hypercoagulability from hemolysis, dehydration, and iron overload due to long-term transfusion therapy. 3 Anticoagulation is the standard treatment as is used for cerebral venous thrombosis in other contexts. POSTERIOR REVERSIBLE ENCEPHALOPATHY SYNDROME. PRES has been reported in patients with sickle cell disease, particularly in the setting of acute hypertension, transfusion therapy, or vasoocclusive crisis. 3 Management includes blood pressure control and supportive care. 136 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Paroxysmal Nocturnal Hemoglobinuria Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria is an acquired clonal disorder of hematopoietic stem cells caused by a somatic variant in the X-linked phosphatidylinositol glycan anchor biosynthesis class A protein (PIGA) gene. 15 This variation affects complement binding on red blood cells, resulting in complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis, platelet activation, and increased risk of thrombosis. Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria most commonly presents in the third decade of life and can manifest as fatigue, dyspnea, abdominal pain, hemoglobinuria, and smooth muscle dystonia, such as esophageal spasm and erectile dysfunction. Potentially life-threatening systemic complications include chronic kidney disease, pulmonary hypertension, and venous and arterial thromboembolic events, with venous events (eg, Budd-Chiari syndrome, deep vein thrombosis [DVT], pulmonary embolism) far more common than arterial events. 15,16 Patients with paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria have an increased risk of ischemic stroke and cerebral venous thrombosis, with cerebral venous thrombosis accounting for 9.1% of all thrombotic events in one study. 16 Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria should be considered in patients with cryptogenic arterial stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis who have unexplained Coombs-negative hemolysis, aplastic anemia, leukopenia, or thrombocytopenia. 15 Diagnosis is conﬁrmed with ﬂow cytometry demonstrating deﬁcient glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored proteins on red blood cells. Although paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria was previously associated with high morbidity and mortality, survival has signiﬁcantly improved with complement inhibition therapy. 15 Complement inhibition with ravulizumab is used to treat paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria in patients without bone marrow failure; allogeneic stem cell transplant is used in patients with bone marrow failure. 17 Anticoagulation is used in patients with arterial stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis, with platelet transfusion considered in the setting of thrombocytopenia. 15 Polycythemia Vera Polycythemia vera is a myeloproliferative neoplasm caused by somatic JAK2 variants, leading to erythrocytosis, hyperviscosity, and increased thrombotic risk. 18 Common manifestations include palpable splenomegaly, erythromelalgia, pruritus, transient visual disturbances, headaches, and thrombotic events. Thrombosis is a common presenting feature, occurring before or at the time of diagnosis as arterial events (16%), venous events (7.4%), or major hemorrhage (4.2%); 19 ischemic stroke accounts for approximately 40% of arterial events, and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis for 1% of venous events. 20 Chorea and neuropsychiatric disturbances, including delirium, dementia, abulia, depression, and frontal lobe syndromes, may occur in patients with polycythemia vera and may be the presenting feature. 21 Polycythemia vera should be suspected in patients with ischemic stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis accompanied by unexplained erythrocytosis (hemoglobin greater than 18.5 g/dL), hematocrit greater than 55%, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, or low erythropoietin levels. 22 Initial evaluation should include a complete blood cell count, erythropoietin level, and JAK2 variant testing. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 137 KEY POINTS •Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria should be considered in patients with cryptogenic ischemic stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis, especially when accompanied by hemolytic anemia or aplastic anemia and bone marrow failure. •Consider polycythemia vera in patients presenting with ischemic stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis who have unexplained erythrocytosis, leukocytosis, thrombocytosis, or low erythropoietin levels. •For patients with polycythemia vera and thrombosis, maintain hematocrit less than 45% and initiate antithrombotic and cytoreductive therapy. •Neurologists should suspect immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) in patients with stroke or intracranial hemorrhage and isolated thrombocytopenia, especially when secondary causes of thrombocytopenia are absent. •Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia presents 5 to 10 days after heparin exposure with thrombocytopenia and arterial thrombosis, venous thrombosis, or both; urgent heparin cessation and nonheparin anticoagulation are critical. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Treatment of polycythemia vera is with cytoreductive therapy using phlebotomy, hydroxyurea, and pegylated interferon targeting a hematocrit less than 45%. 23 Patients with a history of polycythemia vera–associated arterial thrombosis should be treated with aspirin and vascular risk factor modiﬁcation in addition to cytoreductive therapy; patients with venous thrombosis should be treated with anticoagulation in addition to cytoreductive therapy. CASE 6-1 A 10-year-old boy with a history of sickle cell disease complicated by splenic sequestration prompting splenectomy, remote acute chest syndrome, silent cerebral infarcts, and asymptomatic cerebral vasculopathy presented to the pediatric neurology clinic for follow-up. He had been diagnosed with hemoglobin SS sickle cell disease on newborn screening and prescribed hydroxyurea, aspirin, and penicillin prophylaxis, and he had no history of focal neurologic deﬁcits or cognitive changes. His transcranial Doppler ultrasonography demonstrated elevated velocities in the bilateral middle cerebral arteries (right middle cerebral artery 182 cm/s, left middle cerebral artery 177 cm/s) and a brain MRI and brain magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) showed scattered subcortical T2 hyperintense foci in the right greater than left middle cerebral artery-anterior cerebral artery watershed territories consistent with ischemic infarcts and narrowing of the bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries (FIGURE 6-1). His neurologic examination was entirely normal. He was referred for digital subtraction angiography, which showed mild to moderate non–ﬂow-limiting stenosis of the bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries without evidence of moyamoya collateral vessels (FIGURE 6-2). He was started on long-term transfusion therapy for silent cerebral infarcts and cerebral vasculopathy. At an interval clinic visit 1 year later, surveillance transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities improved, and MRI and MRA showed stable ﬁndings. One year later, he reported a transient episode of left arm and leg weakness lasting 2 minutes. His neurologic examination remained normal. Surveillance transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities were elevated, with right middle cerebral artery velocity of 202 cm/s and left middle cerebral artery velocity of 196 cm/s. Brain MRI showed an increased burden of T2 white matter hyperintense changes and interval progression of bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid artery stenoses on MRA (FIGURE 6-3). He was referred for repeat digital subtraction angiography, which conﬁrmed worsening vasculopathy, most notable in the bilateral supraclinoid internal carotid arteries, and interval development of moyamoya collateral vessels (FIGURE 6-3). Given the evidence of progressive vasculopathy and possible transient ischemic attack, he was referred to a neurosurgeon for consideration of cerebral revascularization. He underwent indirect cerebral revascularization without complication and with no evidence of progressive silent cerebral infarct or further neurologic symptoms. 138 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

DISORDERS OF PLATELET FUNCTION Disorders of platelet function can lead to both thrombotic and hemorrhagic neurologic complications. This section reviews the epidemiology, diagnosis, and treatment of cerebrovascular complications of ITP, heparin-induced thrombocytopenia, and essential thrombocythemia. Platelet dysfunction can result in both excessive clot formation (leading to thrombotic events such as FIGURE 6-1 Imaging of the patient in CASE 6-1 with sickle cell disease. Axial and coronal ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequence showing right periventricular (A, B, arrows) and left periventricular white matter hyperintense lesions (C, D, arrows), with cystic encephalomalacia. This ﬁgure demonstrates lesions at least 3 mm in dimension that are present in two planes on the FLAIR sequence; in the setting of a normal neurologic examination and no history of neurologic symptoms, these ﬁndings are consistent with silent cerebral infarcts. CONTINUED ON PAGE 140 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 139 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 6-1 CONTINUED FROM PAGE 139 FIGURE 6-2 Imaging of the patient in CASE 6-1 with sickle cell disease. A, Anteroposterior view cerebral digital subtraction angiogram with right internal carotid artery injection showing mild-moderate, non–ﬂow-limiting stenosis of the right supraclinoid internal carotid artery (arrow). B, Anteroposterior view cerebral digital subtraction angiogram with left internal carotid artery injection showing mild, non–ﬂow-limiting stenosis of the left supraclinoid internal carotid artery consistent with early moyamoya vasculopathy (arrow). COMMENT This case underscores the critical role of regular transcranial Doppler ultrasonography and MRI screening in stroke risk assessment and prevention in patients with sickle cell disease. The patient initially presented with elevated transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities, silent cerebral ischemia detected on screening MRI, and early signs of moyamoya syndrome on MRA, necessitating intensiﬁed transfusion therapy and ongoing surveillance. Despite initial stability, the patient experienced progressive worsening of vasculopathy and increased transcranial Doppler ultrasound velocities, indicating a heightened risk of overt stroke. Unfortunately, many patients with sickle cell disease continue to develop worsening silent cerebral infarcts and progressive vasculopathy despite optimal blood transfusion therapy and maintenance of normal hemoglobin levels. The correlation between advancing vasculopathy and progressive infarcts on MRI in patients with sickle cell disease requires consideration of cerebral revascularization alongside ongoing transfusion therapy. 140 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 6-1 FIGURE 6-3 Imaging of the patient in CASE 6-1 with sickle cell disease. A, Axial ﬂuid- attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequence showing bilateral subcortical white matter hyperintense lesions (arrows), increased compared with imaging in FIGURE 6-1. Diagnostic anteroposterior (B) and sagittal (C) view cerebral digital subtraction angiogram with right internal carotid artery injection showing moderate-severe stenosis of the right supraclinoid internal carotid artery (arrow) with marked proliferation of the lenticulostriate arteries (“puff of smoke”; arrowhead). Diagnostic anteroposterior (D) and sagittal (E) view cerebral digital subtraction angiogram with left internal carotid artery injection showing moderate stenosis of the left supraclinoid internal carotid artery, consistent with progressively worsening moyamoya vasculopathy (arrow). CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 141 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

ischemic stroke) and impaired clotting (contributing to hemorrhagic events such as intracranial hemorrhage). Immune Thrombocytopenia ITP is an autoimmune disorder deﬁned by a platelet count less than 100 × 10 9 /L that may be primary or secondary to conditions such as systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, infections, or medications. 24 ITP increases the risk of both bleeding and thrombosis. Intracranial hemorrhage may occur with severe thrombocytopenia (especially platelet counts less than 30 × 10 9 /L), and ischemic stroke may result from platelet microparticle–mediated endothelial dysfunction, particularly in older patients or patients receiving thrombopoietin receptor agonists. 24 Cerebral venous thrombosis has been reported in the context of vaccine- induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia, a very rare complication of adenoviral COVID-19 vaccines, occurring in approximately 3 to 15 people per 1 million initial vaccinations. 25 Neurologists should suspect ITP in patients with stroke or intracranial hemorrhage and isolated thrombocytopenia, especially when secondary causes of thrombocytopenia are absent. Treatment focuses on preventing clinically signiﬁcant bleeding rather than normalizing platelet counts. First-line treatments include corticosteroids or IV immunoglobulin, with platelet transfusions reserved for life-threatening hemorrhage. 26 Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is an immune-mediated prothrombotic disorder caused by antibodies against platelet factor 4–heparin complexes, typically developing 5 to 10 days after heparin exposure and associated with a decrease in platelet count of more than 50%. 27 Both arterial and venous thromboses may occur, including ischemic stroke and, rarely, cerebral venous thrombosis. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia should be suspected in patients with stroke in the setting of recent heparin use and thrombocytopenia. The diagnosis is based on clinical scoring using the 4Ts score, which assesses thrombocytopenia severity, thrombosis, timing of thrombocytopenia after heparin exposure, and other causes of thrombocytopenia. Conﬁrmatory antibody testing is used in intermediate- or high-risk cases. 27 Immediate discontinuation of heparin is essential, and anticoagulation should be initiated with a nonheparin agent such as argatroban, bivalirudin, fondaparinux, or danaparoid. Warfarin is contraindicated in patients with acute heparin-induced thrombocytopenia because of the risk of exacerbating thrombosis, but direct oral anticoagulants may be considered in stable patients. 28 Autoimmune heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is a rare but severe variant associated with delayed or persistent thrombocytopenia and thrombosis despite heparin cessation; it may present with refractory thromboembolic complications, including stroke. Management includes IV immunoglobulin (IVIg), with plasma exchange considered for refractory cases. 29 Essential Thrombocythemia Essential thrombocythemia is a myeloproliferative neoplasm characterized by clonal megakaryocyte proliferation and thrombocytosis (platelet count greater 142 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

than 450 × 10 9 /L), most commonly associated with JAK2 or CALR variants. 30 Although many patients are asymptomatic, some patients develop symptoms such as headache, dizziness, paresthesia, or erythromelalgia. Arterial thrombosis (14%), venous thrombosis (8%), and hemorrhage (4%) can occur, with ischemic stroke being the most common neurologic event. 30 Ischemic stroke in patients with essential thrombocythemia may affect large or small vessels, and intracranial hemorrhage may be intracerebral or subarachnoid. Essential thrombocythemia should be considered in patients presenting with ischemic stroke or intracranial hemorrhage who have unexplained thrombocytosis, especially if it is persistent and without an apparent cause such as inﬂammatory conditions (eg, rheumatoid arthritis or inﬂammatory bowel disease) or reactive causes (eg, infection or trauma). Evaluation includes a complete blood count, peripheral blood smear, and JAK2, CALR, or MPL variant testing. Treatment of essential thrombocythemia includes antiplatelet therapy (typically aspirin) and, in patients at high risk (older than 60 years, prior thrombosis, cardiovascular risk factors, or JAK2 positivity), cytoreductive therapy, most often with hydroxyurea. Direct oral anticoagulants are not currently approved for secondary stroke prevention in patients with essential thrombocythemia. Cardiovascular risk factors should be treated. THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHIES Thrombotic microangiopathies are deﬁned by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, and end organ injury. 31 They include thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), hemolytic uremic syndrome, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). TTP is caused by hereditary or acquired ADAMTS13 deﬁciency, leading to large von Willebrand factor multimers and microvascular thrombosis. 31 DIC is typically secondary to systemic disorders (eg, sepsis, malignancy, or obstetric complications), whereas hemolytic uremic syndrome often follows Shiga toxin–producing Escherichia coli infection. 31 Neurologic complications of thrombotic microangiopathies are common and include encephalopathy, seizures, and both ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke. Ischemia may result from small or large vessel occlusion due to microthrombi; hemorrhage is more likely in the setting of thrombocytopenia or DIC. Thrombotic microangiopathies should be suspected in patients with new neurologic symptoms, unexplained thrombocytopenia, schistocytes on peripheral blood smear, elevated lactate dehydrogenase, and low haptoglobin. 31 ADAMTS13 testing should be performed in the setting of suspected TTP, but plasma exchange and corticosteroids should begin immediately in suspected cases without awaiting this test result. 31 Early recognition of thrombotic microangiopathies in patients with stroke and thrombocytopenia is critical because timely treatment can prevent irreversible injury. Supportive care includes transfusion to prevent bleeding and treatment of seizures if they occur. DISORDERS OF COAGULATION Disorders of coagulation increase the risk of thrombosis, which can lead to ischemic stroke and cerebral venous thrombosis. This section reviews the epidemiology, diagnosis, and treatment of cerebrovascular complications of CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 143 KEY POINTS •In most cases, essential thrombocythemia is diagnosed with a platelet count greater than 450 × 10 9 /L and JAK2 variants. It can lead to both thrombotic and hemorrhagic complications. •Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura results from ADAMTS13 deﬁciency. The classic pentad (thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, renal dysfunction, fever, and neurologic symptoms) occurs in a minority of patients. •Factor V Leiden; activated protein C resistance; prothrombin G20210A; deﬁciencies in antithrombin, protein C, and protein S; and elevated factor VII increase thromboembolism risk in patients with inherited thrombophilia. •Thrombophilia testing is low yield but may be considered in young patients with cryptogenic stroke and history of thrombosis. •Antiphospholipid syndrome increases thrombotic risk and is diagnosed by positive lupus anticoagulant or antibodies to anticardiolipin or anti-β2- glycoprotein. •Antiphospholipid testing should be considered for patients younger than 50 years with cryptogenic stroke, transient ischemic attack, or unprovoked venous thromboembolism, especially with concurrent autoimmune conditions. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

inherited thrombophilia and antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. Coagulation disorders promote abnormal clot formation by increasing platelet aggregation and altering coagulation pathways, leading to thrombosis in cerebral vessels and subsequent cerebrovascular events. Inherited Thrombophilia Established risk factors for venous thromboembolism (VTE) include factor V Leiden; activated protein C resistance (90% of cases are caused by factor V Leiden); prothrombin G20210A variation; elevated factor VIII; and deﬁciencies in antithrombin, protein C, and protein S. The highest VTE risk occurs with homozygous factor V Leiden, homozygous prothrombin variation, and compound heterozygous states; protein C, protein S, and antithrombin deﬁciencies confer intermediate risk, and heterozygous factor V Leiden and prothrombin variant carry the lowest risk. 32 VTE can involve cerebral veins and venous sinuses or cause arterial stroke via paradoxical embolism through a right-to-left shunt (eg, patent foramen ovale). A meta-analysis found a small but signiﬁcant association between inherited thrombophilia and arterial stroke, particularly in young patients, with the strongest links to homozygous factor V Leiden and prothrombin G20210A variant. 33 Proposed mechanisms include paradoxical embolism, enhanced thrombin generation, endothelial dysfunction, and vascular inﬂammation. 34 Routine thrombophilia testing is generally not recommended in patients with stroke because the diagnostic yield is low. However, it may be considered in patients 50 years or younger with cerebral venous thrombosis or cryptogenic stroke or transient ischemic attack in the setting of right-to-left shunt (particularly if there is a personal or family history of thrombosis); thrombophilia testing is usually not recommended in patients with stroke who are older than 50 years. 35 Thrombophilia testing may need to be deferred or repeated 4 to 6 weeks after stroke or VTE because acute thrombosis can affect protein C, protein S, antithrombin, and factor VIII levels. Factor VIII has no standardized cutoffs and may be elevated during acute thrombosis due to the acute phase response. If elevated, repeat testing after several months is advised, although routine measurement of factor VIII is not recommended in thrombophilia screening. 36 Antiplatelet therapy (usually with aspirin) is the primary treatment for secondary stroke prevention in patients with inherited thrombophilia. Anticoagulation is reserved for patients with concurrent VTE. Patent foramen ovale closure should be considered if a patent foramen ovale is detected. Further research is needed to reﬁne management strategies for patients with stroke related to thrombophilia. 37 Antiphospholipid Antibody Syndrome Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome is an acquired autoimmune thrombophilia associated with arterial and venous thromboembolism, including ischemic stroke. It is deﬁned by the presence of antiphospholipid antibodies: lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin IgG and IgM, and anti-β2 glycoprotein-I IgG and IgM. 38 Stroke may result from in situ thrombosis, intracardiac thrombi, or Libman-Sacks endocarditis. 38 144 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome testing should be considered in patients younger than 50 years with cryptogenic stroke, transient ischemic attack, or unprovoked venous thromboembolism, especially in the setting of recurrent early miscarriage, recurrent late pregnancy loss, or autoimmune disease (eg, systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sj¨ ogren disease). Although antiphospholipid antibody syndrome mainly affects women of childbearing age, it can present de novo in older adults with arterial events. 39 Testing in patients older than 60 years should be considered when stroke or cerebral venous thrombosis occur without a clear etiology. 39 Laboratory diagnostic criteria for antiphospholipid antibody syndrome require positive results on two or more occasions at least 12 weeks apart for one of the following tests: (1) lupus anticoagulant, (2) anticardiolipin IgG or IgM antibodies at medium (greater than 40 units) or high titer (greater than 80 units), or (3) anti-β2 glycoprotein-I IgG or IgM at medium or high titer. 38 Repeat testing is essential because transient antibody elevations can occur with infections (eg, COVID-19) or acute thrombotic events. Although anticardiolipin and anti-β2 glycoprotein-I IgA may be associated with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome manifestations, their isolated presence is rare, and an antiphospholipid antibody syndrome diagnosis should not be based exclusively on the presence of these antibodies. 40 Anticoagulants, including direct oral anticoagulants, heparin, and vitamin K antagonists, can cause false-positive or false-negative lupus anticoagulant results. 40 Although lupus anticoagulant testing is generally discouraged in patients receiving anticoagulation, it may be considered during trough low- molecular-weight heparin or direct oral anticoagulant levels. Taipan snake venom time/ecarin time offers an alternative test in patients on warfarin or anti-Xa direct oral anticoagulants, although availability is limited. Lupus anticoagulant testing should ideally be delayed 1 to 2 weeks after stopping a vitamin K antagonist, but this is not always feasible because of the risk of thrombotic events, so decision-making should be individualized. 38 Antiphosphatidylserine/prothrombin antibodies can aid in interpreting lupus anticoagulant results, particularly in patients receiving anticoagulation. These antibodies may help identify patients with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome who have false-negative lupus anticoagulant results and help to determine the thrombotic risk in patients who test positive for two different isotypes of antiphospholipid antibodies. 40 Management of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome-related thrombosis involves antithrombotic therapy, but the precise regimen is debated. Recommended treatment regimens include warfarin with an international normalized ratio (INR) target of 2 to 3 or 3 to 4, with or without concurrent aspirin. 41,42 Although some experts favor warfarin monotherapy, others recommend adding aspirin or using high-intensity warfarin for arterial thrombosis. Direct oral anticoagulants are generally not recommended for stroke prevention in patients with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome because they have been linked to increased thrombotic risk in these patients, particularly for arterial events. Emerging data from future trials may further clarify the optimal anticoagulation strategy. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 145 KEY POINTS •Warfarin is preferred for the treatment of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome–related thrombosis. Timing, method, and repeat testing are critical for accurate interpretation of lupus anticoagulant and antibody results. •Plasma cell dyscrasias can cause peripheral neuropathy via immune- mediated injury, paraprotein deposition, or hyperviscosity. •IgM paraproteins are more likely to be pathogenic than IgG or IgA and are strongly associated with distal acquired demyelinating symmetric neuropathy (DADS), particularly when anti– myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG) antibodies are present. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

DISORDERS OF PLASMA CELLS Plasma cell dyscrasias are a set of disorders characterized by abnormal proliferation of plasma cells, memory B cells and lymphoplasmacytic cells, or plasmablast cells. Under normal conditions, plasma cells play a critical role in the immune response by producing antibodies against foreign pathogens. In patients with plasma cell dyscrasias, these cells undergo clonal expansion, sometimes with malignant transformation, leading to the overproduction of a single type of immunoglobulin known as a monoclonal protein (M protein) or paraprotein. 43 These diseases span a wide clinical spectrum, from benign clinical conditions (eg, monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined signiﬁcance [MGUS]) to more aggressive malignancies (eg, multiple myeloma, Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia). 44-46 MGUS is a premalignant condition in which a monoclonal protein is present without evidence of organ damage, whereas multiple myeloma is a malignant plasma cell disorder deﬁned by CRAB (hypercalcemia, renal failure, anemia, and bone lesions) criteria. The paraproteins found in the majority of multiple myeloma cases are IgG and IgA antibodies; only a small percentage (0.5%) are IgM. 47 Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia is characterized by lymphoplasmacytic inﬁltration of the bone marrow and excessive IgM production, often leading to hyperviscosity syndrome and neuropathy; rarely, Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia can cause lesions in the brain parenchyma or leptomeninges (Bing-Neel syndrome). Paraproteins can be divided into heavy chain and light chain proteins. Heavy chain paraproteins consist of a full immunoglobulin molecule of the IgG, IgA, or IgM protein subclasses. Light chain paraproteins consist of only free light chains (kappa or lambda) without the accompanying heavy chain. The class and subtype of paraprotein in conjunction with the type of neuropathy help to determine whether the paraprotein and neuropathy are causally related or coincidental. Plasma cell dyscrasia and paraproteinemia can affect the nervous system through several mechanisms, including immune-mediated injury (eg, anti– myelin-associated glycoprotein [anti-MAG] antibodies causing peripheral nerve demyelination), protein deposition (eg, AL amyloidosis causing light chain deposition in nerve and muscle), and hyperviscosity (excessive production of IgM paraproteins can increase serum viscosity, leading to ischemic axonal neuropathy as well as retinal or cerebrovascular ischemia). 48 Paraprotein-Associated Neuropathies Neurologists may encounter patients with a serum paraprotein in one of two ways. A paraprotein may be discovered in the course of the initial evaluation of patients with peripheral neuropathy because the American Academy of Neurology (AAN) practice guidelines recommend SPEP and immunoﬁxation as part of this evaluation. 49 In other cases, a patient is already diagnosed with paraproteinemia or plasma cell dyscrasia and is subsequently referred to a neurologist to evaluate the patient’s neurologic symptoms. In both scenarios, the neurologist’s role is to determine whether the relationship between the paraprotein and the patient’s neurologic syndrome is causative or coincidental. This distinction is crucial for guiding appropriate management. 146 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

The most common neurologic syndrome associated with paraproteinemia is peripheral neuropathy. Paraproteins are associated with a wide variety of neuropathies, including (1) chronic progressive, symmetric, sensory predominant axonal length-dependent polyneuropathy, (2) chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP), (3) distal acquired demyelinating symmetric neuropathy (DADS), and 4) severe, painful small ﬁber neuropathy with autonomic failure, among others. 50 A single paraprotein may be linked to multiple different neuropathy phenotypes. To correctly establish causation between a paraprotein and a patient’s neuropathy, neurologists must correlate the patient’s clinical history, neurophysiologic testing, paraprotein subtype, serologic markers, and pathologic ﬁndings (eg, nerve biopsy, fat pad biopsy, and bone marrow biopsy). In most cases, collaboration with a hematologist is recommended. This section is organized by the type of paraprotein and the neuropathy types with which each can be associated. IgG and IgA paraproteins are the most prevalent in the general population. They are most commonly incidental and unrelated to a patient’s neuropathy but can be associated with a CIDP-like presentation or POEMS syndrome. IgM paraproteins are more likely to be causally related to a patient’s neuropathy, including anti–myelin-associated glycoprotein (MAG)-associated DADS, CANOMAD (chronic ataxic neuropathy, ophthalmoplegia, IgM paraprotein, cold agglutinins, and disialosyl antibodies), and neuropathy associated with Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia. 51 Light chain disease is associated with AL amyloidosis and its associated neuropathic manifestations, including axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy, autonomic dysfunction, and median neuropathy at the wrist. 52 Determining whether a paraprotein is related to a patient’s neuropathy is crucial because it determines whether the cause of the neuropathy has been determined (ie, due to the paraprotein) or requires further evaluation, whether the patient requires any further systemic evaluation related to the paraprotein, and whether treatment should be targeted to the neuropathy itself or the underlying causative hematologic condition. For example, a patient with CIDP found to have MGUS would be treated for CIDP and undergo ongoing surveillance for evolution of MGUS into hematologic malignancy, whereas a patient with CIDP and multiple myeloma would require treatment speciﬁc to multiple myeloma. 44 FIGURE 6-4 53 provides an approach to identifying the signiﬁcance of paraproteinemia in patients with neuropathy and correlating the paraprotein, neuropathy phenotype, systemic symptoms and signs, and abnormalities on hematologic laboratory testing. IGM-ASSOCIATED NEUROPATHIES. IgM-related neuropathies account for 60% of paraprotein-associated neuropathies. 54 The most commonly associated hematologic conditions are MGUS and Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia. Unlike MGUS, which is a benign condition with minimal bone marrow inﬁltration and no end organ damage, Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia is a malignant lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma associated with symptomatic hyperviscosity, anemia, and organ involvement. IgM paraproteins are less commonly caused by chronic lymphocytic leukemia. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 147 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

PATHOPHYSIOLOGY. Between 50% and 75% of IgM paraproteins target MAG, leading to immune-mediated demyelination. 55 However, IgM paraproteins may have other targets including gangliosides on peripheral nerves, including the cranial nerves. 56 Nerve damage may be related to complement activation, initiation of the complement membrane attack complex, and tissue destruction. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS. The most common IgM-associated neuropathy is DADS associated with anti-MAG antibodies. DADS presents with insidious onset of distal upper and lower extremity sensory loss, paresthesia, sensory ataxia with imbalance and unsteady gait, and postural tremor. On examination, the patient may have mild distal weakness, distal limb multimodal sensory loss, and reduced or absent distal muscle stretch reﬂexes, especially ankle jerks. The cranial nerves and autonomic function are usually spared. IgM may also be associated with diffuse demyelination, similar to CIDP. 57 Rarely, the clinical syndrome of DADS may also occur without associated IgM anti-MAG antibodies; in these patients, DADS is considered to be a variant of CIDP and responds to typical treatment for CIDP (eg, IVIg), whereas DADS associated with anti-MAG antibodies does not. A rare IgM-associated neuropathy is CANOMAD, caused by autoantibodies against gangliosides containing disialosyl moieties. 58 As its name implies, it is Figure 6-4 Approach to evaluating patients with a monoclonal gammopathy and peripheral neuropathy. CRAB = hypercalcemia, renal failure, anemia, or bone lesions; POEMS = polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, and skin changes; VEGF = vascular endothelial growth factor. a The presence of marked autonomic failure with any type of monoclonal gammopathy should raise suspicion for amyloidosis. Modiﬁed with permission from Naddaf E, et al, Continuum (Minneap Minn). 53 © 2020 American Academy of Neurology. 148 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

characterized by sensory ataxia, progressive external ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, dysarthria, bulbar dysfunction, and distal-limb sensory changes. In some cases, patients also have cold agglutinins, which are autoantibodies that cause red blood cells to clump together at cold temperatures, leading to cold-induced hemolytic anemia. DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION. In addition to identifying an IgM paraprotein with SPEP and immunoﬁxation, speciﬁc anti-MAG antibody testing is positive in 50% to 75% of patients with IgM-associated neuropathy in both MGUS and Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia. 55 EMG and nerve conduction studies may demonstrate evidence of isolated distal demyelination (eg, prolonged distal motor latencies and sensory nerve conduction slowing) with spared proximal conduction. If CANOMAD is suspected, patients should be tested for antiganglioside antibodies with IgM speciﬁcity for disialosyl (GD1b, GD3, GT1b, and GQ1b). 58 TREATMENT. First-line therapy for IgM-related neuropathies, including anti- MAG neuropathy and CANOMAD, is rituximab or another anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody therapy. 59 IVIg may be considered in cases of CANOMAD or IgM-associated classic CIDP-like presentations but is generally ineffective in patients with an anti-MAG-associated DADS phenotype. 55 For patients with Waldenstr¨ om macroglobulinemia, additional chemotherapy may be required under the guidance of a hematologist. 60 IGA-ASSOCIATED AND IGG-ASSOCIATED NEUROPATHIES. IgA and IgG paraproteins are more common than IgM paraproteins. However, IgA- and IgG-associated neuropathies are less common than IgM-associated neuropathies, and therefore, IgA and IgG are more commonly incidental with respect to the patient’s neuropathy. 61 IgA- and IgG-associated neuropathies can occur with MGUS, multiple myeloma, and POEMS syndrome. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY. It is unclear how IgA and IgG paraproteins contribute to nerve injury. In the setting of POEMS syndrome, cytokines such as vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) are thought to cause aberrant angiogenesis, increased permeability of the blood-nerve barrier to toxic agents, and accelerated coagulation causing nerve injury. 62,63 In patients with multiple myeloma, the pathophysiology of nerve injury is not well understood, but amyloid deposition can occasionally contribute to nerve dysfunction. 64 CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS. There is debate as to whether IgA and IgG paraproteins are causally linked to concurrent axonal neuropathies or merely coincidental. In patients with MGUS or multiple myeloma with IgA or IgG paraproteins, the most common neuropathy is a slowly progressive, length- dependent sensory predominant neuropathy. Its rate of progression closely resembles that of idiopathic peripheral neuropathies, suggesting the paraprotein may be unrelated. 61 A small proportion of patients with IgA or IgG MGUS develop a CIDP-like presentation with bilateral, symmetric proximal and distal extremity weakness, sensory changes, and absent reﬂexes. Symptoms usually progress like typical CIDP and respond to ﬁrst-line CIDP therapy. 65 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 149 KEY POINTS •Anti–MAG-associated neuropathy typically presents with progressive sensory ataxia, postural tremor, and distal areﬂexia. •CANOMAD (chronic ataxic neuropathy, ophthalmoplegia, IgM paraprotein, cold agglutinins, and disialosyl antibodies) is a rare IgM- associated syndrome whose diagnosis requires both clinical recognition and conﬁrmatory serologic testing. •Polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal plasma cell disorder, and skin changes (POEMS) syndrome should be suspected in patients with demyelinating neuropathy plus systemic features like organomegaly, endocrinopathy, and skin changes. •AL amyloidosis causes a progressive, length- dependent, axonal polyneuropathy with prominent autonomic dysfunction. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Patients with multiple myeloma can develop a progressive sensorimotor neuropathy that can be related to direct axonal damage to nerve cells by the paraprotein or toxic effects of chemotherapy such as bortezomib or thalidomide. 66 Additionally, when multiple myeloma causes signiﬁcant vertebral disease, patients are at increased risk of back pain, central canal compression, and neural foraminal compression, causing myelopathic and radicular syndromes that should be distinguished from neuropathy. POEMS is a rare paraneoplastic syndrome associated with IgA or IgM paraproteinemia with elevated lambda light chain component. 62 The associated neuropathy is rapidly progressive, sensorimotor, demyelinating and axonal (although predominantly demyelinating), and often painful. Patients may also have skin changes (eg, hyperpigmentation, hemangiomas, or scleroderma-like ﬁndings), organomegaly (hepatosplenomegaly), and endocrinopathy (eg, diabetes, hypothyroidism, gonadal failure). POEMS is strongly associated with TABLE 6-3 Criteria for the Diagnosis of Polyneuropathy, Organomegaly, Endocrinopathy, Monoclonal Plasma Cell Disorder, and Skin Changes (POEMS) Syndrome a,b Mandatory major criteria u Polyneuropathy (typically demyelinating) u Monoclonal plasma cell-proliferative disorder (almost always lambda) Other major criteria u Castleman disease c (one required) u Sclerotic bone lesions u Vascular endothelial growth factor elevation Minor criteria u Organomegaly (splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, or lymphadenopathy) u Extravascular volume overload (edema, pleural effusion, or ascites) u Endocrinopathy (adrenal, thyroid, d pituitary, gonadal, parathyroid, pancreatic d ) u Skin changes (hyperpigmentation, hypertrichosis, glomeruloid hemangiomata, plethora, acrocyanosis, ﬂushing, white nails) u Papilledema u Thrombocytosis or polycythemia e Other symptoms and signs u Clubbing, weight loss, hyperhidrosis, pulmonary hypertension and restrictive lung disease, thrombotic diatheses, diarrhea, low vitamin B 12 values a Modiﬁed with permission from Dispenzieri A, Am J Hematol. 68 © 2021 Wiley Periodicals LLC. b The diagnosis of POEMS syndrome is conﬁrmed when both of the mandatory major criteria, one of the three other major criteria, and one of the six minor criteria are present. c There is a Castleman disease variant of POEMS syndrome that occurs without evidence of a clonal plasma cell disorder that is not accounted for in this table. This entity should be considered separately. d Because of the high prevalence of diabetes and thyroid abnormalities, this diagnosis alone is not sufﬁcient to meet this minor criterion. e Approximately 50% of patients will have bone marrow changes that distinguish it from a typical MGUS or myeloma bone marrow. 69 Anemia, thrombocytopenia, or both are distinctively unusual in this syndrome unless Castleman disease is present. 150 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

osteosclerotic myeloma, a distinct plasma cell disorder with sclerotic bone lesions that differ from the typical lytic lesions seen in multiple myeloma. 67 TABLE 6-3 68,69 lists additional features of POEMS syndrome and diagnostic criteria for POEMS syndrome. DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION. EMG and nerve conduction studies are necessary to characterize the neuropathy as demyelinating or axonal. If the neuropathy is purely axonal, a careful evaluation for alternative causes should be pursued, given the possibility of the paraprotein being coincident rather than causative. If the patient has multiple myeloma rather than MGUS, further evaluation for amyloidosis should be pursued. Serum VEGF is elevated in nearly all patients with POEMS syndrome. 70,71 Additional studies that may be considered in evaluating for this condition include bone marrow biopsy, blood smear, skeletal survey, and CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis or positron emission tomography (PET)-CT to evaluate for lymphadenopathy, organomegaly, or other systemic manifestations that would fulﬁll criteria for POEMS. Notably, some patients with POEMS may be initially misdiagnosed with CIDP early in their course; signiﬁcant limb edema or skin changes, signiﬁcant axonal changes on nerve conduction studies, or poor response to steroids or IVIg should lead to evaluation for POEMS. TREATMENT. No disease-modifying treatment is necessary for axonal neuropathies with IgA or IgG MGUS. For CIDP-like presentations with IgA or IgG MGUS, patients should receive typical CIDP treatments, such as steroids, IVIg, plasma exchange, or efgartigimod-alfa. 72 For patients with any neuropathy associated with multiple myeloma, treatment is generally directed by hematologists and may include plasma cell-directed therapies, including bortezomib, lenalidomide, or autologous stem cell transplantation. POEMS syndrome is treated by hematologists with chemotherapy, radiation, or autologous stem cell transplantation, although neurologic deﬁcits may take up to 3 years to improve. 62 ,73 Notably, therapies such as IVIg and plasma exchange have limited efﬁcacy for POEMS syndrome. 74 AL AMYLOIDOSIS (LIGHT CHAIN–ASSOCIATED NEUROPATHIES). Amyloidosis refers to a group of diseases caused by the deposition of amyloid ﬁbrils, which are misfolded protein aggregates that can accumulate in tissues, causing organ dysfunction. AL (primary) amyloidosis occurs from deposits of monoclonal light chains produced by clonal plasma cells, including multiple myeloma. 52 Neuropathy is present in 25% of patients with AL amyloidosis and is the presenting syndrome in a minority of patients. 75 AL amyloidosis differs from secondary amyloidosis (which occurs in the setting of chronic inﬂammatory diseases from persistent excessive inﬂammation) and familial amyloidosis (most commonly caused by a variant in the TTR gene, which causes abnormal transthyretin accumulation in tissues). 76,77 Both secondary amyloidosis and familial amyloidosis can cause polyneuropathy but not related to a plasma cell dyscrasia. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY. In AL amyloidosis, amyloid ﬁbril deposition in peripheral nerves predominantly affects the small nerve ﬁbers, causing neuropathic pain CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 151 KEY POINTS •Diagnosis of AL amyloidosis requires tissue biopsy showing positive Congo red staining deposits with apple-green birefringence under polarized light. •Neurolymphomatosis occurs when lymphoma cells invade nerve trunks, nerve roots, cranial nerves, or other peripheral nerves and is most frequently associated with non- Hodgkin lymphoma or leukemia. •Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma is a rare form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma that causes B-cell proliferation within the small blood vessels. Its neurologic presentation includes strokes, encephalopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and progressive dementia due to the accumulation of subcortical infarcts. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

and autonomic dysfunction. 78 Amyloid deposition in blood vessels can cause ischemic injury to nerves. Other organs that may be affected by amyloid deposition include the heart, kidney, liver, and gastrointestinal tract. Myopathy may also occur. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS. The most common neuropathy caused by amyloidosis is axonal sensorimotor neuropathy, presenting as progressive burning dysesthesia and distal sensory loss. Autonomic function may be affected early, leading to orthostatic hypotension, gastroparesis, erectile dysfunction, diarrhea or constipation, urinary retention, or abnormal sweating. Carpal tunnel syndrome is common because of amyloid deposition in the transverse carpal ligament and is often bilateral. 78,79 Rarely, myopathy may be associated with AL amyloidosis (1.5% of cases) due to amyloid ﬁbril deposition within muscle ﬁbers and blood vessels. 80,81 DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION. Serum light chain analysis is critical for diagnosis, particularly when it shows an abnormal kappa to lambda ratio. In cases where suspicion for AL amyloidosis as a cause of neuropathy is high and serum light chain kappa to lambda ratio is normal, urine immunoﬁxation and light chain assay should be performed. 82 EMG and nerve conduction studies can help determine whether there is large ﬁber involvement and will typically demonstrate axonal rather than demyelinating changes; bilateral median neuropathies at the wrists (corresponding to carpal tunnel syndrome) may also be present. If available, autonomic testing may be helpful to determine the extent of autonomic dysfunction. To diagnose AL amyloidosis, a biopsy of the abdominal fat pad, skin, nerve, or other tissue should be obtained with Congo red staining; amyloid shows apple-green birefringence under polarized light. 52 Although abdominal fat pad biopsy is often pursued ﬁrst, its sensitivity for detecting AL amyloidosis ranges from 50% to 100% depending on the size of the biopsy specimen. 83 If the initial biopsy is negative, a repeat fat pad biopsy or biopsy of an alternative site may be necessary to conﬁrm the diagnosis (CASE 6-2). Nerve biopsy is usually chosen as a last resort given its higher risk of morbidity (eg, permanent numbness or weakness). Cardiology and nephrology consultations should also be pursued for further evaluation of commonly involved organs outside the nervous system. TREATMENT. The treatment of AL amyloidosis is dependent on its stage. Treatment by hematologists includes combination therapies with daratumumab, cyclophosphamide, bortezomib and dexamethasone, or autologous stem cell transplantation in select patients. 84 Neurologists usually monitor the effectiveness of therapy with serial neurologic examinations and treat neuropathic pain and autonomic symptoms. Neuropathic pain can be treated with gabapentinoids or serotonin norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs). Orthostatic hypotension is treated with midodrine, salt tabs, droxidopa, and ﬂudrocortisone, but such agents must be used cautiously and in consultation with a cardiologist and nephrologist to limit exacerbation of concurrent cardiomyopathy or nephrotic syndrome in patients with amyloidosis. 85 152 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 6-2 A 68-year-old man with a history of well-controlled hypertension and prediabetes presented with progressive numbness and tingling in his feet that began 2 years earlier. His symptoms were symmetric, distal, and slowly progressive. Examination revealed mild weakness of toe dorsiﬂexion, decreased pinprick and vibration sensation in the feet, and absent ankle reﬂexes. Electrodiagnostic testing demonstrated a length-dependent axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy, and median nerve conduction studies were consistent with mild bilateral median neuropathies and carpal tunnel syndrome. Hemoglobin A 1C , vitamin B 12 , thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), antinuclear antibody, and serum protein electrophoresis with immunoﬁxation were unremarkable. The patient was monitored clinically, but 2 years later, he returned with worsening hand numbness and weakness, accompanied by atrophy of the thenar eminence bilaterally. Repeat electrodiagnostic testing showed progression of his median neuropathies at both wrists, as well as a further decrease in distal lower extremity sensory nerve action potential amplitudes. These ﬁndings prompted repeat evaluation, including serum free light chains, which demonstrated a markedly elevated lambda to kappa ratio. Abdominal fat pad biopsy was negative for amyloid on Congo red staining. However, given the prominent and progressive bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, a biopsy of the ﬂexor retinaculum was performed during surgical decompression. Congo red staining of this specimen revealed classic apple-green birefringence under polarized light, consistent with AL (light chain) amyloidosis. The patient was referred to hematology and started on daratumumab, bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone. He tolerated treatment well and reported stabilization of his neuropathic symptoms. He continued follow-up with hematology and neurology, with plans for serial monitoring of light chains and neurologic stability. COMMENT This case illustrates the challenge of diagnosing AL amyloidosis in the context of a seemingly typical axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy. The initial negative serum protein electrophoresis with immunoﬁxation and fat pad biopsy should not be relied upon because they are not 100% sensitive. AL amyloidosis should be considered in patients with rapidly progressive and severe entrapment neuropathies, particularly bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome. Serum-free light chain testing may reveal abnormalities not detected by serum protein electrophoresis with immunoﬁxation. Tissue diagnosis remains essential, and biopsy of affected sites, such as the ﬂexor retinaculum during carpal tunnel release, can yield deﬁnitive evidence of amyloid deposition, particularly given only modest sensitivity of a fat pad biopsy. In some cases, a skin biopsy or nerve biopsy may be required to conﬁrm the diagnosis. Early identiﬁcation allows for prompt treatment that may stabilize the progression of the neuropathy and improve outcomes. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 153 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

B-CELL LYMPHOMA B-cell lymphomas are clonal malignancies arising from B lymphocytes. Although B-cell lymphomas most commonly present with systemic symptoms (eg, fever, weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy), neurologic involvement can be the presenting manifestation in some patients (eg, peripheral neuropathies, paraneoplastic syndromes, neurolymphomatosis, leptomeningeal involvement, stroke [in intravascular large B-cell lymphoma]). Approximately 8% of patients with lymphoma have clinical symptoms of neuropathy, although up to 35% may have electrodiagnostic evidence of neuropathy. 86,87 The neuropathy can be sensory, motor, or sensorimotor, and may be paraneoplastic or caused by direct inﬁltration (neurolymphomatosis). The paraneoplastic neuropathy associated with lymphoma may be sensory, motor, or sensorimotor, and may be axonal, demyelinating, or mixed. 86 Treatment of the underlying lymphoma may improve the neuropathy. Notably, the majority of presumed paraneoplastic neuropathies in patients with lymphoma occur in the absence of identiﬁed autoantibodies. 88 Neurolymphomatosis occurs when lymphoma cells invade nerve trunks, nerve roots, cranial nerves, or other peripheral nerves and is most frequently associated with non-Hodgkin lymphoma or leukemia. 89 The typical presentation is rapidly progressive, painful radiculopathy with both motor and sensory ﬁndings localized to speciﬁc nerve roots or cranial nerves. CSF may show increased white blood cells and protein with abnormal cytology, ﬂow cytometry, or both. Spine MRI and MR neurography may show enhancement of the meninges, plexus, or speciﬁc nerves. Fludeoxyglucose (FDG)-PET has become the primary modality to diagnose neurolymphomatosis and guide selection of a biopsy site. 89 Treatment usually involves systemic chemotherapy for the underlying lymphoma or leukemia. Radiation therapy may provide additional beneﬁt in some cases. Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma is a rare form of non-Hodgkin lymphoma that causes B-cell proliferation within small blood vessels. Its neurologic presentation includes strokes, encephalopathy, peripheral neuropathy, and progressive dementia due to the accumulation of subcortical infarcts. 90 In addition to constitutional B symptoms, a rash may appear on the torso and lower body. MRI demonstrates T2 hyperintensities in the white matter (which may be diffusion restricting or enhancing depending on the age of the underlying infarcts), and vessel wall imaging may show vascular enhancement. 91 CSF may show malignant cells and nonspeciﬁc signs of inﬂammation but may be normal. Diagnosis requires tissue biopsy demonstrating lymphoma cells within small blood vessels. Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma may be diagnosed by random skin biopsy, but biopsy of affected nervous system tissue is required for diagnosis if skin or other tissue biopsy is unrevealing. Treatment involves systemic chemotherapy for advanced lymphoma, although methotrexate or radiation may be used when there is central nervous system involvement. CONCLUSION Neurologic complications of hematologic disorders range from ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke to peripheral neuropathy. Therefore, hematologic disorders should be considered in the differential diagnosis of unexplained ischemic 154 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF HEMATOLOGIC DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

stroke, cerebral venous thrombosis, or peripheral neuropathy. Additionally, careful correlation between the hematologic disorder and neurologic syndrome is essential to determine whether there is a causal or incidental relationship, particularly in the case of paraprotein-associated disorders and peripheral neuropathy. Given the complex, multisystem nature of many hematologic diseases, interdisciplinary collaboration between neurologists and hematologists is essential for diagnosis and treatment of these conditions. Neurologic Complications of Pregnancy and Menopause By Sara C. LaHue, MD ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article reviews neurologic complications associated with pregnancy and menopause, the inﬂuence of pregnancy and menopause on preexisting neurologic disorders, and the management of neurologic disorders during pregnancy and menopause. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Pregnancy and menopause can signiﬁcantly impact neurologic health. Neurologic consultation regarding mode of delivery and anesthesia in patients with neurologic conditions is common, and very few neurologic conditions warrant cesarean delivery. Pregnancy is not a contraindication to acute ischemic stroke therapies or targeted temperature management after cardiac arrest. Both pregnancy complications and earlier age at menopause are associated with worse cognitive and cerebrovascular outcomes later in life. Hormone therapy is approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the treatment of vasomotor symptoms, osteoporosis prevention, and genitourinary syndrome of menopause, all of which are conditions that may be exacerbated by underlying neurologic conditions or their treatments. The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) Women’s Neurology curriculum provides core competencies guiding neurologic care through the lens of sex and gender across the lifespan. ESSENTIAL POINTS: Neurologic care during pregnancy and menopause requires careful attention to the dynamic interplay between hormonal transitions, evolving evidence on diagnostic and treatment safety, and the lifelong neurologic risks associated with neurologic complications of pregnancy. INTRODUCTION D espite the high prevalence of neurologic conditions in women, critical gaps remain in training, research, and clinical guidelines on sex- and gender-speciﬁc considerations across the lifespan. 1 In 1977, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) recommended excluding all women of reproductive potential from phase 1 and phase 2 studies, which was not reversed by federal law until 1993. Today, more than 90% of FDA-approved medications have unknown teratogenic risks, and CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 243 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):243 – 276. Address correspondence to Dr Sara C. LaHue, 513 Parnassus Ave, San Francisco, CA 94143, Sara.LaHue@ucsf.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr LaHue has received royalties from a publication relating to health care. Hillblom Foundation, Longevity Impetus Grants, the National Institute on Aging, and the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke.
