Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

MODE OF DELIVERY CONSIDERATIONS IN NEUROLOGIC CONDITIONS Neurologists are often consulted regarding the planning for delivery in patients with neurologic conditions. Such decisions should involve a multidisciplinary team, including obstetricians, anesthesiologists, and neurologists. Obstetric Considerations in Patients With Elevated Intracranial Pressure For patients with neurologic conditions sensitive to increased intracranial pressure (eg, idiopathic intracranial hypertension), neurologists are often consulted on whether vaginal delivery with pushing (Valsalva maneuver) is safe or if an assisted second stage of vaginal delivery or cesarean delivery should be pursued. Neurologists are also consulted on the safety of neuraxial anesthesia (versus general anesthesia) because of the potential risk of increased intracranial pressure with an epidural injection or risk of a change in CSF gradient with accidental dural puncture. Although current data on the safety of vaginal versus cesarean delivery in patients with neurologic conditions are mostly based on retrospective case series, very few neurologic conditions seem to warrant cesarean delivery (which is associated with an increased risk of adverse maternal outcomes compared with vaginal delivery in the general population). Valsalva maneuver or neuraxial anesthesia is associated with an increased risk of herniation in patients with evidence of increased intracranial pressure from a space-occupying lesion with mass effect or obstruction of CSF ﬂow at or above the foramen magnum, in which case cesarean delivery with general anesthesia is advised. 71 In contrast, transient increases in intracranial pressure during vaginal delivery have not been shown to be harmful in patients with stable idiopathic intracranial hypertension (ie, absence of severe papilledema with rapidly declining vision). As a general principle, the author recommends avoiding vaginal delivery in any patient in whom lumbar puncture would be considered unsafe. Obstetric Considerations in Patients With Cerebral Aneurysm Unruptured intracranial aneurysms have historically been considered an absolute contraindication to vaginal delivery, but several studies have not found higher rates of rupture with vaginal delivery, leading some experts to recommend basing the mode of delivery on obstetric indications. 72 There is no contraindication to vaginal delivery in patients with prior intracranial hemorrhage that was treated (eg, clipping or embolization of an aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation). 71 COMPLICATIONS OF OBSTETRIC NEURAXIAL ANESTHESIA The most common complication of obstetric epidural and spinal anesthesia is postdural puncture headache, discussed previously. Rare complications include epidural hematoma (in patients on antithrombotic medications or with coagulopathy [eg, due to HELLP syndrome]), meningitis (typically occurring after dural puncture), epidural abscess (more likely to occur after prolonged epidural catheterization), and anesthetic toxicity. High or total spinal anesthesia is a rare complication of spinal or epidural anesthesia due to local anesthetic either entering the subdural or subarachnoid space, or an overdose of epidural anesthetic. 73 This can present with rapidly ascending 266 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF PREGNANCY AND MENOPAUSE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

weakness and numbness that can lead to paralysis of the phrenic nerve, leading to respiratory depression, cardiac collapse, and coma due to the anesthetic extending to the brainstem. Management involves elevating the head of the bed to prevent further cephalad spread of the anesthetic, cardiovascular and respiratory support, and emergent delivery if there is evidence of fetal distress. Local anesthetic systemic toxicity is due to systemic absorption of the local anesthetic that can lead to neurologic symptoms (from metallic taste, perioral paresthesia, and tinnitus to encephalopathy and seizures) and cardiovascular symptoms, including hypotension, arrhythmia, and cardiac arrest. Treatment requires rapid identiﬁcation of this complication and administration of an IV lipid emulsion. 74 EMERGENCY CONTRACEPTION AND PREGNANCY TERMINATION IN PATIENTS WITH NEUROLOGIC DISORDERS Emergency contraception refers to contraceptive options that can decrease the risk of becoming pregnant. The primary consideration for patients with neurologic conditions requesting emergency contraception is drug-drug interactions. For example, if a patient used an enzyme-inducing drug (eg, phenytoin) within 4 weeks, then the dose of levonorgestrel (used in many over-the-counter emergency contraception pills) may need to be double the standard dose. For this reason, placement of a copper intrauterine device may be considered. Pregnancy may be terminated with the use of oral medications or procedures (uterine aspiration or dilation and evacuation). Limited published data are available to guide recommendations for navigating abortion in patients with preexisting neurologic disorders, which requires careful consideration of medication interactions and whether in-hospital observation is warranted. Patients on anticoagulation require counseling about the indications for their medication and whether it is safe to temporarily stop therapy before an abortion. Because of the risk of bleeding complications, anticoagulation is a contraindication to mifepristone, and hospital-based abortion care may be preferred in these cases. Patients taking enzyme- inducing antiseizure medications may require an increased dose of mifepristone before an abortion. In addition to its antiprogesterone action, mifepristone also competitively blocks glucocorticoid receptors, and caution is recommended in patients requiring corticosteroids for the treatment of immune-mediated neurologic conditions. Patients with spinal cord injury above the level of T6 may experience autonomic dysreﬂexia triggered by uterine contractions and likely beneﬁt from in-hospital abortion and use of spinal or epidural analgesia regardless of the presence or absence of sensation. NEUROLOGIC CONSULTATION DURING MENOPAUSE Perimenopause is a transitional period that extends from the onset of menstrual cycle irregularities until 12 months after the ﬁnal menstrual period, at which point menopause is conﬁrmed. Perimenopause is due to a gradual decrease in ovarian function, and resultant increasing hormonal variability can lead to new neurologic symptoms or affect preexisting neurologic disorders. Spontaneous (natural) menopause is conﬁrmed retrospectively by the cessation of menses KEY POINTS •Pregnancy is associated with an increased risk of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD) relapse, and NMOSD is also associated with obstetric complications such as miscarriage and preeclampsia. •Methylprednisolone, prednisolone, and prednisone are preferred treatments for acute multiple sclerosis relapse during pregnancy because they are inactivated by placental 11-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase and do not enter the fetal circulation. •First-line therapies for acute management of autoimmune encephalitis during pregnancy include glucocorticoids, plasma exchange, and IV immunoglobulin (IVIg). •Oral pyridostigmine to treat myasthenia gravis is safe during pregnancy but may require dose adjustment in later trimesters because of changes in volume of distribution and renal clearance. •In patients with myasthenia gravis who develop preeclampsia or eclampsia, magnesium sulfate is contraindicated because it may precipitate myasthenic crisis; instead, alternative antiseizure medications (eg, levetiracetam) can be used for seizure management. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 267 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

for 12 months and occurs at a median age of 51 years in the United States. In contrast, induced menopause occurs when medical treatments or procedures (eg, chemotherapy, bilateral oophorectomy) affect ovarian function, leading to abrupt alterations in circulating hormones. 75 Induced menopause and earlier age of onset of menopause (before age 45 years) may negatively affect neurologic health outcomes. The clinical manifestations and severity of perimenopause symptoms demonstrate substantial variability across individuals and include vasomotor symptoms (eg, hot ﬂushes, night sweats), sleep disturbances, and changes in cognition or mood. Management may include medications, such as hormone therapy or nonhormonal therapy (eg, fezolinetant, SNRIs), as well as nonpharmacologic options (eg, cognitive behavioral therapy). Hormone therapy is FDA approved for the treatment of vasomotor symptoms, osteoporosis prevention, and genitourinary syndrome of menopause (conditions that may be exacerbated by medications commonly prescribed by neurologists). The current understanding of how hormone therapy impacts neurologic conditions (or their treatments) is limited because of advances in hormone therapy formulations, dosing, administration routes, and initiation timing not represented in older studies discussed later. Primary Headache Disorders During Perimenopause Approximately one-half of female patients with migraine report a link between migraine and their menstrual cycle. Those with hormonally sensitive migraine (eg, menstrual migraine) often report an initial worsening during perimenopause (likely due to erratic estrogen ﬂuctuations that may cause spikes in CGRP) and a subsequent improvement after the ﬁnal menstrual period, when hormone levels stabilize. Migraine without aura tends to improve after menopause, whereas migraine with aura may persist. 76 Tension-type headache may also become more frequent during perimenopause due to sleep disturbances, mood changes, and medication overuse. CGRP is a potent vasodilator and increases during vasomotor symptom episodes. One cross-sectional study of more than 5000 women age 45 to 60 years found that women with migraine were more likely to report severe or very severe vasomotor symptoms versus no vasomotor symptoms compared with women without migraine (OR, 1.34; 95% CI, 1.08 to 1.66). 77 Postmenopausal women on stable estrogen-containing hormone therapy have higher CGRP levels compared with untreated postmenopausal women but often report improved migraine and vasomotor symptoms control, likely because of achieving stable steady-state CGRP levels as opposed to episodic spikes. Although migraine frequency tends to improve in the postmenopausal period, patients with induced menopause tend to report worsening migraine, likely due to the abrupt and marked decrease in serum estradiol levels. Migraine management during perimenopause involves standard therapies, such as CGRP antagonists and strategies targeting hormonal ﬂuctuations. Although biologically plausible, data are mixed regarding whether hormone therapy prevents migraine during perimenopause. Migraine, including 268 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF PREGNANCY AND MENOPAUSE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

migraine with aura, is not a contraindication to hormone therapy, although the lowest effective dose necessary should be used, and estrogens may be preferred. Stroke Risk And Prevention During Perimenopause Loss of endogenous estrogen production during the peri- and postmenopausal periods is associated with hypertension, central adiposity, and hyperlipidemia, increasing the risk of stroke. Menopause before age 45 years, and particularly before 40 years, is associated with increased stroke risk, and so the AHA/ASA recommends screening for a history of menopause before age 45 years and modifying vascular risk factors in these patients. Some studies report that severe or frequent vasomotor symptoms are associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, but evidence of causality is lacking, and women with more severe vasomotor symptoms tend to have a higher-risk cardiovascular disease proﬁle overall due to factors such as obesity and tobacco use. 39 Although the Women’s Health Initiative found increased stroke risk with oral hormone therapy (conjugated equine estrogen alone or with medroxyprogesterone acetate), subsequent analyses and additional studies suggest that timing of initiation (age younger than 60 years and initiation within 5 to 10 years of menopause onset), route of administration (transdermal or intravaginal), and formulation (progestogen-only if oral) may reduce or ameliorate this risk. 78,79 A pooled analysis of 203,767 postmenopausal women across 10 observational studies found that women with induced menopause before age 50 years and hormone therapy use had a lower risk of incident heart disease than those who did not use hormone therapy. 80 Some studies demonstrate that transdermal administration, especially a low-dose (50 mcg or less) patch, may be a safer alternative to oral formulations because it bypasses ﬁrst-pass hepatic metabolism. 81 In patients with an elevated risk of stroke or history of stroke, low-dose transdermal formulations may be considered; when considering oral estrogen-containing formulations, the excess risk of stroke must be weighed against potential beneﬁts. 39 Epilepsy During Perimenopause Women with epilepsy may experience an earlier age of menopause because of hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis dysfunction, particularly patients with comparatively higher seizure frequency or number of lifetime seizures. 82 Perimenopause can also affect seizure frequency in people with epilepsy. Up to two-thirds of women reported seizure worsening during perimenopause, with seizure frequency often stabilizing or even improving in the postmenopausal period. 83 Seizure thresholds can be decreased by sleep disruption due to vasomotor symptoms and due to hormonal ﬂuctuations (particularly an elevated estrogen-to-progesterone ratio), especially in individuals with a history of catamenial epilepsy. Although hormone therapy may offer beneﬁts for women with epilepsy, such as reducing the risk of osteoporosis, clinicians should be aware of potential interactions with certain antiseizure medications. Exogenous estrogen can decrease serum concentrations of lamotrigine, potentially compromising seizure control. Conversely, antiseizure medications that induce cytochrome P450 enzymes can accelerate the metabolism of systemic hormone therapy, potentially reducing its effectiveness. In such cases, KEY POINTS •Nerve injuries occur in up to 2% of vaginal births, most commonly affecting the femoral nerve and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. •The primary consideration for patients with neurologic conditions requesting emergency contraception is drug-drug interactions. •Patients taking enzyme- inducing antiseizure medications may require an increased dose of mifepristone before abortion. •Patients with hormonally sensitive migraine (eg, menstrual migraine) often report an initial worsening during perimenopause and a subsequent improvement after the ﬁnal menstrual period, when hormone levels stabilize. •Although migraine frequency tends to improve in the postmenopausal period, patients with induced menopause tend to report worsening migraine, likely due to the abrupt and marked decrease in serum estradiol levels. •The AHA/ASA recommends screening for a history of menopause before age 45 years and modifying vascular risk factors. •In postmenopausal patients with an elevated risk of stroke or history of stroke, low-dose transdermal estrogen formulations may be considered. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 269 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

transdermal hormone therapy may be a preferred option because it bypasses hepatic metabolism. Women with epilepsy should be monitored closely for bone health, cardiovascular risk, and sexual dysfunction, which may be compounded by both menopause and antiseizure medication use. Cognitive Changes During Perimenopause Approximately 60% of women report subjective cognitive decline during perimenopause, and memory concerns are the third most frequent menopause symptom globally. 84 The longitudinal SWAN study (Study of Women’s Health Across the Nation) and others have found a temporary, subtle decrement in verbal learning and memory during perimenopause that resolves postmenopause. 85 Higher perimenopausal symptom burden, especially nighttime vasomotor symptoms, correlates with poorer cognitive function later in life, greater white matter hyperintensity volume, and lower amyloid β42 to amyloid β40 ratio levels. 86 Earlier age of menopause, regardless of type, is also associated with cognitive decline and Alzheimer disease pathology later in life. For example, bilateral oophorectomy before age 46 years is associated with a 70% increased risk of dementia (hazard ratio[HR], 1.70; 95% CI, 1.07 to 2.69), although some studies have shown that use of hormone therapy may offset this risk. 87,88 Thus far, short-term longitudinal results from several randomized clinical trials testing the effects of hormone therapy on cognition in perimenopausal and early postmenopausal women demonstrated neither clear cognitive beneﬁt nor harm, although longer follow-up is needed. 89 Sleep disruption may contribute to cognitive dysfunction during perimenopause. Vasomotor symptoms and circadian rhythm shifts may lead to fragmented sleep, and surgical menopause is associated with increased obstructive sleep apnea risk (HR, 1.26; 95% CI, 1.15 to 1.38). 90 Addressing sleep is essential in managing cognitive concerns in perimenopause and may include behavioral therapy, hormonal strategies, and management of comorbid conditions. Multiple Sclerosis During Perimenopause Symptoms of multiple sclerosis progression can overlap with menopause- related symptoms and age-related changes, leading to diagnostic challenges. For example, patients may report worsening fatigue, genitourinary symptoms, or cognitive dysfunction during perimenopause without corresponding radiographic evidence of multiple sclerosis disease activity. Age-related white matter hyperintensities that emerge in the postmenopausal period may be misattributed to multiple sclerosis, particularly in patients with long-standing disease. Although some longitudinal studies of women with multiple sclerosis have found that the ﬁnal menstrual period is associated with reduced inﬂammatory activity, this may also serve as an inﬂection point for accelerated disease progression and elevated biomarkers of neuronal injury. 91 Vasomotor symptoms may worsen underlying multiple sclerosis symptoms by triggering the Uthoff phenomenon and by disrupting sleep. Patients who have received steroids for multiple sclerosis treatment may also be more vulnerable to osteoporosis. For these reasons, treating perimenopause symptoms, including 270 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF PREGNANCY AND MENOPAUSE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

initiating hormone therapy in patients without contraindications, may be beneﬁcial. CONCLUSION This article explores the profound physiologic changes that occur during pregnancy and menopause, which may alter the course of preexisting neurologic disorders or contribute to the emergence of new neurologic conditions. Conversely, neurologic disorders and their treatment may substantially affect pregnancy outcomes and the menopausal transition. Critical gaps remain in clinical guidelines on sex- and gender-speciﬁc considerations across the lifespan. These gaps are especially evident when comparing the limited number of treatment options deemed safe during pregnancy with the expanding array available to the general population. In the absence of safety data, medications are often presumed harmful, although this assumption stems from a lack of scientiﬁc evidence, not from evidence of harm. Neurologists should remain mindful of this distinction because gaps in the current evidence may motivate ongoing research to inform future guidelines and improve care for these patients. When faced with the question, “Would my diagnostic or management approach be different if this patient were not pregnant?” and the answer is “yes,” it is essential to collaborate with obstetricians, maternal-fetal medicine specialists, anesthesiologists, and related clinicians and communicate risks and beneﬁts clearly to support informed, patient-centered decision-making. USEFUL RESOURCES ACOG COMMITTEE OPINION: GUIDELINES FOR DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING DURING PREGNANCY AND LACTATION This committee opinion, published by the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists, provides guidelines for imaging during pregnancy and lactation. acog.org/-/media/project/acog/acogorg/ clinical/ﬁles/committee-opinion/articles/2017/ 10/guidelines-for-diagnostic-imaging-during- pregnancy-and-lactation.pdf?rev= 63e891f5b7364466839f7bab78c0bc03&hash= F82E4C65BB4C25612D8956620FC6D046 DAILYMED This National Library of Medicine database contains the most recent labeling submitted to the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA), which includes prescription and nonprescription drugs and devices for human and animal use. dailymed.nlm.nih.gov DRUGS AND LACTATION DATABASE (LACTMED) This National Library of Medicine database provides information on medications and other substances that may be encountered during lactation, including their concentrations and potential adverse effects on nursing infants. ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK501922 THE INTERNATIONAL MENOPAUSE SOCIETY This organization, based in the United Kingdom, provides evidenced-based resources from a wide international community. imsociety.org THE MENOPAUSE SOCIETY (FORMERLY THE NORTH AMERICAN MENOPAUSE SOCIETY) This organization provides independent and evidence-based resources during the menopause transition and beyond. menopause.org KEY POINTS •Up to two-thirds of women reported seizure worsening during perimenopause, with seizure frequency often stabilizing or even improving in the postmenopausal period. •Earlier age of menopause, regardless of type, is associated with cognitive decline and Alzheimer disease pathology later in life. Neurologic Complications of Critical Illness By Shivani Ghoshal, MD ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article provides an overview of common neurologic complications of critical illness, with attention to advances in pathophysiology, neuromonitoring, and treatment. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : There have been advances in screening tools for delirium and evolving consensus for its pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic treatments. Effects of altered cerebral autoregulation and options for its neuromonitoring in patients with organ-speciﬁc encephalopathy are developing areas of research. There is increased understanding of the underlying pathophysiology of septic encephalopathy and critical illness-associated diaphragm weakness. ESSENTIAL POINTS : The neurologic complications of critical illness can be unique to the processes underlying the critical illness and can persist as independent disease states even after resolution of the inciting critical illness. Acquired neurologic conditions in patients who have critical illness may be organized into those associated with procedural complications, end organ dysfunction, and prolonged critical illness. INTRODUCTION N eurologic complications are common in patients with systemic critical illness. Nearly one in ﬁve patients in intensive care units (ICUs) requires neurologic consultation. 1,2 In patients with neurologic complications of systemic critical illness, both ICU length of stay and ICU mortality are nearly doubled, and long-term sequelae can include cognitive impairment and reduced quality of life. 3 Speciﬁc systemic conditions that can lead to critical illness are reviewed in other articles of this issue of Continuum. This article reviews complications of ICU procedures, encephalopathy in the ICU, and intensive care unit-acquired weakness. COMPLICATIONS OF ICU PROCEDURES Critical care procedures such as central venous catheter placement, arterial line placement, and intubation are often necessary for stabilization and treatment of critically ill patients. Neurologic injury is rare in these CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 191 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):191 – 210. Address correspondence to Dr Shivani Ghoshal, 177 Fort Washington Ave, Milstein 8GS, New York, New York 10032, sg3450@cumc.columbia.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Ghoshal reports no disclosure. UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Dr Ghoshal reports no disclosure. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

commonly performed procedures. Venipuncture and arterial catheterization procedures can cause injury to adjacent nerves, and tracheal intubation can cause cervical spine injury. Extracorporeal life support and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), used in patients with severe cardiac and pulmonary dysfunction, carry a risk of stroke in addition to potential injury to adjacent nerves associated with arterial and venous puncture. Central Venous Catheter Placement The internal jugular, subclavian, and common femoral veins are the most common sites of central venous catheter insertion. Of these three, the risk of neurologic injury is greatest with cannulation of the internal jugular vein because of its proximity to nervous system structures. Common associated complications at this site are phrenic nerve palsy, recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy, carotid artery injury, and oculosympathetic tract injury. Structures at risk of injury with subclavian vein puncture include the cervical and brachial plexuses, phrenic nerve, recurrent laryngeal nerve, axillary nerve, and subclavian artery. Intimal injury to the carotid or subclavian artery can result in embolic stroke. 4 Femoral vein puncture may be associated with femoral nerve injury. The rate of neurologic injury due to central venous catheter placements is fewer than 1 in 1000 patients, with inadvertent arterial cannulation in only 2.8 per 1000 catheterizations in a 2024 review, likely accounted for by increased use of ultrasound guidance. 5 Arterial Catheter Placement Neurologic injury arising from arterial catheter placement is more frequent than from central venous catheter placement, although it is still rare. Neurologic injury can occur due to direct needle puncture of local nerves or nerve compression from hematoma or manual pressure. The radial artery is the most common site for arterial catheterization, with 0.1% to 1% incidence of acquired neurologic injury, most often affecting the median nerve or superﬁcial radial nerve. 6 Radial artery pneumatic compression devices providing nonocclusive hemostasis may decrease rates of hematoma formation and associated neurologic injury. The brachial and axillary arteries are the most common sites of arterial catheterization–associated neurologic injury, with an incidence of nearly 10%. 6 The medial brachial fascial compartment encloses the axillary and brachial vessels, as well as the median and ulnar nerves. These nerves are therefore susceptible to injury due to direct puncture or compression from hematoma formation. Femoral nerve injury arising from femoral artery catheterization is the least commonly reported injury associated with arterial puncture, with a reported incidence of 3.8 per 100,000 procedures. 7 Tracheal Intubation Tracheal intubation for mechanical ventilation in patients with respiratory failure can be associated with neurologic injury due to physical maneuvers affecting the cervical spine. Lower cranial nerves can be injured from direct trauma, compression from cuff insufﬂation, and stretch injury. Isolated or combined injuries to the hypoglossal nerve, vagus nerve, lingual nerve, and 192 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

recurrent laryngeal nerve have been reported, including Tapia syndrome (injury to the recurrent laryngeal and hypoglossal nerves leading to dysphonia, dysphagia, and unilateral tongue paralysis). Physical maneuvers of chin lift and direct laryngoscopy, essential for tracheal intubation, cause signiﬁcant movement of the cervical spine. Patients with preexisting cervical stenosis and degenerative cervical spine pathology may be at increased risk for cervical spinal cord injury during intubation. 8 Precautions to limit cervical motion, such as manual in-line stabilization and ﬁber-optic intubation, decrease this risk. 8 For obtunded patients at suspected risk of acute cervical spine injury, a 2017 review found that 6% of patients had unstable cervical spine injury noted on MRI despite an unremarkable CT scan. 9 Postintubation cervical spine injury should be suspected in patients who develop quadriparesis, quadriplegia, bilateral upper extremity weakness, or lower cranial nerve deﬁcits following intubation. Evaluation with MRI of the cervical spine should be performed within 72 hours of injury because T2 hyperintensities associated with spinal ligamentous injury may pseudonormalize beyond this time. 9 Extracorporeal Life Support Extracorporeal life support uses venoarterial and venovenous ECMO to provide temporary mechanical circulatory support after cardiac arrest. 10 Although extracorporeal life support can dramatically improve outcomes in patients with severe cardiopulmonary dysfunction, these interventions can lead to intravascular hemolysis, thrombocytopenia, disordered endothelial function, impaired cerebral autoregulation, required anticoagulation, and altered cerebral blood ﬂow, all of which lead to a signiﬁcant risk of cerebrovascular complications. Many of these patients may be deeply sedated, limiting the neurologic examination. Advances in extracorporeal life support neuromonitoring, including portable ultra-low-ﬁeld MRI to assess for structural changes, near-infrared spectroscopy to evaluate for cerebral oxygenation and autoregulation, and transcranial Doppler ultrasonography to assess cerebral blood ﬂow, can aid in assessment for acquired neurologic injury. 11,12 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF VENOVENOUS EXTRACORPOREAL MEMBRANE OXYGENATION. Venovenous ECMO extracts blood from the right jugular vein or femoral vein, oxygenates it via the ECMO circuit, and returns blood to either the right jugular vein or femoral vein, bypassing the pulmonary circulation in patients who have severe pulmonary dysfunction, such as from acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) or other acute lung pathology. Procedural risks of venous cannulation sites are detailed in the previous section. A 2021 retrospective study from the Extracorporeal Life Support Organization database found 7% of patients receiving venovenous ECMO had acquired neurologic injury, most commonly intracranial hemorrhage, although ischemic stroke and seizure were also reported. 13 The pathophysiology of these complications is thought to be due to cerebral microemboli, cerebral venous congestion, rapid decrease of PaCO 2 , abrupt changes in systemic blood pressure, and anticoagulation. 14 Both single- and double-lumen catheters are used in patients receiving venovenous ECMO. Although the larger double-lumen catheter allows for CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 193 KEY POINTS •Acquired neurologic complications of systemic critical illness are associated with a nearly doubled intensive care unit mortality risk. •Among sites for internal vein catheterization, the internal jugular vein has the highest rate of neurologic complications. The phrenic nerve, recurrent laryngeal nerve, and cervical sympathetic trunk are in close proximity and may be injured during venipuncture. •Neurologic complications of central venous catheterization are increasingly rare, at fewer than 1 in 1000 patients in a 2024 review. •Of arterial puncture sites, the brachial and axillary arteries are most associated with neurologic injury with an incidence of nearly 10%. The juxtaposition of artery and nerves at brachial and axillary sites increases the risk of accidental puncture of peripheral nerves. •Complications of intubation may include Tapia syndrome, a combined paralysis of the hypoglossal nerve and recurrent laryngeal nerve, leading to dysphonia, dysphagia, and unilateral tongue paralysis. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

single-vessel cannulation instead of two separate cannulation sites, there is a theoretical risk of cerebral venous congestion and altered hemodynamics with internal jugular vein cannulation. 15 A 2021 retrospective review from the Extracorporeal Life Support Organization registry found rates and subtypes of neurologic injury did not differ signiﬁcantly between single- and double-lumen catheter venovenous ECMO. 13 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF VENOARTERIAL EXTRACORPOREAL MEMBRANE OXYGENATION. Venoarterial ECMO bypasses both the heart and lungs, providing both circulatory support and blood oxygenation. Venoarterial ECMO may be performed through peripheral cannulation or central cannulation. In patients who receive peripheral cannulation, blood is removed from the vena cava or right atrium, oxygenated, and returned to the femoral, axillary, or carotid artery. The femoral artery is the most common cannulation site, followed by the axillary artery. In patients who receive central cannulation, blood is returned to the ascending aorta instead of a peripheral artery. Incidence of neurologic injury is considerably higher in patients who receive venoarterial ECMO than venovenous ECMO, with neurologic complications in nearly 20% of patients with axillary or carotid artery cannulation, and 12% to 16% of patients with femoral or aortic cannulation. 15 Venoarterial ECMO has a higher incidence of microemboli and a greater degree of cerebral autoregulation impairment compared with venovenous ECMO. 10,15 Venoarterial ECMO delivers nonpulsatile arterial ﬂow, an independent risk factor for hemorrhagic stroke. 10 Systemic blood ﬂow in patients who receive venoarterial ECMO is classiﬁed as anterograde (along blood ﬂow from the heart) or retrograde (against blood ﬂow from the heart). In patients who receive peripheral venoarterial ECMO, the majority of blood ﬂow is retrograde. This creates opposing ﬂows with differing oxygen content and a “mixing point” where these ﬂows meet in the aorta. These altered ﬂow dynamics can lead to north-south ECMO syndrome, in which the upper body receives native poorly oxygenated blood while the lower body receives ECMO-oxygenated blood, leading to upper body cyanosis and decreased arousal from global cerebral hypoxia. ALTERED MENTAL STATUS IN THE INTENSIVE CARE UNIT Delirium, coma, and acute encephalopathy are commonly used terms to describe disorders of consciousness in critically ill patients. Delirium is deﬁned by Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (Fifth Edition) criteria as a ﬂuctuating disturbance in attention and awareness. 16 Coma refers to unarousable unresponsiveness to environmental stimuli, assessed by measures such as the Glasgow Coma Scale and Full Outline of UnResponsiveness Score. Acute encephalopathy is deﬁned as the rapid development of alteration from baseline mental status, ranging from confusion to coma. Delirium in the Intensive Care Unit Delirium affects nearly one-third of critically ill patients, increases the length of ICU stay and inpatient mortality, and is strongly associated with long-term cognitive impairment and mortality. 16,17 Delirium can be clinically classiﬁed as hyperactive (characterized by restlessness and agitation) or hypoactive (characterized by lethargy and decreased arousal). Risk factors for delirium 194 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

include decreased cognitive reserve, increased frailty, and increased severity of critical illness. Many medications can increase the risk and severity of delirium. In addition to benzodiazepines, ﬁrst-generation antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, opiates, and medications with anticholinergic properties may all contribute to delirium. The American Geriatric Society Beers Criteria serve as a useful reference for selecting medications for patients at risk of delirium. 18 The pathogenesis of delirium is theorized to involve dopaminergic and serotonergic overactivity, cholinergic underactivity, altered neuronal network connectivity, and inﬂammation with disruption of the blood-brain barrier. 18,19 Given the poorer outcomes associated with delirium, screening is essential for the timely initiation of interventions. Management involves both nonpharmacologic and pharmacologic treatment. DELIRIUM SCREENING TOOLS. The Intensive Care Delirium Screening Checklist and Confusion Assessment Method for the Intensive Care Unit are commonly used screening measures. 20 Because delirium ﬂuctuates, screening tools should be administered at least twice a day. Prediction models such as the Prediction of Delirium in ICU Patients and Early Prediction of Delirium in ICU Patients may help to identify such high-risk patients so preventive measures for delirium can be initiated early in patients who are critically ill. 20,21 Although the Intensive Care Delirium Screening Checklist and Confusion Assessment Method for the ICU are widely used in clinical care, the scales may perform poorly in patients with hypoactive delirium, baseline dementia, and acute or chronic brain injury. 22 The Fluctuating Mental Status Evaluation is designed for delirium screening in patients with acute brain injury. 22,23 NONPHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT OF DELIRIUM. The Hospital Elder Life Program prioritizes reorientation, early mobilization, sleep optimization, hydration, and provision of vision and hearing aids. 24 This program dramatically reduced the incidence of delirium in inpatients who are not critically ill, but it does not have similarly robust effects in patients who are critically ill. The ABCDEF bundle (Assess, prevent, and manage pain; Both spontaneous awakening and breathing trials; Choice of analgesia and sedation; Delirium: assess, prevent, and manage; Early mobility and exercise; and Family engagement and empowerment) was created for critical care settings and prioritizes delirium monitoring and management, early mobilization, and family engagement. These integrated measures appear safe and effective for improving delirium outcomes in critical care. 25,26 Early twice-daily mobilization in the ICU may shorten delirium duration, even among patients receiving mechanical ventilation. 27 Virtual reality immersive sensory stimulation is being studied as a new modality for delirium prevention. 28 PHARMACOLOGIC MANAGEMENT OF DELIRIUM. Although various pharmacologic agents have been studied for delirium prevention or treatment, there is currently no clear evidence to support any pharmacologic agents for these indications. 29 Because excessive dopamine activity is thought to contribute to the pathophysiology of delirium, dopamine receptor antagonists have been studied as a treatment for delirium. However, results from multiple studies CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 195 KEY POINTS •In cases of suspected cervical spine injury after intubation, evaluation with MRI of the cervical spine should be performed within 72 hours of injury because T2 hyperintensities associated with spinal ligamentous injury may pseudonormalize beyond this time. •Extracorporeal life support is associated with increased intravascular hemolysis, thrombocytopenia, disordered endothelial function, impaired cerebral autoregulation, and altered cerebral blood ﬂow. •Venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation delivers nonpulsatile arterial ﬂow, an independent risk factor for hemorrhagic stroke. •Delirium affects nearly one-third of critically ill patients. Risk factors for delirium include decreased cognitive reserve, increased frailty, and increased severity of critical illness. •2025 Society of Critical Care Medicine guidelines recommend consideration of antidopaminergic antipsychotics for short- term use in patients with acute agitation but not for routine use to prevent or treat delirium. •The MENDS, MENDS2, and SEDCOM trials demonstrated superiority of dexmedetomidine compared with midazolam and equivalence to propofol for delirium outcomes in patients who are critically ill. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

(REDUCE [Prophylactic Haloperidol Use for Delirium in ICU Patients; a Randomized Placebo-controlled Double-blind Multicentre Trial], HOPE-ICU [HalOPeridol Effectiveness in ICU delirium], AID-ICU [Agents Intervening Against Delirium in Intensive Care Unit], and MIND-USA-3 [MIND-USA Study: Modifying the Impact of ICU-Associated Neurological Dysfunction]) have failed to demonstrate improvement in delirium or long-term patient outcomes in patients treated with antipsychotics compared with placebo. 30-34 The 2025 Society of Critical Care Medicine guidelines recommend consideration of antidopaminergic antipsychotics for short-term use in patients with acute agitation but not for routine use to prevent or treat delirium. 35 Atypical antipsychotics such as quetiapine and olanzapine may have similar efﬁcacy to haloperidol for short-term use, along with antihistamine activity for greater sedative effect in patients with acute agitation. 36 Studies evaluating cholinesterase inhibition for treatment of delirium have similarly shown no improvement in outcomes and possible harm when provided as an adjunct treatment to haloperidol, although it may serve a role in patients with conﬁrmed preexisting cognitive impairment. 37 Benzodiazepines are associated with the development of delirium and should be limited to use in patients with alcohol withdrawal or benzodiazepine withdrawal. Deep sedation may contribute to delirium. 2,16 Although daily sedation interruption is recommended in patients receiving deep sedation, sedation interruption for patients with lighter sedation was not associated with decreased rates of delirium. 38 The MENDS (A Randomized, Double-blind Trial in Ventilated ICU Patients Comparing Treatment With an Alpha2 Agonist Versus a Gamma Aminobutyric Acid [GABA]-Agonist to Determine Delirium Rates, Efﬁcacy of Sedation, Analgesia and Discharge Cognitive Status), MENDS2 (Altering Sedation Paradigms to Improve Brain Injury and Survival in Severe Sepsis), and SEDCOM (A Phase 4, Randomized, Double-Blind, Multi-Center, Comparator Study Evaluating the Safety and Efﬁcacy of Dexmedetomidine Compared to IV Midazolam in ICU Subjects Requiring Greater Than Twenty- Four Hours of Continuous Sedation) trials demonstrated superiority of dexmedetomidine sedation compared with midazolam and equivalence to propofol for delirium outcomes in patients who are critically ill. 39,40 Encephalopathy in the Intensive Care Unit Encephalopathy may be caused by a primary neurologic condition, but it is most commonly a manifestation of a systemic illness or exposure to a medication, drug, or toxin. This section discusses the encephalopathies associated with sepsis, hypoxic-ischemic brain injury, renal disease, hepatic disease, antibiotics, and malnutrition. SEPSIS-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY. An encephalopathy occurs in up to 70% of patients with sepsis or septic shock and is associated with increased rates of in-hospital mortality and long-term cognitive impairment. 41 Clinical presentation ranges from delirium to coma. Blood-brain barrier disruption and endothelial dysfunction can manifest radiographically as posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), increased periventricular white matter hyperintensities, and hippocampal atrophy. 42 Although mean arterial pressure 196 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

targets in patients with sepsis are recommended to be greater than 65 mm Hg, advances in neuromonitoring suggest some patients may beneﬁt from a higher target because of impaired cerebral autoregulation. 43 Many processes contribute to sepsis-associated encephalopathy pathophysiology. Prominent among these is the disruption of the blood-brain barrier, leading to an inﬂammatory milieu and disordered cerebral autoregulation. Cerebral inﬂammatory activation is associated with subsequent microglial activation, cytokine release, and reactive oxygen species formation, which can result in neuronal damage and oxidative stress. Disordered cerebral autoregulation may lead to changes in cerebral microcirculation, resulting in cerebral hypoxia. 44,45 Cerebral autoregulation is impaired in up to 60% of patients with sepsis- associated encephalopathy. 43 In healthy patients, intact cerebral autoregulation ensures stable brain perfusion among a range of blood pressures through cerebral vasodilation and constriction. Outside these ranges, cerebrovascular reactivity fails, and cerebral blood ﬂow changes passively to alterations in blood pressure. In patients with sepsis, impaired cerebral autoregulation likely arises from both sepsis-related hypotension below ranges for vasoreactivity and vascular dysfunction secondary to systemic inﬂammation. 45 Although current hemodynamic guidelines for patients with sepsis recommend a universal blood pressure target not informed by changes in cerebral circulation, noninvasive neuromonitoring studies using near-infrared spectroscopy have shown that hemodynamic targets for optimal cerebral autoregulation vary widely between patients. Maintenance of blood pressure within optimal cerebral autoregulation ranges is strongly associated with improved neurologic status. 43-45 Cerebral inﬂammation and disordered autoregulation in patients with sepsis-associated encephalopathy can lead to both stroke and seizure. Ischemic cerebral lesions are present in up to one-fourth of critically ill patients with persistent sepsis-associated encephalopathy. 46 These ﬁndings, along with hippocampal atrophy and white matter injury, are presumed to be caused by sequelae of microcirculatory dysfunction and insufﬁcient cerebral blood ﬂow. 42-44 Seizures in patients with sepsis-associated encephalopathy may result from increased neuronal excitotoxicity, cerebral metabolic alterations in the setting of cerebral inﬂammation, and antibiotic-associated seizures (see the section Antibiotic-associated encephalopathy). EEG in patients with sepsis-associated encephalopathy demonstrates nonspeciﬁc ﬁndings seen across various causes of encephalopathy, such as diffuse triphasic waves or periodic discharges, which may be shared among encephalopathies. Blood biomarkers for diagnosis of neuronal injury in patients with sepsis- associated encephalopathy, such as neuron-speciﬁc enolase, glial ﬁbrillary acidic protein (GFAP), and protein S100 beta, have shown mixed results and are not routinely used in clinical care. 47,48 POST–CARDIAC ARREST BRAIN INJURY–ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY. Post–cardiac arrest brain injury–associated encephalopathy occurs due to both global cerebral ischemia during cardiac arrest and disordered cerebral blood ﬂow after return of spontaneous circulation. 49 Cerebral blood ﬂow remains compromised even with return of spontaneous circulation, ﬁrst with a period of hyperemia followed by a “no-reﬂow” hypoperfusion state. These alterations CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 197 KEY POINTS •Although mean arterial pressure targets in patients with sepsis are recommended to be greater than 65 mm Hg, advances in monitoring suggest some patients may beneﬁt from a higher target because of impaired cerebral autoregulation. •Disordered cerebral autoregulation in patients with sepsis-associated encephalopathy may lead to changes in cerebral microcirculation and cerebral hypoxia. •Neuronal injury in patients with post–cardiac arrest brain injury has two stages: whole-brain ischemia during the cardiac arrest itself and disordered cerebral blood ﬂow after return of spontaneous circulation. •One-third of patients with post–cardiac arrest brain injury develop clinical seizures. EEG monitoring and seizure control are essential, although treatment of periodic EEG patterns is not associated with improved outcomes. •Although hypothermia after return of spontaneous circulation is thought to decrease cerebral metabolism and decrease proapoptotic factors, pragmatic trials in patients after post–cardiac arrest brain injury did not show a clinical beneﬁt of hypothermia compared with targeted normothermia. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

in global cerebral perfusion in patients with post–cardiac arrest brain injury commonly lead to altered consciousness and coma. 50 Reperfusion injury contributes to post–cardiac arrest brain injury–associated encephalopathy by causing seizures, increased intracranial pressure, and ischemic stroke. One- third of patients with post–cardiac arrest brain injury develop clinical seizures due to the release of calcium and other excitotoxic mediators from neuronal cell death that create an excitotoxic environment; persistent seizures can result in epileptic encephalopathy. 51 Patients may have increased intracranial pressure from cytotoxic and vasogenic edema caused by cell death due to oligemia during both cardiac arrest and reperfusion. Increased blood viscosity and microthrombi in the setting of endothelial dysfunction additionally contribute to imbalanced delivery of and demand for oxygen. 52 Effective treatment of post–cardiac arrest brain injury relies on optimization of cerebral perfusion and oxygenation, targeted temperature management, seizure control, and management of cerebral edema and intracranial hypertension. Optimization of neurologic outcomes in patients with post– cardiac arrest brain injury is evolving based on research investigating invasive neuromonitoring and optimal physiologic targets in the setting of disordered cerebral blood ﬂow. 53,54 EEG monitoring and seizure control are essential, although treatment of periodic EEG patterns was not associated with improved outcomes in the 2022 TELSTAR (Treatment of Electroencephalographic Status Epilepticus After Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation) trial. 51 Numerous studies of targeted temperature management after post–cardiac arrest brain injury found poor outcomes with hyperthermia. Although therapeutic hypothermia after return of spontaneous circulation is thought to decrease cerebral metabolism and decrease proapoptotic factors, pragmatic trials did not show a clinical beneﬁt of therapeutic hypothermia compared with targeted normothermia. 55 UREMIC ENCEPHALOPATHY. Both acute and chronic kidney disease can affect cerebral function. Uremic encephalopathy can cause hypoactive delirium, hyperactive delirium, or coma. Seizures may also occur. Radiographic ﬁndings include cerebral edema as well as ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke. There are no deﬁning clinical or laboratory ﬁndings pathognomonic for uremic encephalopathy. Dialysis may result in improvement in patients with encephalopathy. Although uremic encephalopathy generally improves within days of dialysis initiation, time to resolution can vary between patients. 56 The pathophysiology of uremic encephalopathy is thought to be due to cytotoxic edema from the accumulation of urea and associated toxic guanidine compounds in the cerebral cortex and thalamus, central nervous system acidiﬁcation causing impaired neurohormonal balance and solute transport, and inﬂammation in the setting of poor cytokine clearance and neuronal apoptosis. 55,56 These changes may impair cerebral autoregulation and blood- brain barrier integrity. Uremia is associated with an increased risk of seizure and stroke, which must be considered as potential contributors to encephalopathy in patients with renal failure. Continuous EEG monitoring should be considered in patients with uremia and ﬂuctuating mental status. An increased risk of seizure may be due to metabolic acidosis (which alters neurotransmitter concentrations and increases activation of acid-sensing ion channels, promoting excitotoxicity) and 198 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

proconvulsant properties of retained creatinine metabolites (causing inhibition of γ-aminobutyric acid [GABA] receptors and stimulation of excitatory N-methyl-D-aspartate [NMDA] receptors). 57 Clearance of weakly protein- bound antiseizure medications during dialysis, in addition to hypoperfusion, cortical edema, and microemboli, may all increase the risk of seizure in the immediate postdialysis period. Stroke in patients with uremia is caused by impaired cerebral autoregulation, endothelial dysfunction, and hyperemia from central nervous system acidiﬁcation. Findings of PRES may be seen on neuroimaging. 58,59 Intradialytic stroke is common because of changes in cerebral blood ﬂow, uremic coagulopathy, and cerebral microemboli introduced by air contamination, particularly in the setting of high dialysis ﬂow rates. Shorter dialysis times and lower pump speeds may decrease this risk. 59-61 Brain MRI to evaluate for stroke should be considered in patients with uremia and focal neurologic deﬁcits or persistent altered mental status. Critically ill patients with kidney injury requiring dialysis have unique risks for acquired neurologic injury from cerebral edema, impaired cerebral blood ﬂow, and stroke. Although intradialytic cerebral edema is most commonly associated with intermittent hemodialysis, patients receiving continuous and low-efﬁciency dialysis treatments are also at risk in settings of rapid solute removal and plasma osmolality change. 59 HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY. Hepatic encephalopathy can occur in patients with acute or chronic liver disease. The clinical presentation of hepatic encephalopathy varies from inattention to coma. Ammonia is thought to be the primary toxin contributing to encephalopathy through mechanisms including osmotic shifts, oxidative stress, and altered synaptic transmission. 62-64 There is no direct relationship between ammonia level and hepatic encephalopathy severity because peripheral ammonia levels may not reﬂect central nervous system levels (CASE 8-1). A serum ammonia level greater than 120 µmol/L may identify patients at higher risk for cerebral edema. 65 First-line measures for hepatic encephalopathy treatment include osmotic agents to increase fecal excretion of ammonia (eg, lactulose, rifaximin) and N-acetylcysteine to limit oxidative stress. Response to ammonia-lowering treatment conﬁrms the diagnosis of hepatic encephalopathy. Invasive neuromonitoring can improve treatment outcomes for increased intracranial pressure in patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy, although it does not necessarily improve survival. Placement of intracranial monitors requires reversal of liver dysfunction–associated coagulopathies, and placement- associated intracranial bleeding may alter transplant eligibility. Noninvasive multimodality neuromonitoring with pupillometry, optic nerve sheath diameter ultrasonography, transcranial Doppler ultrasonography, and continuous EEG may all provide helpful trends to follow intracranial pressure and cerebral perfusion when encephalopathy precludes participation in serial examinations. 66 In patients with severe hepatic encephalopathy, albumin dialysis using a molecular adsorbents recirculating system can signiﬁcantly reduce time to hepatic encephalopathy resolution, although dialysis and rapid osmotic shifts carry their own risks of cerebral complications. 67 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 199 KEY POINTS •Uremic encephalopathy pathophysiology includes cytotoxic edema, central nervous system acidiﬁcation, and cerebral inﬂammation. The blood- brain barrier may be impaired in patients with uremic encephalopathy. •Uremic encephalopathy is associated with an increased risk of seizure and stroke. •Patients receiving repeated dialysis treatments are at risk of cerebral microemboli introduced by air contamination, particularly in the setting of high dialysis ﬂow rates. •Ammonia is thought to be the primary toxin contributing to hepatic encephalopathy through mechanisms including osmotic shifts, oxidative stress, and altered synaptic transmission. There is no direct relationship between ammonia level and hepatic encephalopathy severity. •Median time for neurotoxicity development after cefepime administration is 4 days. Although serum-level thresholds for cefepime encephalopathy can vary, trough cefepime levels greater than 20 mg/L are associated with increased risk of neurotoxicity. •Risk factors for thiamine deﬁciency in the intensive care unit include high catabolic rates leading to rapid thiamine depletion. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

ANTIBIOTIC-ASSOCIATED ENCEPHALOPATHY. Antibiotics are an underrecognized cause of encephalopathy, in part because of the many concurrent systemic inﬂammatory processes that can contribute to encephalopathy in critically ill patients who receive antibiotics. Antibiotic-associated encephalopathy is classiﬁed into three subtypes. 68 The ﬁrst subtype of antibiotic-associated encephalopathy, associated most commonly with beta-lactam antibiotics such as cephalosporins and penicillin, is an acute-onset encephalopathy with seizure, myoclonus, or both, within days of antibiotic use. The mechanism is thought to be due to the similar structures of cephalosporins and GABA, leading to decreased synaptic GABA release and competitive antagonism at GABA A receptors. 69 The second subtype of antibiotic-associated encephalopathy, associated with quinolone, macrolide, or sulfonamide use, is an acute-onset psychosis. The mechanisms are thought to be due to GABA CASE 8-1 A 39-year-old woman with a history of depression and previous suicide attempts presented to the emergency department with emesis 36 hours after ingesting 200 tablets of acetaminophen in a suicide attempt. On examination, she was ill-appearing but had a normal mental status. Initial noncontrast head CT showed no abnormalities. Her serum studies were notable for a serum sodium of 126 mEq/L, bicarbonate less than 5 mEq/L, arterial lactate 11 mmol/L, acetaminophen level 128 mcg/mL, aspartate transaminase (AST) and alanine transaminase (ALT) greater than 7000 U/L, and ammonia of 88 mcg/dL, concerning for acute liver failure in the setting of acetaminophen toxicity. Over the subsequent 24 hours, her ammonia rose from 88 mcg/dL to 151 mcg/dL, and she developed inattention followed by hyperactive delirium with profound agitation and then by progressive decrease in level of consciousness. A repeat noncontrast head CT revealed interval development of diffuse global cerebral edema with absent sulcation (FIGURE 8-1A) and central herniation with effacement of the lateral ventricles and interpeduncular cistern (FIGURE 8-1B), concerning for cerebral edema in the setting of worsening acute hepatic encephalopathy. Despite treatment with lactulose and rifaximin for hyperammonemia, N-acetylcysteine for oxidative stress, and hypertonic and hyperosmolar therapies for cerebral edema, she developed acute renal failure, gastrointestinal bleeding, and septic shock and died of these complications. COMMENT Ammonia is thought to be the primary driver of encephalopathy in patients with acute hepatic failure. Serum ammonia levels may not reﬂect central nervous system levels, and there is no direct relationship between serum ammonia level and hepatic encephalopathy severity. First-line treatment of acute hepatic encephalopathy includes lactulose and rifaximin to increase ammonia excretion, N-acetylcysteine to limit oxidative stress, and hypertonic and hyperosmolar therapy as needed for cerebral edema. 200 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

receptor inhibition and NMDA receptor activation by ﬂuoroquinolones and direct neurotoxicity with macrolides. 70 The third subtype of antibiotic- associated encephalopathy, most commonly associated with metronidazole, is a subacute encephalopathy, developing weeks into the antibiotic course and characterized by cerebellar dysfunction and symmetric T2 hyperintensities within the cerebellar dentate nuclei, midbrain, dorsal pons, and splenium of the corpus callosum. The mechanism is thought to be interference with RNA synthesis and increased free radical formation. 71 Cefepime is the antibiotic most frequently associated with encephalopathy, most often in patients with renal dysfunction. 69 Median time to development of neurotoxicity after cefepime administration is 4 days. Although serum levels above which cefepime encephalopathy occurs can vary, trough cefepime levels greater than 20 mg/L are associated with increased risk of neurotoxicity. 72 FIGURE 8-1 Axial head CT images of the patient in CASE 8-1 showing global cerebral edema with absent sulcation (A), mass effect on the lateral ventricles, and downward herniation with effacement of the interpeduncular cistern (B). CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 201 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Continuous EEG monitoring and cefepime dose adjustment or discontinuation are recommended for patients with suspected cefepime-associated encephalopathy. Nonconvulsive status epilepticus or triphasic waves may be seen on EEG in patients with cefepime-associated encephalopathy. 72 ENCEPHALOPATHY ASSOCIATED WITH MALNUTRITION. Wernicke encephalopathy is associated with the triad of confusion, ataxia, and eye movement abnormalities, classically associated with alcohol use disorder. However, this triad is present in less than 40% of patients, and alcohol use disorder accounts for only one-half of cases. 73 Successful diagnosis of Wernicke encephalopathy begins with appropriate clinical suspicion by identifying patients who are at risk. Risk factors for thiamine deﬁciency in the ICU include high catabolic rates leading to rapid thiamine depletion (eg, in patients with sepsis or prolonged inﬂammatory states), decreased thiamine absorption resulting from gastric or intestinal resection, decreased oral thiamine intake due to hyperemesis gravidarum or prolonged nil per os status, and increased thiamine clearance caused by excessive diuresis, hemodialysis, and plasma exchange (CASE 8-2). 74 Rarely, severe kidney injury and accumulation of the uremic toxin oxythiamin can lead to inhibition of transketolase, causing functional thiamine deﬁciency. 75 Brain MRI in patients with Wernicke encephalopathy most commonly demonstrates symmetric signal abnormalities in the medial thalamus, hypothalamus, mamillary bodies, and periaqueductal region; symmetric CASE 8-2 A 72-year-old man with a 2-week history of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea presented to the emergency department with hypoxic respiratory failure in the setting of aspiration of emesis. He was intubated for airway protection in the emergency department and admitted to the critical care unit. His hospital course was complicated by the development of septic shock and severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), the latter requiring prone positioning for oxygenation and paralysis for ventilator synchrony. The patient developed severe ileus in the setting of high-dose continuous infusions of fentanyl for analgesia for ventilator synchrony. His enteral feeds were limited to trickle feeds and intermittent over 3 weeks because of ongoing prone positioning, paralysis, and repeated episodes of emesis in the setting of ileus. At 3 weeks, upon ARDS resolution and weaning of analgesia, the patient was noted to have decreased arousal on examination and ophthalmoplegia with nystagmus. A brain MRI (FIGURE 8-2) showed symmetric restricted diffusion of his bilateral globus pallidus and dorsal midbrain, concerning for Wernicke encephalopathy. He was treated with high-dose thiamine, but his mental status remained persistently poor at the time of intensive care unit discharge. COMMENT This patient’s increased thiamine consumption from high catabolic rates from septic shock and ARDS combined with limited thiamine intake from prolonged nil per os status were risk factors leading to his eventual development of Wernicke encephalopathy. 202 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

hemorrhages on susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), restricted diffusion on diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI), and hyperintensity on ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) may also be seen in these regions. Similar signal abnormalities can also be seen in the putamen, splenium, dorsal brainstem, and dentate nucleus. 76 Thiamine is directly measured in the blood as free thiamine or thiamine pyrophosphate, although this does not correlate with intracellular thiamine; therefore, a normal serum thiamine level does not preclude the diagnosis of Wernicke encephalopathy. 77 Magnesium deﬁciency may also impair thiamine function. Thiamine and magnesium supplementation are therefore recommended when there is clinical suspicion of Wernicke encephalopathy. 77 Vitamin B 12 deﬁciency can also cause encephalopathy. Megaloblastic anemia may be absent if there is concurrent iron deﬁciency anemia or anemia of chronic disease. Paresthesia, sensory ataxia, anosmia, glossitis, and mood changes may be clues to vitamin B 12 deﬁciency as a cause of encephalopathy. 78 Patients with hemolysis, small bowel feeding, and hemoﬁltration therapies such as dialysis or plasma exchange may be at particular risk. WEAKNESS IN THE INTENSIVE CARE UNIT Weakness in the ICU can arise from preexisting neurologic conditions affecting the motor system or acquired injury due to critical illness (referred to as critical illness weakness or ICU-acquired weakness). Critical illness weakness FIGURE 8-2 Axial diffusion-weighted MRI of the patient in CASE 8-2 showing symmetric restricted diffusion of the bilateral globus pallidus and dorsal midbrain. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 203 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

is deﬁned as generalized muscle weakness that develops after critical illness, is localized to the peripheral nervous system, and is organized into critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness, critical illness polyneuropathy, and critical illness myopathy. 79 Critical Illness–Associated Diaphragm Weakness Acquired diaphragmatic weakness is common in patients who are critically ill and receive mechanical ventilation, affecting an estimated 60% to 80% of patients. 80 Onset may be rapid. In one study, more than 60% of patients developed diaphragmatic weakness within the ﬁrst 24 hours of intubation, and the proportion of patients with diaphragmatic weakness increased with increased duration of mechanical ventilation. 81 Critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness may involve the diaphragm itself, the phrenic nerve, or both. Its clinical impact is signiﬁcant, increasing the duration of mechanical ventilation and ICU mortality. 80,81 Critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness is multifactorial; sepsis, systemic inﬂammation, surgery, medications, and patient-speciﬁc ventilator mechanics may all contribute to critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness. 80 In patients with normal physiology, respiratory muscle function depends on intact diaphragmatic contraction and the generation of transdiaphragmatic pressure between the thoracic and abdominal cavities. Sepsis and systemic inﬂammation can directly weaken contractile proteins and alter blood ﬂow distribution, both increasing the risk of load-related diaphragm injury. 82 Surgery, particularly upper abdominal and cardiothoracic surgery, can directly injure the phrenic nerve or its afferent pathways, impairing diaphragm contraction. 83 Corticosteroids may limit protein synthesis and promote protein degradation, both of which impair the contractility of diaphragmatic striated muscle. 84 Neuromuscular blockade and propofol may also contribute. 84,85 Low diaphragm activity in the setting of mechanical ventilation without spontaneous breathing trials may lead to diaphragm atrophy. Excess diaphragm activity in the setting of ventilator dyssynchrony leads to diaphragmatic injury and increased thickness. Both processes impair patients’ ability to be weaned from mechanical ventilation. 86-88 Diaphragmatic biopsies from patients with critical illness– associated diaphragm weakness show proteolysis, muscle ﬁber atrophy, and inﬂammatory inﬁltrates, all of which contribute to worsened contractility. 89 Bedside diagnosis of critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness may be difﬁcult because ventilator maneuvers such as vital capacity and maximum inspiratory pressure require patient participation, and many intubated patients are sedated, encephalopathic, or both from their primary critical illness. Measurement of transdiaphragmatic pressure by twitch magnetic stimulation of the phrenic nerve does not require patient effort and is the recommended method for critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness diagnosis. Diaphragm ultrasonography is an emerging technique that allows visual measurement of diaphragm thickening during tidal breathing for critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness diagnosis, although measurements are operator dependent and may correlate poorly with transdiaphragmatic pressure. 90 The timing of onset of diaphragmatic weakness after critical illness and electrophysiologic testing can help differentiate critical illness–associated 204 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF CRITICAL ILLNESS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

diaphragm weakness from primary neuromuscular etiologies of diaphragmatic weakness, such as myasthenia gravis or motor neuron disease. Electrodiagnostic evaluation of critical illness–associated diaphragm weakness demonstrates low amplitude phrenic compound muscle action potentials within the ﬁrst week, and ﬁbrillation potentials on EMG in the subsequent weeks. 91 Critical Illness Polyneuropathy and Critical Illness Myopathy Critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy are the most common acquired peripheral neurologic conditions in the setting of critical illness. Critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy commonly co-occur. The limbs are affected symmetrically and proximally more than distally in patients with critical illness polyneuropathy and patients with critical illness myopathy. Facial and extraocular muscles are typically spared. 91 Sepsis, moderate to severe ARDS, acute kidney injury, and overall illness severity are the major risk factors for critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy. 91 Hyperglycemia, vasopressors, corticosteroids, and neuromuscular blocking agents have also been associated with critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy. 92 The pathophysiology of critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy is thought to relate to altered amino acid metabolism, systemic inﬂammation, and immobility. On histopathology, muscle biopsy in patients with critical illness myopathy shows loss of myosin, type II ﬁber drop-out, myoﬁbrillar disorganization, and, in some cases, necrosis. 93 Nerve biopsies in patients with critical illness polyneuropathy show sensorimotor axonal degeneration without inﬂammation. 94 Various methods can be used for diagnosis of critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy. In patients who are not encephalopathic and can participate in the examination, muscle strength may be assessed at the bedside using the Medical Research Council sum score. Critical illness weakness is deﬁned as a Medical Research Council sum score less than 48, although even low-normal Medical Research Council sum score values of 48 to 55 are associated with increased long-term morbidity. The Medical Research Council sum score shows high interrater reliability, although it is limited by patient participation, operator expertise, and time. 95 For patients with encephalopathy who cannot participate in the examination, nerve conduction studies and EMG help differentiate critical illness polyneuropathy from critical illness myopathy, and also distinguish these two entities from preexisting neuromuscular disorders. The nerve conduction study simpliﬁed peroneal nerve test can be used to diagnose critical illness weakness if a low amplitude compound muscle action potential of the common peroneal nerve is observed. Peroneal nerve test results may serve as a screening test to determine the need for further electrophysiologic study to differentiate between critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy. Nerve conduction studies in patients with critical illness polyneuropathy demonstrate a sensorimotor axonal neuropathy (low amplitude compound muscle action potentials with relatively preserved velocities). Electrodiagnostic studies in patients with critical illness myopathy are characterized by long-duration compound muscle action potentials, ﬁbrillation potentials, and small, polyphasic motor unit potentials consistent CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 205 KEY POINTS •Acquired diaphragmatic weakness is common among patients who are critically ill and receive mechanical ventilation, affecting an estimated 60% to 80% of patients. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

with muscle damage. 96 Nerve and muscle ultrasonography may be used in the diagnosis of critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy, although further validation studies are needed. 97 Treatment options remain limited for patients with critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy, so preventive measures are essential. Hyperglycemia is a modiﬁable risk factor, and intensive insulin therapy may reduce the incidence of critical illness polyneuropathy and critical illness myopathy and decrease the duration of mechanical ventilation, although treated patients may be at risk of hypoglycemia. Early mobilization may have similar preventive effects. Limited data evaluating transcutaneous electromagnetic stimulation have not shown beneﬁt in the treatment of patients with critical illness polyneuropathy or critical illness myopathy. 98 CONCLUSION Acquired neurologic injury is common among patients with critical illness, and disability may continue even after the resolution of the inciting critical condition. Evaluation for neurologic complications of critical illness is challenging because patients are often unable to participate in the neurologic examination because of impaired level of consciousness. Neurologists must therefore screen for evidence of neurologic injury in the ICU, with a low threshold for neurodiagnostic testing if the neurologic examination is confounded by the patient’s mental state. Neurologists are essential in the diagnosis and management of ICU-associated neurologic complications, which will likely increase as the global incidence of critical illness grows.
