Neurologic Manifestations of Rheumatologic Disease By Shamik Bhattacharyya, MD, FAAN ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article provides readers with a foundational approach to the diagnosis and treatment of neurologic syndromes that occur in patients with rheumatologic diseases. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Neuroimaging ﬁndings such as the central vein sign on MRI to distinguish the white matter lesions of multiple sclerosis from those of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), the trident sign on axial postcontrast spine MRI in patients with sarcoid myelopathy, and vessel wall imaging to differentiate central nervous system vasculitis from vasculopathy, can improve diagnostic precision in patients with neurorheumatologic disorders. Treatment of neurologic complications of rheumatologic disease is guided largely by case series but increasingly incorporates targeted therapies such as inﬂiximab for neurosarcoidosis and neuro-Behçet syndrome. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Systemic rheumatologic diseases can cause diverse neurologic symptoms that can be causally related, treatment induced, or comorbid but unrelated. Diagnosis relies on clinical criteria, serology, imaging, and, if these are unrevealing or inconclusive, tissue biopsy. INTRODUCTION S ystemic autoimmune rheumatologic disorders affect an estimated 20 million individuals in the United States. 1 Neurologic symptoms in patients with rheumatologic disease can be causally related to the underlying condition, treatment induced, or comorbid but unrelated to the patient’s rheumatologic disorder. This article discusses the neurologic conditions that can occur in patients with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, sarcoidosis, Behçet syndrome, systemic vasculitis, systemic sclerosis, and Sj¨ ogren disease, with a focus on diagnosis and treatment of neurologic complications of these diseases. SYSTEMIC LUPUS ERYTHEMATOSUS SLE is a multisystem disease caused by a breakdown of immunologic self- tolerance, dysregulation of immune activation, and disordered immune cell populations and functions. Chronic immune activation results in multiple autoantibodies that cause tissue injury by immune complex deposition or CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 159 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):159 – 190. Address correspondence to Dr Shamik Bhattacharyya, Brigham and Women’s Hospital, 75 Francis St, Boston, MA 02115, sbhattacharyya3@ bwh.harvard.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Bhattacharyya has received personal compensation in the range of $500 to $4999 for serving as a consultant for NeuroLambda Therapeutics, on a scientiﬁc advisory or data safety monitoring board for Amgen, Inc, and TG Therapeutics, Inc; in the range Continued on page 190 UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Dr Bhattacharyya discusses the unlabeled use of mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab for systemic lupus erythematosus; rituximab and inﬂiximab for rheumatoid meningitis; rituximab and cyclophosphamide for rheumatoid vasculitis; inﬂiximab, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and azathioprine for neurosarcoidosis; intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg), mycophenolate mofetil, rituximab, and cyclophosphamide for Sj¨ ogren disease–associated neuropathy; and IVIg for systemic sclerosis myopathy. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

direct antigen binding. Injury also occurs from cell-mediated inﬂammation and secondary systemic effects of this prolonged inﬂammatory state. The annual global incidence of SLE is 400,000, and the estimated prevalence is 3.4 million people. 2 Of individuals with SLE, 90% are women, and Black women have approximately a threefold higher incidence of SLE compared with White women. 3 Systemic manifestations of SLE include skin rashes (eg, malar rash), nonerosive arthritis, oral ulcers, constitutional symptoms (eg, fever and fatigue), nephritis, pleuritis, and pericarditis. SLE is associated with serum antinuclear antibodies (ANA), low complement levels, and cytopenias. Low- titer ANA alone has low speciﬁcity for SLE, estimated at 67% for ANA titers of 1:40. 4 SLE classiﬁcation criteria (TABLE 7-1 5 ) have estimated sensitivity and speciﬁcity of 96% and 93%, respectively. 5 The entry criterion of an ANA titer of at least 1:80 emphasizes the importance of high-titer ANA in the diagnosis of SLE. When neurologic syndromes occur in patients with SLE, they are called neuropsychiatric SLE. Terms such as lupus cerebritis, lupus sclerosis, and lupus vasculitis have fallen out of favor as pathologic studies of cerebral involvement in patients with SLE have revealed multiple mechanisms of central nervous system (CNS) injury, including small vessel vasculopathy without clear vasculitis, infectious and noninfectious embolism without vasculitis, white matter structural alterations, endothelial injury, microglial chronic activation, blood-brain barrier disruption, and speciﬁc neural autoantibodies. Although neuropsychiatric symptoms are common in patients with SLE, whether such symptoms are causally related to SLE is often uncertain. Headache, anxiety, mild depression, mild cognitive impairment, and neuropathic symptoms without an electrophysiologic correlate are common in the general population and have limited evidence for a causal relationship to an SLE-associated inﬂammatory etiology. 6 Neurologic symptoms emerging within 6 months of systemic SLE onset generally have a higher probability of being causally related to SLE. 6 Headache Headache is the most common neurologic symptom in patients with SLE, reported in 50% or more of patients. 7 The headache syndrome can be migraine, tension-type, or chronic daily headache. However, the prevalence of headache is similar between healthy controls and patients with SLE. 8 Even when grouped by headache type, headache is not associated with SLE disease severity indices, use of immunomodulatory medications, or speciﬁc autoantibodies such as antiphospholipid antibodies. Patients with SLE should be evaluated for secondary causes of headache for which they are at risk, such as cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, idiopathic intracranial hypertension, and infectious meningitis in patients on immunomodulatory therapies, before their headache is treated symptomatically. Psychiatric Conditions Patients with SLE frequently develop depression (20% to 60%) 9 and anxiety, but the causal relationship to SLE is unclear because mood disorders are prevalent in the general population. Psychosis, in contrast, is uncommon in patients with SLE (1.5%) but is considered causally related to SLE (90% of 160 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

TABLE 7-1 European League Against Rheumatism and American College of Rheumatology Classiﬁcation Criteria for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE) a Domain Criteria b Points c Entry criterion Positive antinuclear antibodies (titer ≥1:80 on HEp-2 cells or an equivalent positive test) at least once Required Constitutional Fever >38.3°C (100.9°F) (in the absence of other causes) 2 Hematologic Leukopenia (white blood cell count <4000/mm 3 ) 3 Thrombocytopenia (platelet count <100,000/mm 3 ) 4 Autoimmune hemolysis 4 Neuropsychiatric Delirium 2 Psychosis 3 Seizure 5 Mucocutaneous Nonscarring alopecia 2 Oral ulcers 2 Subacute cutaneous or discoid lupus 4 Acute cutaneous lupus (including malar rash) 6 Serosal Pleural or pericardial effusion 5 Acute pericarditis 6 Musculoskeletal Joint involvement (two or more joints with synovitis or tenderness with morning stiffness) 6 Renal Proteinuria >0.5 g/24h 4 Class II or Class V lupus nephritis on biopsy 8 Class III or Class IV lupus nephritis on biopsy 10 Antiphospholipid antibody Positive anticardiolipin, lupus anticoagulant, or anti-β 2 GP1 2 Complement protein Low C3 or low C4 3 Low C3 and low C4 4 SLE-speciﬁc antibody Anti-dsDNA or anti-Sm 6 a Modiﬁed with permission from Aringer M, et al, Ann Rheum Dis. 5 © 2019 Author(s) (or their employer[s]). b Only the highest weighted criterion counts in each domain. A clinical symptom or sign is required in addition to immunologic criteria and can occur at any time in the course of the disease. c A score of 10 or greater classiﬁes a patient as having SLE in addition to the exclusion of other causes. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 161 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

psychotic episodes were attributable to SLE in a multicenter study of 1826 patients over 7.4 years). 10 Most cases of psychosis in patients with SLE occur in the year before SLE diagnosis or within 3 years of diagnosis, and most patients have a single episode of psychosis without recurrence. 10 Secondary causes of psychosis in patients with SLE that should be excluded before attributing psychosis to SLE include corticosteroid treatment and encephalopathy from systemic illness. Brain MRI and CSF analysis in patients with SLE-associated psychosis are generally unremarkable. Serum antiribosomal P antibodies have historically been associated with the development of SLE-associated psychosis, but this has not been borne out in longitudinal studies. 10 Preliminary data suggest CSF interleukin 6 (IL-6) may be elevated in patients with SLE-associated psychosis, but this remains to be validated in larger studies. 11 Treatment involves both antipsychotic medications and SLE-directed treatment with corticosteroids and nonsteroidal immunomodulatory therapy such as mycophenolate mofetil, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab. Seizure Seizures occur in approximately 5% to 7% of individuals with SLE and may be associated with disease activity, systemic complications (eg, uremia from lupus nephritis), or occur independently. 12 Seizures in patients with SLE can be generalized tonic-clonic or focal, and the majority are likely attributable to SLE disease activity based on attribution models. 12 Similar to psychosis, seizures attributable to SLE tend to occur early after SLE diagnosis. 13 The cause of seizures in patients with SLE is unclear because there are typically no associated MRI ﬁndings, serum markers of disease activity, or CSF abnormalities. Patients of African ancestry with SLE have a higher risk of seizures, whereas hydroxychloroquine treatment appears to have a protective effect. 12,14 Seizures in patients with SLE are generally isolated and do not result in epilepsy unless there is an underlying lesion (eg, prior stroke due to antiphospholipid antibody syndrome), in which case recurrence risk is elevated, as it would be outside the context of SLE. When there is no focal lesion, most patients with SLE do not require long-term antiseizure medications, although decision-making should be individualized based on EEG and brain MRI ﬁndings as well as patient preference. Stroke Cerebrovascular complications of SLE include ischemic stroke, intraparenchymal hemorrhage, subarachnoid hemorrhage, and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. Of these, ischemic stroke is the most frequent, with an approximate twofold increased risk of stroke in patients with SLE compared with the general population. 15 The relative risk of stroke in patients with SLE is particularly elevated for women and patients younger than 50 years because of the overall low baseline risk in the general population. Stroke associated with SLE can occur early or late in the disease. 16 Ischemic stroke mechanisms in patients with SLE include antiphospholipid syndrome (present in as many as 48% of patients with SLE), 16 cardioembolism, intracranial vasculopathy, small vessel disease (present in 50% to 60% of patients based on neuroimaging and pathologic studies 17 ), and cryptogenic stroke; more than one mechanism may be present in a given patient (CASE 7-1). Treatment of stroke in patients with SLE is the same as in the general 162 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 7-1 A 25-year-old woman with a 5-year history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) treated with hydroxychloroquine developed acute left-sided weakness. She had a history of upper respiratory infection 1 week prior. Neurologic examination demonstrated weakness in the left face (impaired ability to smile) and the left arm (complete paralysis) with preserved strength in the left leg. She had sensory neglect in the left face, arm, and leg. Noncontrast head CT was normal. CT angiography showed luminal narrowing of the distal segment of the right middle cerebral artery. She was treated with IV tenecteplase and improved symptomatically. MRI of the brain showed an ischemic infarct in the right putamen (FIGURE 7-1). Investigations for causes of ischemic stroke included transthoracic echocardiography, heart rhythm monitoring for 30 days, and serologies for antiphospholipid syndrome. None of these investigations showed a secondary cause. The patient was started on aspirin 81 mg a day. She was followed for 3 years without stroke recurrence. FIGURE 7-1 Imaging of the patient in CASE 7-1 with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI shows an ischemic stroke in the right putamen. COMMENT SLE is associated with increased risk of ischemic stroke throughout the lifespan. Some patients have antiphospholipid syndrome comorbid with SLE. However, others may experience ischemic stroke without a clear secondary cause other than SLE. Treatment is generally with antiplatelet therapy unless antiphospholipid antibody syndrome is identiﬁed or the patient has another indication for anticoagulation (eg, atrial ﬁbrillation). CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 163 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

population in both the acute and chronic settings; if antiphospholipid syndrome is present, anticoagulation is required, with the choice of agent guided by antibody testing results and history of prior thromboembolic events. For further discussion of antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, refer to the article “Neurologic Complications of Hematologic Disorders” by Drs Lauren Patrick, MD and Mark Terrelonge, MD, MPH, 18 in this issue of Continuum. Myelopathy Spinal cord involvement in SLE is rare and may be caused by ischemia or inﬂammation. Myelitis in patients with SLE is associated with anti–aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibodies in approximately 50% of patients (or less in some series that include patients with comorbid multiple sclerosis). 19-21 Patients with SLE and anti-AQP4 antibodies generally have relapsing disease affecting the spinal cord and optic nerves, typical of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), and should be treated for this condition. The mechanism of myelitis in patients with SLE who do not have anti-AQP4 antibodies is unclear, but the disease course in these patients is generally monophasic. 20 Less than 10% of patients with SLE who develop myelitis have myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody–associated disease comorbid with SLE. 21 Focal Brain Lesions Focal brain lesions in patients with SLE could represent stroke, comorbid NMOSD or multiple sclerosis, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) (due to lupus nephritis or immunomodulatory therapy), infection, or rarely primary inﬂammatory lesions associated with SLE (FIGURE 7-2). Cerebrovascular lesions due to small vessel disease are more common than inﬂammatory lesions. Some patients with SLE develop multiple sclerosis, but there is no clear association between the conditions, and they may be coincidental because of the similar demographics of young women affected by both conditions. A central vein sign in brain lesions on MRI distinguishes multiple sclerosis lesions from SLE lesions, 22 but oligoclonal bands can be present in patients with both conditions and are therefore nonspeciﬁc. Inﬂammatory brain lesions attributed to SLE (ie, after excluding cerebrovascular disease, PRES, infection, NMOSD, multiple sclerosis, and concurrent hemophagocytic FIGURE 7-2 Basal ganglia involvement in a patient with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE). Axial ﬂuid- attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI demonstrates bilateral T2 hyperintensities in the basal ganglia in a 30-year-old woman with encephalopathy and fever. She was diagnosed with hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis in the context of SLE. 164 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

lymphohistiocytosis) have heterogeneous clinical and imaging characteristics ranging from pituitary stalk enhancing lesions to basal ganglia T2 hyperintensity (striatal encephalitis). 23,24 The mechanism of these lesions is unclear because of the rarity of these cases, lack of associated neuropathology, and lack of speciﬁcity of the imaging ﬁndings. Therefore, appropriate management is unclear, and current therapeutic approaches rely on corticosteroids in the acute setting and control of systemic disease activity with long-term immunomodulatory therapy. Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral neuropathy occurs in approximately 7% of patients with SLE. 25 Most cases of neuropathy in patients with SLE are attributable to SLE, although physicians should evaluate patients for alternative etiologies and contributing factors, such as diabetes, vitamin B 12 deﬁciency, and paraproteinemia. Axonal polyneuropathy is the most frequent type of neuropathy in patients with SLE, accounting for approximately 30% to 40% of cases. 25 The axonal neuropathy can be variable in severity, ranging from indolent numbness in the feet to, more rarely, progressive, ascending numbness and weakness in the legs. The next most common phenotype is cranial neuropathy, accounting for 30% of cases. 25 Among the cranial nerves, cranial nerve VIII appears to be most frequently affected, followed by the ocular motor nerves; 25 the optic, trigeminal, and facial nerves can also be affected. Mononeuropathy and multiple mononeuropathies may also occur; when noncompressive, nerve biopsies in these patients may show histologic signs of vasculitis. The prevalence of small ﬁber neuropathy may have been historically underestimated in patients with SLE, and small ﬁber neuropathy in patients with SLE may present in a non–length-dependent pattern more suggestive of small ﬁber–predominant ganglionopathy. 26 Rarely, ganglionopathy and autonomic neuropathy can occur. Demyelinating neuropathies are uncommon in patients with SLE and of unclear relationship to SLE when they occur in this patient population. RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic, inﬂammatory arthritis that affects 0.5% to 1.0% of adults in the United States, typically presents in the third to ﬁfth decade of life, and is 2 to 3 times more common in women than men. 27 Rheumatoid arthritis generally causes symmetric pain and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal joints, proximal interphalangeal joints, wrist joints, and small joints of the feet. The pain is accompanied by joint stiffness that is worse in the morning and improves with use. Rheumatoid arthritis also affects larger joints such as the shoulders, knees, and elbows and causes chronic, irreversible joint injury if left untreated. In addition to joint inﬂammation, rheumatoid arthritis can cause extraarticular features such as interstitial lung disease, rheumatoid nodules, accelerated atherosclerosis, and rheumatoid vasculitis. Serum biomarkers include elevated acute phase reactants, rheumatoid factor, and anticitrullinated protein antibody. The 2010 American College of Rheumatology and European League Against Rheumatism classiﬁcation criteria for rheumatoid arthritis require clinical evidence of deﬁnite synovitis and six or more points according to a score-based algorithm (up to ﬁve points for the number of joints involved, three points for CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 165 KEY POINTS •Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is nine times more prevalent in women than in men. •The diagnosis of SLE generally requires an antinuclear antibody titer of at least 1:80. •Many neurologic symptoms arising in patients with SLE are not causally related to SLE disease activity. •Psychosis in patients with SLE is often related to SLE inﬂammatory disease activity. •Myelitis in patients with SLE may be associated with positive anti–aquaporin-4 antibodies, or these patients may be seronegative. •The diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis is contingent on ﬁndings of clinical synovitis with serum evidence of inﬂammation. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

high-positive rheumatoid factor or high-positive anticitrullinated protein antibody titers, one point for abnormally elevated acute phase reactants [erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) or C-reactive protein (CRP)], and one point for duration of symptoms greater than 6 weeks). 28 SLE and gout can meet criteria for rheumatoid arthritis and must therefore be excluded. Neurologic manifestations of rheumatoid arthritis include spinal disease (causing radiculopathy and myelopathy), inﬂammatory meningitis, and peripheral neuropathy (entrapment mononeuropathy, polyneuropathy, or multiple mononeuropathies). Spinal Disease Rheumatoid arthritis affects the synovial joints of the spine: the facet joints, atlantoaxial joint, and atlantooccipital joints. Spinal disease in patients with rheumatoid arthritis can cause axial musculoskeletal pain (worse after rest and improving with movement), decreased range of lateral rotation of the neck, compressive radiculopathy (including at C2, causing occipital neuralgia), compressive myelopathy, or brainstem compression due to vertical subluxation of the upper cervical spine. Neuroimaging evidence of spinal disease is present in 75% to 86% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis, 29 most commonly anterior atlantoaxial and subaxial subluxation. MRI is more sensitive than x-ray for identifying spinal disease in patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Spinal disease in patients with rheumatoid arthritis is a dynamic process. In a prospective study of patients with rheumatoid arthritis without spinal disease followed for an average of 6 years, 44% developed new imaging evidence of cervical spinal disease, most commonly atlantoaxial subluxation. 30 Important predictive factors are baseline presence of erosive disease in peripheral joints such as the hands and new emergence of erosive peripheral arthritic disease during follow-up. Corticosteroid administration increases the risk of severe spinal arthritic disease, possibly attributable to the deleterious effects of chronic corticosteroid use on bone density and spinal ligaments. Management of spinal disease in patients with rheumatoid arthritis relies ﬁrst on control of systemic inﬂammation. When there are disabling neurologic symptoms not responsive to conservative symptomatic pain management, surgical intervention should be considered. Many patients with imaging evidence of cervical spine subluxation are asymptomatic and do not develop symptoms. Therefore, imaging evidence of spinal arthritic disease in patients with rheumatoid arthritis should be correlated with clinical symptoms to determine whether intervention is needed. Meningitis Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may rarely develop chronic inﬂammatory meningitis, causing headache, seizures, and focal neurologic signs (CASE 7-2). Neuroimaging demonstrates gadolinium enhancement of the pachymeninges, leptomeninges, or both, particularly in the frontal, temporal, and parietal regions (imaging ﬁndings do not distinguish rheumatoid meningitis from other immune-mediated or infectious causes of meningitis). 31 CSF analysis demonstrates modest CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein, 32 CSF glucose that may be low, 31 and CSF anticitrullinated protein antibody and rheumatoid factor that may be elevated. 33 Meningeal biopsy can show a 166 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 7-2 A 45-year-old woman experienced worsening frontal headaches over 3 months. The headaches were constant and accompanied by light and sound sensitivity. There was no nausea. Initial treatment by the patient’s primary care physician with propranolol and topiramate did not help the headache, leading to referral to a neurologist. Neurologic examination was normal. Because of new-onset worsening headache, a brain MRI was obtained that showed gadolinium enhancement of the falx cerebri (FIGURE 7-3). Lumbar puncture showed 20 cells/μL (90% lymphocytes), protein of 60 mg/dL, and glucose of 80 mg/dL. CSF bacterial and mycobacterial cultures were negative. CSF ﬂow cytometry did not show clonal populations. Serum syphilis and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody testing were negative. IgG4 serum levels were normal. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis did not show lymphadenopathy or provide other systemic evidence of sarcoidosis or IgG4-related disease. Serum rheumatoid factor and anticitrullinated protein antibody were positive. Clinical evaluation by a rheumatologist showed mild synovitis in the metacarpophalangeal joints and uncovered a history of morning hand stiffness for the preceding 2 years. She was treated with an 8-week taper of oral corticosteroids and started on rituximab. Her headache improved over the next 3 months. FIGURE 7-3 Imaging of the patient in CASE 7-2 with rheumatoid meningitis. Axial T1 postcontrast MRI demonstrates gadolinium enhancement of the falx cerebri. COMMENT Rheumatoid meningitis can cause pachymeningitis and may precede other overt clinical symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis. The diagnosis relies on clinical and serologic evidence of rheumatoid arthritis and exclusion of infectious, neoplastic, or alternative inﬂammatory causes (eg, sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease). CSF typically shows modest lymphocytosis without other speciﬁc ﬁndings. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 167 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

range of pathologies, including nonspeciﬁc cellular inﬂammation, rheumatoid nodules, granulomatous inﬂammation, and vasculitis. In 15% of patients with rheumatoid meningitis, meningitis is the ﬁrst manifestation of rheumatoid arthritis; therefore, patients with chronic meningitis of unclear etiology should be evaluated for rheumatoid arthritis clinically and serologically with rheumatoid factor and anticitrullinated protein antibody. 31 There are no clinical trials to guide treatment for rheumatoid meningitis. Treatment of rheumatoid arthritis is typically with corticosteroids in the acute phase and maintenance therapy with a steroid-sparing agent such as methotrexate, rituximab, or inﬂiximab. There is no clear consensus on the optimal treatment regimen or how long to treat. The author typically treats rheumatoid meningitis with corticosteroids acutely and uses rituximab for maintenance therapy because of its beneﬁcial effects in patients with systemic rheumatoid arthritis. Peripheral Neuropathy Rheumatoid arthritis can cause mononeuropathy (due to entrapment), polyneuropathy (various mechanisms), or multiple mononeuropathies (due to peripheral nerve vasculitis). Entrapment mononeuropathies in patients with rheumatoid arthritis are due to compression caused by joint inﬂammation and swelling. Median neuropathy at the wrist (carpal tunnel syndrome) is the most common, occurring in approximately 10% of patients with rheumatoid arthritis; 34 ulnar neuropathy at the elbow and tarsal tunnel syndrome causing distal tibial nerve compression can also occur. Subclinical electrodiagnostic evidence of median nerve compression at the wrist is common in patients with rheumatoid arthritis and does not require treatment if asymptomatic. Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may develop a chronic sensory, motor, or sensorimotor axonal polyneuropathy due to cellular inﬂammation, perineural thickening, vasculitis, or amyloid deposition. 34 They may also develop neuropathic symptoms without electrodiagnostic evidence of neuropathy or electrodiagnostic evidence of neuropathy without clinical symptoms. Longer disease duration and the presence of serum rheumatoid factor may be potential risk factors for neuropathy in patients with rheumatoid arthritis, although data are inconsistent. 35 Treatment of polyneuropathy in patients with rheumatoid arthritis is typically symptomatic because of the unclear correlation with systemic inﬂammation and the heterogeneous underlying pathology. Rarely, rheumatoid arthritis can cause multiple mononeuropathies due to rheumatoid vasculitis, usually accompanied by ischemic skin lesions (eg, purpura and ulcers) and systemic symptoms of fever and weight loss. 36 Treatment is based on protocols for primary vasculitic neuropathy, using a combination of corticosteroids and steroid-sparing therapy such as rituximab or cyclophosphamide. As rheumatoid arthritis has become better controlled with modern therapy, the incidence of rheumatoid vasculitis appears to be decreasing. 37 SARCOIDOSIS Sarcoidosis is a chronic, inﬂammatory disease deﬁned histopathologically by noncaseating granulomas without a secondary cause. The lung is the most common site of involvement in patients with sarcoidosis, but any organ can be involved, including the nervous system. The cause of sarcoidosis is unknown, 168 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

although both environmental and occupational exposures (eg, insecticides or mold and mildew) and genetic susceptibility likely contribute. 38 Sarcoidosis presents at a mean age of 44 years and is more common in women and African American individuals. 39 Systemic sarcoidosis often presents with pulmonary symptoms such as cough, chest pain, and dyspnea, with lung pathology conﬁrming granulomatous inﬂammation. There is no prototypical presentation of sarcoidosis because the spectrum of clinical symptoms from sarcoidosis is broad, including skin rash, uveitis, myocarditis, parotid gland enlargement, renal impairment, and nervous system dysfunction. With more frequent use of imaging, sarcoidosis is increasingly diagnosed in asymptomatic patients found to have hilar lymphadenopathy. Lung involvement may be asymptomatic in 30% to 60% of patients with sarcoidosis. 40 Neurosarcoidosis refers to sarcoid granulomatous involvement of the nervous system, which can manifest as cranial neuropathy, meningitis, polyradiculoneuropathy, parenchymal brain lesions, myelitis, peripheral neuropathy, or myopathy. Neurologic symptoms are the ﬁrst manifestation of sarcoidosis in approximately one-half of patients, 41 and 10% to 20% of patients have isolated neurosarcoidosis without any systemic involvement. 42 Deﬁnite neurosarcoidosis requires pathologic conﬁrmation of granulomatous inﬂammation in the nervous system and exclusion of other causes of granulomas, such as tuberculosis or lymphoma. Probable neurosarcoidosis requires typical clinical ﬁndings of neurosarcoidosis with pathologic conﬁrmation of systemic disease (for example, myelitis with pathologic conﬁrmation of granulomatous inﬂammation in the lungs). Possible neurosarcoidosis denotes clinical ﬁndings suggestive of neurosarcoidosis without pathologic conﬁrmation. Cranial Neuropathy Approximately 30% to 50% of patients with neurosarcoidosis develop one or more cranial neuropathies. 41,43 The optic nerve and facial nerve are the most commonly affected, usually individually, although other cranial nerves may be affected in isolation or in combination. Optic neuropathy in patients with sarcoidosis has distinct features that suggest the disease. In contrast to optic neuritis in patients with multiple sclerosis or NMOSD, which presents acutely, optic neuropathy caused by sarcoidosis is more likely to present insidiously (typically over weeks to months) 44 and is typically painless. CSF analysis typically shows a modestly elevated white blood cell count (median of 14 cells/μL) and elevated protein (median of 60 mg/dL); 44 some patients may have oligoclonal bands. MRI demonstrates contrast enhancement of the affected optic nerve in the orbital apex or orbital segment, with approximately 75% of patients demonstrating enhancement of the perineural region of the optic nerve (either isolated perineural enhancement or combined optic nerve and perineural enhancement). 45 An important diagnostic clue to sarcoidosis as the cause of optic neuropathy is the presence of enhancement beyond the optic nerve in the leptomeninges, pachymeninges, hypothalamus, spinal cord, or nerve roots, which occurs in approximately 70% of patients. 44,45 Serum and CSF angiotensin converting enzyme levels are insensitive and nonspeciﬁc. Approximately 80% of patients with optic neuropathy due to sarcoid have evidence of asymptomatic systemic CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 169 KEY POINTS •Rheumatoid meningitis can affect the pachymeninges, leptomeninges, or both on MRI. •Mononeuropathy multiplex in patients with rheumatoid arthritis is often caused by rheumatoid vasculitis and needs urgent treatment. •Most patients with neurosarcoidosis are diagnosed with probable disease based on pathologic conﬁrmation of granulomatous inﬂammation systemically and typical clinical ﬁndings of neurosarcoidosis. •Sarcoidosis-associated optic neuropathy may progress slowly over weeks to months and cause painless vision loss. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

involvement on CT or ﬂudeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (FDG-PET) of the chest and abdomen and pelvis (with FDG-PET more sensitive). Therefore these imaging studies may offer a biopsy site outside the nervous system to conﬁrm the diagnosis of sarcoidosis. These observations, best described in patients with optic neuropathy, also extend to other cranial neuropathies in patients with sarcoidosis, such as facial nerve palsy. 46 Myelitis The spinal cord is the second most common site of involvement in patients with neurosarcoidosis. 41,43 When myelitis is caused by sarcoidosis, it is often the ﬁrst manifestation of the disease. Myelopathy in patients with neurosarcoidosis typically evolves over weeks in contrast to more rapid evolution in patients with other causes of myelitis, such as NMOSD. 47 Some patients can also have stepwise progression with episodes of acute worsening superimposed on a more chronic course. On MRI, sarcoid myelitis is usually longitudinally extensive (ie, spanning more than three vertebral segments), but can be shorter or multifocal. There is typically spinal cord enhancement, often associated with leptomeningeal enhancement. A characteristic MRI pattern in patients with sarcoid myelopathy is the trident sign on axial postcontrast images, caused by the combination of dorsal subpial and central canal enhancement (FIGURE 7-4). 48 When neurosarcoidosis affects the thoracic spinal cord, it is often in spatial proximity to areas of disk degeneration; enhancement in these patients is often more anterior rather than the classic dorsal subpial pattern of involvement. 47 CSF abnormalities in patients with sarcoid-associated myelitis are nonspeciﬁc, demonstrating lymphocytic pleocytosis and elevated protein, with 20% to 40% of patients having oligoclonal bands. 47 ,49 Serum and CSF angiotensin converting enzyme are insensitive and nonspeciﬁc. CSF-soluble IL-2 receptor has been proposed as a more sensitive marker for neurosarcoidosis, but studies suggest it is neither sensitive nor speciﬁc (other diseases, such as tuberculosis, bacterial meningitis, and CNS lymphoma, can cause similarly elevated levels). 50 ,51 Therefore, in patients in whom there is suspicion for neurosarcoidosis of the spine, CT or PET of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis should be used to evaluate for a site of systemic involvement amenable to biopsy to conﬁrm the diagnosis of sarcoidosis. In patients without a suitable FIGURE 7-4 Trident sign in a patient with sarcoid-associated myelopathy. Axial T1 postcontrast MRI illustrates the trident sign consisting of posterior subpial enhancement (arrow) combined with central canal enhancement (arrowhead). 170 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

systemic target, spinal biopsy of the involved meninges or nerve roots should be obtained to evaluate for granulomatous inﬂammation. Meningitis and Focal Brain Lesions Sarcoidosis can cause inﬂammatory meningitis, either alone or in combination with parenchymal brain lesions. Meningitis in patients with sarcoidosis tends to be slowly progressive over weeks and can be skull base predominant or involve the cerebral sulci. When there is skull base involvement, patients may develop cranial neuropathies, ataxia, hearing loss, hydrocephalus, or hypothalamic-pituitary axis abnormalities leading to hypopituitarism. 52 MRI demonstrates meningeal enhancement (involving the leptomeninges, pachymeninges, or both), although this ﬁnding is indistinguishable from other causes of meningitis, such as infections and other inﬂammatory disorders (FIGURE 7-5). One potential neuroimaging clue to neurosarcoidosis of the brain is the ﬁnding of deep medullary vein engorgement with a corkscrew appearance on susceptibility imaging, seen in approximately one-third of patients with neurosarcoidosis of the CNS. 53 Brain lesions in patients with sarcoidosis may be mass-like or inﬁltrative along perivascular spaces, with MRI demonstrating gadolinium enhancement. Mass lesions are frequently associated with adjacent leptomeningeal enhancement. The previously described neuroimaging ﬁndings are suggestive but not sufﬁciently speciﬁc to distinguish intracranial neurosarcoidosis from alternative diagnoses. Pathologic conﬁrmation of systemic disease should be sought by CT or FDG-PET of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis. If no clear biopsy target is found, a CNS lesion should be biopsied; the ﬁnding of angiocentric perivascular granulomatous inﬂammation is diagnostic. 54 Sarcoidosis leads to immunocompromise even in patients not treated with immunomodulatory medications, putting patients at risk for CNS infection. For example, progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy from JC virus infection may occur as the ﬁrst presentation of systemic sarcoidosis. 55 Therefore, in patients with sarcoidosis who develop meningitis or focal brain lesions, infectious causes should be considered before attributing these ﬁndings to neurosarcoidosis. Peripheral Neuropathy Sarcoidosis can cause two distinct types of neuropathy: granulomatous neuropathy and small ﬁber neuropathy. Granulomatous neuropathy is FIGURE 7-5 Sarcoid-associated meningitis. Axial T1 postcontrast MRI demonstrates multifocal, nodular gadolinium enhancement of the leptomeninges. The discontinuous areas of enhancement with variable thickness give rise to the “nodular” appearance. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 171 KEY POINTS •Myelopathy in patients with sarcoidosis may progress slowly over weeks or have episodes of acute worsening with a more chronic myelopathy. •Sarcoidosis is an immunosuppressive condition, and patients may develop opportunistic central nervous system infections even without treatment of sarcoidosis. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

caused by direct granulomatous invasion of the peripheral nerves, presenting as polyradiculoneuropathy, multiple mononeuropathies, or symmetric sensorimotor polyneuropathy. 56 The presentation may be acute (mimicking Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome) or chronic. There are no speciﬁc clinical or electrophysiologic features, so diagnosis requires evidence of systemic sarcoidosis or nerve biopsy demonstrating granulomatous inﬂammation. Small ﬁber neuropathy is common in patients with sarcoidosis, with a prevalence estimated to be 30% or higher, including patients who are asymptomatic. 56,57 Unlike small ﬁber neuropathy in other contexts, neuropathic symptoms in patients with sarcoidosis may be non–length dependent at presentation. 58 The presumed pathogenesis is from chronic inﬂammation and perineural granulomas. Skin biopsy demonstrates decreased intraepidermal nerve ﬁber density compared with healthy controls. 57 Patients with small ﬁber neuropathy may also experience autonomic symptoms such as orthostatic dizziness, palpitations, gastrointestinal dysfunction, or sweating disturbances. Myopathy Sarcoidosis may cause a chronic proximal myopathy (with scattered histiocytic inﬂammatory inﬁltrate and late ﬁbrosis on biopsy), 59 immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, 60 or nodular myopathy (causing soft tissue nodules in muscle, usually without associated weakness). 61 Subclinical muscle involvement is common on FDG-PET and muscle biopsy in patients with systemic sarcoidosis. Treatment Initial treatment of neurosarcoidosis typically consists of corticosteroids either as an IV pulse or high-dose oral corticosteroids followed by an oral taper. There are no data to guide the duration of treatment, but the author typically uses at least a 2- to 3-month taper given the slow response to treatment. In patients with severe disease, inﬂiximab is recommended by consensus based on observational studies. 62 Methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and azathioprine may also be considered, although azathioprine may be less effective compared with methotrexate. 63 In practice, inﬂiximab may be combined with lower doses of methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil to decrease the risk of antidrug antibodies. Recovery from neurosarcoidosis may be incomplete even with treatment. BEHÇET SYNDROME Behçet syndrome is characterized by recurrent oral aphthous ulcers (painful and often occurring 3 or more times a year), accompanied by recurrent genital ulcers, skin rashes (eg, erythema nodosum, papulopustular lesions), and ocular inﬂammation (anterior or posterior uveitis, retinal vasculitis, and optic neuritis). Some patients develop inﬂammatory vasculitis of both arteries and veins, causing aneurysms, thromboses, or rupture with hemorrhage (eg, pulmonary arterial aneurysm, Budd-Chiari syndrome, and pulmonary arterial-bronchial ﬁstula, respectively). The disease is more common in individuals from the Middle East, Mediterranean basin, and Far East, 64 and is associated with a polymorphism in HLA-B*51 in some regions (eg, 75% of cases in Turkey, but only 36% in Spain). 65 A characteristic feature of Behçet syndrome is a positive pathergy test: a delayed (approximately 24 hours) erythematous response to local skin injury, 172 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

which may be noted after a blood draw or an IV placement. A positive pathergy test is present in 30% to 60% of patients with Behçet syndrome and varies by global region (60% sensitivity in patients of Turkish origin compared with as low as 15% in patients of Korean origin). 66 A positive pathergy test is not speciﬁc to Behçet syndrome and can also be seen in patients with other neutrophilic dermatoses such as Sweet syndrome. Serum ESR, CRP, and ferritin are usually elevated during ﬂares of Behçet syndrome but are nonspeciﬁc markers of inﬂammation. Behçet syndrome does not have a speciﬁc biomarker and is therefore diagnosed by clinical criteria. The International Study Group for Behçet’s Disease criteria are frequently used, which require recurrent oral ulcerations and at least two of the following signs: recurrent genital ulcerations, eye lesions, skin lesions, and a positive pathergy test. 67 Neurologic involvement occurs in 5% to 15% of patients with Behçet syndrome and is referred to as neuro-Behçet syndrome. 64 Neurologic manifestations of Behçet syndrome are classiﬁed as parenchymal (eg, meningitis, encephalitis, myelitis) and nonparenchymal (venous sinus thrombosis). In more than 90% of patients, neurologic manifestations of Behçet syndrome follow the onset of systemic disease, and neuro-Behçet syndrome is therefore unlikely if there is no history of recurrent oral ulcers. 64 Parenchymal Neuro-Behçet Syndrome Parenchymal neuro-Behçet syndrome refers to inﬂammation within the CNS and can cause subacute meningitis, encephalitis, or myelitis that often coincides with a systemic Behçet ﬂare or constitutional symptoms (eg, fever, malaise). The most frequent manifestation of parenchymal neuro-Behçet syndrome relapses is brainstem inﬂammation presenting with cranial nerve abnormalities, weakness, ataxia, and, in severe cases, an altered level of consciousness. MRI shows T2 hyperintense, contrast-enhancing brainstem lesions affecting the upper brainstem extending into the thalamus and cerebellum (FIGURE 7-6). Other neurologic manifestations of Behçet syndrome include recurrent meningitis (which can lead to communicating hydrocephalus) and longitudinally extensive myelitis (which may demonstrate the bagel sign on MRI: a central T2 hypointense spinal cord lesion on axial images). 68 Meningitis and myelitis can overlap with encephalitis in patients with acute presentations. Rarely, Behçet syndrome can cause tumefactive brain lesions or progressive cognitive decline and ataxia (10% of patients) without a clear FIGURE 7-6 Parenchymal neuro-Behçet syndrome. Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI demonstrates T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal brainstem extending into the cerebellar white matter. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 173 KEY POINTS •The pathogenesis of small ﬁber neuropathy in patients with sarcoidosis is complex and includes effects of chronic inﬂammation and perineural granulomas. •The prevalence of HLA- B*51 polymorphism in patients with Behçet syndrome is variable geographically, and its presence or absence should not be relied upon for diagnosis. •Most patients experience neurologic manifestations of Behçet syndrome following the onset of systemic disease. •Brainstem inﬂammation is the most frequent area affected in patients with parenchymal neuro- Behçet syndrome. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

radiologic correlate. 69 During ﬂares of neuro-Behçet syndrome, CSF shows elevated lymphocytic cell count and protein without oligoclonal bands. 64 IL-6 levels in CSF are also often elevated during attacks, but they are not speciﬁc for Behçet syndrome. Parenchymal neuro-Behçet syndrome is generally diagnosed when compatible neurologic manifestations appear in patients with a history of systemic Behçet syndrome and clinical, imaging, and laboratory studies showing signs of inﬂammatory brain disease. If a brain biopsy is pursued, pathology typically demonstrates intense perivascular cellular inﬁltration of mononuclear cells (primarily T lymphocytes) around small vessels and associated neuronal death. 70 There is no speciﬁc pathologic marker in histologic studies. Treatment of neuro-Behçet syndrome is with corticosteroids during an acute ﬂare, followed by steroid-sparing agents. Inﬂiximab treatment is associated with high rates of remission and is the author’s preferred agent. 71 Other agents with evidence of efﬁcacy include cyclosporin A, azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate mofetil, and tocilizumab. 72 There are no randomized comparative trials guiding choice of treatment. Nonparenchymal Neuro-Behçet Syndrome Nonparenchymal neuro-Behçet syndrome refers primarily to cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, which can occur concurrently with or independently from systemic or parenchymal ﬂares. The diagnosis is made by CT or MR venogram. If CSF is obtained, it is typically not inﬂammatory. Treatment of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis is with anticoagulation; in the context of Behçet syndrome, many practitioners also initiate immunomodulatory therapy to decrease inﬂammation and reduce the risk of subsequent thrombosis. SJ¨OGREN DISEASE Sj¨ ogren disease is characterized primarily by dry eyes and dry mouth (sicca syndrome) caused by exocrine gland inﬂammation. Approximately 30% to 40% of patients with Sj¨ ogren disease also develop extraglandular manifestations, including musculoskeletal involvement (arthralgia and myalgia), skin disease (dry skin, Raynaud phenomenon, rashes), pulmonary disease (interstitial lung disease), hematologic dysfunction (eg, cytopenias, cryoglobulinemia, monoclonal gammopathies), and nervous system involvement (fatigue, cognitive slowing, myelitis, neuropathy). 73 Sj¨ ogren disease is nine times more common in women than in men, with the average age of diagnosis of approximately 56 years. 74 Diagnosis of Sj¨ ogren disease combines symptoms of sicca syndrome, objective evidence of exocrine gland dysfunction, and evidence of inﬂammation. Classiﬁcation criteria from the American College of Rheumatology and the European League Against Rheumatism combine these elements through a scoring scheme. 75 Dry eyes and dry mouth in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease are typically severe, with patients requiring tear drops for dry eyes, waking up at night with dry mouth, and needing to drink ﬂuids to swallow food. Ocular dryness can be quantiﬁed and conﬁrmed by the Schirmer test (tear production measured by ﬁlter paper placed in the lower eyelid; abnormal tear production is less than 5 mm after 5 minutes) or ocular surface staining (commonly with rose bengal stain). Salivary gland pathology in the 174 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

parotid glands can be demonstrated on ultrasonography (typically multiple hypoechoic regions) or MRI (nodular pattern of hyperintense and hypointense regions); salivary ﬂow can also be measured directly. Evidence of inﬂammation is demonstrated either serologically or by salivary gland biopsy. Patients with sicca syndrome should be evaluated with serum anti-Ro/SSA, anti-La/SSB, ANA, and rheumatoid factor in addition to other autoantibodies based on symptoms. Anti-Ro antibodies include antibodies against two distinct proteins: Ro52 (referring to a 52kD E3 ubiquitin ligase) and Ro60 (referring to a 60kD cytoplasmic RNA binding protein). Patients with Sj¨ ogren disease often have both anti-Ro52 and anti-Ro60 antibodies. Isolated anti-Ro52 antibodies are less speciﬁc and found in patients with other diseases such as myositis and malignancy. 76 Isolated anti-Ro60 antibody positivity is most frequent in patients with SLE. Overall, antibodies to anti-Ro52 and anti-Ro60 are found in approximately 60% of patients with Sj¨ ogren disease. 77 Anti-La/SSB antibodies are present in approximately 30% to 60% of patients. 78 Isolated positivity for anti-La without concurrent anti-Ro positivity is infrequent. When there is suspicion of Sj¨ ogren disease but negative serology, a lip biopsy can be obtained to evaluate for focal lymphocytic sialoadenitis. Sj¨ ogren disease can occur in isolation or in association with other rheumatologic disorders such as SLE or rheumatoid arthritis. Patients with extraglandular manifestations of Sj¨ ogren disease (such as neuropathy) may not have sicca syndrome. Classiﬁcation criteria require sicca syndrome for the diagnosis of Sj¨ ogren disease, so how to classify patients who develop extraglandular manifestations and serologic or pathologic evidence of Sj¨ ogren disease without sicca syndrome remains unclear. Central Nervous System Manifestations The most frequent disabling neurologic symptom in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease is fatigue, estimated to be present in approximately 40% to 70% of patients, and it is often the reason patients stop working. 79,80 Fatigue is multidimensional in quality and includes both physical and mental aspects. Patients with Sj¨ ogren disease have more physical activity-related fatigue compared with cognitive fatigue. 79 The pathogenesis of fatigue in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease is unclear, and data on its association with inﬂammatory disease activity are conﬂicting. In a 2024 large cohort study of fatigue in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease, both depression and disease activity were predictors of fatigue. 80 Myelitis in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease is rare and typically presents as a disabling subacute myelopathy with longitudinally extensive cord signal change on MRI. In approximately one-half of patients, myelitis precedes a diagnosis of Sj¨ ogren disease. 81 The majority of patients with longitudinally extensive myelitis in Sj¨ ogren disease have antibodies to AQP4. 82 Patients with Sj¨ ogren disease and myelitis have also been described who are seronegative for anti-AQP4 antibodies or are seropositive for other antibodies, including to myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein. Patients who are seropositive for anti- AQP4 antibodies have comorbid NMOSD and Sj¨ ogren disease and should be treated for NMOSD. How best to treat seronegative myelitis in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease is unclear. The initial episode of myelitis is treated with short- term immunotherapy, such as a corticosteroid pulse or plasma exchange. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 175 KEY POINTS •Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies are present in approximately 60% of patients with Sj¨ogren disease and have moderate sensitivity. •Fatigue is the most common neuropsychiatric symptom of Sj¨ogren disease. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Patients with Sj¨ ogren disease may also develop optic neuritis. Some patients with Sj¨ ogren disease–associated optic neuritis test positive for anti-AQP4 antibodies, but other patients remain seronegative. Autoimmune encephalitis and meningoencephalitis may rarely occur in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease. Whether the encephalitis is causally related to Sj¨ ogren disease or from an unidentiﬁed viral infection is unclear. A recurring aseptic meningoencephalitis manifesting as acute-onset fever, meningismus, and confusional state with inﬂammatory CSF that improves spontaneously or with corticosteroids has been reported. 83 Inﬂammatory lesions can sometimes be seen in the parenchyma or meninges on MRI in these patients. Some patients with autoimmune encephalitis associated with Sj¨ ogren disease test positive for known antineuronal antibodies, likely suggesting a comorbid disorder. Peripheral Nervous System Manifestations Neuropathy is present in approximately 10% of patients with Sj¨ ogren disease and generally precedes the diagnosis of Sj¨ ogren disease and the onset of sicca syndrome. 84,85 Serologic testing for anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB has more limited sensitivity in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease–associated neuropathy (estimated at approximately 50%) compared with patients with an established diagnosis of Sj¨ ogren disease with sicca syndrome. 84 Compared with serology, other testing, such as a Schirmer test and salivary gland biopsy, may show subclinical evidence of exocrine glandular inﬂammation, which manifests clinically later. Sensorimotor and sensory axonal neuropathy are the most common types of neuropathy in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease, accounting for approximately 44% of neuropathies in this patient population. 85 Axonal neuropathy in these patients typically manifests as slowly progressive distal- predominant sensory loss that ascends over months to years. Weakness in the toes or feet may occur later in the disease course. Muscle stretch reﬂexes are generally reduced or absent. The neuropathy can continue to slowly progress despite treatment of Sj¨ ogren disease. Sj¨ ogren disease-associated sensory ganglionopathy (also known as sensory neuronopathy) manifests as non–length-dependent sensory loss with preserved motor function and accounts for approximately 27% of neuropathies in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease. In patients with Sj¨ ogren disease, ganglionopathy can present in a variety of ways, including isolated trigeminal neuropathy (causing progressive unilateral or bilateral facial numbness), non–length- dependent small ﬁber neuropathy, or severe, mixed small and large ﬁber sensory neuronopathy causing numbness, imbalance, and sensory ataxic gait. The onset of ganglionopathy can be asymmetric and progress over months to years. 86 Many patients develop dysautonomia in addition to sensory loss. EMG and nerve conduction studies show preserved compound muscle action potentials with a non–length-dependent decrease in sensory nerve action potential amplitudes. Pathologically, there is cell-mediated inﬂammation of sensory ganglia with neuronal death. 87 An uncommon form of neuropathy in patients with Sj¨ ogren disease is acute mononeuropathy multiplex, which presents with painful, sequential dysfunction of multiple peripheral nerves (such as the peroneal nerve followed by the radial nerve). Necrotizing vasculitic neuropathy associated with Sj¨ ogren disease accounts for some of these cases. As in other patients with vasculitic neuropathy, patients should be evaluated for cryoglobulinemia, monoclonal 176 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

gammopathy, Lyme disease, hepatitis B, human immunodeﬁciency virus (HIV) infection, and other evidence of systemic vasculitis comorbid with Sj¨ ogren disease. The optimal treatment for Sj¨ ogren disease–associated neuropathy is unclear and based on retrospective, observational studies that are often conﬂicting. IV immunoglobulin (IVIg) is often used initially. Some case series show marked improvement, whereas in others, the majority of patients do not respond. 88,89 Other immunomodulatory agents such as glucocorticoids, mycophenolate mofetil, rituximab, and cyclophosphamide have modest evidence. In patients with multiple mononeuropathies, rituximab and cyclophosphamide are preferred over IVIg based on clinical experience from the treatment of vasculitic neuropathies. Because of the uncertainties about optimal treatment and potential toxicities from immunosuppressive agents, many patients with indolent disease are treated symptomatically and followed clinically. VASCULITIS Vasculitis refers to inﬂammation of blood vessels, causing ischemic tissue injury (or sometimes aneurysmal vessel dilation and rupture). Vasculitis can affect the central or peripheral nervous systems. Vasculitis can be isolated to the nervous system (such as primary angiitis of the CNS or nonsystemic vasculitic neuropathy), a systemic syndrome affecting multiple organ systems (including the nervous system), or secondary to another cause such as infection (eg, syphilis) or malignancy. Vasculitis syndromes are categorized according to the size of the vessels affected: large vessel vasculitis (giant cell arteritis, Takayasu arteritis), medium vessel vasculitis (polyarteritis nodosa, Kawasaki disease), or small vessel vasculitis (many syndromes, including IgA vasculitis, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, and others). 90 This classiﬁcation is not absolute, and there is overlap among the syndromes. Behçet syndrome, for example, can affect both large and small vessels. Antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody–associated vasculitis can affect both small and medium vessels. TABLE 7-2 lists vasculitis syndromes, their clinical and diagnostic features, and their nervous system manifestations. Giant cell arteritis is the most common systemic vasculitis and is discussed in further detail in the section Giant Cell Arteritis. Central Nervous System Vasculitis Whether primary or secondary, CNS vasculitis causes ischemic strokes with symptoms depending on the caliber of vessel affected (middle cerebral artery syndrome in patients with medium vessel vasculitis versus small vessel stroke in patients with small vessel vasculitis). Vasculitis can also cause intracranial hemorrhage or cause tissue injury by hypoperfusion or systemic embolism. CNS involvement in patients with systemic vasculitis typically occurs in the context of a systemic ﬂare with multiorgan involvement, such as stroke with renal dysfunction and purpuric rash. In contrast, primary CNS vasculitis is isolated to the CNS. The most frequent symptom of primary CNS vasculitis is headache (present in approximately 60% to 70% of patients), which can precede other neurologic symptoms by weeks. 91 Other symptoms and signs of CNS vasculitis are confusion, weakness, visual blurriness, or aphasia that accrue insidiously or progress with successive strokes. Brain MRI is abnormal in patients with CNS vasculitis, but there is no speciﬁc imaging pattern. The sensitivity of vascular neuroimaging depends on CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 177 KEY POINTS •Neuropathy frequently precedes the diagnosis of Sj¨ ogren disease. •Sj¨ ogren disease– associated ganglionopathy can be asymmetric, slowly progressing over months to years. •Systemic vasculitis is classiﬁed by the size of the vessel primarily affected. •Systemic vasculitis can cause ischemic strokes by hypoperfusion and central embolism in addition to intracranial vasculitis. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

the vasculitis syndrome. In patients with large and medium vessel vasculitis syndromes, the internal carotid arteries and proximal circle of Willis vessels may demonstrate stenosis or occlusion. In patients with small vessel vasculitis syndromes, angiography has low sensitivity because vessels smaller than 500 μm in diameter are not well visualized. Angiographic “beading” has low sensitivity and speciﬁcity for CNS vasculitis. Vessel wall imaging may help distinguish vasculitis from other causes of vasculopathy. Concentric vessel wall enhancement distinguishes vasculitis from intracranial atherosclerosis, which causes eccentric vessel wall enhancement. Although the technique may help to distinguish between these two etiologies, it is unclear how well it distinguishes inﬂammatory CNS vasculitis from infectious vasculitis, genetic vasculopathy, and hypercoagulable states with TABLE 7-2 Common Vasculitis Syndromes and Associated Central Nervous System and Peripheral Nervous System Neurologic Manifestations Vasculitis syndrome Clinical ﬁndings painful peripheral neuropathy CONTINUED ON PAGE 179 178 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. accumulation of microthrombi in small-caliber blood vessels. On CSF analysis, approximately 90% of patients with primary CNS vasculitis have elevated protein or a lymphocytic pleocytosis 92 ; although there are fewer data on patterns of CSF abnormalities in patients with secondary CNS vasculitis, most patients also demonstrate nonspeciﬁc inﬂammatory changes. 93 Deﬁnitive diagnosis of CNS vasculitis requires biopsy unless the patient has a well-established diagnosis of a systemic cause of vasculitis. Clues to systemic vasculitis include myocardial infarction without coronary artery disease, progressive nonatherosclerotic carotid artery stenosis (as can be seen in patients with Takayasu arteritis), purpuric rash, limb claudication, and severe abdominal or testicular pain (TABLE 7-2). Granulomatosis with polyangiitis can also cause pachymeningitis, manifesting as headaches and focal neurologic deﬁcits. 94 MRI in these patients CONTINUED FROM PAGE 178 Vasculitis syndrome Clinical ﬁndings Diagnostic clues Central nervous system manifestations Peripheral nervous system manifestations Small (and medium) vessel vasculitis Microscopic polyangiitis Pulmonary hemorrhage, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis Positive antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, pauci-immune glomerulonephritis on biopsy, no granulomas Ischemic stroke Multiple mononeuropathies, progressive painful peripheral neuropathy Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis Asthma, eosinophilia, sinusitis, pulmonary inﬁltrates Eosinophils greater than 10%, positive antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies, biopsy showing eosinophilic granulomas or vasculitis, asthma history Ischemic stroke Multiple mononeuropathies, progressive painful peripheral neuropathy Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis Palpable purpura, glomerulonephritis, arthralgia, association with hepatitis C virus Cryoglobulins in serum, low complement levels, positive hepatitis C virus serology Rare central nervous system involvement Painful symmetric peripheral neuropathy, multiple mononeuropathies Henoch- Sch¨ onlein purpura (IgA vasculitis) Palpable purpura (especially legs), arthralgia, abdominal pain, renal involvement Biopsy with IgA deposition, mostly pediatric Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), ischemic stroke (rare) Rare, possible peripheral neuropathy Primary angiitis of the central nervous system Headache, encephalopathy, stroke syndromes Biopsy showing vasculitis with ischemic injury, CSF with elevated cell countor protein, angiographic beading abnormalities (low speciﬁcity) Ischemic stroke, headache, encephalopathy, intracranial hemorrhage None CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 179 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology.
