shows isolated pachymeningeal enhancement, or more rarely, a mass-like meningeal or orbital lesion. Biopsy in these patients shows granulomatous inﬂammation with vasculitis. Peripheral Nervous System Vasculitis Many of the medium and small vessel vasculitis syndromes affect the peripheral nervous system (TABLE 7-2). Broadly, peripheral nervous system vasculitis manifests clinically in one of three patterns. The ﬁrst and most recognized pattern is multiple mononeuropathies (also known as mononeuritis multiplex), which refers to multiple nerves being affected either sequentially or synchronously. For example, a patient may experience sciatic neuropathy followed by partial radial neuropathy. Multiple mononeuropathies are most prevalent in patients with medium vessel vasculitis syndromes such as polyarteritis nodosa. The second clinical pattern presenting in approximately 30% of patients with vasculitic neuropathy is a subacute progressive, nonstepwise painful neuropathy that is noted to be asymmetric only with careful clinical examination. 95 The diagnosis of vasculitis is often not considered in patients with this presentation because it is less recognized as potentially associated with vasculitis as compared with multiple mononeuropathies. The third clinical pattern is radiculoplexus neuropathy, which is most characteristic of microvasculitis found in patients with diabetic amyotrophy. 96 In all of these patterns of presentation, unifying features are the presence of severe pain and rapid progression over days to weeks. 95 In patients with vasculitic neuropathies, careful clinical examination shows areas of asymmetry that may not be noticed by the patient. This is a key ﬁnding to support the diagnosis. EMG and nerve conduction studies in patients with vasculitic neuropathy demonstrate axonal changes without demyelination. Following acute segmental nerve infarcts, nerve conduction studies may show a transient conduction block. CSF analysis is not routinely performed unless there are other indications. Deﬁnitive diagnosis of vasculitic neuropathy requires a nerve biopsy, typically of an affected sensory nerve such as the sural nerve, superﬁcial peroneal nerve, or superﬁcial radial nerve. In patients with vasculitic neuropathy, the sensitivity of biopsy is estimated to be approximately 60%. 97 Inclusion of muscle in the biopsy sample improves biopsy yield by approximately 5% to 15%. 98 Giant Cell Arteritis Giant cell arteritis is the most common systemic vasculitis, with a peak incidence between the ages of 70 and 80 years. 99 The disease is not seen in individuals younger than 50 years. The incidence of giant cell arteritis is higher in Scandinavian countries and in populations of Scandinavian descent than in other global regions (for example, Australia has less than one-half the incidence of giant cell arteritis in older adults compared with Sweden). 100 Giant cell arteritis is a large vessel vasculitis affecting the aorta and its major branches, such as the subclavian, axillary, vertebral, and internal and external carotid arteries. Branches of the carotid arteries, such as the ophthalmic artery, may be affected even though these are not large arteries. Systemic symptoms of fever, fatigue, and weight loss herald the onset of giant cell arteritis in more than 50% of patients. Polymyalgia rheumatica (characterized by aching and stiffness in the neck, shoulders, arms, and pelvic girdle) occurs in approximately 50% of patients, and may precede or follow 180 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis. 99 Although the headache of giant cell arteritis is classically associated with temporal pain, headache patterns in patients with giant cell arteritis are variable in location. Headaches in patients with giant cell arteritis are typically new in onset, of at least moderate severity, and often resistant to simple analgesics. Jaw claudication is a speciﬁc but insensitive feature of giant cell arteritis, present in approximately 34% of patients. 101 On examination, temporal artery enlargement, tenderness, or a decrease in pulse are speciﬁc but insensitive, present in only 40% to 50% of patients. 101 Vision loss is a severe complication of giant cell arteritis due to arteritic involvement of branches of the ophthalmic artery (eg, central retinal artery, cilioretinal artery). Approximately 25% of patients have transient monocular vision loss preceding permanent vision loss. 102 Vision loss is generally severe (median visual acuity is counting ﬁngers). 102 Ophthalmic examination shows optic nerve edema and pallor from ischemic injury to the nerve. Stroke is rare in patients with giant cell arteritis, occurring in approximately 3% of patients, most commonly in the posterior circulation (60% to 80% of patients). 103 Ischemic strokes in patients with giant cell arteritis may occur from arteritis in cervical segments of involved arteries, causing hypoperfusion or embolism. True intracranial arteritis is rare but has been reported. 104 Serum ESR and CRP are elevated in 80% to 90% of patients with giant cell arteritis. Some patients may have only elevated CRP but normal age-adjusted ESR. Approximately 4% of patients with giant cell arteritis have normal ESR and CRP. ESR and CRP may be normal in 10% of patients started on corticosteroids before laboratory testing. 105 The diagnosis of giant cell arteritis is conﬁrmed by temporal artery biopsy or by color Doppler ultrasonography of the temporal artery. Temporal artery biopsy has an estimated sensitivity of approximately 75%. 106 Pathology shows giant cell formation in the vessel walls with loss and fragmentation of vascular smooth muscle cells and elastic ﬁbers. The pathologic changes are not uniform and may focally affect some areas of an artery while sparing other segments. The optimal length of biopsy samples and whether to pursue unilateral versus bilateral temporal artery biopsies are areas of controversy. Color Doppler ultrasonography of the temporal arteries and other extracranial vessels demonstrating a hypoechoic signal surrounding the affected arteries (the halo sign) has an estimated sensitivity of approximately 77% and speciﬁcity greater than 95% for giant cell arteritis. 107 In centers with expertise in color Doppler ultrasonography, temporal artery biopsy is typically not pursued if the patient has been diagnosed by ultrasonography and a compatible clinical syndrome. Treatment with corticosteroids should be started upon suspicion of the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis, such as new-onset headache in an older individual with elevated ESR (a threshold of 50 mm/h is often used). Arteritic ischemic optic neuropathy usually does not occur once corticosteroids are started. Upon conﬁrmation of the diagnosis with either temporal artery biopsy or color Doppler ultrasonography, all patients are treated with high-dose corticosteroids (prednisone doses ranging between 40 mg and 60 mg a day, tapered over weeks). Treatment with tocilizumab or upadacitinib in addition to corticosteroids achieved improved rates of sustained remission compared with corticosteroids alone in randomized trials. 108,109 However, how best to select patients for adjunctive treatment with tocilizumab or upadacitinib or CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 181 KEY POINTS •Granulomatosis with polyangiitis can cause granulomatous inﬂammatory intracranial lesions. •Vasculitic neuropathy from systemic vasculitis may present as multiple mononeuropathies or progressive, nonstepwise polyneuropathy. •Giant cell arteritis is not seen in individuals younger than 50 years. •Polymyalgia rheumatica may precede or follow the diagnosis of giant cell arteritis. •Strokes in patients with giant cell arteritis are infrequent and have a predisposition for the posterior circulation. •In patients with a clinical syndrome compatible with giant cell arteritis, a clear halo sign on color Doppler ultrasonography may substitute for temporal artery biopsy. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

whether to treat all patients with these agents remains unclear. Aspirin may be considered in patients with giant cell arteritis who have giant cell arteritis– associated stroke or additional cerebrovascular risk factors. SYSTEMIC SCLEROSIS Systemic sclerosis (also known as scleroderma) is a heterogeneous group of immune-mediated disorders causing vasculopathy and ﬁbrosis of the skin and systemic organs such as the lung, heart, kidney, and gastrointestinal system. The global prevalence of systemic sclerosis is approximately 17.6 per 100,000 people, with women affected 4 to 5 times more than men. 110 There is geographical variation with a lower prevalence of systemic sclerosis in Asia compared with Europe or North America. The pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis is incompletely understood but involves early changes in vascular tone and function followed by ﬁbrosis from activated ﬁbroblasts. Systemic sclerosis has distinct clinical subtypes with different prognoses: u Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis is associated with cutaneous sclerosis in the distal limbs, face, and trunk preceded by a prodrome of Raynaud phenomenon. Systemic organs are often spared except for esophageal dysmotility and pulmonary hypertension. Approximately 50% or more patients in this subgroup have anticentromere antibodies. 111 u Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis is associated with Raynaud phenomenon, more proximal sclerosis in the limbs, and ﬁbrosis of the lungs, gastrointestinal tract, kidneys, and cardiovascular system. Patients with this subtype may have anti-topoisomerase I (also known as anti-Scl-70) and anti-RNA polymerase III antibodies. These antibodies are speciﬁc for systemic sclerosis but not sensitive (present in less than 50% of patients). u Systemic sclerosis without skin sclerosis (sine scleroderma) is associated with typical systemic sclerosis organ involvement, such as interstitial lung disease and Raynaud phenomenon, but without sclerotic skin changes. u Systemic sclerosis overlaps with other autoimmune disorders, such as SLE or rheumatoid arthritis. Linear scleroderma and morphea are not considered forms of systemic sclerosis. The diagnosis of systemic sclerosis is made by integrating the examination ﬁndings of sclerodactyly, abnormal nailfold capillaries (dilated capillary loops or dropout), laboratory testing, and imaging screening for systemic disease (eg, interstitial lung disease or inﬂammatory myocardial changes). The most common neurologic manifestations of systemic sclerosis are myopathy and peripheral neuropathy. In the brain, there can be signs of early small vessel disease on MRI, and the prevalence of white matter lesions is more than would be expected by chance or explained by other risk factors, but inﬂammatory involvement of the brain is infrequent. 112 Myopathy Clinically evident myopathy occurs in approximately 17% of patients with systemic sclerosis, 113 but MRI demonstrates muscle edema in up to 41% of patients. 114 Patterns of myopathy on muscle biopsy in patients with systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy include noninﬂammatory ﬁbrosis and inﬂammatory myositis patterns (eg, polymyositis-like, immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, nonspeciﬁc myositis, and dermatomyositis). 115 Myositis with systemic sclerosis is classiﬁed as overlap myositis, a term referring to the presence of inﬂammatory myopathy in conjunction with systemic rheumatologic disease. 182 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RHEUMATOLOGIC DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy causes subacute-onset, symmetric, proximal limb weakness. The neck and pharyngeal muscles may also be affected. Creatine kinase is elevated in approximately 67% of patients with more marked elevation in patients with inﬂammatory myopathy compared with ﬁbrosis alone. 115 EMG shows a myopathic pattern in more than 80% of patients, and MRI of clinically weak muscles shows signal abnormalities in approximately 80% of patients. 115 Because of the relative insensitivity of creatine kinase levels compared with EMG and muscle MRI, a normal creatine kinase value alone should not be used to exclude myopathy in a patient with systemic sclerosis and progressive weakness. Myositis-speciﬁc antibodies that may occur in patients with systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy include anti–PM/Scl-75 and anti–PM/Scl-100, anti–U3 RNP, and anti-Ku. The sensitivity of each of these antibodies is less than 25%. Anti–PM/Scl 75 and anti–PM/Scl-100 antibody–positive myositis is associated with weakness of the arm abductors more so than the hip ﬂexors. 116 Anti-Ku is associated with an immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy phenotype with predominantly proximal weakness of the limbs. 115 The diagnosis of systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy is made based on clinical, laboratory, and EMG or MRI parameters, with muscle biopsy obtained if there is ambiguity about the cause of myopathy or if weakness progresses despite treatment. Systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy is treated with IVIg for acute ﬂares and immunomodulatory agents such as mycophenolate mofetil for chronic maintenance therapy. Unlike in patients with other inﬂammatory myopathies, glucocorticoids are generally avoided in patients with systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy because they may provoke scleroderma renal crisis, especially in susceptible patients with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis. However, in patients with progressive weakness resistant to other therapies, low-dose glucocorticoids (less than 20 mg prednisone equivalent a day) may be used with careful monitoring for the emergence of hypertension that could indicate systemic sclerosis–associated renal dysfunction. Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral neuropathy occurs in approximately 27% of patients with systemic sclerosis and may precede or follow the diagnosis of systemic sclerosis. 117 The neuropathy is typically sensory or sensorimotor and axonal. However, it is unclear if such neuropathies can truly be attributed to systemic sclerosis because up to 80% of patients with systemic sclerosis who develop polyneuropathy have causes other than systemic sclerosis, such as diabetes, renal dysfunction, or another comorbid autoimmune disease. 118 Patients with systemic sclerosis can also develop multiple mononeuropathies, characterized on biopsy by perivascular cellular inﬂammation with the occasional ﬁnding of necrotizing vasculitis. 119 Systemic sclerosis–associated mononeuropathy multiplex is painful and asymmetric with a subacute onset. Treatment is generally immunomodulatory therapy. Compression neuropathies can also occur in patients with systemic sclerosis due to entrapment in sclerotic tissue. Neuropathic pain should be distinguished from pain caused by a musculoskeletal etiology or digital ischemia, both of which are common in patients with systemic sclerosis. Less commonly, systemic sclerosis can cause cranial neuropathies, most commonly CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 183 KEY POINTS •Systemic sclerosis has diverse clinical presentations with varying risks for systemic ﬁbrotic disease. •Creatine kinase is elevated in approximately 67% of patients with systemic sclerosis– associated myopathy. •Glucocorticoids should be used judiciously in patients with systemic sclerosis–associated myopathy because of the risk of scleroderma renal crisis. •Trigeminal neuropathy may be the presenting sign of systemic sclerosis. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

trigeminal neuropathy, which can be the initial sign of the disease. Patients typically present with facial and oral numbness and paresthesia (FIGURE 7-7) without symptoms of trigeminal neuralgia (ie, lancinating pain). An absent corneal reﬂex on examination supports the diagnosis. Some patients develop additional cranial neuropathies involving the facial, hypoglossal, and glossopharyngeal nerves. 120 Cranial neuropathy in patients with systemic sclerosis is treated by control of systemic disease with immunomodulatory therapy, along with symptomatic treatment of craniofacial pain, if present. CONCLUSION Patients with rheumatologic disorders can develop a wide variety of neurologic manifestations. These may be related to the underlying disease, associated with treatment of the underlying disease, or coincidental comorbid conditions unrelated to the underlying disease. Neurologists play a crucial role in evaluating such patients to determine the clinical syndrome and whether it is related to the primary rheumatologic condition. Some patients with rheumatologic disorders present with neurologic syndromes, requiring neurologists to include rheumatologic conditions in the differential diagnosis for unexplained neurologic presentations and understand the appropriate serologic and imaging evaluation. Multidisciplinary collaboration between neurologists and rheumatologists improves the care of patients with rheumatologic disorders who develop neurologic conditions. Neurologic Manifestations of Hepatic and Gastrointestinal Disease By Jessamyn Conell-Price, MD, MS ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article discusses the pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of neurologic complications of hepatic and gastrointestinal diseases. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Hepatic and gastrointestinal diseases such as hepatitis C, Wilson disease, inﬂammatory bowel disease, and celiac disease can initially present with neurologic symptoms, and neurologic syndromes may be the only manifestations of these conditions. Renal replacement therapy to decrease ammonia levels reduces mortality in patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy. Patients with chronic liver disease should be screened for covert cognitive changes to facilitate earlier initiation of ammonia-lowering therapies to decrease episodes of overt hepatic encephalopathy. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Hepatic encephalopathy has different clinical presentations and management depending on whether it is due to acute or chronic liver disease. Rare complications of chronic liver disease include hepatic myelopathy and acquired hepatocellular degeneration. Gastrointestinal disorders and bariatric surgery can cause malabsorption, leading to vitamin and mineral deﬁciencies that can present as neurologic disorders such as neuropathy, myelopathy, and neuropsychiatric symptoms. Celiac disease can cause neurologic symptoms such as ataxia without associated gastrointestinal symptoms and should therefore be considered in the differential diagnosis of ataxia. Inﬂammatory bowel disease increases the risk of thrombotic events, and treatment of inﬂammatory bowel disease with tumor necrosis factor-α inhibitor therapy can cause central and peripheral nervous system inﬂammatory complications. INTRODUCTION H epatic and gastrointestinal disorders can affect the nervous system through several mechanisms, including shared genetic risk, immune dysregulation, systemic inﬂammation, hypercoagulability, malabsorption, and the neurologic toxicity of gut metabolites in patients with hepatic dysfunction. This CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 75 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):75 – 104. Address correspondence to Dr Jessamyn Conell-Price, 400 Parnassus Ave, 8 th ﬂoor, San Francisco, CA 94143, JConell- price@ucsf.edu. article reviews the diagnosis and management of the neurologic manifestations of acute and chronic liver failure, hepatitis C virus infection, Wilson disease, malabsorption, bariatric surgery, inﬂammatory bowel disease, celiac disease, and Whipple disease. HEPATIC ENCEPHALOPATHY Hepatic encephalopathy is the most common neurologic manifestation of liver disease and can arise in patients with acute liver injury and patients with chronic liver failure. Hepatic encephalopathy is caused by the failure of normal hepatic metabolism and by portosystemic shunting of ammonia and other gut metabolites that are toxic to the nervous system. The clinical manifestations and treatment of hepatic encephalopathy differ depending on whether it arises acutely or chronically. In both contexts, there are prominent ﬂuctuations in cognition and level of arousal, with worsening triggered by comorbid infection, gastrointestinal bleeding, and systemic inﬂammatory response syndrome. Clinical and laboratory clues to chronic hepatic disease as the cause of encephalopathy include loss of appetite, jaundice, hypotension, transaminitis, and elevated international normalized ratio (INR). Patients with chronic hepatic disease may also develop sequelae of portal hypertension, such as ascites and spider angiomata. Asterixis is frequently present but is nonspeciﬁc because it can be seen in any cause of toxic-metabolic encephalopathy. In patients with acute hepatic failure, there is a risk of cerebral edema due to a lack of osmotic compensation. Acute Hepatic Encephalopathy Acute liver failure is deﬁned as liver dysfunction accompanied by both encephalopathy and coagulopathy (INR of 1.5 or greater) that develops within 26 weeks of liver disease onset. Causes of acute liver failure include viral infections, drugs, toxins, Wilson disease, autoimmune hepatitis, pregnancy- associated conditions (ie, acute fatty liver of pregnancy and HELLP [hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets] syndrome), ischemic hepatitis (aka “shock liver”), and hepatic venous outﬂow obstruction (ie, Budd-Chiari syndrome). In patients with acute liver failure, the rapid rise of ammonia and its metabolite glutamine can cause cerebral edema both directly due to osmotic forces and secondarily due to cytotoxic edema from necrosis of neurons and astrocytes. Intracranial pressure may be further elevated by cerebral vascular vasodilation. 1 Risks for cerebral edema include sepsis or systemic inﬂammatory response syndrome, higher-grade encephalopathy, serum ammonia greater than 100 mcg/dL (58 µmol/L), and renal failure. 2 CLINICAL FEATURES. Initial symptoms of acute hepatic encephalopathy include impaired judgment, hypersomnia, apathy, delirium, and mania. Symptoms subsequently progress to inattention, disorientation in time, and apraxia, usually accompanied by asterixis. Further progression can lead to worsening disorientation, somnolence, and coma. Examination of patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy may include ﬁndings of increased tone, hyperreﬂexia, multifocal myoclonus, exaggerated startle, alternating conjugate gaze deviation, and dysconjugate gaze (although if the latter is present, it warrants neuroimaging to evaluate for a focal lesion). Pupillary responses are normal; ﬁnding pupillary asymmetry or nonreactive pupils should prompt evaluation 76 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

for herniation from elevated intracranial pressure. Focal neurologic ﬁndings require neuroimaging to evaluate for a focal lesion such as intracranial hemorrhage or ischemic stroke. 2 Focal or generalized seizures occur in 6% to 25% of patients with acute liver failure, and up to one-third of patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy have subclinical seizures. 3 DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION. All patients with acute liver failure should have frequent cognitive assessments to evaluate for the development of hepatic encephalopathy, which should be graded according to the West Haven Criteria to determine appropriate management. 1 Patients should also undergo noncontrast head CT to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage and cerebral edema (FIGURE 4-1), but results should be interpreted with caution because CT ﬁndings of cerebral edema may lag behind clinical symptoms. 4 MRI is more sensitive than CT for cerebral edema, but patients are often too severely ill to tolerate MRI. 4 Head CT should be repeated if patients develop new focal neurologic deﬁcits or declining levels of consciousness with decreased responsiveness (West Haven Criteria grade 3) or coma (West Haven Criteria grade 4). Cerebral edema occurs in 25% to 35% of patients with West Haven Criteria grade 3 encephalopathy and in 75% of those with West Haven Criteria grade 4 encephalopathy. 2 EEG can be considered for evaluation of subclinical seizures in patients with ﬂuctuating or depressed level of consciousness that are not explained by the degree of liver dysfunction. TREATMENT. Management of acute hepatic encephalopathy involves treatment of the underlying cause of liver disease (including consideration of liver transplant), managing cerebral edema and elevated intracranial pressure, and treating symptomatic seizures (TABLE 4-1 5 ). FIGURE 4-1 Rapid development of cerebral edema in a patient with hepatic encephalopathy. Serial axial CT scans of a 32-year-old patient admitted with acute liver failure with no evidence of edema on noncontrasted head CT at admission (A) and repeat head CT 48 hours later showing loss of gray-white differentiation and effacement of sulci and cisterns (B). Reprinted with permission from Tujios S, et al, Semin Liver Dis. 1 © 2022 Thieme. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 77 KEY POINTS •Patients with acute liver failure should have frequent cognitive assessments to evaluate for the development of hepatic encephalopathy, which should be graded according to the West Haven Criteria to determine appropriate management. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Patients with acute liver failure should be managed in an intensive care unit at a center with the capability for liver transplantation. If patients present to a hospital without this capacity, they should be transferred as early as possible because transit is dangerous once patients develop severe coagulopathy or increased intracranial pressure. 2 Medical management involves continuous renal replacement therapy to lower ammonia levels, which should be initiated in patients with West Haven Criteria grades 2 through 4 hepatic encephalopathy even in the absence of acute renal injury. 2 Although there is no evidence to support the use of oral ammonia-lowering therapies such as lactulose and rifaximin in the treatment of acute liver failure, oral ammonia- lowering therapies are often administered to patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy based on evidence for their use in patients with chronic hepatic encephalopathy. 2 However, lactulose treatment can cause diarrhea and bowel distension that can lead to ﬂuid-electrolyte imbalances and increase the risk of requiring liver transplantation. 2 Continuous renal replacement therapy is a safer and more effective means of lowering ammonia in patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy. When treating elevated intracranial pressure in patients with hepatic encephalopathy, hypertonic saline is preferred over mannitol because of higher rates of renal failure and rebound increases in intracranial pressure when mannitol is used. 1 Induced hypothermia does not improve overall survival in patients with acute liver failure. 1 Invasive intracranial pressure monitoring can be considered, but whether it leads to a mortality beneﬁt is uncertain. However, intracranial pressure monitoring may have prognostic value because patients with severely elevated intracranial pressure are at high risk for herniation during transplant surgery and have a lower likelihood of beneﬁt from transplantation. 2 Noninvasive intracranial pressure monitoring with transcranial Doppler ultrasound ﬂow velocities, transcranial Doppler TABLE 4-1 Management According to Grade of Hepatic Encephalopathy (West Haven Criteria) a Grade of hepatic encephalopathy Symptom description Management in acute liver failure Grade 1 Trivial lack of awareness, shortened attention span, impairment of addition or subtraction, altered sleep rhythm Contact the transplant center and initiate transfer, obtain a baseline head CT Grade 2 Lethargy or apathy, disorientation for time, obvious personality change, inappropriate behavior, dyspraxia, asterixis Transfer to the intensive care unit, neurologic examination every hour Grade 3 Somnolence to semistupor, responsive to stimuli, confusion, gross disorientation, bizarre behavior Intubation if appropriate, repeat head CT, avoid opioids and benzodiazepines for sedation, consider propofol due to short half-life Grade 4 Coma Repeat head CT, consider an intracranial pressure monitor if the patient is a transplant candidate, initiate treatment for cerebral edema a Data from Vilstrup H, et al, Hepatology. 5 Reprinted with permission from Shingina A, et al, Gastroenterol. 2 © 2023 American College of Gastroenterology. 78 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

ultrasound pulsatility index, EEG, and optic nerve sheath diameter measurement by ultrasonography can be considered. However, optic nerve sheath diameter and Doppler ultrasound pulsatility may overestimate intracranial pressure in patients with acute liver failure because of confounding from cytotoxic edema and cerebrovascular vasodilation. 1,4 Prophylactic antiseizure medication is not indicated in patients with hepatic encephalopathy. 1 If an antiseizure medication is indicated because a patient with hepatic encephalopathy develops seizures, levetiracetam and lacosamide are the recommended agents because both have IV formulations and can be used in patients with liver failure. 6 Phenytoin, sodium valproate, and carbamazepine should be avoided because they are hepatically metabolized. Improved intensive care, early use of continuous renal replacement therapy, and maintenance of serum sodium at high-normal levels have all led to improved outcomes in patients with acute hepatic encephalopathy. 1 Chronic Hepatic Encephalopathy Causes of chronic liver failure include hepatitis B, hepatitis C, alcohol, metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease, Wilson disease, hemochromatosis, and autoimmune hepatitis. Chronic liver injury causes fatty changes that can progress to inﬂammation, leading to ﬁbrosis of the liver and structural changes known as cirrhosis. Decline in hepatic function due to cirrhosis can lead to portal hypertension, ascites, gastrointestinal bleeding, systemic coagulopathy, renal dysfunction, hypotension, and hyponatremia. The pathophysiology of encephalopathy in patients with chronic liver disease is thought to be due to portosystemic shunting, inﬂammation, hyperammonemia, and increased blood-brain barrier permeability. 7 Portosystemic shunting develops spontaneously when portal hypertension creates communications between the portal vein and the systemic circulation, allowing toxins and metabolites to bypass the liver. Portosystemic shunting can also be iatrogenic due to the placement of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt to treat refractory variceal bleeding. Hepatic encephalopathy in patients with chronic liver disease commonly ﬂuctuates, with exacerbations occurring due to triggers such as gastrointestinal bleeding, systemic infection, constipation, alcohol use, and hyponatremia. 2 CLINICAL FEATURES. Chronic hepatic encephalopathy can be classiﬁed as covert hepatic encephalopathy or overt hepatic encephalopathy. Patients with covert hepatic encephalopathy have a mild dysexecutive syndrome on detailed neuropsychological assessment but have normal bedside cognitive testing. Patients with overt hepatic encephalopathy have persistent or recurrent cognitive impairment. Covert hepatic encephalopathy occurs in up to 85% of patients with cirrhosis, is associated with lower quality of life and increased risk of driving accidents, and is a risk factor for the development of overt hepatic encephalopathy. 8 Overt hepatic encephalopathy occurs in 30% to 50% of patients with cirrhosis and is associated with progression of liver disease and increased mortality risk in the subsequent 1 to 3 years after the onset of cirrhosis. 8 Symptoms of covert hepatic encephalopathy can include apraxia, sleep- wake inversion, and personality changes ranging from joviality and disinhibition to apathy and psychomotor slowing. 8 Covert hepatic encephalopathy can be CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 79 KEY POINTS •Medical management of acute hepatic encephalopathy involves continuous renal replacement therapy to lower ammonia levels, which should be initiated in patients with West Haven Criteria grades 2 through 4 hepatic encephalopathy, even in the absence of acute renal injury. •Prophylactic antiseizure medication is not indicated in patients with hepatic encephalopathy. •Symptoms of covert hepatic encephalopathy can include apraxia, sleep- wake inversion, and personality changes ranging from joviality and disinhibition to apathy. •Covert hepatic encephalopathy can be diagnosed using the 25- minute Psychometric Hepatic Encephalopathy Score or the 60-second Animal Naming Test. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

diagnosed using the 25-minute Psychometric Hepatic Encephalopathy Score or the 60-second Animal Naming Test. In the Animal Naming Test, the patient is asked to name as many animals as possible in 60 seconds; listing fewer than 15 animals correlates with a diagnosis of covert hepatic encephalopathy on neuropsychological testing and predicts development of overt hepatic encephalopathy, which can lead to earlier initiation of ammonia-lowering therapies. 8,9 The Animal Naming Test has been validated as a tool to screen for covert hepatic encephalopathy in multiple countries and languages. 9 Overt hepatic encephalopathy is characterized by deﬁcits in attention, vigilance, and orientation that may be persistent or episodic. Asterixis typically occurs once patients develop West Haven Criteria grade 2 encephalopathy, but it is not universally present. 10 Although asterixis was initially described in the setting of hepatic encephalopathy, it is nonspeciﬁc and can be seen in patients with other toxic-metabolic causes of encephalopathy. 8 DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION. Serum ammonia level and brain imaging should be obtained after the ﬁrst episode of overt hepatic encephalopathy. Ammonia is typically elevated during exacerbations but can also be elevated in patients with chronic liver disease without associated cognitive symptoms. However, a normal serum ammonia level suggests an alternative cause of encephalopathy rather than hepatic encephalopathy. A good clinical response to ammonia- lowering therapy is supportive of a diagnosis of hepatic encephalopathy. CT or MRI should be obtained to evaluate for potential alternative causes of altered mental status, such as intracranial hemorrhage due to the coagulopathy that frequently accompanies chronic liver disease. In patients with hepatic encephalopathy, an EEG should be obtained if there is a concern for nonconvulsive seizures. Each West Haven Criteria grade of hepatic encephalopathy has characteristic EEG ﬁndings: grades 1 and 2 are associated with increased theta, grade 3 is associated with disorganization and generalized periodic discharges with triphasic morphology, and grade 4 is characterized by diffuse suppression (FIGURE 4-2 11 ). Of note, generalized periodic discharges with triphasic morphology are nonspeciﬁc and can be seen in many patients with metabolic, toxic, and structural causes of encephalopathy, as well as in patients with neurodegenerative conditions (CASE 4-1). 12 TREATMENT. Ammonia-lowering agents are used for the treatment of chronic hepatic encephalopathy and to reduce the risk of overt hepatic encephalopathy in patients with covert hepatic encephalopathy. Lactulose and lactitol are nonabsorbable disaccharides that work as laxatives with adverse effects of diarrhea, bloating, nausea, and dehydration. Serious adverse effects of lactulose are rare when patients are hemodynamically stable, but there is a small risk of hyponatremia and acute renal injury. Rifaximin is an antibiotic that modiﬁes the intestinal microbiome without increased risk of bacterial resistance or Clostridium difﬁcile infections and has minimal side effects. 8 Initiation of either lactulose or rifaximin is recommended for patients with covert hepatic encephalopathy to improve cognition and reduce the risk of overt hepatic encephalopathy. 8 In patients with exacerbations of overt hepatic encephalopathy, rapid lactulose initiation has been shown to reduce mortality, gastrointestinal bleeding, renal dysfunction, and peritoneal bacterial infection. 8 Once a patient has had an episode of overt hepatic encephalopathy, 80 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

lactulose should be continued to prevent recurrence; if overt hepatic encephalopathy reoccurs despite lactulose or lactulose is not tolerated, rifaximin should be initiated (TABLE 4-2). Patients with an acute exacerbation of overt hepatic encephalopathy should be screened for precipitating factors such as infection, acute kidney injury, constipation, gastrointestinal bleeding, dehydration, hyponatremia, and sedating medications (eg, opioids, benzodiazepines). Predisposing factors are identiﬁed in 50% of patients, and treatment of provoking triggers improves symptoms in 90% of patients. 8 Serum sodium should be maintained at a level greater than 130 mmol/L (often requiring diuretics), and sedating medications should be discontinued. 8,13 Proton pump inhibitors should be avoided if possible because chronic proton pump inhibitor use increases the risk for hepatic encephalopathy, likely due to suppression of gastric acid leading to bacterial overgrowth. 13 Approximately 71% of patients with refractory hepatic encephalopathy have persistent portosystemic shunting, which may be effectively treated with endovascular techniques such as balloon-occlusion retrograde transvenous obliteration. 14 In patients with refractory hepatic encephalopathy despite medical management, liver transplantation may be considered. ACQUIRED HEPATOCELLULAR DEGENERATION Acquired hepatocellular degeneration is a progressive, nonﬂuctuating syndrome affecting a small subset of patients with chronic liver disease that is characterized by neuropsychiatric symptoms accompanied by movement disorders, including tremor, dystonia, oral dyskinesias, parkinsonism, and cerebellar dysfunction. 15 Compared with cognitive ﬂuctuations in patients with chronic hepatic encephalopathy, cognitive symptoms of acquired hepatocellular degeneration are typically progressive over months and do not improve with ammonia-lowering therapies. Approximately 1% to 5% of people FIGURE 4-2 EEG in a patient with hepatic encephalopathy. A normal EEG (A) and EEG ﬁndings in each West Haven Criteria grade of hepatic encephalopathy,with increased theta in grade 1 (B) and grade 2 (C) encephalopathy, triphasic waves and disorganization in grade 3 encephalopathy (D), and diffuse suppression in grade 4 encephalopathy (E). Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CASE 4-1 A 55-year-old man with a history of severe traumatic brain injury and decompensated cirrhosis due to alcohol use disorder was referred to the neurology clinic by the liver transplant team. The clinical question was whether his cognitive syndrome was from hepatic encephalopathy or due to an irreversible cause such as his prior traumatic brain injury or neurodegenerative disease. He and his care partner reported 1 year of intermittent symptoms of poor nocturnal sleep, daytime sleepiness, changes in behavior, jerking movements, and periods of confusion lasting up to one day. The patient noted memory loss and disorganization that were most severe after his traumatic brain injury 6 years prior. These symptoms had improved initially but not completely, leaving him with persistent, stable mild cognitive impairment until the preceding year, when confusion developed. He had been abstinent from alcohol for 6 years. On examination, he was alert and attentive with a digit span of 6. His Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) score was 23 with deﬁcits in visuospatial-executive function, naming, and 3/5 delayed recall. His Animal Naming Test score was 9 (normal is greater than 15). His cranial nerve, coordination, and motor examinations were normal. There was no asterixis present. He had mild lower extremity edema and ascites. Laboratory testing revealed a serum ammonia level of 105 mcg/dL (normal: 15 mcg/dL to 45 mcg/dL), thrombocytopenia, mild hyponatremia, elevated serum transaminases, and normal international normalized ratio (INR). His brain MRI was notable for cerebellar atrophy consistent with his history of alcohol use and mild bifrontal encephalomalacia from his prior traumatic brain injury. He was diagnosed with a chronic cognitive syndrome with amnestic and behavioral dysfunction, as well as overt hepatic encephalopathy (West Haven grade 1) with unprovoked ﬂares. His transplant team was advised that his most disabling cognitive symptoms were most consistent with hepatic encephalopathy and were potentially treatable with ammonia-lowering therapy, avoidance of triggers, and consideration of a liver transplant if these measures were ineffective. His care team started rifaximin and used shared decision-making to create a plan to use low-dose lactulose. His episodes of hepatic encephalopathy decreased, and his cognitive syndrome improved to near his recent baseline. COMMENT This is a characteristic presentation of overt hepatic encephalopathy with prominent ﬂuctuations in mental status associated with reports of jerking during episodes suggestive of asterixis, sleep-wake inversion, and inattention. The patient had a static cognitive syndrome with amnestic and behavioral features attributable to his prior alcohol use disorder and severe traumatic brain injury. Patients with hepatic encephalopathy often have comorbid causes of cognitive impairment as in this case. A careful history and close attention to the pattern of cognitive deﬁcits on examination are essential when patients present with multiple potential etiologies of cognitive changes in addition to hepatic encephalopathy. 82 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

with chronic liver disease develop acquired hepatocellular degeneration, 16 which is thought to be due to manganese deposition in the basal ganglia caused by portosystemic shunting of manganese. 17,18 The risk of developing acquired hepatocellular degeneration does not correlate with the duration of liver disease or the cause of cirrhosis. 15 Acquired hepatocellular degeneration is diagnosed based on the clinical syndrome accompanied by typical brain MRI ﬁndings demonstrating symmetric T1 hyperintensities in the globus pallidus (FIGURE 4-3). The subcortical white matter, putamen, caudate, midbrain, and cerebellum may also be involved. 17 T1 hyperintensities in the globus pallidus can also be seen in the absence of clinical manifestations of acquired hepatocellular degeneration. 17 Serum manganese levels can be elevated in patients with acquired hepatocellular degeneration, but they are not associated with clinical symptoms of acquired hepatocellular degeneration and are not required for diagnosis. 15 Ammonia-lowering agents do not improve symptoms of acquired hepatocellular degeneration. Extrapyramidal symptoms generally do not respond to dopaminergic therapy, although levodopa or bromocriptine can be considered based on small trials demonstrating improvement with these agents. 16,19 The most effective reported treatment for acquired hepatocellular degeneration is liver transplantation, which can lead to complete resolution of symptoms and MRI changes, but it may fail to TABLE 4-2 Management of Hepatic Encephalopathy Population Cognitive syndrome Prevalence (if known) Testing Treatment All patients with cirrhosis Variable Not known Screen for covert hepatic encephalopathy with Animal Naming Test Maintain sodium level greater than 130 mEq/L, minimize use of proton pump inhibitors, opioids, and benzodiazepines Covert hepatic encephalopathy Dysexecutive syndrome, joviality, apraxia, apathy, disinhibition, normal bedside cognitive testing 85%ofpatients with cirrhosis Animal Naming Test or Psychometric Hepatic Encephalopathy Score Primary prophylaxis with lactulose,smallmealsduring the day Overt hepatic encephalopathy Recurrent or persistent symptoms of global confusion, decreased attention, vigilance, disorientation, sleep-wake inversion, asterixis 30% to 50% of patients with cirrhosis Evaluate for precipitating factors: infection, acute kidney injury, constipation, gastrointestinal bleeding, dehydration, hyponatremia, alcohol, sedating medications; obtain head imaging, ammonia Immediately start lactulose; after the ﬁrst acute ﬂare, use lactulose, rifaximin, or both to prevent recurrence Refractory or persistent hepatic encephalopathy Ongoing cognitive symptoms Not known Expand workup for alternate causes of cognitive impairment Add rifaximin to lactulose, consider portosystemic shunt closure, consider liver transplant CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 83 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

improve symptoms in some patients. 15,17,18 Persistent portosystemic shunting despite liver transplantation has been identiﬁed as a cause of refractory symptoms. 15,18 HEPATIC MYELOPATHY Hepatic myelopathy causes a progressive spastic paraparesis without sensory or sphincter dysfunction; quadriparesis occurs rarely. 18,20 Hepatic myelopathy is rare, occurring in approximately 2% of patients with chronic liver disease. 16 The pathophysiology is thought to be toxic injury to the bilateral corticospinal tracts in the setting of portosystemic shunting. 21 Spine imaging is typically normal in patients with hepatic myelopathy, but there are case reports of brain MRI demonstrating bilateral T2 hyperintensity in the corticospinal tracts. 19,22 There is no speciﬁc treatment for hepatic myelopathy, but there are reports of improvement after splenic artery embolization, reversal of a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt, hepatitis C treatment, and liver transplantation, with recovery more likely if treatment is initiated soon after the onset of weakness. 18,20 WILSON DISEASE Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by a variant in the ATP7B gene. This variant prevents biliary copper excretion, leading to accumulation of copper in the liver and brain that can result in progressive liver failure and severe neurologic disease characterized by neuropsychiatric symptoms and movement disorders. Neurologic manifestations may be the presenting feature, and approximately one-half of patients have no hepatic disease at the time of onset of neurologic symptoms. 23 The prevalence of Wilson disease is approximately 1 in 30,000 people. Onset is typically before the age of 50 years, with a mean age of diagnosis of 17; most patients are diagnosed by the age of 35 years. 23 Wilson disease is treatable with chelation therapy and should therefore be considered in the differential diagnosis of any movement disorder in younger patients. Clinical Presentation The neurologic symptoms of Wilson disease include cognitive decline, psychiatric symptoms, movement disorders (including dystonia, tremor, chorea, parkinsonism, and ataxia), gait impairment, dysarthria, dysphagia, and seizures. 13,24,25 Neurologic symptoms are key to the diagnosis of Wilson FIGURE 4-3 Axial T1-weighted MRI demonstrating symmetric hyperintensity of the globus pallidus in a patient with acquired hepatocellular degeneration. Reprinted with permission from Malaquias MJ, et al, Eur J Neurol. 15 © 2020 European Academy of Neurology. 84 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

disease because they may be the initial manifestation of the disease and because the hepatic features are nonspeciﬁc (eg, elevated serum transaminases, fatty liver disease, cirrhosis, and acute liver failure). 23 Renal disease, hemolytic anemia, and endocrine dysfunction, including infertility, can occur. Corneal deposition of copper causes characteristic Kayser-Fleischer rings, which can be seen as a greenish-gold ring at the border of the cornea and the sclera. Kayser- Fleischer rings can be seen on direct visualization in patients with light-colored eyes or advanced disease but may require slit-lamp examination to detect. With slit-lamp examination, Kayser-Fleischer rings are apparent in 90% to 99% of patients with Wilson disease affecting the nervous system (FIGURE 4-4 26 ). Dysarthria is present in up to 91% of patients with Wilson disease and may have spastic, cerebellar, dystonic, or parkinsonian features. 27 Dystonia in patients with Wilson disease can present with any pattern from focal to generalized, although isolated cervical dystonia is unlikely to be due to Wilson disease. 23,24 The most common tremor in patients with Wilson disease is a low- amplitude, moderate-frequency postural and action tremor of the upper extremities similar to essential tremor. 27 Patients with advanced Wilson disease may develop a characteristic “wing-beating tremor” involving the proximal upper extremities that increases in amplitude as the arms are maintained in an abducted, ﬂexed posture; this phenomenon was frequently described in early case reports, but is relatively rare at initial diagnosis in more recent series. 27 Cognitive features are related to frontostriatal dysfunction, resulting in impaired judgment, inattention, slow processing speed, dyscalculia, executive dysfunction, and emotional lability. Cognitive symptoms can be the earliest neurologic feature of Wilson disease but are sometimes only identiﬁed in retrospect as decreased performance at school or work preceding the onset of other symptoms of the disease. 24,25 Psychiatric symptoms include irritability, personality change, hypomania, and depression. 24 Diagnostic Evaluation Wilson disease is diagnosed by low serum ceruloplasmin and high urinary copper in the appropriate clinical context. Serum ceruloplasmin is normal in 15% of patients, so 24-hour urine copper collection, slit-lamp examination for Kayser-Fleischer rings, and liver function tests to screen for subclinical liver involvement should also be part of the initial evaluation (TABLE 4-3). 24 Brain MRI should be obtained to evaluate for other potential causes of neurologic symptoms and to look for characteristic ﬁndings of Wilson disease (FIGURE 4-5 28 ). Brain MRI in patients with Wilson disease demonstrates symmetric T2 hyperintensities in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, and midbrain. A series of 56 patients with Wilson disease noted T2 hyperintensity of the tectal plate in 75% of patients and central pontine T2 hyperintensities (similar to central pontine myelinolysis) in 65% of patients; neither imaging pattern was observed among patients with other movement disorders. 28 The classic face of the giant panda sign caused by T2 hyperintensity of the midbrain tegmentum sparing the red nuclei is only seen in 14% to 27% of patients, and the split thalamus sign caused by T2 hyperintensity of the internal medullary lamina of the thalamus is seen in only 12% of patients. 15,29 These ﬁndings have also been described in patients with other metabolic and neurodegenerative CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 85 KEY POINTS •Patients with hepatic encephalopathy often have comorbid causes of cognitive impairment. •A careful history and close attention to the pattern of cognitive deﬁcits are essential when patients present with multiple potential etiologies of cognitive changes in addition to hepatic encephalopathy. •Initiation of either lactulose or rifaximin is recommended for patients with covert hepatic encephalopathy to improve cognition and reduce the risk of overt hepatic encephalopathy. •Patients with an acute exacerbation of overt hepatic encephalopathy should be screened for precipitating factors such as infection, acute kidney injury, constipation, gastrointestinal bleeding, dehydration, hyponatremia, and sedating medications. •Predisposing factors are identiﬁed in 50% of patients with acute exacerbation of overt hepatic encephalopathy, and treatment of triggers improves symptoms in 90% of patients. •Proton pump inhibitors should be minimized as much as possible in patients with a history of acute exacerbation of hepatic encephalopathy because chronic proton pump inhibitor use increases the risk for hepatic encephalopathy. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

TABLE 4-3 Diagnostic Testing in Patients With Wilson Disease a Test Finding suggestive of Wilson disease Sensitivity in patients with Wilson disease Other conditions in which the ﬁnding may be present Ocular examination Corneal copper deposition indicating Kayser-Fleischer rings seen on direct visualization, by slit- lamp examination, or by anterior segment optical tomography 90% Chronic liver disease 24-hour urine copper b Greater than 100 mcg/24 h is diagnostic in patients who are symptomatic, 40-100 mcg/24 h should prompt further testing, less than 40 mcg/24 h is normal 88% for a threshold greater than 100 mcg/24 h, incomplete collection increases the risk of false negatives Chronic liver disease Serum ceruloplasmin Less than 5 mg/dL is highly predictive of Wilson disease, less than 14 mg/dL is likely, less than 14-20 mg/dL is suggestive and requires further testing 85% Severe chronic liver disease, copper deﬁciency (gastric surgery, excess zinc), other copper metabolism disorders Brain MRI without contrast Symmetric and tract-speciﬁc T2 hyperintensity in the bilateral basal ganglia, thalamus, and midbrain (sometimes sparing the red nuclei), face of the giant panda sign or split thalamus sign 90% Nonspeciﬁc ﬁndings that can be seen in patients with other metabolic and infectious causes, including osmotic demyelination, leukoencephalopathy, viral encephalitis a Data from Schilsky ML, et al, Hepatol 13 and Shribman S, et al, J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 24 b Serum copper assays are affected by ceruloplasmin and are not considered helpful because levels can be low, normal, or high in patients with Wilson disease. Random urine copper tests are unreliable and should not be used. FIGURE 4-4 Kayser-Fleischer rings in patients with Wilson disease. With direct visualization, Kayser- Fleischer rings appear as uneven greenish-gold rims at the outer edge of the cornea. The thickness increases with advanced disease; the arrows indicate the thickness of a golden Kayser-Fleischer ring in a brown-eyed patient with advanced disease. Reprinted with permission from Tharageshwari S, et al, Int J Innov Res Sci Eng Technol. 26 © 2014 IJIRSET. 86 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

diseases, but are more common in patients with Wilson disease than in patients with other early-onset movement disorders. If a patient has a suggestive clinical phenotype but serum and urine studies are not deﬁnitive, ATP7B sequencing should be performed. 13 All ﬁrst-degree relatives of patients with Wilson disease should be screened either with genetic testing or by comprehensive evaluation with liver function tests, serum ceruloplasmin, 24-hour urine copper testing, and slit lamp examination (TABLE 4-3). 13 In patients with hepatic manifestations of Wilson disease without neurologic symptoms, brain MRI should be performed to evaluate for T2 hyperintensities consistent with occult neurologic involvement from Wilson disease. Treatment Treatment of Wilson disease is with copper chelating agents and dietary copper restriction by avoiding high-copper foods, particularly shellﬁsh and liver. 30 Recommended chelation regimens vary, and approaches include zinc monotherapy, chelation with D-penicillamine or trientine followed by zinc maintenance therapy, and combination therapy with both chelators and zinc. If patients are treated before the onset of cirrhosis and remain on treatment, life expectancy is normal. 13 Without treatment, patients with Wilson disease are at risk of liver failure. Improvement of liver disease usually occurs within 2 to 6 months of treatment for Wilson disease, whereas neurologic symptoms may take 1 to 3 years to improve. 13 Paradoxical neurologic worsening occurs in 14% to 23% of patients within the ﬁrst 6 months of treatment, and this worsening is irreversible in approximately one-half of patients. 31 The mechanism of neurologic worsening is poorly understood, but may be due to rapid increases in free copper in the blood and CSF. 31 There is no consensus on management of neurologic worsening with Wilson disease treatment; treatment strategies include decreasing or increasing the dose of the agent being used, switching to another agent, adding dimercaprol, and consideration of liver transplantation. 31 In FIGURE 4-5 MRI patterns in patients with neurologic Wilson disease. A, Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) sequence demonstrating T2 hyperintensities in the bilateral caudate, putamen, and thalamus. B, Axial T2 sequence showing the face of the giant panda sign with hyperintensity of the midbrain sparing the red nuclei. C, Axial FLAIR sequence showing T2 hyperintensity of the tectum. D, Axial T2 sequence demonstrating hyperintensities in the bilateral pons. Reprinted with permission from Prashanth LK, et al. Move Disord. 28 © 2010 Movement Disorder Society. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 87 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

patients with neurologic involvement of Wilson disease, treatment should begin at a low dose and should be uptitrated slowly to minimize side effects and reduce the risk of neurologic worsening. Wilson disease is often diagnosed in teenagers and young adults, who may have challenges with adherence to lifelong treatment, which is compounded by cognitive symptoms of impulsivity and impaired judgment due to the disease itself. These patients should therefore have ongoing education and support because rebound symptoms from disruptions in treatment can result in irreversible neurologic decline. 13 HEPATITIS C Hepatitis C virus is an RNA virus that causes chronic liver infection. Hepatitis C virus infection may be asymptomatic, but it puts patients at risk for cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. More than 50% of patients with chronic hepatitis C virus infection develop neurologic complications, including peripheral neuropathy, mixed cryoglobulinemia–associated vasculitis (which can lead to vasculitic neuropathy and stroke), inﬂammatory central nervous system (CNS) syndromes (eg, myelitis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis), and cognitive dysfunction. The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommends universal hepatitis C virus screening because initiation of direct-acting oral antiviral drug therapy eradicates the infection and leads to improvement in liver disease and associated neurologic syndromes. 32 Peripheral Neuropathy Peripheral neuropathy occurs in 10% to 75% of patients with hepatitis C virus infection. 33 Mechanisms include direct viral toxicity, virus-triggered immune- mediated vasculitis, and cryoglobulin-associated vasculitis. 33 The most common type of neuropathy in patients with hepatitis C virus infection is a painful small ﬁber neuropathy, which may present asymmetrically but generally evolves to symmetric length-dependent polyneuropathy. The prevalence of neuropathy in patients with hepatitis C virus infection increases with disease duration and cryoglobulin positivity, which is present in approximately one-quarter of patients with hepatitis C virus infection. 34 Cryoglobulins are abnormal proteins made of immunoglobulins and complement, which precipitate in cold conditions; they can be asymptomatic or cause a cold-induced vasculitic syndrome called mixed cryoglobulinemia. Patients with hepatitis C virus infection–associated mixed cryoglobulinemia have a cold- induced syndrome of palpable purpura, fatigue, and arthralgias (FIGURE 4-6 35 ). Approximately 2% to 3% of patients with hepatitis C virus infection have mixed cryoglobulinemia. 34 Patients with hepatitis C virus infection– associated mixed cryoglobulinemia are at higher risk of developing a severe FIGURE 4-6 Patient with hepatitis C virus infection–associated cryoglobulinemia with palpable purpura. Reprinted with permission from Charles ED, et al, Kidney Int. 35 © 2009 International Society of Nephrology. 88 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

vasculitic neuropathy, which often presents as a severe progressive sensorimotor axonal neuropathy, but can also cause an acute demyelinating polyneuropathy or mononeuritis multiplex. 34 Use of direct-acting oral antiviral drug therapy leads to improvement of neuropathy in 40% of patients with hepatitis C virus infection–associated mixed cryoglobulinemia. 36 For severe hepatitis C virus infection–associated mixed cryoglobulinemic neuropathy, rituximab may be considered in addition to antiviral treatment. 36,37 Hepatitis C Virus Infection–Associated Stroke Chronic hepatitis C virus infection causes endothelial damage, increasing the risk of atherosclerosis and leading patients with hepatitis C virus infection to have a 25% higher risk of ischemic stroke than the general population. 36 Stroke in patients with hepatitis C virus infection can also be related to mixed cryoglobulinemia–associated vasculitis, antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody– associated vasculitis, and antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. 38 If a patient with hepatitis C virus infection has an ischemic stroke, they should be evaluated for cryoglobulins, antiphospholipid antibodies, and antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody positivity. Antiviral treatment for hepatitis C virus infection has been shown to decrease the risk of vascular events. 36 Because one-third of patients with new diagnoses of hepatitis C virus infection are not from traditional at-risk populations, guidelines recommend universal screening for the condition. 32 Therefore, clinicians should consider testing for hepatitis C virus infection in patients with ischemic stroke who have not been screened for hepatitis C virus. Central Nervous System Inﬂammatory Syndromes There are case reports of hepatitis C virus infection–associated CNS inﬂammatory syndromes, including myelitis, acute disseminated encephalomyelitis, and rapidly progressive leukoencephalitis. 33 These syndromes are rare despite the high prevalence of hepatitis C virus infection. Causality from hepatitis C virus infection is supported by the presence of hepatitis C virus and associated inﬂammation in nervous system tissue at autopsy of patients with fulminant CNS inﬂammatory syndromes. 33 The potential pathophysiologic mechanisms include direct viral infection and associated immune-mediated sequelae. Support for hepatitis C virus infection as the primary cause also comes from reports of radiographic and clinical improvement with antiviral treatment. 39 Cognitive Impairment Chronic hepatitis C virus infection is associated with cognitive impairment even in the absence of signiﬁcant liver disease. Anxiety, depression, fatigue, and deﬁcits in executive function, attention, working memory, and verbal learning have been described. 40 Hepatitis C virus antiviral treatment signiﬁcantly decreases fatigue, depression, and anxiety, and some studies report improvement in cognition. 38 Assessment of hepatitis C virus infection–related cognitive impairment is challenging because patients may have comorbid conditions that can affect cognition, such as hepatic encephalopathy, alcohol and polysubstance use, human immunodeﬁciency virus (HIV) infection, and psychiatric disease. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 89 KEY POINTS •Approximately 71% of patients with refractory hepatic encephalopathy have persistent portosystemic shunting. •Acquired hepatocellular degeneration is a progressive, nonﬂuctuating syndrome affecting a small subset of patients with chronic liver disease that is characterized by neuropsychiatric symptoms accompanied by movement disorders. •Acquired hepatocellular degeneration is diagnosed based on the clinical syndrome accompanied by typical brain MRI ﬁndings demonstrating symmetric T1 hyperintensities in the globus pallidus. •Hepatic myelopathy causes a progressive spastic paraparesis without sensory or sphincter dysfunction; quadriparesis occurs rarely. •Hepatic myelopathy is rare, occurring in approximately 2% of patients with chronic liver disease. Spine imaging is typically normal in patients with hepatic myelopathy. •Neurologic symptoms are key to the diagnosis of Wilson disease because they may be the initial manifestation of the disease and because the hepatic features are nonspeciﬁc. •Wilson disease is diagnosed by low serum ceruloplasmin and high urinary copper in the appropriate clinical context.
