NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF MALABSORPTION Malabsorption can be caused by gastrointestinal disease or defects in micronutrient transport. Common gastrointestinal causes of malabsorption include bariatric surgery, chemotherapy-induced mucositis, intractable nausea and vomiting, and gut microbiome changes such as small intestinal bacterial overgrowth. Impaired gastric function can reduce intrinsic factor production, leading to vitamin B 12 deﬁciency. Duodenal disease leads to reduced absorption of calcium, iron, and vitamin B 1 (thiamine). 41 Terminal ileum pathology causes decreased absorption of vitamin B 12 and the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K. 41 Malabsorption-associated vitamin and nutrient deﬁciencies can cause diverse neurologic manifestations that usually improve with supplementation. Therefore, neurologists should be familiar with the clinical syndromes associated with vitamin and nutrient deﬁciencies so these can be diagnosed and promptly treated (TABLE 4-4). In the diagnostic evaluation of patients with malabsorption due to gastrointestinal disease, there are often multiple simultaneous nutritional deﬁciencies. Neurologic manifestations of nutritional deﬁciencies related to bariatric surgery are discussed in the following section, TABLE 4-4 Neurologic Complications of Malabsorption-Related Nutritional Deﬁciencies a Syndrome Nutritional deﬁciency Symptoms and signs Wernicke encephalopathy Vitamin B 1 (thiamine) Nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, altered mental status, gait ataxia, vestibular dysfunction, urinary incontinence, hypothermia Korsakoff syndrome Thiamine Anterograde and retrograde amnesia, confabulation Pellagra Vitamin B 3 (niacin) Photosensitivity, altered mental status, hallucinations, rash on sun-exposed areas, diarrhea, tongue inﬂammation Optic neuropathy Vitamins A, B 6 , B 12 ; copper Blurred vision with central scotoma, night blindness Myelopathy Vitamins B 12 , E; copper Gait ataxia, lower extremity spasticity, paresthesia, impaired proprioception Subacute or chronic peripheral neuropathy b Thiamine, vitamins B 6 , B 12 , E; copper, folate Symmetric, stocking-glove small and large ﬁber neuropathy, sometimes painful paresthesia, gait ataxia Acute polyneuropathy and polyradiculoneuropathy Thiamine (dry beriberi) Pain in lower limbs, weakness, ascending paralysis, areﬂexia, mimics acute inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP) (but nerve conduction studies show an axonal neuropathy or polyradiculopathy) Myopathy Protein, copper, selenium, magnesium, calcium, vitamin D Myalgia, proximal weakness Symptomatic hypocalcemia Vitamin D, calcium Facial twitching, carpopedal spasms, ophthalmoplegia a Data from Goel R, et al, Springer 41 and Landais A, Obes Surg. 42 b Also consider causes other than nutritional deﬁciency, such as metabolic syndrome, diabetes, and rapid glycemic control (causing insulin neuritis or diabetic amyotrophy). 90 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

and those related to celiac disease and inﬂammatory bowel disease are discussed later in the article. NEUROLOGIC COMPLICATIONS OF BARIATRIC SURGERY Bariatric surgery can be an effective and safe way to manage obesity, leading to sustainable weight loss, improved lipid metabolism, and decreased diabetic complications. Bariatric surgery has therefore become the standard of care for patients with refractory obesity with a body mass index greater than 40 or for patients with a body mass index greater than 30 with complications related to obesity, such as type 2 diabetes, obstructive sleep apnea, and idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Bariatric surgeries include restrictive surgeries (laparoscopic gastric banding and sleeve gastrectomy) that decrease dietary intake and Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, which decreases dietary intake and may cause malabsorption by decreasing mixing of digestive enzymes. Neurologic complications of bariatric surgery include vitamin and nutrient deﬁciencies (particularly thiamine, vitamin B 12 , vitamin B 6 , vitamin D, vitamin E, and iron) and orthostatic hypotension (CASE 4-2). 41 Neurologic complications of vitamin and nutrient deﬁciencies are listed in TABLE 4-4. 42 Micronutrient deﬁciencies are present in 20% to 30% of patients with obesity before bariatric surgery. 43 Bariatric surgery results in vitamin deﬁciencies due to decreased dietary intake, decreased absorptive surface area, decreased gastric acid and intrinsic factor, and bacterial overgrowth. Rapid weight loss, recurrent emesis, and lack of vitamin supplementation all increase the risk of nutritional deﬁciencies after bariatric surgery. 42 Bariatric surgery leads to vitamin B 12 deﬁciency in 25% of patients, vitamin B 9 (folate) deﬁciency in 20% of patients, and symptomatic thiamine deﬁciency (ie, peripheral neuropathy and Wernicke encephalopathy) in 1% of patients. 41 Low serum iron, low vitamin D, and low calcium levels are also common. Deﬁciencies in vitamin E, copper, and zinc may occur but are less common. 43 After all bariatric surgeries, lifelong multivitamin supplementation is recommended, and nutrient levels should be monitored at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months after surgery and then annually thereafter. 42 One of the most common severe nutrient deﬁciencies is thiamine deﬁciency, which typically presents within the ﬁrst 6 months after surgery. Thiamine deﬁciency is associated with peripheral neuropathy, Wernicke encephalopathy, and rarely Korsakoff syndrome. 42 After bariatric surgery, 4% of patients develop orthostatic hypotension within 5 months to 2 years, with syncope or near syncope in one-half of the affected patients. 44 There are higher rates of orthostatic hypotension after Roux-en-Y procedures. The mechanism of orthostatic hypotension after bariatric surgery is unclear, although it does not appear to be related to vitamin deﬁciency or weight loss. Reduced sympathetic activity due to cardiac autonomic changes or rapid glycemic control has been theorized as a potential mechanism, although patients without rapid weight loss or rapid glycemic control are also at risk of developing severe orthostatic dysfunction after bariatric surgery. 44 Treatment is symptomatic. With increasing use of glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists for obesity, the number of gastric bypass surgeries is decreasing. Further experience and research are necessary to understand the neurologic risks and beneﬁts of these agents. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 91 KEY POINTS •Serum ceruloplasmin is normal in 15% of patients with Wilson disease, so 24- hour urine copper collection, slit-lamp examination for Kayser- Fleischer rings, and liver function tests to screen for subclinical liver involvement should also be part of the initial evaluation. •All ﬁrst-degree relatives of patients with Wilson disease should be screened either with genetic testing or by comprehensive evaluation with liver function tests, serum ceruloplasmin tests, 24-hour urine copper tests, and slit lamp examination. •Improvement of liver disease usually happens within 2 to 6 months of treatment for Wilson disease, whereas neurologic symptoms may take 1 to 3 years to improve. •Paradoxical neurologic worsening occurs in 14% to 23% of patients with Wilson disease within the ﬁrst 6 months of treatment. •The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention recommends universal hepatitis C virus screening. Initiation of direct-acting oral antiviral drug therapy eradicates the infection and leads to improvement in liver disease and associated neurologic syndromes. •The most common type of neuropathy in patients with hepatitis C virus infection is a painful small ﬁber neuropathy. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

CELIAC DISEASE AND GLUTEN-RELATED DISORDERS Celiac disease and gluten-related disorders are immune-mediated syndromes triggered by gluten exposure that cause intestinal damage, which may be accompanied by skin ﬁndings (ie, dermatitis herpetiformis) (FIGURE 4-7 45 ) and neurologic manifestations, including gluten ataxia and gluten neuropathy. There are also reports of impaired attention, cognitive slowing (“brain fog”), and headaches in patients with gluten sensitivity that improve with a gluten-free diet. 46,47 Celiac disease is a chronic, immune-mediated enteropathy of the small intestine characterized by malabsorption, diarrhea, weight loss, abdominal pain, and constipation, and it is associated with antigliadin and antitransglutaminase 6 antibodies. 48 The term gluten-related disorders refers to neurologic and dermatologic manifestations of gluten sensitivity associated CASE 4-2 A 33-year-old woman presented for evaluation of falls and pain in her extremities. She had a history of sleeve gastrectomy 2 years prior. Immediately after surgery, she had a 20-pound weight loss and took a daily multivitamin and additional vitamin B 12 supplementation. She developed nausea and vomiting, fatigue, paresthesia of her bilateral arms and legs, and gait disturbance 3 months after surgery. Over the subsequent 2 years, she developed increasingly frequent falls and worsening burning pain in her extremities. Her examination was notable for inability to rise from a chair unassisted, decreased vibration sensation in her lower extremities up to the knees, allodynia with light touch of her arms and legs, trace biceps reﬂexes, and otherwise absent reﬂexes in the upper and lower extremities, and a wide-based and waddling gait. Nerve conduction studies and EMG showed an axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy. Complete blood cell count, hemoglobin A 1C , vitamin B 12 level, metabolic proﬁle, human immunodeﬁciency virus (HIV), copper, and serum protein electrophoresis and immunoﬁxation were normal. Evaluation was notable for low serum vitamin B 1 (thiamine) (21 nmol/L; normal: 66 nmol/L to 200 nmol/L), vitamin B 6 (8 nmol/L; normal: 35 nmol/L to 100 nmol/L), and vitamin D (15 nmol/L; normal: 20 nmol/L to 50 nmol/L). She was diagnosed with multifactorial peripheral neuropathy due to thiamine and vitamin B 6 deﬁciencies and proximal myopathy due to vitamin D deﬁciency. Thiamine, vitamin D, and vitamin B complex supplementation were initiated, and the patient’s gait and sensory symptoms improved completely within 1 year. COMMENT Patients can develop neurologic syndromes related to nutritional deﬁciencies after bariatric surgery. The presence of nausea and vomiting should raise concern for thiamine deﬁciency, which can cause polyneuropathy, Wernicke encephalopathy, or both. The presence of proximal weakness in a patient at risk of nutritional deﬁciency should raise concern for vitamin D deﬁciency. Patients with vitamin or mineral deﬁciency often have several deﬁciencies, as in this case. 92 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

with antigliadin and antitransglutaminase 6 antibodies but may occur in the absence of intestinal disease. Celiac disease and gluten-related disorders occur in genetically susceptible individuals. Nearly all individuals with celiac and gluten disorders have human leukocyte antigen (HLA)-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 alleles, and there are higher rates of gluten sensitivity among people with type 1 diabetes and other autoimmune disorders, as well as Down syndrome and other developmental disorders. 48 Gluten Ataxia Ataxia is the most common neurologic manifestation of gluten sensitivity, with a prevalence of approximately 6% in patients with celiac disease. 49 Gluten ataxia is typically an insidiously progressive cerebellar ataxia with prominent gait and lower limb ataxia, commonly accompanied by dysarthria, optic ataxia, and gaze-evoked nystagmus. Less commonly, the presentation may be subacute. In one series of patients with gluten ataxia, 57% had no gastrointestinal symptoms at the time of ataxia evaluation but had associated celiac antibodies and beneﬁted from a gluten-free diet. 50 Up to 20% of cases of sporadic ataxia may be caused by gluten-related disorders. 49 Gluten ataxia is caused by immune-mediated damage to the cerebellum and is characterized pathologically by deposition of antitransglutaminase antibodies on blood vessels in the cerebellum and brainstem, lymphocytic inﬁltration, and irreversible damage to Purkinje cells. 51 On MRI, gluten ataxia and other immune-mediated syndromes are sometimes associated with atrophy of the cerebellar vermis, in contrast with global cerebellar atrophy typically seen in genetic and degenerative causes of ataxia. Even in patients without signiﬁcant atrophy on MRI, MR spectroscopy may show lower N-acetylaspartate (NAA)-to-creatine ratios in the cerebellar vermis; this ﬁnding may improve 1 year after initiation of a gluten-free diet. 52 Gluten Neuropathy Approximately 2% to 8% of patients with celiac disease have neuropathy, compared with 1% in the general population. 49 The most common types of neuropathy associated with celiac disease are length-dependent sensorimotor axonal neuropathy, small ﬁber neuropathy, and sensory ganglionopathy; rarely, mononeuritis multiplex and motor neuropathy can occur. Gluten neuropathy is typically slowly progressive. Symptoms range from mild to severe, and severity is not associated with the length of time since the diagnosis of gluten sensitivity. 46,49 Nerve biopsy in patients with gluten neuropathy has demonstrated axonopathy and perivascular lymphocytic inﬁltration around epineural vessels. 51,53 Diagnosis Serum antigliadin antibodies (IgA and IgG) are associated with both intestinal and neurologic gluten-associated disorders and should be used to screen for gluten sensitivity when there is clinical suspicion for gluten-related disorders based on neurologic symptoms, such as ataxia, unexplained peripheral neuropathy, or progressive executive dysfunction. Antigliadin IgA and IgG each have a sensitivity of approximately 85% to detect intestinal involvement in patients with celiac disease. 54 If antigliadin antibodies are not present and CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 93 KEY POINTS •Patients with hepatitis C virus infection–associated mixed cryoglobulinemia have a cold-induced syndrome of palpable purpura, fatigue, and arthralgias. Patients with hepatitis C virus infection– associated mixed cryoglobulinemia are at higher risk of developing a severe vasculitic neuropathy. •Chronic hepatitis C virus infection causes endothelial damage, increasing the risk of atherosclerosis and leading patients with hepatitis C virus infection to have a 25% higher risk of ischemic stroke than the general population. •After all bariatric surgeries, lifelong multivitamin supplementation is recommended. •Patients who have had bariatric surgery should have monitoring of nutrient levels at 6 weeks, 3 months, 6 months, and 12 months after surgery and then annually thereafter. •One of the most common severe nutrient deﬁciencies in patients who have had bariatric surgery is B 1 (thiamine) deﬁciency, which typically presents within the ﬁrst 6 months after surgery. •Gluten ataxia is typically an insidiously progressive cerebellar ataxia with prominent gait and lower limb ataxia. •Up to 20% of cases of sporadic ataxia are caused by gluten-related disorders. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

suspicion for celiac disease is high, serum tissue transglutaminase testing should be performed, which has up to 98% sensitivity for celiac disease intestinal involvement. 54 Antibody testing normalizes with a gluten-free diet. Within 5 weeks of a gluten-free diet, low titers can become negative, and within 5 years of a gluten-free diet, 90% of patients have negative antibody testing. 55 Patients with neurologic symptoms in the context of celiac disease should also be evaluated for concurrent copper deﬁciency, zinc excess, and vitamin B 12 deﬁciency that may be contributing to symptoms. 53 Abnormal antibody results should lead to consultation with a gastrointestinal specialist to consider a small bowel biopsy to conﬁrm the diagnosis of celiac disease. Neuronal transglutaminase antibodies are the most sensitive antibodies for isolated neurologic manifestations of gluten-related disorders, but these are not readily available in clinical settings. 56 Treatment and Prognosis Treatment of celiac disease and its neurologic complications is a gluten-free diet (ie, avoidance of wheat, barley, rye, oats, and other related grains). 53 In case reports and two small case series, a gluten-free diet was partially effective for the treatment of gluten ataxia, with a reduction in symptoms and improvement in MRI spectroscopy in the ﬁrst year. 49 However, one series of children with gluten ataxia did not demonstrate improvement of symptoms. 49 Many other gluten-related neurologic manifestations, including myopathy, subtle cognitive symptoms, headaches, and migratory paresthesia, have been reported to improve with a gluten-free diet. 57 FIGURE 4-7 Clustered vesicular pruritic lesions on extensor surfaces of the elbows (A) and knees (B) in a patient with dermatitis herpetiformis. This rash is always associated with celiac or other gluten-related disease. These images show secondary lesions due to excoriation and postinﬂammatory hypopigmentation. INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE Neurologic complications of inﬂammatory bowel disease are due to immune dysregulation, increased risk of arterial and venous thromboembolism, nutritional deﬁciencies, and side effects of treatment (TABLE 4-5 58 ). Inﬂammatory bowel disease refers to ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease, both chronic inﬂammatory conditions of the gastrointestinal tract. Ulcerative colitis is limited to the colon and rectum, whereas Crohn disease can affect the entire digestive tract from the mouth to the anus. Inﬂammatory bowel disease occurs in 0.3% of the population 59 and typically starts in young adulthood with a relapsing and remitting course characterized by ﬂares of disease activity. During TABLE 4-5 Neurologic Toxicities of Inﬂammatory Bowel Disease Medicationsa Medication class for inﬂammatory bowel disease Uses in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease Neurologic risks Neurologic disease-speciﬁc guidance, monitoring, treatment Prednisone Induce remission Increases the risk of venous and arterial thromboembolism, risk of metabolic syndrome Use standard monitoring Metronidazole Induce or maintain remission in patients with Crohn disease Axonal sensory polyneuropathy, particularly at a dose greater than 1.5 g a day When peripheral neuropathy occurs, sensory neuropathy usually reverses after stopping metronidazole Thalidomide Treat refractory disease or induce remission 20% risk of peripheral neuropathy Because of the high risk of polyneuropathy, monitor all patients with regular examination, consider discontinuation if polyneuropathy develops, neuropathy may be irreversible Cyclosporine Maintain remission in patients with Crohn disease Low risk of neurotoxicity Neuropathic symptoms are usually mild and reversible, low risk of continuing treatment if symptoms are mild Tumor necrosis factor-α inhibitor therapy (inﬂiximab, adalimumab, certolizumab) Treat moderate to severe Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis Low absolute risk but increased risk of central demyelinating disease (including multiple sclerosis and optic neuritis), acute inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP), chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP), and multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) Do not use if the patient has a personal or family history of multiple sclerosis, screen for neuropathy before treatment initiation, discontinue treatment if central nervous system or peripheral nervous system demyelinating disease develops, test for antiganglioside antibodies in patients with CIDP and MMN Anti-integrin therapy (natalizumab and vedolizumab) Treat moderate to severe Crohn disease, may be used if tumor necrosis factor-α inhibitor is discontinued Low risk Natalizumab and vedolizumab have minimal neurologic adverse effects, although natalizumab increases the risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy a Data from Hey GE, et al, Semin Neurol. 58 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 95 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

disease ﬂares, patients develop abdominal pain, worsening diarrhea, fever (in Crohn disease) and rectal bleeding (in ulcerative colitis), and elevated serum markers of inﬂammation (eg, C-reactive protein, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and calprotectin). Treatment of inﬂammatory bowel disease includes corticosteroids, sulfasalazine (for ulcerative colitis only), and immunomodulatory therapy for both ulcerative colitis and Crohn disease. Biologic agents such as tumor necrosis factor (TNF)-α inhibitors have decreased the rates of refractory disease and need for colectomy. However, these agents can cause peripheral and central nervous system demyelinating disease. Immune-Mediated Central Nervous System Manifestations of Inﬂammatory Bowel Disease Multiple sclerosis and inﬂammatory bowel disease commonly co-occur. Patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease have a 50% increased risk of multiple sclerosis, and patients with multiple sclerosis have a 50% increased risk of inﬂammatory bowel disease. 60 This interaction may be due to shared genetic risk, brain-gut-microbiota interactions, and increased systemic inﬂammation. There have been case reports of TNF-α inhibitor therapy–associated demyelinating disease, including myelitis, optic neuritis, multiple sclerosis, and acute disseminated encephalomyelitis-like syndrome 60 ; however, data are conﬂicting. In patients with optic neuritis, a review found that cases occur at similar rates with or without TNF-α therapy. 61 One retrospective analysis of 9095 patients on TNF-α inhibitor therapy for inﬂammatory bowel disease found 9 cases of demyelinating disease and no difference in risk for patients receiving this treatment compared with controls, 60 whereas another series found a two-fold risk of central demyelinating disease in patients on TNF-α inhibitor for inﬂammatory bowel disease with a low absolute risk of 7.5 events per 1000 person-years. 60 Based on these associations, TNF-α inhibitor therapy is not recommended in patients with comorbid multiple sclerosis and inﬂammatory bowel disease because these therapies can potentially induce or unmask central demyelinating disease. Natalizumab is effective for both multiple sclerosis and inﬂammatory bowel disease and can be considered for patients with both conditions. Venous Thromboembolism Patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease have a two- to fourfold higher risk of venous thromboembolism, with the highest risk during ﬂares. 62,63 Inﬂammatory bowel disease is a signiﬁcant risk factor for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, with an estimated annual incidence of 12 in 1000 people. 62 Prophylaxis for venous thromboembolism decreases rates of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, but there is a case report of a patient with inﬂammatory bowel disease developing cerebral venous sinus thrombosis even while receiving venous thromboembolism thromboprophylaxis. 64 The highest risk of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis is in patients with active inﬂammatory bowel disease. In one retrospective case series, 96% of cases of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis occurred during an inﬂammatory bowel disease ﬂare. 62,64 Male sex, ulcerative colitis, and anemia are associated with higher rates of cerebral venous sinus thrombosis in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease. 62 96 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Treatment for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis with low-molecular-weight heparin or direct oral anticoagulants is safe in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease ﬂares and does not increase the risk of major gastrointestinal bleeding. 64 Thromboprophylaxis for venous thromboembolism is recommended for patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease during any active inﬂammatory bowel disease ﬂare and during pregnancy due to increased risk of hypercoagulability. 63,64 When patients have active inﬂammatory bowel disease and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, oral anticoagulant therapy should be continued for at least 3 to 12 months, with consideration for ongoing anticoagulation until inﬂammatory bowel disease has been in remission for at least 3 months. 60 One-half of patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease and cerebral venous sinus thrombosis have an additional risk factor for venous thromboembolism, so patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease who develop cerebral venous sinus thrombosis should be evaluated for other risk factors, including tobacco use, oral contraceptive use, severe anemia, thrombocytosis, elevated homocysteine, lupus anticoagulant, and inherited thrombophilia. 60,64 Arterial Stroke Patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease have approximately a 1.3-fold increased risk of arterial ischemic stroke. 63 Systemic inﬂammation leads to accelerated atherosclerotic disease and higher rates of cardiovascular disease compared with age-matched controls. Periods of active disease (within 3 months of a disease ﬂare) are associated with higher rates of ischemic stroke. 63 There are increased rates of all mechanisms of ischemic stroke, including large artery disease, small vessel disease, and cardioembolic causes, including atrial ﬁbrillation and paradoxical embolism from deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or mesenteric ischemia in patients with patent foramen ovale. Control of inﬂammatory bowel disease, smoking cessation, and cardiovascular risk factor modiﬁcation reduce the risk of stroke. To prevent metabolic syndrome, prednisone should only be used for inﬂammatory bowel disease treatment when necessary. 63 For patients younger than 70 years with inﬂammatory bowel disease, primary prevention of stroke with aspirin can be considered when patients have concurrent uncontrolled cardiovascular risk factors. For patients older than 70 years with inﬂammatory bowel disease, primary stroke prevention with antiplatelet therapy is not recommended because of the increased risk of bleeding. 63 For secondary prevention after ischemic stroke, long-term low-dose aspirin has not been linked to higher rates of hospitalization or gastrointestinal bleeding. 63 Peripheral Neuropathy Patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease have increased rates of peripheral neuropathy compared with the general population, but the reported prevalence ranges from 1% to 67%, and true prevalence is uncertain. 65 Inﬂammatory bowel disease can be associated with an axonal polyneuropathy, and TNF-α inhibitor therapy has been associated with acute and chronic immune- mediated demyelinating neuropathy. 60 In one series of patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease and peripheral neuropathy, inﬂammatory bowel disease was the only identiﬁed risk factor in 80% of the patients. 65 Axonal CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 97 KEY POINTS •Serum antigliadin antibodies (IgA and IgG) should be used to screen for gluten sensitivity when there is clinical suspicion based on neurologic symptoms such as unexplained ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, or executive dysfunction. •Multiple sclerosis and inﬂammatory bowel disease commonly co- occur. Patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease have a 50% increased risk of multiple sclerosis, and patients with multiple sclerosis have a 50% increased risk of inﬂammatory bowel disease. •One case series found a two-fold risk of central demyelinating disease in patients on TNF-α inhibitors for inﬂammatory bowel disease, with a low absolute risk of 7.5 events per 1000 person-years. •Inﬂammatory bowel disease is a signiﬁcant risk factor for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, with an estimated annual incidence of 12 in 1000. •Thromboprophylaxis for venous thromboembolism is recommended for patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease during any active inﬂammatory bowel disease ﬂare. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

neuropathy in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease is not typically associated with disease activity and does not generally improve when the disease is under control. Treatment is symptomatic. Although some clinicians advocate for a limited trial of steroids or IV immunoglobulin (IVIg), there is no strong evidence to support this practice. 65,66 When peripheral neuropathy is diagnosed in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease, they should be evaluated for vitamin and nutrient deﬁciencies, including vitamin B 12 and folate and additional testing for thiamine, vitamin E, and copper deﬁciency in patients with Crohn disease. Because treatments for inﬂammatory bowel disease, including thalidomide, methotrexate, and TNF-α inhibitor therapy, can cause peripheral neuropathy, patients should be examined for signs of subclinical neuropathy before treatment initiation and examined regularly during treatment. For patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease who are diagnosed with peripheral neuropathy while being treated with one of these agents, the medication should generally be discontinued. 65 If a demyelinating neuropathy occurs in a patient with inﬂammatory bowel disease, it is most commonly due to TNF-α inhibitor therapy, although case TABLE 4-6 Diagnosis of Central Nervous System Whipple Disease a Deﬁnite central nervous system (CNS)-Whipple disease u Must have one of the following three criteria 1 Oculomasticatory myorhythmia or oculofacial skeletal myorhythmia 2 Positive tissue biopsy (either periodic acid–Schiff stain positive or bacteria on electron microscopy) 3 Positive polymerase chain reaction (PCR) u If histologic or PCR analysis is not performed on CNS tissue, then the patient must also have neurologic signs u If histologic or PCR analysis is performed on CNS tissue, then the patient does not need to have neurologic signs Possible CNS-Whipple disease u Must have any one of four systemic symptoms, not due to another known etiology 1 Fever of unknown origin 2 Gastrointestinal symptoms (steatorrhea, chronic diarrhea, abdominal distention, or pain) 3 Chronic migratory arthralgias or polyarthralgias 4 Unexplained lymphadenopathy, night sweats, or malaise u Also must have any one of four neurologic signs that are not due to another known etiology 1 Supranuclear vertical gaze palsy 2 Rhythmic myoclonus 3 Dementia with psychiatric symptoms 4 Hypothalamic manifestations a Data from Louis ED, et al, Ann Neurol 72 98 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

reports have described patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease and demyelinating neuropathy who have not received TNF-α inhibitor therapy. 65 Acute inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (AIDP) is the most common demyelinating neuropathy associated with TNF-α inhibitor therapy and can occur from 6 days to 2 years after initiation of treatment. 65 Chronic inﬂammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy (CIDP) and multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) have also been described in the context of TNF-α inhibitor therapy. Treatment of AIDP and CIDP in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease is the same as in other contexts. Myasthenia Gravis, Myopathy, and Myositis Clinicians have described myasthenia gravis, inﬂammatory myopathy, and orbital myositis in patients with inﬂammatory bowel disease. 60 The extent of the increased risk of these conditions is unclear and likely represents an underlying autoimmune diathesis among these patients. Myopathy and myositis symptoms often correlate with disease activity and improve with treatment of ﬂares. 67 Vitamin and Mineral Deﬁciency Inﬂammatory bowel disease can cause vitamin and mineral deﬁciencies, including vitamin B 12 , folate, thiamine, vitamin E, copper, iron, and selenium deﬁciency. The risk of nutritional deﬁciency is particularly high in patients with Crohn disease due to involvement of the terminal ileum or in the setting of bowel resection. WHIPPLE DISEASE Whipple disease is a rare chronic disease caused by bacterial infection with Tropheryma whipplei, affecting about one in one million people. 68 Whipple disease often begins with a prodromal stage of fatigue and migratory distal arthralgias, followed by gastrointestinal symptoms, including diarrhea and abdominal pain. Up to one-half of patients with gastrointestinal Whipple disease have neurologic symptoms (CNS-Whipple disease), 68 which may be the only manifestation of Whipple disease in 5% to 20% of patients. 68 , 69 The most frequent neurologic manifestations of Whipple disease are encephalopathy, hypothalamic dysfunction, ocular motor palsies, and extrapyramidal movement disorders. 69 Whipple disease is partially treatable with antibiotics and, if left untreated, has high rates of neurologic complications. The risk of Whipple disease is higher among people who have had close contact with chronic carriers and in populations with exposure to sewage and other contaminated water sources. 68 , 70 Neurologic Manifestations of Whipple Disease Neurologic symptoms in patients with Whipple disease typically present subacutely over weeks to months, leading to psychiatric and behavioral changes, reduced alertness, supranuclear ophthalmoplegia, ocular motor palsies, hypothalamic dysfunction, dysarthria, myoclonus, and ataxia. The progression of neurologic symptoms is caused by lesions involving periventricular and periaqueductal regions, including the mesial temporal lobes, brainstem, deep gray nuclei, hypothalamus, and cerebellum. Headaches, aqueductal stenosis, and hydrocephalus are less common but can occur. 71 A CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 99 KEY POINTS •Neurologic symptoms in patients with Whipple disease typically present subacutely over weeks to months, leading to psychiatric and behavioral changes, reduced alertness, supranuclear ophthalmoplegia, ocular motor palsies, hypothalamic dysfunction, dysarthria, myoclonus, and ataxia. •The progression of neurologic symptoms in patients with Whipple disease is caused by lesions involving the periventricular and periaqueductal regions. •The initial diagnostic test to evaluate for central nervous system (CNS)- Whipple disease should be CSF Tropheryma whipplei polymerase chain reaction (PCR), which is positive in 69% of patients with possible or deﬁnite CNS- Whipple disease. •Routine CSF studies in patients with Whipple disease may demonstrate mildly elevated protein or lymphocytic pleocytosis but can be normal. •If CSF PCR testing is negative but suspicion for CNS-Whipple disease is high, biopsy of the involved tissue should be undertaken. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

pathognomonic feature of Whipple disease is slow rhythmic movement of the mouth and eyes called oculomasticatory myorhythmia or synchronized oculo- facial-skeletal myorhythmia with pendular vergence oscillations of the eyes that occur in synchrony with myoclonus of the masticatory and lingual muscles. Seizures have been reported in up to 20% of patients. 71 There are rarer reports of isolated spinal cord lesions, cranial neuropathies, tremor, dystonia, chorea, myalgias, and embolic stroke (the latter is secondary to endocarditis). 71 Diagnostic Testing Diagnostic criteria for Whipple disease are summarized in TABLE 4-6. 72 CNS-Whipple disease is diagnosed by CSF T. whipplei polymerase chain reaction (PCR), which is positive in 69% of cases with possible or deﬁnite CNS-Whipple disease. 71 Routine CSF studies may demonstrate mildly elevated protein or lymphocytic pleocytosis but can be normal. If CSF PCR testing is negative but suspicion for CNS-Whipple disease is high, biopsy of the involved tissue should be undertaken. Ideal biopsy sites are the small bowel or an involved lymph node, but a brain biopsy may be necessary in cases of isolated nervous system involvement or a normal or inconclusive biopsy of other sites. Brain MRI abnormalities are present in 50% to 85% of patients with CNS-Whipple disease. 71 The most common pattern is relatively symmetric midline T2 hyperintense lesions involving the brainstem, hypothalamus, mesial temporal lobes, and periventricular areas (FIGURE 4-8). Enhancement is present in 25% of patients, and tumefactive ring-enhancing lesions and T2 hyperintense cortical lesions have also been reported. 71 Patients with CNS-Whipple disease are at risk of neurologic immune reconstitution inﬂammatory syndrome (IRIS), which may be caused by reversal of Whipple disease–associated suppression of the immune system. 71,73 When IRIS occurs, it can exacerbate existing neurologic symptoms or unmask latent neurologic symptoms, particularly in the ﬁrst 3 months of treatment. Therefore, because 20% to 70% of patients with Whipple disease have neurologic complications, T. whipplei PCR of CSF should be performed in all patients with conﬁrmed Whipple disease (independent of neurologic symptoms) before treatment initiation to evaluate for subclinical neurologic involvement that could worsen with treatment. FIGURE 4-8 Whipple disease. Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI of the brain in a patient with Whipple disease showing T2 hyperintensities lining periventricular regions and involving deep gray nuclei. Reprinted with permission from El-Abassi R, et al, J Neurol Sci. 68 © 2019 Elsevier BV. 100 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF HEPATIC AND GASTROINTESTINAL DISEASE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Treatment and Prognosis Approximately one-half of patients with CNS-Whipple disease improve with treatment, but permanent neurologic deﬁcits are common. Patients with neurologic involvement of Whipple disease are generally treated with a 2- to 4-week induction phase with IV ceftriaxone followed by a prolonged maintenance phase with either doxycycline and hydroxychloroquine or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole. 69,71 Patients remain at risk of reinfection with T. whipplei bacilli and should be treated with lifelong antibiotic prophylaxis. Clinical follow-up, brain MRI, and CSF PCR should be repeated at 6 months to document resolution of infection. Patients should be carefully monitored for IRIS in the ﬁrst 3 months of treatment; IRIS should be treated with steroids if it occurs. 73 CONCLUSION Diseases of the hepatic and gastrointestinal systems increase the likelihood of neurologic disease because of shared genetic risk, immune dysregulation, systemic inﬂammation, hypercoagulability, malabsorption, and the neurologic toxicity of gut metabolites in hepatic dysfunction. Increased collaboration between neurologists and gastroenterologists can enhance understanding of the pathophysiology of these conditions and improve the diagnosis and treatment of patients who develop neurologic complications of gastrointestinal and hepatic diseases. Neurologic Manifestations of Renal and Electrolyte Disorders By Eelco F. M. Wijdicks, MD, PhD, FAAN, FACP, FNCS ABSTRACT OBJECTIVE : This article reviews the clinical manifestations and treatment of the neurologic manifestations of renal and electrolyte disorders. LATEST DEVELOPMENTS : Osmotic demyelination caused by rapid correction of hyponatremia is now considered exceptionally rare, with studies calling into question current protocols that emphasize slow correction of hyponatremia. Patients with acute renal failure and encephalopathy may have posterior encephalopathy syndrome rather than uremic encephalopathy. Cefepime neurotoxicity in patients with renal failure is increasingly recognized. ESSENTIAL POINTS : Electrolyte disorders and uremia usually result in reversible cerebral dysfunction leading to encephalopathy, seizures, and muscle weakness. Hyponatremia is the only electrolyte abnormality associated with irreversible brain injury. Neuroimaging should be considered in patients with acute renal failure and encephalopathy to evaluate for posterior encephalopathy syndrome or subdural hematoma. INTRODUCTION E lectrolyte balance and distribution across body compartments are essential for cellular physiology. Electrolyte disturbances can affect neuronal function, leading to neurologic symptoms. The kidneys serve an essential role in electrolyte balance and excretion of toxins, and therefore, impaired renal function can also lead to neurologic symptoms. Neurologists most frequently encounter patients with renal and electrolyte disorders in the inpatient setting. In this context, the neurologist must determine if the patient’s neurologic symptoms and signs are related to the renal and electrolyte disturbances or whether a concurrent primary neurologic process is at play because many serious medical illnesses are associated with some degree of serum electrolyte abnormality, renal impairment, or both. ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Most electrolyte abnormalities do not affect the nervous system unless there is a rapid shift from the baseline value. Therefore, when new neurologic symptoms occur in patients with abnormal serum electrolytes, the clinician CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 57 REVIEW ARTICLE CON TINUUM AUDIO INT ERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CON TINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):57– 74. Address correspondence to Dr Eelco F. M. Wijdicks, Neurosciences Intensive Care Unit, Mayo Clinic Hospital Saint Marys Campus, 200 First Street SW, Rochester, MN 55905, wijde@mayo.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Wijdicks has received publishing royalties from publications relating to health care. UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Dr Wijdicks reports no disclosure. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

must determine whether the electrolyte disturbance is the culprit. Factors to consider are the degree of electrolyte disturbance, the rapidity of change in the electrolyte, and any attempts by clinicians to correct the electrolyte abnormality, which could lead to overcorrection or overly rapid correction. Hyponatremia and Hypernatremia The brain consists of approximately 75% water, which is incompressible when volume increases (a reason intracranial pressure can increase quickly and exponentially). Brain water content is dependent on the osmotic conditions of intracellular and extracellular compartments. Hyperosmolarity within vascular systems (eg, hypernatremia, hyperglycemia) pulls water out of the brain through osmotic forces. Hypo-osmolality within vascular systems (eg, hyponatremia) can cause the brain to gain water (and increase in volume) due to the osmotic gradient. 1 The brain can adapt to a change in homeostasis even when there are profound changes in body-ﬂuid osmolality, but only up to a certain point (FIGURE 3-1). In hypo-osmolar states, there is an initial rapid adaptation over hours through cellular loss of sodium, potassium, chloride, and water. A hypo-osmolar state is corrected gradually (days) through loss of the so-called osmolytes. These extruded solutes are glutamate, glutamine, taurine, and myo-inositol, but the activation of transporters for these solutes takes time (slow adaptation). Brain adaptation to acute hyperosmolality involves rapid sodium accumulation from CSF, plasma, and extracellular ﬂuid, resulting in correction of intracellular brain water. 2,3 Causes of hyponatremia and hypernatremia are grouped according to extracellular volume, which is assessed by the patient’s clinical volume status and history of ﬂuid intake. Signs of dehydration (dry skin, dry mouth, dark urine) or evidence of large volumes of free water intake (eg, IV infusions) provide valuable information. In disturbances of serum sodium, extracellular volume is often increased (hypervolemic) and less commonly decreased (hypovolemic); euvolemic hyponatremia occurs in the syndrome of inappropriate secretion of antidiuretic hormone (SIADH), which may be paraneoplastic or medication induced. Hyponatremia results from an intravascular water surplus relative to intravascular sodium. Hypernatremia results from an intravascular water deﬁcit relative to intravascular sodium. The early neurologic manifestations of hyponatremia and hypernatremia are nonspeciﬁc ﬂuctuations in alertness and easily overlooked until the appearance of a more obvious acute confusional state, delirium, worsening drowsiness, or a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. The question then becomes FIGURE 3-1 Brain adaptation to osmotic changes. Used with permission from Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. © 2025 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. 58 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

to what extent these manifestations are correlated with hyponatremia or hypernatremia. HYPONATREMIA. Common causes of hyponatremia include thiazide diuretics, ﬂuid overload, and medications (antidepressants [eg, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) and venlafaxine] and antiseizure medications [eg, oxcarbazepine, carbamazepine, and sodium valproate]). Severe hyponatremia can also be caused by any major acute brain injury due to cerebral salt wasting syndrome. Hyponatremia is neurologically asymptomatic when serum sodium remains above approximately 125 mmol/L. However, when serum sodium values decrease below approximately 120 mmol/L to 125 mmol/L, patients develop progressively impaired responsiveness. Patients with a rapid decrease from baseline (more than 20 mmol/L) may develop seizures. The degree of impairment in level of consciousness correlates not only with the absolute serum sodium level but also with the rate of change in serum sodium (FIGURE 3-2). Although theoretically hyponatremia could lead to cerebral edema due to osmotic shifts from the extracellular space into cells, this appears to be rare in practice. A 12-year review of in-hospital death in patients with severe hyponatremia (deﬁned as less than 120 mmol/L) found only one death from cerebral edema (0.15%), 4 and a study of hundreds of thousands of young women undergoing surgical procedures failed to identify a single patient dying from brain edema and hyponatremia 5 ; rather, poor outcomes in patients with severe hyponatremia are thought to be related to the underlying disorder rather than neurologic complications of hyponatremia. The recommended speed of serum sodium correction in patients with hyponatremia has been contentious, with varying recommendations (TABLE 3-1 6 ). There is general agreement that hyponatremia should be corrected slowly, given the risk of osmotic demyelination. Guidance for correction of hyponatremia is provided in TABLE 3-2. 7,8 Guidelines from 2024 state that a correction of a sodium level of 120 mmol/L or less by more than 10 mmol/L within 24 hours or by more than 18 mmol/L within 48 hours should be avoided. Severe hyponatremia increases the risk for injury from excessive correction, but it is not inevitable. If the sodium is 105 mmol/L or less or with additional risk factors for osmotic demyelination syndrome (eg, alcohol use disorder, hypokalemia, malnutrition, or advanced liver disease), correction by more than 8 mmol/L a day is considered excessive. 9 OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME. Severe hyponatremia has been associated with acute demyelination of the brain, referred to as osmotic demyelination syndrome. The FIGURE 3-2 Relationship between level of consciousness and rapidity of decrease of serum sodium. Used with permission from Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. © 2025 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. KEY POINTS •When there are new neurologic symptoms in patients with abnormal serum electrolytes, the clinician must determine whether the electrolyte disturbance is the culprit. •Attempts by clinicians to correct electrolyte abnormalities in patients with new neurologic symptoms could lead to overcorrection or overly rapid correction. •The degree of impairment in level of consciousness in hyponatremic patients with sodium levels below 120 mmol/L to 125 mmol/L correlates not only with the absolute serum sodium level but also with the rate of change in serum sodium. •There is general agreement that hyponatremia should be corrected slowly, given the risk of osmotic demyelination. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 59 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. nomenclature relates to its topography, affecting the pons (central pontine myelinolysis) or hemispheric white matter (extrapontine myelinolysis). The two may occur concurrently. Despite the incidence of hyponatremia in hospitalized patients, both syndromes are uncommon. However, there should be a low threshold for obtaining neuroimaging in patients with acute hyponatremia who fail to improve neurologically after correction of hyponatremia. The term central pontine myelinolysis was coined by Raymond Adams 6,10 to describe the condition’s location and pathology. Historically, central pontine myelinolysis has been associated with severe underlying systemic disease, such as advanced cancer or chronic alcohol use disorder with or without hyponatremia. 11 The association with hyponatremia, rate of correction, and the role of sudden major ﬂuid shifts was a much later insight. Central pontine myelinolysis became associated with major osmolality ﬂuctuations, as may be seen in severely burned patients and after complicated liver transplantation. Rapid sodium correction was not always implicated. Moreover, hyponatremia in chronic liver disease may already predispose TABLE 3-1 Recommendations for Correction of Hyponatremia a u More than 20 mmol/L over 3 days is too rapid u If serum sodium is less than 105 mmol/L, correct at 2 mmol/L/h for the ﬁrst 20 mmol/L, then allow to drift to normal. If serum sodium is 105 mmol/L or greater, correct at 2 mmol/L/h to 125 mmol/L to 130 mmol/L u Not more than 12 mmol/L/d for the ﬁrst day, and on subsequent days, slower u Less than 2.5 mmol/L/h and no more than 20 mmol/d u Less than 15 mmol/L in 24 hours u Less than 10 mmol/L/d in the ﬁrst 24 hours and less on subsequent days u Should not exceed 1 mmol/L/h to 2 mmol/L/h u Should not exceed 8 mmol/L on any day of correction a Modiﬁed with permission from Martin RJ, J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 6 © 2004 Journal of Neurology Neurosurgery and Psychiatry. TABLE 3-2 Correction Methods for Sodium Derangements Hyponatremia 7 u Calculate the need to normalize serum sodium by using 3% sodium chloride (513 mmol/L) in the formula to provide total millimoles per liter: [513-current serum sodium] ÷ 0.5 × [body weight (kg) + 1] u Rate: increase serum sodium 1 mmol/L/hr Hypernatremia 8 u Calculate total body water deﬁcit: 0.6 × body weight (kg) × (serum sodium ÷ 140-1) a a Assuming 140 mmol/L is the serum sodium goal. 60 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

patients to central pontine myelinolysis, and the author has observed central pontine myelinolysis even with “slow” correction of hyponatremia (CASE 3-1). 6,12 ,13 Central pontine myelinolysis typically occurs after correction of severe hyponatremia with hypertonic saline. Patients are generally initially awake without any major appreciable deﬁcit after correction of serum sodium, only to deteriorate over subsequent days. Symptoms include supranuclear bulbar dysfunction (causing facial weakness, dysarthria, and dysphagia) and tetraparesis. Symptoms may be asymmetric or unilateral in some cases. Limitation of conjugate horizontal eye movements occurs in some cases. CASE 3-1 A 40-year-old woman with alcohol use disorder and malnutrition presented with shock, hyperammonemia, gastrointestinal bleeding, and coagulopathy due to cirrhosis. Neurology was consulted after the patient was noted to be nonverbal and quadriplegic. A brain MRI showed hyperintensity in the pons concerning for central pontine myelinolysis (FIGURE 3-3). On review of her hospital course, her initial serum sodium was 118 mmol/L, and her hemoglobin was 3 g/dL in the setting of active gastric bleeding. She required intubation, three vasopressors, and was resuscitated with multiple blood products. Following ﬂuid resuscitation, her serum sodium was 123 mmol/L 12 hours later. Over the next 4 days, her sodium gradually but steadily increased to 151 mmol/L. FIGURE 3-3 Central pontine myelinolysis in the patient in CASE 3-1. Axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI; A), ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR; B), T2-weighted (C), and T1- weighted (D) MRI showing restricted diffusion and T2 hyperintensity in the pons. COMMENT The neuroimaging is characteristic of central pontine myelinolysis. Although there was an “overshoot” into hypernatremia, it occurred over several days. Whether this was causative or due to malnutrition and chronic liver disease is unclear. This case illustrates the difﬁculties with interpreting sodium correction and linking it to central pontine myelinolysis. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 61 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

In extreme cases, central pontine myelinolysis may cause locked-in syndrome. 6,10,11 Extrapontine myelinolysis affects the subcortical white matter or deep gray matter, which can cause impairment of consciousness, corticobulbar signs, and a parkinsonian-like picture with severe dysarthria and dysphagia. Extrapontine myelinolysis and central pontine myelinolysis may occur together. MRI in patients with central pontine myelinolysis most commonly demonstrates a symmetric, round area of T2 hyperintensity in the central basis pontis; the lesion may also be trident- or bat-shaped because of preferential involvement of horizontal tracts and sparing of vertical tracts. In extrapontine myelinolysis, T2 hyperintensity can be seen in the subcortical white matter, deep gray matter, or both. Because there are no strict clinical criteria and no strict MRI criteria, any abnormal ﬁnding could inaccurately lead to a diagnosis of osmotic demyelination syndrome. 14-16 Recovery is possible, even in patients with a locked-in syndrome, but disabling bulbar dysfunction and spastic quadriparesis may persist. Demyelination as the underlying pathology has been conﬁrmed at autopsy. 17 The mechanisms that trigger demyelination are not known. Some have postulated a “grid” phenomenon, suggesting “strangulation” of myelinated ﬁbers by surrounding edema in the pons or through “osmotic endothelial injury,” which again suggests that rapid osmotic changes of endothelial cells release factors toxic to myelin. Osmotic demyelination syndrome has classically been correlated with overly rapid correction of serum sodium, but this dogma has been called into question. In one series of 1024 patients in the intensive care unit with severe hyponatremia (less than 120 mmol/L), rapid sodium correction (greater than 8 mmol/L/d) was associated with improved patient outcomes (decreased mortality, lengths of hospital and intensive care unit stays) and no identiﬁable cases of osmotic demyelination syndrome, with rates of correction of 8 mmol/L/d to 12 mmol/L/d and greater than 12 mmol/L/d more commonly leading to decreased length of stay (compared with less than 8 mmol/L/d) and rates between 10 mmol/L/d and 20 mmol/L/d associated with the shortest length of stay. 18 Another retrospective study of 22,858 patients hospitalized with hyponatremia showed that osmotic demyelination syndrome is exceptionally rare (affecting 0.05% of the patients in the study) and unrelated to rapid correction (deﬁned as an increase in serum sodium of greater than 8 mmol/L/d); most patients who developed osmotic demyelination syndrome actually had not undergone rapid correction of serum sodium. 19 These studies suggest that the apprehensiveness felt when correcting hyponatremia may not be justiﬁed. However, it remains prudent to follow the guidelines for correction of hyponatremia previously cited. 20 HYPERNATREMIA. Serum sodium increases when there is a water deﬁcit, which can be caused by decreased water intake, iatrogenic administration of hypertonic sodium bicarbonate to correct metabolic acidosis, infusion of large volumes of isotonic sodium chloride in patients with hypotonic ﬂuid loss or shock, and failure to replace insensible losses from the skin and respiratory tract in hospitalized patients. 8,21,22 Additionally, hypernatremia can be due to central diabetes insipidus, which may be caused by pituitary surgery, traumatic brain injury, or aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage. 62 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Clinical signs of extracellular volume depletion resulting from dehydration include dry mucous membranes and tachycardia, but these ﬁndings may be less apparent in older adults. As dehydration and hypernatremia become severe (generally deﬁned as greater than 160 mmol/L), patients become unresponsive, with the reduction in level of consciousness roughly correlated with both the degree and the rapidity of serum sodium increase (FIGURE 3-4). Generalized tonic-clonic seizures are unusual, even in patients with markedly increased sodium levels (ie, greater than 160 mmol/L). The major caveat with attributing clinical signs to hypernatremia is that hypernatremia is common in patients with severe illness, which itself can cause neurologic manifestations and generally determines the overall prognosis. Mortality from severe hypernatremia (greater than 160 mmol/L) can reach 70% in susceptible patients. The ideal rate of ﬂuid resuscitation in patients with hypernatremia is not known but may depend in part on the rate at which hypernatremia develops. As in hyponatremia, overly rapid correction may be hazardous, but there is no well-documented clinical evidence of this risk. In patients with hypovolemia, intravascular volume should be repleted with isotonic saline or colloids, aiming for a reduction of 1 mmol/L to 2 mmol/L of sodium per hour. Hypernatremia may reoccur if the underlying systemic disease is not corrected and triggering drugs (eg, diuretics, amphotericin B, corticosteroids) are not discontinued. If hypernatremia is associated with diabetes insipidus, output should be matched with ﬂuid intake and free water ﬂushes if a nasogastric tube is in place. If serum sodium increases above 150 mmol/L, desmopressin IV (0.5 mcg to 1.0 mcg) should be administered with 0.45% sodium chloride or 5% dextrose. Given concerns about using hypotonic IV ﬂuids in patients with brain edema, serum sodium should generally be corrected to at least a more moderate degree of hypernatremia (150 mmol/L to 155 mmol/L) because aggressive attempts at correction to a normal sodium value may lead to hyponatremia. A formula for correction of hypernatremia is shown in TABLE 3-2. Hypokalemia and Hyperkalemia Disorders of serum potassium are common in hospitalized patients because of loss of potassium through the gastrointestinal tract or sweating or accumulation of potassium due to renal failure. 23 In contrast to neurologic complications of sodium disorders, which typically affect the central nervous system, potassium disturbances most commonly affect the peripheral nervous system. The initial manifestations of potassium disturbances are cardiac, and neurologic manifestations of potassium derangements are not expected in patients with normal ECG morphology. FIGURE 3-4 Relationship between level of consciousness and rapidity of increase of serum sodium. Used with permission from Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. © 2025 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. KEY POINTS •Central pontine myelinolysis typically occurs after correction of severe hyponatremia with hypertonic saline. •Extrapontine myelinolysis affects the subcortical white matter or deep gray matter, which can cause impairment of consciousness, corticobulbar signs, and a parkinsonian picture with severe dysarthria and dysphagia. •MRI in patients with central pontine myelinolysis most commonly demonstrates a symmetric, round area of T2 hyperintensity in the central basis pontis. •Osmotic demyelination syndrome has classically been correlated with overly rapid correction of serum sodium, but this dogma has been called into question. •As dehydration and hypernatremia become severe, patients become unresponsive, with the reduction in level of consciousness roughly correlated with both the degree and the rapidity of serum sodium increase. •Serum sodium should generally be corrected to at least a more moderate degree of hypernatremia because aggressive attempts at correction to a normal sodium value may lead to hyponatremia. •The primary manifestations of potassium disturbances are cardiac, and neurologic manifestations of potassium derangements are not expected in patients with normal ECG morphology. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 63 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

HYPOKALEMIA. Common causes of hypokalemia include gastrointestinal disorders causing vomiting or diarrhea, skin losses through sweating, and diuretics. Metabolic alkalosis from nasogastric suctioning may also cause an increase in renal potassium excretion. Neurologic manifestations of hypokalemia are predominantly neuromuscular and generally do not occur unless serum potassium concentrations decrease below 2 mmol/L. Potassium loss reduces muscle cell excitability, increasing the threshold for initiation of the action potential; alternatively, depletion of glycogen stores or rhabdomyolysis may be the main cause of muscle weakness. Patients with severe hypokalemia can manifest a range of neuromuscular complications from mildly painful, swollen muscles to cramping (prominent at night, potentially mimicking restless leg syndrome) to quadriplegia and respiratory failure. Muscle weakness usually begins in the proximal leg, neck, and trunk muscles and progresses over several days, often accompanied by aching pain in the back and larger proximal muscles. Rarely, hypokalemia may be associated with rapidly ascending weakness and respiratory failure, suggesting Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome but without the usual accompanying signs of this disorder, such as sensory changes, areﬂexia, or cranial nerve deﬁcits. Ventilatory failure, difﬁculty swallowing secretions, and slurred speech can occur, but are rare because the electrocardiographic abnormalities of severe hypokalemia (prominent U waves and ST segment depression) are generally recognized and hypokalemia is corrected before neurologic manifestations emerge. Correction of hypokalemia results in marked improvement of weakness in a few weeks (TABLE 3-3). HYPERKALEMIA. Hyperkalemia is most commonly encountered in patients with renal or adrenal failure. Hyperkalemia has also been anecdotally described in patients on cardiac bypass; increased plasma potassium may be induced by washout of ischemic underperfused areas during the body’s bypass period or may be caused by rewarming after hypothermia. The pathophysiologic consequences of hyperkalemia (potassium concentrations greater than 7 mmol/L) are also predominantly cardiac, including ECG changes (peaked T wave, shortened QT interval, and prolonged PR interval with widened QRS complex) and arrhythmias. Neurologic symptoms include weakness (which may progress to quadriplegia and respiratory failure) 24 and myotonia, but these generally do not occur without cardiac manifestations, which are usually rapidly recognized and corrected before neurologic manifestations occur. Guidance for correction of hyperkalemia can be found in TABLE 3-4. TABLE 3-3 Treatment of Hypokalemia Severity Serum potassium concentration (mmol/L) IV potassium replacement dose (mmol) Mild to moderate 2.5-3.4 20-40 Severe Less than 2.5 40-80 64 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Hypophosphatemia and Hyperphosphatemia Phosphorus is required for building cell membranes and DNA and RNA molecules, signal transduction, and pH buffering, among other functions. Hypophosphatemia is common in hospitalized patients because phosphate stores are easily depleted. Neurologic complications of phosphate abnormalities are uncommon because phosphate is one of the commonly measured electrolytes and often rapidly supplemented. HYPOPHOSPHATEMIA. Hypophosphatemia (less than 2.5 mg/dL) may cause respiratory insufﬁciency, decreased myocardial contractility, rhabdomyolysis, hemolysis, and hepatic dysfunction. Common causes of hypophosphatemia include chronic gastrointestinal diseases that impair phosphate absorption; excessive use of antacids, which bind phosphate in the gut; inadequate phosphate intake; refeeding syndrome (resumption of nutrition in severely malnourished patients); and critical illness, which causes increased phosphate demand. 25,26 The neurologic manifestations of hypophosphatemia include seizures and weakness, which generally only occur when serum phosphate levels decrease below 0.5 mg/dL to 1 mg/dL. Severe hypophosphatemia can cause diaphragmatic weakness, which may result in the inability to be weaned from a ventilator, particularly in patients with preexisting chronic pulmonary disease. In such cases, phosphate repletion improves inspiratory pressures and may allow for weaning from a ventilator (for supplementation see TABLE 3-5). Rapidly progressive weakness leading to complete quadriplegia, ptosis, and difﬁculty swallowing has been described with hypophosphatemia but is now extremely rare because phosphate is commonly measured and repleted. HYPERPHOSPHATEMIA. Acute hyperphosphatemia is invariably associated with acute hypocalcemia, which may be severe enough to cause tetanic cramps (see the section Hypocalcemia). Severe hyperphosphatemia should therefore lead the clinician to evaluate for hypocalcemia. See TABLE 3-6 for the treatment of hyperphosphatemia. TABLE 3-4 Treatment for Hyperkalemia Treatment Dose Route Time to onset Duration of effect Calcium gluconate 1-2 g (4.56-9.12 mg/dL calcium) IV over 5-10 minutes 1-2 minutes 10-30 minutes Sodium bicarbonate 50-100 mg/dL IV over 2-5 minutes 30 minutes 2-6 hours Insulin (regular) (with dextrose) 5-10 units IV with 50 mL of 50% dextrose injection 15-45 minutes 2-6 hours 50% dextrose 50 mL (25 g) IV over 5 minutes 30 minutes 2-6 hours 10% dextrose 1000 mL (100 g) IV over 5-10 minutes 30 minutes 2-6 hours CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 65 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Hypocalcemia and Hypercalcemia Calcium levels are regulated by vitamin D, parathyroid hormone, and calcitonin to ensure appropriate concentrations for many intracellular processes. Approximately one-half of total serum calcium is bound by albumin. Differences in serum albumin concentration and state of hydration cause variations in total serum calcium concentration. The effect of albumin can be corrected by using the following formula: Adjusted measured serum calcium ¼ serum calcium þ 0:8 × ð4  serum albuminÞ HYPOCALCEMIA. Hypocalcemia can be caused by hypoalbuminemia, sepsis, acute pancreatitis, crush injury, thyroid surgery (causing hypoparathyroidism), hypomagnesemia, vitamin D deﬁciency, hyperphosphatemia, chronic use of certain antiseizure medications such as phenytoin, tumor lysis syndrome, and rhabdomyolysis (due to tissue calcium sequestration). Hypocalcemia associated with hypoalbuminemia does not cause symptoms because the ionized fraction remains similar. Hypocalcemia is generally asymptomatic, mostly reﬂecting underlying illness severity. 27 With severe hypocalcemia (less than 3 mg/dL), paresthesia in the hands, feet, and around the mouth can progress to muscle cramps, spasms of the hands and feet, and laryngeal stridor may follow as unchecked hypocalcemia progresses. 28 Plantar ﬂexion of the toes, arching of the feet, and contraction of calf muscles are also cardinal signs. The Chvostek sign is elicited by tapping TABLE 3-5 Treatment of Hypophosphatemia Severity Serum phosphate concentration (mg/dL) IV phosphate replacement dose (mmol/kg) Mild 2.3-2.7 0.08-0.16 Moderate 1.5-2.2 0.16-0.32 Severe Less than 1.5 0.32-0.64 TABLE 3-6 Treatment of Hyperphosphatemia Medication Dosage forms Initial dosage Calcium acetate Tablet: 667 mg Gelcap: 667 mg 2 tablets or gelcaps 3 times a day with meals Calcium carbonate Tablet, capsule, liquid, and powder; various strengths 1-2 g 3 times a day with meals Aluminum hydroxide Tablets: 300 and 600 mg Suspension: 320 mg/5 mL 1-2 tablets or 15-30 mL of suspension 3 or 4 times a day with meals and at bedtime 66 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

three ﬁngers over the branches of the facial nerve anterior to the tragus. A positive response is indicated by twitching of all ipsilateral facial muscles (lip at the angle of the mouth, nasal bridge, and lateral angle of the eye), although there may be a graded response. Twitching of the mouth alone may occur in 25% of patients without hypocalcemia. Flexion of the ﬁngers and adduction of the thumb produce a muscle spasm; this may also be elicited by inﬂating a blood pressure cuff around the arm, known as the Trousseau sign. 29 Tonic-clonic seizures can occur when serum calcium concentration is less than 2 mg/dL. Focal or nonconvulsive status epilepticus may develop but rapidly resolves after correction of hypocalcemia (TABLE 3-7). Prolonged coma from hypocalcemia has been reported but is only seen because of anoxia (eg, with laryngeal stridor or long-standing status epilepticus). HYPERCALCEMIA. Hypercalcemia can be caused by primary hyperparathyroidism and cancer with or without bony metastases. Symptoms of hypercalcemia include fatigue, bone and muscle aches, constipation, headaches, hypertension, irritability, anxiety, depression, and decreased memory or concentration. More severe neurologic manifestations, including lethargy, confusion, stupor, and coma, may occur in patients with severe hypercalcemia (calcium greater than 14 mg/dL). Many patients recover after general measures have been taken to correct dehydration (volume expansion with normal saline; calcitonin and loop diuretics, which both increase urinary calcium excretion). Hypomagnesemia and Hypermagnesemia Magnesium is an important mineral component of the bony skeleton, a cofactor for many metabolic enzymes, and a regulator of ion channels and transporters in excitable tissues. At the neuromuscular junction, magnesium competes with calcium and displaces it from its target site. As a result, acetylcholine release and the excitability of the muscle membrane are susceptible to changes in serum magnesium. Magnesium’s effects on the central nervous system are less well characterized; it may stabilize the synaptic membrane, which leads to decreased excitability. In hospitalized patients, hypermagnesemia (greater than 2.3 mg/dL) and hypomagnesemia (less than 1.7 mg/dL) are relatively common. Both conditions can lead to adverse outcomes, including longer hospital stays and increased risk of death. Patients with mild magnesium derangements may have no symptoms, but severe chronic hypomagnesemia can lead to muscle weakness, paresthesia, tetany, seizures, and cardiac arrhythmia. HYPOMAGNESEMIA. Hypomagnesemia is common in hospitalized patients. Causes include renal wasting (secondary to diuretics, aminoglycosides, or certain chemotherapeutic agents) and diarrhea associated with nasogastric feeding. The frequent association of hypomagnesemia with hypocalcemia, hypokalemia, and alkalosis markedly confounds the association between hypomagnesemia and clinical manifestations that are potentially due to other electrolyte disturbances. In addition, magnesium administration alone often corrects other laboratory abnormalities. KEY POINTS •Neurologic manifestations of hypokalemia are predominantly neuromuscular and generally do not occur unless serum potassium concentrations fall below 2 mmol/L. •Neurologic symptoms of hyperkalemia include weakness and myotonia, but these generally do not occur without cardiac manifestations, which are usually rapidly recognized and corrected before neurologic manifestations occur. •The neurologic manifestations of hypophosphatemia include seizures and weakness, which generally only occur when serum phosphate levels decrease below 0.5 mg/dL to 1 mg/dL. •Severe hypophosphatemia can cause diaphragmatic weakness, which may result in the inability to be weaned from a ventilator, particularly in patients with preexisting chronic pulmonary disease. •With severe hypocalcemia, paresthesia in the hands, feet, and around the mouth can progress to muscle cramps, spasms of the hand and feet, and laryngeal stridor may follow as unchecked hypocalcemia progresses. •Tonic-clonic seizures can occur in patients with hypocalcemia when serum calcium concentration is less than 2mg/dL.
