Hypomagnesemia (less than 1.7 mg/dL) can cause muscle twitching, myoclonus, startle responses, and postural tremor. Trousseau and Chvostek signs, spasms of the hands and feet, and, rarely, tetany have been reported but are more characteristic of hypocalcemia. Unrecognized severe hypomagnesemia may predispose to tonic-clonic seizures. 30,31 An unusual case of myasthenia gravis unmasked by mild hypermagnesemia (magnesium was given for eclampsia) has been reported, 32 illustrating the importance of considering myasthenia in patients with sensitivity to magnesium. Correction guidance for hypomagnesemia is shown in TABLE 3-8. HYPERMAGNESEMIA. Chronic renal failure, lithium therapy, hypothyroidism, hyperparathyroidism with renal disease, and pheochromocytoma may all cause susceptibility to hypermagnesemia, but neurologic manifestations generally do not occur without exogenous magnesium (eg, when magnesium sulfate is used to treat eclampsia). 33 Systemic symptoms of hypermagnesemia include nausea, vomiting, cutaneous ﬂushing, and dry mouth. Decreased or absent muscle stretch reﬂexes are typical. In some patients, rapidly progressive limb weakness may occur, sometimes accompanied by bifacial weakness, or rarely, extraocular and bulbar weakness. 34 NEUROLOGY OF RENAL FAILURE Acute kidney injury refers to a rapid decline in glomerular ﬁltration rate and is demonstrated by rising serum creatinine. Causes are divided into prerenal (decreased perfusion), renal (intrinsic kidney injury), and postrenal (obstructive) mechanisms. Prerenal azotemia is the most common mechanism, although intrinsic acute kidney injury (often due to sepsis) has increased substantially over the past three decades. 35,36 Risk factors for acute kidney TABLE 3-8 Treatment of Hypomagnesemia Severity Serum magnesium concentration (mg/dL) IV magnesium replacement dose Mild to moderate 1.0-1.5 8-32 mg/dL magnesium (1-4 g magnesium sulfate), up to 1 mg/dL/kg Severe Less than 1 32-64mg/dLmagnesium(4-8 gmagnesium sulfate), up to 1.5 mg/dL/kg TABLE 3-7 Treatment of Hypocalcemia Hypocalcemia Intermittent bolus dosage Mild to moderate (less than 7 mg/dL) Oral calcium supplements Severe (less than 3 mg/dL) 1-2 g calcium gluconate over 30-60 minutes, may repeat every 6 hours as needed 68 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

injury include diabetes, hypertension, and nephrotoxic medications. Acute kidney injury can present clinically with polyuria, oliguria, or anuria; nausea and vomiting; drowsiness; increased respiratory rate due to respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis; and hypertension. Uremic Encephalopathy The neurologic manifestations of uremia are nonspeciﬁc and no different from other causes of encephalopathy. 37 Historically, uremia was considered toxic to the brain: urea is normally excreted in the urine, increases during renal failure, and is dialyzed, leading to improvement. 38 However, other factors or intermediate substances may also contribute to uremic encephalopathy, including hypertension (leading to posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome [PRES]), toxic levels of renally excreted medications (eg, cefepime), 39,40 and subdural hematoma due to thrombocytopenia. Patients with shock-induced acute kidney injury may not improve with dialysis (suggesting coincident anoxic-ischemic brain injury). Therefore, neurologists should maintain a broader differential diagnosis for encephalopathy in patients with acute kidney injury before attributing mental status changes to uremic encephalopathy. Uremic encephalopathy generally occurs with a substantial increase in blood urea nitrogen, whether gradually or acutely. Uremic encephalopathy manifests when glomerular ﬁltration rate decreases below 15 mL/min/1.73 m 2 or when patients with kidney failure receive inadequate dialysis. 41-45 Uremic encephalopathy presents with lassitude and torpor, distractibility and inattention, and frequent yawning, which are commonly accompanied by multifocal myoclonus, asterixis, and tremor with posture and action. Asterixis (from Greek a-sterixis, translated as no steadiness or loss of posture) can be elicited by asking patients to hold out their arms and hands (as if to stop trafﬁc) or sustain puckering of the lips, a smile, or tongue protrusion. (FIGURE 3-5). Many textbooks refer to asterixis as ﬂapping tremor, but tremor is a misnomer because the movement is arrhythmic. 46 Asterixis is not speciﬁc to uremic encephalopathy and can be seen with many toxic- metabolic disorders. 47 Neuroimaging in patients with uremic encephalopathy is generally normal (aside from chronic changes of long-standing hypertension such as severe leukoaraiosis and lacunar infarcts). However, neuroimaging should be scrutinized for evidence of subdural hematoma or PRES, although usually FIGURE 3-5 Asterixes. Asterixis provoked by puckering of the lips (A); sudden shock-like ﬂexion in wrist, ﬁngers, and thumb when the patient is asked to hold out the arms (B); and ﬂexion in the hip and abduction with 60 ◦ between the legs (C). All these maneuvers cause lapses of contraction and thus position, often with a rapid jerk. Used with permission from Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. © 2025 Mayo Foundation for Medical Education and Research. KEY POINTS •Hypomagnesemia (less than 1.7 mg/dL) can cause muscle twitching, myoclonus, startle responses, and postural tremor. •Factors other than uremia that may also contribute to uremic encephalopathy include hypertension, toxic levels of renally excreted medications, and subdural hematoma due to thrombocytopenia. •Neurologists should maintain a broad differential diagnosis for encephalopathy in patients with acute kidney injury before attributing mental status changes to uremic encephalopathy. •Uremic encephalopathy presents with lassitude and torpor, distractibility and inattention, and frequent yawning, which are commonly accompanied by multifocal myoclonus, asterixis, and tremor with posture and action. CASE 3-2 A 76-year-old man with dialysis-dependent renal failure presented to the emergency department after a ﬁrst seizure. He did not recall the event itself but noted blurred vision preceding it. He had missed several dialysis runs over the prior weeks. On examination, he was disoriented in place and time and had slurred speech with preserved syntax and comprehension. His visual acuity was limited to hand movements, and his blink reﬂex was inconsistently present. The remainder of his neurologic examination was normal with no asterixis or myoclonus. His blood pressure was 200/130 mm Hg, 30% higher than recent recordings. His blood urea nitrogen was 50 mg/dL. MRI (FIGURE 3-6) showed hyperintensities in the frontoparietal and parieto-occipital regions, consistent with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). Improvement was noted in 2 weeks after control of blood pressure. FIGURE 3-6 Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) in the patient in CASE 3-2. Axial ﬂuid-attenuated inversion recovery (FLAIR) MRI with hyperintensities in typical locations (subcortical white matter [A] and occipital white matter [B]) in a patient with dialysis-dependent renal failure and a recent surge in blood pressure. COMMENT Seizure and encephalopathy in a patient with renal failure should not be immediately attributed to uremic encephalopathy. This patient’s visual symptoms, seizure, and hypertension in the absence of asterixis and myoclonus should point to PRES, and the patient requires MRI. 70 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 NEUROLOGIC MANIFESTATIONS OF RE NAL AND ELECTROLYTE DISORDERS Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

these disorders present with their characteristic features beyond encephalopathy (CASE 3-2). Electroencephalographic ﬁndings in patients with uremia are nonspeciﬁc but may reﬂect the degree of drowsiness with predominance of delta wave patterns, intermittent bursts of semirhythmic slow waves, and periodic epileptiform abnormalities, all without clinical evidence of seizures. Triphasic waves have been noted. Many patients with chronic renal failure have bilateral spike-and-wave abnormalities, which have no clinical signiﬁcance. Nonconvulsive status epilepticus is very uncommon but could be suspected clinically when patients have marked ﬂuctuations in alertness and subtle vermiform eyelid and arm twitching and intermittent gaze deviation, which warrant continuous EEG monitoring. As noted previously, patients with renal failure are predisposed to cefepime neurotoxicity, which can cause nonconvulsive status epilepticus. 48 Uremic encephalopathy should generally improve with hemodialysis, which is typically started with low ﬂow rates, shorter time periods, dialyzers with smaller membrane surface, and an attempt to limit major changes in plasma osmolality. Dialysis Disequilibrium Syndrome Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome is a rare condition characterized by symptoms ranging from headache and confusion to seizures and coma occurring during hemodialysis, most commonly with the ﬁrst session of dialysis at the end of the procedure. The condition is thought to be caused by cerebral vasodilation and cerebral edema resulting from dialysis-induced osmotic shifts due to urea removal. The culprit in this syndrome is the osmotic gradient remaining when urea clears more slowly from the CSF and the brain than from the blood (known as the reverse urea effect). Predisposing factors include high blood urea nitrogen, rapid dialysis, large-surface membranes, and high bicarbonate dialysate. 49-54 Modern dialysis protocols use short-duration runs and lower dialysate ﬂow rate, so this syndrome has become extremely rare (the author has not seen a recent single convincing case in an adult). Therefore, if new neurologic symptoms emerge during dialysis, PRES, drug toxicity, or subdural hematoma should be considered, with dialysis disequilibrium syndrome as a diagnosis of exclusion. Some patients develop mild headache and tremor during dialysis that subside after the procedure and recur when hemodialysis is repeated; these symptoms can be mitigated by adjusting the rate and ﬂuid composition of dialysis. Peripheral Nervous System Complications of Renal Failure Polyneuropathy is common in patients with chronic renal failure, typically presenting as a chronic length-dependent sensorimotor neuropathy due to axonal loss and secondary demyelination caused by unexcreted toxins. Symptoms are usually sensory at onset, but as motor symptoms develop, patients may develop difﬁculty with ambulation. More rarely (0.6% of dialyzed patients 55 ), an acute, fulminant, severe motor polyneuropathy mimicking Guillain-Barr´ e syndrome (but without ophthalmoplegia, severe dysautonomia, or need for mechanical ventilation) has been described. KEY POINTS •Neuroimaging in patients with uremic encephalopathy is generally normal. However, scans should be scrutinized for evidence of subdural hematoma or posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES), although usually these disorders present with their characteristic features beyond encephalopathy. •Dialysis disequilibrium syndrome is a rare condition characterized by symptoms ranging from headache and confusion to seizures and coma occurring during hemodialysis, most commonly with the ﬁrst session of dialysis at the end of the procedure. •If new neurologic symptoms emerge during dialysis, PRES, drug toxicity, or subdural hematoma should be considered, with dialysis disequilibrium syndrome as a diagnosis of exclusion. •Polyneuropathy is common in patients with chronic renal failure, typically presenting as a chronic length-dependent sensorimotor neuropathy due to axonal loss and secondary demyelination caused by unexcreted toxins. CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 71 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Acute mononeuropathy of the upper extremity following arteriovenous ﬁstula placement is referred to as ischemic monomelic neuropathy. These shunt-related neuropathies are due to sudden diversion of blood supply from the affected nerves. Patients with diabetes may be predisposed because of vulnerability to ischemia. Usually, the pulse is not affected, and skin temperature remains normal, but marked sensory loss, pain, and weakness occur in the distribution of the median, ulnar, and radial nerves on the side of the newly placed ﬁstula. New-onset pain and weakness soon after the procedure trigger the surgeon to intervene immediately. Urgent ﬁstula revision is usually curative if deﬁcits are minor, but symptoms may persist even with shunt correction if the injury is more severe. Chronically, atrophic tissue changes, claw hand, constant pain, and a profound loss of manual function can occur. 56-59 There is little information about the prevalence of ischemic monomelic neuropathy; the paucity of case reports suggests the disorder is uncommon. CONCLUSION Neurologists are often consulted to evaluate hospitalized patients with encephalopathy, seizures, or weakness. Most hospitalized patients have electrolyte disturbances, renal failure, or both, but neurologists should not be complacent and quickly attribute clinical ﬁndings to a toxic-metabolic encephalopathy. Factors to consider include degree of metabolic disturbance, rapidity of development of the disturbance, if and how rapidly the disturbance has been corrected, and whether other associated neurologic conditions may be at play, such as PRES, medication toxicity (eg, cefepime toxicity in patients with renal failure), or subdural hematoma (eg, due to platelet dysfunction in uremia). Teaching Neurology in a Busy Clinical Practice By Mariah Lyn Robertson, MD, MPH; Rachel Marie E. Salas, MD, MEd, FAAN ABSTRACT The history and evolution of neurologic education span from Hippocratic concepts of the brain as the center of thought to modern innovations in digital and arts-based learning. Nineteenth-century pioneers established neurology as a distinct discipline, and contemporary advances in neuroimaging, genetics, and telemedicine continue to reshape both what and how we teach. In today’s fast-paced clinical environments, educators can still be effective at teaching by using learner-centered strategies such as prebrieﬁng, microteaching, and structured feedback frameworks like the One-Minute Preceptor and the SNAPPS (summarize, narrow, analyze, probe, plan, select) model. These methods are central to integrating high-impact instruction into demanding settings of clinical care. In addition to traditional methods of teaching, emerging modalities, including simulation, blended learning, and intentional team-based methods, further enhance diagnostic reasoning and interprofessional collaboration. Technology-driven innovations such as teleneurology, virtual reality, and gamiﬁcation expand access and engagement, whereas arts and humanities approaches foster observation, empathy, and reﬂective capacity. Together, these strategies illustrate that even amid clinical demands, neurologic education can remain central to the work. By intentionally planning for and integrating education into our clinical care, we are preparing the next generation of neurologists to think critically, act compassionately, and teach adaptively. INTRODUCTION T he history of neurology and neurologic education dates back to Hippocrates with the establishment of the brain as the center of intelligence, willpower, and thinking, and the identiﬁcation of neurologic disease processes, such as epilepsy and hemiplegia. 1 The 19th century was a distinct time for neurology as a medical discipline, with Charles-´Edouard Brown-S´ equard at Harvard University and Sir William Osler at McGill University playing critical roles in identifying and understanding the nervous system and documenting neurologic conditions and pathologic reports, including Osler’s authorship of The Principles and Practice of Medicine. 2 In Europe, neurology emerged as a distinct specialty from psychiatry in the late-19th to early-20th century. Notably, two authoritative handbooks, both titled Handbuch der Neurologie, were published, one coauthored by Max Lewandowsky and Oswald Bumke and the other independently authored by Otfrid Foerster. These 310 CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM SELECTED TOPICS IN NEUROLOGY PRACTICE CONTINUUM AUDIO INTERVIEW AVAI LAB LE ON LINE CONTINUUM A LOUD RECORDING AVAI LAB LE ON LINE C I T E A S : CONTINUUM (MINNEAP MINN) 2026;32(1, NEUROLOGY OF SYSTEMIC DISEASE):310 – 325. Address correspondence to Dr Mariah Robertson, 5200 Eastern Avenue, Mason F. Lord Building, Center Tower, Ste 2200, Baltimore, Maryland 21224, mdewsna1@jhmi.edu. RELATIONSHIP DISCLOSURE: Dr Robertson reports no disclosure. Dr Salas has received publishing royalties from a publication relating to health care and personal compensation in the range of $500 to $4999 for serving as an editor, associate editor, or editorial advisory board member for HMP Global and PRIME Education, LLC. UNLABELED USE OF PRODUCTS/ INVESTIGATIONAL USE DISCLOSURE: Drs Robertson and Salas report no disclosure. © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

neurology-focused handbooks provided essential training for undergraduate and postgraduate students. Over time, clinical neurology has expanded on these rich foundations, keeping stride with advances in technology and understanding of the human body. In current practice, there exists a tension between delivering clinically excellent care and teaching the next generation of neurology practitioners. However, there also remains a clear understanding that recruitment, retention, and training the future of the ﬁeld is essential. Additionally, the richest learning happens in clinical settings where learners can apply content to real patients. In this article, we address these tensions and provide clear, practical ways to teach effectively in a busy clinical practice. EVOLUTION OF NEUROLOGIC EDUCATION Technologic and scientiﬁc advances have reshaped the content of neurologic education. For example, improved neuroimaging techniques and neuropsychological testing have expanded our understanding of neurologic disease, providing richer material for teaching pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management. 3 Similarly, developments in genetic testing and biomarkers such as those used in patients with Alzheimer disease have made precision medicine an increasingly vital component of neurologic curricula. 4,5 These content- driven advances have required educators to update and broaden teaching in undergraduate and postgraduate settings. At the same time, technologic innovation has transformed the delivery of neurologic education. The emergence of electronic learning platforms, telemedicine, and virtual reality tools has introduced new formats for instruction that support ﬂexibility, accessibility, and simulation-based practice. 3 Additionally, precision education (ie, using technology to tailor instruction to individual learners) has become more common, leveraging digital platforms and artiﬁcial intelligence to meet diverse educational needs. These delivery methods have become especially critical in an era of rapidly evolving knowledge and increasing demand for individualized learning experiences. Moreover, neurology curricula are incorporating health systems science, health humanities, and health disparities. 6 Educators should understand the importance of a diverse workforce and appreciate the necessity for curricula that are inclusive of the broad spectrum of patients who require neurologic care. This integration aims to prepare future neurologists to effectively address the diverse needs of their patients. It is important to include health equity–centered curricula in training programs, and these have been well received by clinic staff who feel that this training assists in understanding the unique perspectives of patients with intersecting, marginalized identities from across the socioeconomic spectrum. 7 FOUNDATIONAL TIPS FOR TEACHING IN A BUSY CLINICAL SETTING Neurologic education should mirror the complexity, nuance, and real-time demands of neurologic care. Effective teaching in this ﬁeld requires more than delivering content; it depends on intentional strategies that support learning amid clinical unpredictability. Foundational practices, such as prebrieﬁng, direct observation, and structured reﬂection, set expectations and create space for growth. By leveraging the natural rhythm of the clinical day and using brief CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 311 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

pauses while awaiting imaging, consults, or documentation, educators can deliver high-impact “microbursts” of teaching. A key principle to remember is that anyone can teach, even in a busy clinical environment. Teaching does not always require a classroom, formal lecture, or signiﬁcant dedicated time. Instead, it can happen naturally in brief moments throughout the day. These in-the-moment teaching opportunities, such as offering a clinical pearl, asking a guiding question, or explaining a decision, can be just as powerful as structured didactic sessions (TABLE 1). Over time, these bite-sized interactions will build learners’ skills and conﬁdence. The One-Minute Preceptor model 8,9 and the SNAPPS (summarize, narrow, analyze, probe, plan, select) model 10,11 are widely used, evidence-based models for clinical teaching in time-constrained environments. The One-Minute Preceptor model is best suited for preceptors seeking a structured, efﬁcient approach to engage learners in brief teaching encounters. This model might be TABLE 1 Teaching Principles and Strategies in Fast-Paced Clinical Environments Prebrief, observe, and reﬂect u Begin rounds with 1-minute learning goals from each learner u Observe one discrete skill (eg, neurologic examination, handoff) and debrief brieﬂy u Use the “What? So what? Now what?” reﬂection structure postencounter Use the rhythm of the clinical day u During downtime (eg, imaging wait), ask a “teaching question of the day” u Debrief a clinical decision while walking between rooms u Use hallway whiteboards to sketch quick neuroanatomic pathways or differentials Empower through frameworks u Reinforce scripts like “Where is the lesion?” or “Is it focal, multifocal, or diffuse?” u Distribute laminated cards or digital tools with heuristics or decision trees u Encourage learners to verbalize their reasoning aloud using the framework Prioritize psychological safety u Say explicitly, “It’s okay not to know; let’s think it through together.” u Invite all voices by asking open-ended questions to quieter team members u Celebrate effort and curiosity, not just correctness Think beyond traditional learners u Tailor teaching to interdisciplinary team roles (eg, nursing focus on stroke signs,physical or occupational therapists on motor syndromes) u Invite advanced practice providers to lead teaching segments on areas of expertise u Use interprofessional case reviews to model shared clinical reasoning 312 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 TEACHING NEUROLOGY IN A BUSY CLINICAL PRACTICE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

best for learners who are new to a clinical space or working in a clinical environment for a short duration (eg, half a day in an outpatient clinic). Speciﬁc to neurology teaching, it might also be suited for inpatient or emergency settings (eg, stroke codes) to allow for brief but impactful teaching moments focused on decision-making and feedback. SNAPPS is especially valuable for promoting autonomy and diagnostic reasoning in more advanced learners and may be suited for house staff. This model could also be used for outpatient neurology clinics where history-taking dominates time, as it can help foster diagnostic reasoning and autonomy. Instructors should consider learner level, time constraints, and desired educational outcomes when selecting or blending these models. Together, they reﬂect a shift in medical education toward intentional, learner-centered teaching in the context of clinical demands. TABLE 2 shows a side-by-side comparison of the two models for quick teaching in fast-paced clinical settings. TABLE 2 One-Minute Preceptor and SNAPPS Models Feature One-Minute Preceptor 8,9 SNAPPS 10,11 Origin and purpose Developed in the 1990s to promote efﬁcient, structured clinical teaching Created to enhance learner engagement and diagnostic reasoning during case presentations Target audience Primarily designed for preceptors to structure their teaching Primarily designed for learners to take an active role in clinical discussions Acronym or steps 1) Get a commitment 2) Probe for supporting evidence 3) Teach general rules 4) Reinforce what was done well 5) Correct mistakes 1) Summarize the case 2) Narrow the differential 3) Analyze the differential 4) Probe preceptor about uncertainties 5) Plan management 6) Select a case-related issue for self-study Teaching focus Emphasizes clinical reasoning, feedback, and teaching pearls Emphasizes learner initiative, reasoning transparency, and self-directed learning Cognitive load Low to moderate for the learner; guided by the preceptor Moderate to high for the learner; requires preparation and initiative Preceptor’s role Directive and facilitative; guides learning through targeted questions and feedback Responsive and collaborative; responds to the learner’s analysis and questions Learner’s role More passive; responds to preceptor prompts Active; drives the conversation and identiﬁes learning goals Time efﬁciency Highly efﬁcient for quick teaching moments during rounds or clinics Also efﬁcient, but may require slightly more time per case because of learner-led analysis Strengths Easy to learn and apply, reinforces feedback culture, works well with less experienced learners Encourages critical thinking, promotes self-directed learning, helps assess the learner’s clinical reasoning Limitations May underengage more advanced learners, preceptor-dependent Requires learner training and preparation, may be difﬁcult for novice learners CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 313 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

PRACTICAL STRATEGIES FOR NEUROLOGIC EDUCATION IN BUSY CLINICAL SETTINGS It is important to acknowledge that not all neurologic teaching occurs within traditional academic structures or with medical students or house staff. In many clinical environments, particularly nonacademic practices, neurologists lead team-based care and work alongside a diverse team of professionals, including nurse practitioners, physician assistants, nurses, therapists, and patients and their family members. Teaching in nonacademic practices often involves interprofessional learners, and many of the strategies discussed here are adaptable to each team member. Evidence in medical education increasingly supports the value of interprofessional workplace-based learning, particularly when guided by clear goals, brief structured teaching, and psychologically safe environments. 12,13 Although a deep exploration of interprofessional neurologic teaching is beyond the scope of this article, it is important to note that teaching moments are not solely for learners in formal educational programs. Prebrieﬁng and Priming One foundational strategy in busy clinical settings is prebrieﬁng and priming. Before seeing a patient, instructors can ask learners to focus on a single diagnostic goal, such as differentiating tremors, or on a speciﬁc skill, such as localizing based on the neurologic examination. This focused prebrief allows learners to enter the encounter with intention, a strategy supported by cognitive load theory and pretask instructional design. 14 Instructors can also use just-in-time priming strategies such as whiteboard sketches, quick checklists, or a framing question like “What are the localizing signs you notice?” to rapidly orient learners. Goal Setting Learners also beneﬁt from clear role deﬁnition and goal setting, especially in fast-paced clinical settings. Instructors can ask learners “What’s one thing you’re working on today?” or assign focused responsibilities (eg, “You’re going to perform the neurologic examination on the next patient.”). This approach supports autonomy and intentionality, which are central to Self-Determination Theory and clinical learning outcomes. 15 Self-Determination Theory is a psychological framework that proposes human motivation and well-being stem from fulﬁlling three innate psychological needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. When these needs are met, individuals experience intrinsic motivation and optimal functioning rather than acting from external pressure or reward. Reﬂective Practice Incorporating reﬂection during clinical care enhances diagnostic reasoning. Even brief prompts, such as “What surprised you in this patient presentation?” or “What would you do differently?” can prompt metacognition (the awareness and understanding of one’s own thought processes), encourage a growth mindset, and deepen clinical learning. 16 These reﬂection moments are also opportunities for educators to model their own thinking, uncertainty, or learning from prior cases, which reinforces a transparent and collaborative learning climate. 314 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 TEACHING NEUROLOGY IN A BUSY CLINICAL PRACTICE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

Structured Observation and Feedback Structured observation and feedback can be built into busy workﬂows by focusing on discrete tasks rather than full encounters. Watching a learner counsel a patient about imaging results, perform a cranial nerve examination, or document a complex case provides a framework for feedback. Research shows that brief, speciﬁc, and task-focused feedback (eg, “Your motor examination presentation was clear; next time, emphasize symmetry.”) is practical and more likely to be internalized and acted upon. 17,18 When delivered consistently over time, these microinterventions can result in measurable changes in learner performance. Frameworks and Schemata Neurology preceptors can also support learning by teaching diagnostic frameworks, such as the mantra “localize, generate a differential, prioritize tests.” These schema-based approaches support clinical reasoning under pressure and are particularly valuable in fast-paced environments. 19 Quick verbal scripts, such as “Where is the lesion?”, “What else could this be?”, or “What do we need to know now?”, make the reasoning process apparent and help learners develop illness scripts. Maximizing Downtime Opportunities In high-volume settings, instructors can maximize downtime moments to embed teaching without disrupting care. Waiting for imaging or room turnover can be an opportunity for 1-minute to 2-minute teaching pearls, image interpretation, or “neurology in 30 seconds” question-and-answer rounds. These microbursts of learning are consistent with evidence on spacing and retrieval practice, which supports durable knowledge retention. 20 Just-In-Time Teaching Tools Resources such as neurology pocket-card sets, article summaries, or decision support apps offer learners quick access to high-yield content during or immediately after clinical care, reinforcing self-directed learning in context. 21 Creating a Psychologically Safe Learning Environment None of the aforementioned strategies is effective without a psychologically safe learning environment. Neurology, with its complexity and diagnostic ambiguity, can be particularly intimidating for learners. Instructors can normalize uncertainty (eg, “Even experienced neurologists are humbled by dizziness.”) and promote curiosity over perfectionism. Modeling humility, openness, and analytic thinking creates a climate where learners feel safe to ask questions, acknowledge gaps, and grow, which are key aspects for workplace learning. 22,23 EXAMPLE TEACHING METHODS FOR NEUROLOGY Besides the ubiquitous role of clinical teaching for the practicing neurologist covered by the aforementioned techniques, there may be opportunities to apply evidence-based teaching formats to more formal teaching moments. For decades, lectures dominated formal teaching sessions, and to some extent, still do. Modern education theory encourages integrating active learning formats into the clinical curricula. This section reviews modern, learner-centered, CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 315 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

active techniques used for individual sessions, course design, and large-scale changes to the entire clinical curriculum (TABLE 3 24-28 ). Although many learning opportunities in neurologic education arise in inpatient settings, outpatient neurology clinics, although often busy and time-constrained, offer valuable and underutilized opportunities for clinical teaching across learner levels. As such, the teaching modalities discussed in this section can be offered in and adapted for outpatient and inpatient clinical experiences. Small Group Interactive Seminars Small group interactive seminars 24 are typically composed of a small number of learners (maximum of 10) and a facilitator, and they create space for active dialogue, problem solving, and collaborative learning that differs from passive lecture-based formats. Implementing small-group interactive seminars might be the easiest modiﬁcation to traditional lectures and the simplest way to convert to a more active learning format. Small group interactive seminars in neurology can create a rich learning environment for building diagnostic reasoning, integrating neuroanatomy with clinical ﬁndings, and reinforcing TABLE 3 Applying Teaching Methods In a Busy Clinical Setting Teaching method Description Application in a busy clinical setting Traditional small group interactive seminars (eg, image interpretation seminars) 24 Interactive sessions with small groups of students discussing clinical cases and concepts Effective for in-depth discussion and immediate feedback; could focus on a single image to save time Simpliﬁed team-based learning (eg, peer teaching, multiple levels of learners) 24 Active learning strategy involving small groups working on clinical problems Preferred by students, effective for complex topics, and can be integrated into clinical rounds in a busy neurology inpatient service Blended learning (eg, ask learners to apply asynchronous reading on localization to identify a lesion in a patient with hemiparesis) 25 Combination of online materials (videos, handbooks) and face-to- face sessions Useful in busy practice; can provide prereading or online videos and pair with speciﬁc clinical experiences Simulation-based training (eg, just-in-time simulationsbeforebusystroke callshift, run through rapid National Institutes of Health [NIH] stroke scale simulation) 26 Use of standardized patients or mannequins to simulate clinical scenarios Standardized, controlled environment for learning, useful for rareconditions,butmay lack real-world variability; could leverage online standardized patient platforms to facilitate this if in-person standardized patients are not available Outpatient clinic rotations (eg, 2- to 3-minute teaching bursts, maximizing microteaching, “most common causes of foot drop?” or “what factors would push you toward ordering an EMG?”) 27,28 Clinical rotations in outpatient settings, focusing on initial consultations and follow-ups Enhances clinical reasoning and diagnostic skills, directly applicable to real-world practice; partner with other specialties that care for neurologic conditions to expand breadth of exposures (eg, geriatric medicine) Longitudinal integrated clerkships (eg, integration of microteaching moments over several weeks or months, spaced repetition of concepts) 26 Extended clinical experiences over a longer period, integrating neurology with other disciplines Provides continuous exposure and learning, adaptable to various clinical settings 316 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 TEACHING NEUROLOGY IN A BUSY CLINICAL PRACTICE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

key communication and reﬂection skills. When anchored in real or simulated cases, these seminars allow learners to actively apply concepts in a collaborative setting while also engaging with the cognitive and emotional complexity of neurologic care. Whether through roleplay, visual mapping (use of diagrams or visual images to organize or connect ideas), or symptom-based frameworks (use of systematic approaches focused on signs or symptoms of a condition as a starting point for clinical reasoning), these sessions complement clinical teaching by offering protected space for deliberate practice, peer feedback, and deeper exploration of challenging neurologic presentations. Simpliﬁed Team-Based Learning Simpliﬁed team-based learning 24 is an adaptation of the more structured team-based learning pedagogy, designed to make active, collaborative learning more accessible in clinical settings. Traditional team-based learning is a highly structured, multiphase approach that includes presession preparation, individual and team readiness assurance tests, application exercises using real-world cases, and structured peer feedback. It is commonly used in formal coursework and requires signiﬁcant planning and facilitation. In contrast, simpliﬁed team-based learning maintains the core principles of team-based learning, including accountability, teamwork, and applied learning, but offers a more ﬂexible, streamlined model better suited for busy clinical environments (TABLE 4). In clinical neurologic education, simpliﬁed team-based learning allows learners to engage with case-based problems in small teams, often without the need for formal testing or extensive prework. This practical approach enables meaningful discussion, reinforces diagnostic reasoning, promotes peer-to-peer learning, and ﬁts naturally into the ﬂow of clinical teaching. By combining focused presession preparation with a small group application of clinical cases, simpliﬁed team-based learning supports the development of diagnostic reasoning, neuroanatomic localization, and management planning without requiring extensive faculty or technologic resources. Its ﬂexibility allows for meaningful engagement across learner levels and can be integrated into clerkships, residency didactics, or interdisciplinary teaching settings. Neurology’s emphasis on structured problem solving and complex clinical presentations makes it especially well suited to the simpliﬁed TABLE 4 Examples of Simpliﬁed Team-Based Learning Topic Case-based application Teaching focus Seizures 22-year-old patient with ﬁrst-time seizure Classiﬁcation, workup, safety counseling Weakness 72-year-old patient with acute right hemiparesis Localization, stroke syndromes Headache 23-year-old patient with episodic headache Red ﬂags, migraine management Vertigo 65-year-old patient with dizziness Peripheral versus central vertigo Neuropharmacology 82-year-old patient with Parkinson disease with medication side effects Mechanism, side effects, titration strategy CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 317 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

team-based learning format, which fosters peer teaching, collaborative reasoning, and a durable understanding of challenging content. Blended Learning Blended learning, 25 which combines asynchronous prelearning with real-time clinical application and postencounter reﬂection, offers a powerful framework for neurologic education in time-constrained clinical environments. In this model, learners engage with concise, targeted neurology content, such as video modules or interactive case vignettes, before clinical encounters, allowing synchronous teaching to focus on higher-order reasoning, localization skills, and patient-centered decision-making. Faculty can reinforce these concepts at the bedside through microdebriefs and use postencounter tasks to foster reﬂection and clinical integration. Blended learning accommodates varying learner levels and clinical settings, promoting ﬂexibility, efﬁciency, and a deeper understanding of complex neurologic conditions. Its adaptability makes it a particularly effective strategy for teaching neurology in busy inpatient and outpatient contexts. Simulation-Based Training Simulation-based training 26 offers an invaluable strategy for reinforcing neurologic competencies in time-constrained clinical environments. By providing a safe and efﬁcient space for deliberate practice, simulation enhances learner readiness for high-stakes and low-frequency events, such as acute stroke management, seizure response, and neurologic deterioration. Even brief, targeted sessions can meaningfully improve performance and conﬁdence, particularly when combined with microdebrieﬁng and repetition over time. Simulation also supports interprofessional collaboration, procedural ﬂuency, and communication skills, aligning with the cognitive and emotional demands of neurology. When embedded thoughtfully into existing clinical workﬂows through just-in-time cases, brief examination stations, or portable toolkits, simulation becomes a ﬂexible, high-impact educational tool for neurologic teaching in busy settings. This approach does not require standardized patients or separate simulation spaces; it could include practicing reﬂexes or other neurologic examination maneuvers in the clinic workroom before entering a patient room. Longitudinal Integrated Clerkships Longitudinal integrated clerkships 26 represent a less common alternative to the traditional rotation-based clinical clerkships in medical schools. Instead of spending several weeks on only neurology or other rotations, students spend a certain amount of time in a week or month with speciﬁc preceptors in that ﬁeld (eg, 2 half days per week in medicine and 1 half day per 2-week period in neurology). Longitudinal integrated clerkships offer a powerful platform for teaching neurology in time-constrained clinical environments, especially ambulatory clinics. Through repeated, real-world exposure to patients with neurologic conditions, students can develop diagnostic reasoning, neuroanatomical localization, and management skills over longer periods of time than in rotation-based clinical clerkships. Faculty can reinforce key concepts in small increments, offer iterative feedback, and guide learners in reﬂective practice as their understanding deepens. The longitudinal structure 318 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 TEACHING NEUROLOGY IN A BUSY CLINICAL PRACTICE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

allows for neurologic concepts to emerge across disciplines, whether in primary care, psychiatry, or emergency medicine, enhancing relevance and application. Longitudinal integrated clerkships provide an opportunity to teach about health systems science topics (eg, social drivers of health, access to care, bioinformatics, leadership) that are important for clinicians to know for practice. 29 For busy neurology preceptors, longitudinal integrated clerkships offer a sustainable approach to delivering meaningful role modeling, teaching through continuity, repetition, and relationship building, ultimately supporting deeper learning in the face of clinical demands. INNOVATIONS IN NEUROLOGIC EDUCATION The intersection of technologic advancement and the signiﬁcant increase in telemedicine during and in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic has resulted in a burst of exciting innovation in neurologic education. It can feel daunting to cover the full breadth of neurology for learners, but leveraging technology can help augment and support teaching in the busiest of practices. Telemedicine Telemedicine has now become a core part of neurologic education, allowing for remote consultations, monitoring, and treatment. The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) has highlighted the beneﬁts of telemedicine in neurology, including increased access to care, reduced costs, and improved diagnostic accuracy and health outcomes. 30 Telemedicine tools, such as wearable sensors and smartphone apps, are increasingly used for telemonitoring. 30,31 Moreover, equitable access to tools and the ability to use them are priorities when using these technologies in neurologic education. Virtual Reality and Augmented Reality Virtual reality and augmented reality are emerging as powerful tools in neurologic education. 32,33 These technologies offer immersive learning experiences that can provide a deeper understanding of complex neurologic concepts and improve clinical skills. Virtual-reality and augmented-reality applications in neurologic education include virtual simulations of neurologic examinations and interactive three-dimensional models of neuroanatomy. One signiﬁcant application of virtual reality in neurologic education is the development of virtual simulations for neurologic examinations. For example, the Virtual Reality-Based Neurologic Examination Teaching Tool is effective in teaching medical students how to perform neurologic examinations, with students using this tool scoring higher on neurologic physical examinations compared with those using traditional methods. 34 Virtual reality is also being used to create interactive three-dimensional models of neuroanatomy that allow students to explore and manipulate anatomical structures in a virtual environment. This is particularly beneﬁcial when access to cadavers is limited. A study by Gurses and colleagues 35 demonstrated that virtual reality–based neuroanatomy education signiﬁcantly improves learning outcomes and user satisfaction. Virtual Learning Virtual learning has also seen rapid implementation since the COVID-19 pandemic, with cross-institutional neurology resident education programs CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 319 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

leveraging online platforms to deliver lectures and interactive sessions, facilitating access to expert knowledge from diverse geographical locations. One of the primary beneﬁts of virtual learning is the expansion of access to high-quality neurologic education for learners worldwide. For neurologists teaching in busy clinical settings, this modality also allows for opportunities to leverage virtual resources for teaching learners and team members so that the didactic content can be reinforced in microteaching on the go using the techniques outlined in this article. In 2020, during the COVID-19 pandemic, the development of a cross- institutional, rapid-onset, volunteer virtual learning didactic series drew upwards of 120 residents per lecture, featuring faculty from across the country. 36 Innovative models such as this example broaden the learning community and ensure that residents receive diverse and comprehensive training. Another innovation is the development of virtual neurology morning reports, which are open-access, case-based clinical reasoning conferences available globally. 37 These sessions have been shown to decrease the perceived difﬁculty of neurology and increase conﬁdence in clinical reasoning among participants. This model highlights the potential of digital education to improve equitable access to neurologic education. During the early months of the pandemic, Johns Hopkins School of Medicine created the “Virtual Patient Rounds in Neurology,” which enhanced medical students’ clinical telemedicine skills and created international collaboration. 38 This elective increased students’ conﬁdence in obtaining histories and performing telehealth neurologic examinations. Lessons Learned From Home-Based Medical Care Virtual visits and telemedicine give neurologic educators and clinicians a unique opportunity to reconceptualize the virtual encounter as both a venue for clinical decision-making and a powerful tool for teaching and contextual understanding. Lessons from home-based primary and palliative care, which have long used the home environment to generate critical insights about patient function, support systems, and lived experience, are directly applicable to virtual neurology. Frameworks like INHOMESSS (impairments/immobility, nutrition, home environment, other people, medications, examination, safety, spiritual health, services) offer structured approaches to understanding a patient’s daily context and can be adapted to the teleneurology setting. 39 Remote visits offer a rich teaching opportunity, especially for learners to move beyond the traditional neurologic examination and consider a fuller story of people living with neurologic disease. In their guide to remote neurologic care, Tarolli and colleagues 40 emphasize that, although certain elements of the neurologic examination, such as reﬂexes or detailed sensory testing, are limited virtually, many components can be performed effectively with minor adaptations and may even be enhanced by viewing the patient in their home environment. For instance, functional assessments of strength, balance, and coordination can be completed through observation of real-world movements, such as rising from a chair, walking down a hallway, or performing ﬁne motor tasks with household items. Learners can also gain insight into cognitive and safety concerns by observing environmental cues, such as fewer organized medication setups, mobility aids, or interactions with caregivers. 320 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 TEACHING NEUROLOGY IN A BUSY CLINICAL PRACTICE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

An initial or follow-up virtual encounter can thus serve as a clinical and pedagogic tool. Learners can be encouraged to assess motor or speech symptoms, as well as the patient’s socioenvironmental context, health literacy, and support systems. This approach teaches them to “read the room” and recognize that neurologic illness exists within a broader framework of physical space, relationships, and access to care. Tarolli and colleagues 40 further note that remote visits can facilitate multidisciplinary engagement (eg, with physical therapy, speech therapy, social work), adding further depth to the learner’s understanding of comprehensive neurologic care. Finally, incorporating teleneurology into teaching invites learners to develop ﬂexibility, digital professionalism, and empathic communication in novel ways. The visual and verbal nuances of bedside manner, the importance of camera positioning, and the limitations of virtual interactions all become learning points. Preceptors should embrace this opportunity to guide learners in developing these emerging competencies while simultaneously reinforcing the fundamental skills of neurologic observation and patient-centered care. Arts-Based and Humanities-Based Approaches to Neurology Education There is growing evidence that the arts and humanities can serve as powerful educational tools in neurology, offering a means to enhance clinical observation, reﬂective capacity, and tolerance for ambiguity, which are essential skills in the neurologic examination and diagnostic process. In the context of busy clinical environments, arts-based approaches offer adaptable and efﬁcient strategies for deepening learning without adding time. Harrison and Chiota-McCollum 41 demonstrated the feasibility and value of an arts-based curriculum for neurology residents, showing that visual thinking strategies and narrative medicine exercises improved observational acuity, communication skills, and professional identity formation. Other studies have proposed an integrative model linking the humanities with clinical reasoning, suggesting that TABLE 5 Strategies for Gamifying Neurology Education Gamiﬁcation strategy Description Evidence of effectiveness Application in busy clinical settings Educational board games Integrate neurologic concepts into interactive board game formats, designed for small group play Demonstrated improvement in neurology residents’ knowledge and motivation 45 Can be integrated into inpatient or outpatient academic half-days, resident conferences, or scheduled didactic sessions Physical escape rooms Team-based puzzle-solving activities held in physical spaces, designed around timed challenges that promote knowledge application and collaboration Improved knowledge retention and learner performance compared with traditional methods 46 ; enhanced motivation and understanding of complex medical topics 47 Useful during orientation, academic retreats, or precall teaching sessions focused on reinforcing team-based neurologic decision-making Digital escape rooms Virtual or online escape rooms simulate clinical scenarios requiring collaborative problem- solving in real time or asynchronously Maintains the interactive and engaging nature of escape rooms; allows for ﬂexible and remote implementation 48 Ideal for asynchronous or cross- institutional neurologic education (eg, preclinic assignments, virtual academic days, interprofessional team training) CON TINUUM JOU RNA L.COM 321 Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

engagement with the arts may strengthen analytic thinking and support learners in navigating diagnostic uncertainty. 42 These skills are particularly relevant in neurology because ambiguity, complexity, and pattern recognition are core features of neurologic care. A broader review by Moniz and colleagues 43 identiﬁed widespread use of humanities in medical education, particularly in fostering empathy, critical thinking, and resilience. 42 Although they note the need for more robust program evaluation and theoretical grounding, their ﬁndings underscore the relevance of arts-based education across the continuum. Other studies have further highlighted that the humanities encourage engagement with complex situations and recognizing multiple perspectives, which supports the development of just and compassionate physicians. 44 There is limited empirical evidence directly evaluating the application of arts-based and humanities-based strategies in busy clinical settings, such as outpatient clinics or inpatient hospital rounds, where time and cognitive bandwidth are constrained. Most published interventions are situated in protected educational time, such as electives, workshops, or seminar series, rather than integrated into clinical workﬂow. However, some emerging innovations suggest the feasibility of incorporating arts and humanities-based strategies; for example, in-the-moment reﬂective prompts during rounding, the use of patient artwork or murals in hospital corridors to spark discussion, or brief visual arts interpretation exercises during shift handovers have been piloted informally and described anecdotally, although data are sparse. This gap presents an opportunity. The pressures of busy clinical settings (eg, fragmentation, bias, cognitive overload) are precisely the conditions under which humanities-based approaches may offer beneﬁt by recentering attention, interrupting snap judgments, and supporting reﬂection-in-action. Future research could explore microinterventions, such as 90-second visual reﬂection exercises, narrative “pause points” at the bedside, or museum- inspired mobile prompts integrated into rounding workﬂows. Such adaptations would help determine whether the arts and humanities can shape future clinicians and transform real-time care delivery. The Gamiﬁcation of Neurology Education Newer generations of trainees have found interest in gaming and escape rooms as innovative educational tools in medical education. These methods aim to enhance engagement, motivation, and retention of complex neurologic concepts among medical students and residents. TABLE 5 45–48 outlines quick strategies to consider for the gamiﬁcation of education, with some ideas for how this approach could be leveraged in a busy clinical setting. We add these innovations as food for thought, recognizing that not all busy clinicians can implement these in their practice. CONCLUSION Despite the pace and complexity of neurologic practice, this article presents several opportunities for in-the-moment teaching using microteaching strategies, like structured feedback, reﬂection, and diagnostic frameworks. Models like the One-Minute Preceptor and SNAPPS enable efﬁcient, learner- centered teaching, whereas telemedicine and virtual tools enhance access and 322 F E B RUA R Y 2 0 2 6 TEACHING NEUROLOGY IN A BUSY CLINICAL PRACTICE Copyright © 2026 American Academy of Neurology. Unauthorized reproduction of this article is prohibited. Downloaded from https://continuum.aan.com by David Acero-Garces on 5 February 2026

contextual understanding. Emerging innovations, such as gamiﬁcation and arts-based methods, offer promising ways to build empathy, clinical reasoning, and awareness of bias, even in fast-paced settings. Ultimately, neurologic teaching in busy clinical settings is feasible and essential. By integrating brief, structured, and learner-centered approaches into the realities of clinical practice, instructors can nurture diagnostic reasoning, deepen empathy, and support the development of thoughtful, capable neurologists for the future.
